text: I checked the ledger twice because the first time the figures made me angry and the second time I wanted to be sure the anger was warranted. It was. Six weeks to quarter day, and the column would not stretch. I knew what each line meant without reading the descriptions beside them -- malt, tallow, hay, the farrier's last invoice -- and I knew the sum at the bottom before my eye reached it, the way you know the last step of a staircase in a house where you have lived your whole life. I closed the book and set my hand flat on the cover. The kitchen was cold. The fire had been banked overnight and I had built it up twenty minutes ago, but the heat had not yet reached the table. October had come in hard this year. The windows were rimed at their edges, a thin crust of ice in the corners of each pane that would not melt until midmorning. I pulled my sleeves down over my wrists and went to check the flue. The taproom fire I laid from scratch. Kindling first, then the split logs Jem had stacked beside the hearth two days ago, then the draught adjusted until the flame drew clean and steady. The room smelled of last night's ale and tallow smoke. I opened the small window behind the bar to let the air move through, counted the bottles on the shelf -- gin, brandy, the local perry nobody much wanted but which cost me nothing because the farmer's wife traded it for stabling -- and noted that the port was lower than it should have been. I would speak to Nan. Or I would not speak to Nan, and she would know I had noticed, which amounted to the same thing. The bar top wanted wiping. I wiped it. The brass taps wanted polishing but that was Nan's work and she would do it when she came down. The hinge on the cellar hatch had begun to sing when lifted; I made a note to have Jem look at it, then made a second note that I could do it myself with a half-pennyworth of tallow, and struck through the first. Last night's accounts I had tallied before bed. Seven dinners served, four overnight guests, the private parlour let to a wool merchant from Bradford who drank a bottle of claret and complained about the fire. The fire had been adequate. The claret had been good. I did not write this in the ledger. The morning was the morning. I moved through it the way water moves through a channel it has cut for itself -- not thinking about the route, only following. Every door checked. Every lock sound. The kitchen stores assessed: flour enough for four days, butter for two, the bacon nearly gone. I would need to send to the butcher before Friday. I pulled my sleeves down again. The cold sat in the bones of the building like something that had been there longer than I had. Out in the stable yard the air was sharper, carrying the smell of frost and horse and hay. Jem was at the far end by the tack room, straightening a bridle on its peg with his one hand, the stump of the other arm braced against the post for balance. He did not look up. The yard was swept. The water trough had been broken through where ice had formed overnight. The horses were quiet in their stalls, their breath clouding in the grey light. I noted all of this and went back inside. Nan came through the kitchen door a few minutes later, pins not yet set in her hair, apron already tied. She took her place behind the bar without a word and reached for the polishing cloth. I heard the small sound of brass under her hands as I passed through to check the dining room. We had done this before. Many hundreds of times. The London coach was late, which was not unusual. What was unusual was the shortness of its cargo -- two passengers instead of the expected four, and both of them unhappy about something before they had even cleared the step. The first was a stout woman in a brown travelling cloak whose trunk had a split hinge and who wished everyone within hearing to understand that the roads north of Hatfield were a disgrace to the nation. The second was a thin clergyman who said nothing but looked as though he agreed with her on principle. I directed Jem to the trunk before the woman had finished her first sentence. He appeared at the side of the coach without being called -- he had heard the hinge from the yard, or he had seen the angle of the lid, or he simply knew, the way Jem knew most things, by attending to the world with the blunt patience of a man who had learned that surprises were seldom pleasant. He took the trunk on his shoulder and carried it inside. The woman watched him go with her mouth half open, her complaint stalled by the efficiency of its resolution. I settled her with a room number, a time for dinner, and a tone that did not invite further discussion. She went upstairs. The clergyman followed without being asked. The coachman found me in the yard. He was a heavyset man named Burden whom I had known for three years, and he had the look of someone who had been arguing with his horses since Hatfield. "The road's gone again past the Bell," he said. "Whole stretch of it. Mud to the axle." "The turnpike trust was meant to have it graded last month." "Well, they haven't. I lost twenty minutes and near enough a wheel. Someone ought to write to them." "Someone has. Twice." I handed him the ale Nan had drawn without being asked. "You'll want to water your team before you go on. Jem will see to it." He drank, nodded, and said nothing further. There was nothing further to say. He had a route to finish and I had an inn to run and the road would be mended or it would not, and neither of us had time to stand in a cold yard and wish it otherwise. The coach went. The passengers were settled or gone. Jem took the last of the baggage. The yard cleared and the particular silence of a coaching inn between arrivals settled over the place like dust after a cart has passed. Nan was behind the bar with one eye on the door. I noted it. Said nothing. The chandler arrived at half past ten with his cart and his monthly delivery and his new price. I had been expecting the new price. It was the fourth increase in seven months, and each time the increment was small enough to seem reasonable and large enough to matter. "Tallow's gone up," he said, setting the crate of candles on the kitchen table. "Sixpence more the dozen. Nothing I can do about it." "Tallow hasn't gone up. I buy tallow myself for the kitchen and I know what it costs." He shifted his weight. "It's the transport. The roads --" "You deliver to the Red Lion and the George and the Swan, all on this stretch, and you charge them fourpence less than what you're asking me. You have done since March." A silence. He looked at the crate, then at me, then at the crate again. "I'll take them at last month's price plus a penny," I said. "That's fair and you know it, and it saves you carting them back and finding someone else who'll buy forty dozen at once." He worked his jaw for a moment. "Tuppence." "A penny. And I'll settle today instead of on account." He took it. He had no choice that would cost him less, and we both knew it, and there was no profit in pretending otherwise. He counted the candles out, I counted the coin, and he left. The door closed behind him. The kitchen was quiet. I wrote the adjusted price in the margin of the delivery note and set it on top of the ledger. It was the ledger that caught me. The rent, the chandler's price, the column that would not stretch -- all of it pressed against the same fact, and the fact was this: if I married, the ledger would not be mine. The inn would not be mine. The negotiation I had just won would be conducted by someone else, or not conducted at all. This was not a grievance. It was arithmetic. I had done it before. I set down the pen and went to see about the kitchen stores. I saw him through the window before he knocked. Young -- not more than twenty, perhaps less. Thin in the way of men who have been thin for a long time, not the leanness of constitution but the drawn look of insufficient food over insufficient months. His coat was too large for him, an army coat that had belonged to someone broader. His boots were coming apart at the welt, the left sole separating from the upper so that I could see a line of grey stocking beneath. He stood outside the kitchen door with his hand raised and then lowered it. He looked at the door. He looked at the yard. He was deciding whether he was welcome enough to knock. I opened the door. He startled, then steadied. His eyes were pale and careful. "Have you eaten?" I said. "Not recently, ma'am. No." "Kitchen, not taproom. Sit there." He came in and sat where I pointed, at the end of the table nearest the fire. I set out bread, the end of yesterday's loaf, and a bowl of the broth that had been on the range since morning. He ate without rushing, which told me he had been hungry long enough to have learned that eating too fast would make him sick. His hands around the bowl were red with cold. He did not look up until the bread was gone. "Thank you, ma'am." "Where are you headed?" "North," he said. Then, as though that were not enough, "There's a cousin. Near Nottingham." He stood, and I saw him hesitate -- the brief calculation of a man who knows he should offer something and has nothing to offer. I did not help him through it. He nodded once, put on his cap, and went out. The door closed. I cleared the bowl and the crumbs from the table and set the cloth to soak. The broth wanted more water. I added it and moved the pot to the hotter side of the range. In the taproom, midafternoon, two men who had been sitting too long over too little ale found a disagreement about something -- I did not hear what, and it did not matter what. "Gentlemen. Not in my house." They looked at me. The louder of the two opened his mouth. "Not in my house," I said again. He closed it. They drank. Nan watched from behind the bar and did not need to move. The hours between the afternoon coach and the evening meal were the hours I used for everything that could not be done while guests were watching. Rooms checked and reset. The linen counted -- we were short two sheets, which meant washing tomorrow regardless of the weather. The kitchen set for the evening: mutton, potatoes, the last of the carrots from the garden. Nan restocked the bar. Jem was outside; I could hear the steady sound of his hammer against something, a fence post or a hinge, the particular rhythm of a man who works with one arm and compensates so thoroughly that you forget, until you watch closely, that anything is missing. The inn between rushes was the inn I knew best. Not the performance of hospitality but the machinery of it -- the unseen work that made the visible work possible. I moved through it without hurry because hurrying was wasteful, and without pause because pausing was worse. Evening came the way it always did, not all at once but in degrees -- the light thinning in the yard, the fires needing building up, the lamps lit one by one until the taproom had the particular amber warmth that made men feel they had arrived somewhere worth stopping. Four dinners served. The wool merchant from Bradford ate in the private parlour again and did not complain about the fire. Nan cleared the dining room. I tallied the bar. The last guest went up at half past nine and the inn began its slow descent into quiet. I locked up alone. This was mine, this final circuit -- the taproom fire raked down to coals, the guard set before the hearth. The cellar hatch: locked, the new singing in the hinge noted again and again deferred. The front door: bolted, top and bottom. The kitchen: range damped, surfaces wiped, the morning's kindling laid ready in the box beside the hearth. I checked the stable yard from the back door. Quiet. Jem would have settled the horses an hour ago. The cold came in off the yard in a single clean blade of air and I stood in it for a moment, letting it press against my face. On the second floor, a lit shape at Bridget's window -- the faint glow of a candle behind glass, and behind the candle, the outline of her sitting in the chair where she always sat, watching the yard below. I did not look long. My hands, when I turned back to the kitchen, were reddened from the cold water and the cleaning and the day. There was a small cut on my right index finger, a thin line where the edge of the chandler's delivery note had caught the skin. I had not felt it at the time. It was the invoice that had cut me. The letter was on the table. It had not been there this morning. Someone -- Nan, or Jem, or the afternoon post -- had set it on the ledger while I was occupied with the evening, and I had not seen it until now. The seal was dark red, stamped with an impression I knew. The handwriting on the direction was small and even and unhurried. Mr Phineas Stainforth's hand. My name, the inn's name, written out in full as though there might be some confusion about where I was or who I was. I picked it up. I held it. I turned it over once, feeling the weight of the paper, which was good paper, heavier than anything I used for my own correspondence. The seal was unbroken. I set it back on the table. I had known it was coming. I had known since the last quarter day, since the chandler's prices began to climb, since the small pressures that were not small at all had begun to accumulate with the steady patience of a man who did not need to be dramatic because time and law and custom were already doing his work for him. I had been moving faster ever since. It had not been fast enough. The kitchen was empty. The fire was down. The inn was locked and the day was done and there was no one to hear me and nothing to be done about any of it tonight. "Well," I said. "There it is."

I checked the ledger twice because the first time the numbers were worse than I remembered and I wanted to be certain I had not carried a figure wrong. I had not. Six weeks and two days to the quarter, and the margin between what I held in reserve and what Mr Stainforth's agent would expect was narrowing to something I could feel in my teeth. I wrote the total at the foot of the column, set down the pen, and closed the book. The kitchen was cold. The fire had been banked overnight and I had built it up twenty minutes ago, but the chimney drew badly when the wind came from the north and this morning the wind was doing exactly that. I could hear it testing the casements in the dining room, that particular low moan that meant the putty on the northeast window had cracked again. I would deal with it after breakfast. I pulled my sleeves down over my wrists and went to check the taproom. The taproom fire I built from scratch. The grate had been cleaned -- Nan had done it before she went up last night, I could tell from the way the ash bucket sat, pushed flush against the wall instead of left crooked the way Piers left it. I laid the fire with the efficiency of a woman who has laid ten thousand fires and does not think about the method any longer: paper, kindling, two splits of oak, the draught plate adjusted. It caught on the first match. I stood a moment to be sure, then moved behind the bar. Stock: three casks of ale, the second nearly kicked. Two bottles of brandy, one opened. Port, adequate. Gin, more than adequate because no one had been ordering gin this month, which told me something about the quality of our custom or the season or both. I made a note in my head and checked the till. Last night's take was where I had left it, counted and recorded. Fourteen shillings, eightpence. A Tuesday in late October. I wiped down the bar with a cloth that wanted replacing, rinsed it in the bucket, wrung it out, and hung it on the hook. The windows along the front were rimed at their edges with a fine crust of ice that caught the early light and made the glass look older than it was. Through them the yard was empty, the cobbles dark with overnight damp. Jem was in the stable yard when I checked out the back. He had the tack rail down and was straightening the harness, working through the bridles one-handed with the unhurried precision that meant he had been at it for some time. He did not look up. The horses were quiet in their stalls. I could hear one of them shifting its weight, the soft thud of a shod hoof on straw, and beneath that the steady sound of Jem's breathing as he worked a buckle free with his remaining hand and the stump braced against the rail. I went back inside. Nan came through the kitchen door a few minutes later. Pins not yet set, apron already on. She crossed to the bar without a word and began checking the glasses, holding each one to the grey light from the window and setting aside the two that were clouded. We had done this before. We had done it so many times that the silence between us was not silence at all but a kind of language, fluent and undramatic, that said the day has started and we are here and there is work. The London coach was late. I heard it before I saw it -- the particular grind of iron rims on the road surface that meant a heavy vehicle moving slower than it should. When it came into view through the yard gate it was listing faintly to the left, which meant the rear spring on that side was going again, and the coachman's face as he pulled up confirmed everything the sound had already told me. Two passengers instead of the expected four. The first was a stout woman in a travelling cloak the colour of mud who descended the coach step with the compressed fury of someone who has been uncomfortable for a long time and intends to make others pay for it. She had a trunk. The trunk had a split hinge, and she had opinions about this which she began to share before her second foot touched the cobbles. "The hinge will want a strap until it can be mended properly," I said. "Jem." He was already there. He took the trunk without comment, assessed the hinge with a glance, and carried it toward the inn with the split side braced against his hip. The woman watched him, opened her mouth, reconsidered, and followed. The second passenger was a thin clergyman who wanted nothing except to be shown to a room where he could lie down, and I directed him upstairs with a minimum of ceremony. The coachman climbed down stiffly. He was a man I knew by sight -- Aldridge, who had been running the London-to-Biggleswade route for three years and who treated the Great North Road as a personal grievance. "The surface above Hatfield is gone to pieces," he said. "Ruts a foot deep past the Wrestler's. I've put this to the company twice." "Have you put it to the turnpike trust?" "The trust." He said the word the way a man says the name of a creditor he has given up on. "I'd sooner write to the moon." "Write to Alderman Jessop at the Hatfield vestry. He chairs the trust committee and he keeps a carriage. If the road is damaging his own springs he'll act. If you write to the company they'll write to the trust and the trust will write back and nothing will be mended before Christmas." He looked at me with the particular expression of a man who has been given practical advice by a woman and is deciding whether to be grateful or affronted. He chose grateful, or near enough. He touched his hat, asked for ale, and I sent him to Nan. The yard cleared. Jem had taken the trunk inside. The coachman was at the bar. The clergyman was upstairs. The stout woman was in the dining room, where I could hear her settling herself with the effortful sighing of a woman arranging cushions. Nan had been wiping down the bar with one eye on the door through all of it. I noted this. Said nothing. The chandler arrived at half past ten. His name was Fenton and he had a cart and a boy and forty-eight tallow candles in a crate, and he had adjusted his price upward. "Tenpence the dozen," he said, setting the crate on the kitchen table as though the matter were already settled. "It was eightpence in April." "Tallow's dear this autumn. The renderers--" "The renderers are charging you what they charged you in April, Mr Fenton, because I spoke to Samuel Hart at the Baldock market last Thursday and he told me his price has not moved since Lady Day." Fenton's face did something complicated. "There's the cartage to consider." "Your cartage has not changed. Your route has not changed. You deliver to the Red Lion and the George before you reach me, and neither of them is paying tenpence because Mrs Adkins at the George would have told me so and enjoyed doing it." He was quiet for a moment. The boy on the cart outside was picking his teeth. "Eightpence ha'penny," I said. "And I'll take sixty next month if you can supply them, which will make your journey more worth the candle, if you'll forgive the expression." He did not smile, but he took the price. He marked the delivery note in pencil and pushed it across the table to me. I signed it and he left. The door closed behind him. The kitchen was quiet. I stood at the table and wrote the adjusted figure in the margin of the delivery note: eightpence ha'penny the dozen, sixty ordered for November. The pencil was blunt. I sharpened it with the kitchen knife, brushed the shavings into my palm, and tipped them into the fire. The figures from this morning were still in my head -- not the specific numbers now but the shape of them, the way they pressed against each other. The chandler's half-penny mattered. Every half-penny mattered, because every half-penny was mine to negotiate for only as long as I remained unmarried and the lease remained in my name. I had done this calculation before. I did not need to do it again. I picked up the crate and carried the candles to the store cupboard. The afternoon was ordinary. I reset two of the upstairs rooms, stripped the bed in the third where the sheets had not been changed since Saturday, carried the linen down to the copper. Nan served the stout woman a late dinner and reported that she had complained about the mutton, which was not surprising because the mutton was not good. I made a note to speak to the butcher. Jem reshod one of the post horses in the yard, the ring of his hammer carrying through the kitchen window in a steady rhythm that marked the hours more reliably than the clock. I was in the kitchen when I saw the soldier. He came around the back, as the sign on the front gate directed. Young -- younger than Piers, or perhaps only thinner, which made him look younger. His coat had been a uniform coat once and someone had removed the facings, leaving pale lines where the coloured wool had been. His boots were coming apart at the welt, the left one held together with a twist of cord. He stood outside the kitchen door without knocking. I watched him through the window. He was looking at the door the way a man looks at a thing he is not certain he is permitted to want. He was assessing whether he was welcome enough to knock. I opened the door before he decided. He took a half-step back. Up close his face was weathered past his years, the skin drawn tight across the cheekbones the way it goes when a body has been feeding on itself for some time. "Have you eaten?" I said. "Not since yesterday, ma'am. I had some bread." "Kitchen, not the taproom. Sit there." He sat at the table where the chandler's crate had been. I cut bread, ladled broth from the pot that had been on the range since morning, and set both in front of him. He ate the way hungry men eat -- not fast, not slow, but with a concentration that left no room for anything else. I did not watch him. I went about the kitchen and let him finish. When the bowl was empty he stood. "Thank you, ma'am." "Where are you headed?" He named a direction -- north, toward Baldock, and then perhaps further, toward somewhere a cousin might or might not still be living. He was not certain. He thanked me again and went out through the yard and I did not see which way he turned at the gate. I cleared the bowl and the plate. I washed them, dried them, and put them back on the shelf. I wiped the table. In the taproom after four o'clock two men who had been sitting too long over too little ale began a dispute about something I did not trouble to identify. I heard the pitch of it change -- that particular shift from irritation to intention -- and went through. "Not in my house," I said. "Settle it outside or settle it quietly, but you will not settle it at volume." They settled it quietly. Nan, behind the bar, had not moved. She caught my eye as I turned and I gave her the smallest nod, which meant nothing had happened and nothing would. The inn in the late afternoon was the inn between uses. Coach gone, guests settled or departed, the dining room empty, the taproom holding two old men and their silence. Nan restocked the bar. Jem was somewhere in the yard -- I could hear him, or rather I could hear the absence of the sounds that meant something was wrong, which was its own kind of hearing. I sat at the kitchen table with the accounts book and worked through the week's figures, then went upstairs to check the rooms that would be needed for the evening coach, then came down and started the preparations for supper. Potatoes, the last of the mutton, carrots from the cellar that were still sound. The ordinary work. The work that did not end and did not need to end because it was the thing that held everything else in place. Evening came early, the way it does in late October. The light went out of the yard by half past four and by five the windows were black. I lit the lamps in the taproom, the dining room, the hallway. The evening coach brought three passengers and no difficulties. Nan served supper. I ate standing in the kitchen, a plate of what I had made, not tasting it particularly. Jem came in for his supper and ate it at the table without conversation and went back out to settle the horses for the night. The last guests went up by nine. I tallied the bar with Nan, who handed me the take and said good night and went upstairs. I heard her door close. Then the inn was mine in the way it was only mine at this hour -- not as property, not as obligation, but as a thing I knew so completely that moving through it in the dark was no different from moving through it in the light. I checked the taproom fire. Down to coals, the grate still warm, safe to leave. I locked the cellar hatch. I checked the stable yard from the back door -- quiet, the horses settled, Jem's lantern out. The air was sharp and smelled of frost and woodsmoke and the faint sweetness of hay. I looked up at the second floor. A lit shape at Bridget's window -- the candle, the edge of her curtain, the suggestion of a figure behind the glass. She was watching. She watched every night. I did not look long. I came back inside and bolted the back door. I moved through the hallway to the front, checked the lock, checked the bolt. My hands at the end of the day were reddened from cold water and cleaning, the knuckles rough, and there was a small cut on my right index finger from the edge of Fenton's delivery note that I had not noticed until now. It was the invoice that had cut me. I looked at it for a moment and then I did not look at it. The kitchen. The range banked, the table wiped, the pots on their hooks, the floor swept. Everything accounted for. Nothing wasted. I had done this hundreds of times and each time the inn settled around me like a held breath released, and I stood in the middle of it and felt the specific quiet of a place that has been used hard and is now resting. The letter was on the shelf by the door where the post was left. I had not seen it arrive. It must have come with the evening coach, or Nan had taken it from someone during supper and set it there without mentioning it, which she would have done because she would have seen the seal and known that mentioning it would not improve my evening. Stainforth's seal on the envelope. His handwriting on the direction. Miss D. Dale, The White Hound, Great North Road, Hertfordshire. I picked it up. I held it. I turned it over once. The wax was dark red, pressed with a signet I had seen on three previous letters, each one more pointed than the last. The paper was good -- heavier than anything I used, smooth under my thumb. I did not open it. I had known it was coming since the last rent quarter. I had known it the way you know weather from the colour of the sky at morning -- not the specifics, not the hour, but the certainty that it is on its way and that nothing you do between now and its arrival will change its nature. I had been moving faster ever since. The chandler's half-penny. The extra candles for November. The short margin in the ledger that I checked twice this morning because the first time the numbers were worse than I remembered. I set the letter on the table. I looked at it. The kitchen was empty and the inn was quiet and no one was listening. "Right, then," I said.

I checked the ledger twice because the first time I did not believe the numbers, and the second time I did. Six weeks. That was what remained between the White Hound and the next quarter's rent, and between the next quarter's rent and the conversation I did not intend to have with myself about what came after. I closed the book and set my hand flat on the cover as though the figures might shift if left unattended. They would not. Numbers did not rearrange themselves out of sympathy. I had learned this from my mother, who had learned it from hers, and the lesson had not improved with repetition. The kitchen was cold. The fire I had built twenty minutes earlier was still finding its courage, the coals ticking and settling beneath the first true flames. I could see my breath if I turned toward the window, where the glass was rimed at its edges with a fine, persistent frost that had been there every morning this week and would be there every morning for the next several months. October had no business being this cold. I pulled my sleeves down over my wrists and went to check the bar stock. The taproom was dark and smelled of last night's ale and the particular staleness of a room where men had sat too long. I opened the shutter on the nearest window to let in enough grey light to work by and ran my eye along the casks. The small beer was adequate. The porter was low -- I would need to speak to the brewer before Friday. The gin was untouched, which meant either that last night's guests had been unusually abstemious or that Nan had watered it again, and I suspected the latter, and I did not blame her. I tallied the previous evening's take against what was in the cashbox. The numbers agreed, which was a small mercy. I wiped down the counter where someone had left a sticky ring the size of a saucer, checked that the chairs were pushed in, and moved to the second fireplace. This one I built more carefully than the kitchen fire, because the taproom was the room that paid for everything else, and a cold taproom was an empty one. The wood was damp. I stacked it with the driest pieces on the inside and the greenest on the outside where the draught from the chimney would season them over the course of the morning, and I used the last of the kindling without thinking about it and then stood up and thought about it. I would need to send Jem to split more before the evening. I added this to the list I kept in my head, which was the only list I had ever found entirely reliable. The staircase boards were cold underfoot as I climbed to check the second-floor landing. Two rooms occupied, both doors shut, no sound from within. The linen on the unoccupied beds had been turned yesterday. The washstand in room four had a crack in the basin that I had noticed three days ago and not yet replaced, because the replacement basin cost one and fourpence and one and fourpence was one and fourpence. I noted it again and went back downstairs. Through the kitchen window I could see Jem in the stable yard. He was straightening tack on the wall hooks, working with his one hand and the stump of the other in the efficient, unhurried way that meant he had been up for some time and had already seen to the horses. He did not look toward the house. He did not need to. The morning required the tack to be straight and the horses fed and the yard swept, and Jem attended to what the morning required without being asked, which was the quality I valued most in him and the one I would never embarrass us both by naming. I went back to the kitchen and put the kettle on. Nan came through the back door a few minutes later with her pins not yet set and her apron already tied. She crossed to the bar without a word, took her place, and began laying out the breakfast things. We had done this before. We had done this six mornings out of seven for three years, and the rhythm of it was so fixed that any interruption would have been more conspicuous than the routine itself. I heard her set out the bread board, the butter crock, the knives. The small sounds of a house preparing to be of use. The London coach was late, which was not unusual. What was unusual was that it was also short -- two passengers instead of the expected four, and both of them in the kind of temper that comes from being rattled over bad road in a cold coach for the better part of three hours. The first was a man who said nothing and wanted only to be shown to a room. Nan took him. The second was a woman of perhaps fifty with a trunk that had a split hinge and a great deal to say about it. "The clasp gave way at Hatfield," she told me, holding the damaged trunk as though presenting evidence in a court of law. "The coachman offered no assistance whatsoever." "Jem," I said, and Jem was there, which he had been since the coach pulled into the yard, and he took the trunk from her hands with a nod that conveyed both competence and the absolute minimum of social engagement. The woman watched her trunk leave with the expression of someone who was not yet finished being aggrieved but had temporarily lost the object of her grievance. I directed her to the dining room, where Nan had already set out breakfast, and she went. The coachman had come down from the box and was stamping his boots in the yard. He was a man I knew by sight -- Fenton, who drove the Tuesday and Friday route -- and he wanted a word. "Road north of Hatfield is gone to pieces," he said. "Ruts past the axle in two places. I'll not answer for the springs if it's left another week." "The turnpike trust meets Thursday. I'll send word to Harding." "Harding won't do a damn thing." "Harding will do a damn thing if I tell him his coaches are losing passengers to the Baldock road. Which they are." I handed him a cup of ale, which was what he had actually come for. "Two passengers today, Fenton." He drank, wiped his mouth, and set the cup down. "Aye. Well." He touched his hat and went back to the coach, and that was the end of it. He had his route and I had mine, and neither of us had time for more. The yard cleared. The coach pulled away with a sound of wheels and harness that I could have identified blind -- the particular rattle of Fenton's rig, which wanted a new linchpin on the off side. Jem took the woman's trunk inside. Nan was behind the bar, wiping down the counter with one eye on the door through which the guests had gone. I noticed. I said nothing. The chandler arrived at half past ten with his cart and his monthly delivery and his new price. "Tenpence the dozen," he said, setting the first box of tallow candles on the kitchen table as though the matter were already settled. I did not pick up the box. "It was ninepence last month." "Tallow's gone up." "Tallow has gone up a farthing the pound since March. Your price has gone up a penny the dozen four times in seven months. That is not the same arithmetic." He looked at me with the expression of a man who had hoped this would be easier. I knew his expression well. I had been seeing it for seven months. "I've costs, Miss Dale." "You have costs. And I know what they are, Mr Flood, because you supply every establishment on this stretch of road and they all talk to one another. You pay fourpence halfpenny the pound for tallow from Barnet. You sell to the Red Lion at eightpence the dozen. You sell to the George at eightpence the dozen. You would like to sell to me at tenpence the dozen because I am one woman with one inn and you believe I have no other source. But I do have another source. I can buy direct from Barnet and make my own, and I have done it before, and the only reason I have not done it recently is that your candles are better than mine and we both know it." He said nothing for a moment. Then: "Ninepence ha'penny." "Ninepence. Same as last month. Same as next month." He exhaled through his nose. He left the candles. He took his money. He went. The door closed behind him. I stood in the kitchen with the delivery note in my hand and wrote the adjusted price in the margin: ninepence. The pencil point caught the edge of the paper and I felt it but did not look at my hand. There was the afternoon to manage and the evening after that, and the chandler's ninepence was ninepence I would not have to find somewhere else, and that was the whole of what it meant. I carried the candles to the storeroom. Somewhere between the storeroom and the kitchen -- between one task and the next, in the gap where thought sometimes slipped in uninvited -- it came to me as it always did. The ledger. The rent. The chandler's price edging up and my margin edging down, and all of it weighed against the fact that none of it would be mine to fight for if I were any man's wife. The lease was in my name. The law was not. I had done this calculation before. I did not dwell on it. I went to check the dining room. He came around the back, as the sign on the gate asked. I saw him through the kitchen window before he knocked -- young, thin, standing outside the door with the particular stillness of a man who was deciding whether he was welcome enough to try. His coat was too large for him and had been too large for whoever owned it before him. His boots were coming apart at the welt, the left sole separating from the upper in a way that meant he had been walking a long time on wet ground. He was perhaps twenty. He was not threatening. He was not sure. He raised his hand to knock and I opened the door before his knuckles reached the wood. "Have you eaten?" I said. He looked at me. His eyes were older than his face. "Not recent." "Kitchen. Not the taproom." I stepped back and he understood and came in and sat where I pointed, which was the bench nearest the range. I set out what the kitchen had: bread from this morning, broth from last night reheated, a piece of cheese that was harder than I would have served to a paying guest but was food and he was hungry. He ate without speaking and without haste, which told me he had been hungry long enough to know that eating too fast would make him sick. When the bowl was empty he set down the spoon and looked at the table. "Thank you, ma'am." "Where are you headed?" "North," he said. He did not name a town. I cleared the bowl and the plate and he stood and went out through the kitchen door and I closed it behind him. The latch caught. I washed the bowl and set it on the rack and wiped the table where he had sat and went to see what Nan needed for the evening. In the taproom, two men who had been sitting since midday had found something to disagree about. I could hear the pitch of it rising as I came through the doorway -- not yet a fight, but the particular frequency that preceded one, when voices go tight and chairs begin to scrape. "Gentlemen," I said. "You'll settle it outside or you'll settle it nowhere, but you will not settle it in my house." They settled it nowhere. Nan, behind the bar, had not moved. She did not need to. The afternoon passed the way afternoons at the White Hound passed when there was no coach due and no crisis to manage -- which is to say, it passed in work. I reset the rooms that needed resetting. I checked the kitchen stores against what we would need for the evening meal and made a list that was shorter than I wanted and longer than I could afford. Nan polished glasses. Jem split kindling. The light outside went from grey to the colour of old pewter and then, slowly, to the colour of nothing at all. I locked up alone. This was not unusual. This was every night. The last guests settled or gone, the last fire checked, the bar tallied, the kitchen closed down. I moved through the inn's closing routine the way I had moved through it hundreds of times -- taproom first, where the fire was down to coals and the chairs were pushed in and the floor wanted sweeping but would get it in the morning. The cellar hatch: locked. The front door: bolted. The dining room: cleared, the shutters drawn. I checked the kitchen range and closed the damper halfway for the night. The stable yard was quiet. Jem would have settled the horses. I could see the line of the stable roof against what was left of the sky, and beyond it the second-floor windows of the inn. A light behind the glass in Bridget's room -- the shape of her, sitting at the window, watching the yard below as she watched it every evening. I did not look long. She was there. She was watching. I had seen it too many times for it to need anything from me. I came back inside and stood in the kitchen. My hands at the end of the day were reddened from the cold water and the cleaning, and there was a small cut on my index finger where the edge of the chandler's delivery note had caught the skin. I noticed it the way I noticed everything at this hour -- with the flat attention of a body that had been in use since before dawn and was nearly finished. The letter was on the table. It had not been there this morning. Someone -- Nan, or the afternoon post -- had left it beside the ledger, and I had not seen it until now, or I had seen it and had not let myself know that I had seen it. Stainforth's seal on the envelope. His handwriting on the direction. I picked it up. I held it. I turned it over once and felt the weight of it, which was the weight of good paper and sealing wax and nothing more. I did not open it. I had known it was coming since the last rent quarter. I had known it the way I knew when the porter was running low or the chimney wanted sweeping -- not because anyone told me, but because I paid attention, and the signs were there if you were willing to read them. I had been moving faster ever since. The chandler's price. The short coaches. The six weeks in the ledger. All of it the same current, and this letter the place where it surfaced. I set it down on the table beside the closed ledger. I stood in the kitchen with my cut hand and my reddened fingers and the inn locked and quiet around me. "Right, then," I said. No one heard it. There was no one to hear.

I checked the ledger twice because I did not trust the first answer. The kitchen was cold, the fire only just beginning to assert itself in the grate, and the tallow stub on the table had burned low enough that I had to angle the page toward it to read my own figures. Six weeks. Six weeks until the next quarter's rent came due, and the column would not rearrange itself into comfort no matter how steadily I looked at it. I closed the ledger and pressed my palms flat against the cover, as though I could hold the numbers still by force. The fire wanted building up. I knelt and fed it -- not generously, because the wood store was where it was, and I knew to the log what remained -- until the flame took proper hold and the kitchen began to give back some of the warmth it had absorbed through the night. Then the taproom. I crossed the passage in the dark, unbolted the shutters enough to let in the first grey light, and laid the second fire. The grate had been cleaned the evening before, which meant Nan had done it before she went up, which meant Nan had noticed I'd forgotten. I struck the flint and did not think about that. Bar stock next. I counted the casks by touch and by weight, tipping each one to gauge what was left, and marked the numbers against what I'd served the night before. Two-thirds of a cask of ordinary ale, a full cask of the stronger, the gin nearly out. The gin was a problem. Gin cost more than it earned, but the mail-coach passengers expected it, and a coaching inn that disappointed the mail-coach passengers would hear about it from the coachmen, and a coaching inn that heard about it from the coachmen would not be a coaching inn for long. I wrote gin on the slate behind the bar and underlined it. The windows were rimed at the edges where the putty had cracked. I could feel the cold pressing through them when I passed. I pulled my sleeves down over my wrists and went to the cellar hatch to check the lock, then back through the kitchen to the scullery, where last night's glasses stood washed and inverted on the cloth. All present. Nothing broken. The accounts from the previous evening I had already tallied before bed, but I checked the figures once more against the cash box, because if there was a discrepancy it was better discovered now than at midday when I could do nothing about it except be angry. There was no discrepancy. There rarely was. I locked the box and put it back. The inn was quiet in the particular way of early morning: not silent, but suspended, holding its breath between the last settling of the building's timbers and the first sounds of the day outside. I could hear the stable yard through the kitchen wall -- a hoof shifting on stone, the creak of a door -- and I knew Jem was already out there, though I had not asked him to be and would not have needed to ask. I went to the back door and opened it onto the yard. The air hit my face and I stood in it for a moment, letting the cold do its work. Jem was at the far end by the tack room, straightening a bridle on its hook with his one hand, his pinned sleeve tucked neatly against his side. He did not look up. The horses were fed -- I could hear them working at their hay -- and the yard had been swept. He had been out here since before I came down, I judged. Possibly longer. I went back inside. A few minutes later Nan came through the kitchen door, pins not yet set in her hair, apron already tied. She crossed to the bar without a word and took her place behind it, running one hand along the counter to check for dampness. We had done this before. Many hundreds of times. The London coach was late, which was not unusual, but when it finally came into the yard with a noise of hooves and luggage and a coachman's irritable shout, it was running short -- two passengers instead of the expected four. Both were women, and both were displeased. One had a trunk with a split hinge that had evidently been worrying her since Hatfield, because she began to explain its history the moment her feet touched the ground. "The hinge was perfectly sound when I left town, I assure you, and I was told this was a reputable --" "Jem," I said. He had appeared in the yard without being called, and the trunk was already in his hand before the woman finished her sentence. She blinked at the speed of it and then, finding herself relieved of her grievance's physical evidence, seemed uncertain how to proceed. I directed her to the dining room, told her there would be tea in ten minutes, and spoke with enough plain assurance that she went where I pointed. The coachman wanted a word. He was a broad man with a weathered face and the permanent squint of someone who spent his days looking into the wind. He came to me where I stood by the mounting block, and I gave him his two minutes because he had earned them and because the information was useful. "The road above Hatfield's gone to pieces," he said. "Ruts a foot deep past the second mile marker. Lost half an hour to it and near lost a wheel." "The parish was meant to mend it before Michaelmas." "Well, they didn't." "No." I had heard the same from two other drivers this week. "I'll send word to the turnpike trust. Whether they'll act on it before the ground freezes is another question." "Not my question," he said, and touched his hat, and went to see about his horses. The yard cleared. Jem took the trunk. The second passenger had gone upstairs without requiring attention, which I appreciated. Nan was behind the bar, wiping down the counter with one eye on the door through all of it. I noted this and said nothing. The chandler arrived at half past ten with his cart and his boy and twelve dozen tallow candles wrapped in greased paper. His name was Rudge. He was a short, fleshy man with small eyes and the particular self-satisfaction of a tradesman who believes he has no competition, which in his case was nearly true. He set the candles on the kitchen table as though presenting a gift and named his price. "Fourpence ha'penny the dozen," he said. I looked at the candles and then at him. "It was fourpence last month." "Tallow's gone up. The renderers in Hertford have raised their price and there's nothing I can do about it." "The renderers in Hertford are charging you the same as they charged you in June. I know because I asked them." Rudge's face did something complicated. "You asked --" "I asked them. Mr Arden's boy does the deliveries on Wednesdays. He's not a secretive young man." A silence. Rudge looked at the candles on the table as though they might offer him counsel. They did not. "Fourpence," I said. "Same as last month. Which is already a ha'penny above what you charge the Red Lion, and we both know it." "The Red Lion orders twice the quantity --" "The Red Lion will not be ordering anything next quarter if the rumours about their landlord's debts are accurate. You need my custom more than you need the ha'penny, Mr Rudge. Fourpence." He took a breath and let it out through his nose, which had the effect of making him look like an affronted pig. Then he said, "Fourpence," as though the word had been extracted from him at personal cost, and signed the delivery note I put in front of him, and left. The door closed behind him. The kitchen was quiet. I picked up the delivery note and wrote the adjusted figure in the margin, the nib of the pen pressing a firm line through the price he had printed. The paper's edge caught the side of my index finger as I folded it, a thin cut that I did not notice until later. I put the note with the others in the ledger and stood for a moment with my hand on the book. The figures lived in me whether I looked at them or not -- the rent, the chandler's fourpence, the gin that needed replacing, the coal that would need ordering before November. All of it tallied against the same fact that sat beneath every other calculation I made: that if I married, none of it would be mine to tally. The lease, the accounts, the right to stand in my own kitchen and tell Rudge his price was too high -- all of it would pass to a husband by operation of law, as neatly and completely as if I had never existed. This was not a grievance. It was arithmetic. I had done the sum before and it came out the same way every time. I put the ledger on the shelf and went to check the dining room. I saw the soldier through the kitchen window before he knocked. He had come around the back, as the sign by the front gate asked. He was young -- twenty, perhaps, or a year either side of it -- and thin in the way of men who have been thin for a long time, not the sharp leanness of recent hunger but the settled kind that has redrawn the body to its minimum. His coat was army issue, or had been once. His boots were coming apart at the welt, the left one held together with what looked like cord. He stood outside the kitchen door without knocking and looked at it -- at the door, at the yard, at the building -- with the expression of a man assessing whether he was welcome enough to knock. He was not sure. I opened the door. He straightened. He was taller than I'd thought. His eyes moved to my face and stayed there with the careful attention of someone who has learned to read a situation quickly. "Have you eaten?" I said. He hesitated, then shook his head. "Not recently. No, ma'am." "Kitchen, not the taproom. Sit there." He understood. He came in and sat where I pointed, at the end of the table farthest from the passage to the front of the house, and I put bread and broth in front of him because the broth was hot and the bread was yesterday's and neither would be missed. He ate without haste and without ceremony, holding the bread in both hands, and I went about my work in the kitchen while he did so because there was nothing to be gained by standing over him. When he had finished he stood and brought the bowl to the side of the basin, which was more than most did. He thanked me. His voice was steady and there was no performance in it. "Where are you headed?" I said. "North," he said. "My mother's in Huntingdon." He went out through the kitchen door and I watched him cross the yard to the gate, his broken boots careful on the cobbles. Then the gate shut behind him and the yard was empty. I cleared the bowl and put it with the others to be washed, and wiped the table where he had sat, and went to see about the taproom. Two men at the corner table were disagreeing about something -- I did not catch what, and it did not matter, because the disagreement had reached the stage where the subject was irrelevant and the volume was the point. One of them was half out of his chair. "Sit down," I said. "Both of you. Or finish your drinks and go." They sat. Nan watched from behind the bar with her cloth in her hand and did not need to move. The afternoon passed in the ordinary way. The coach was gone, the dining-room guests had settled their accounts or retreated upstairs, and the inn entered the long stretch between midday and evening when there was nothing to do but the work. Nan at the bar. Jem outside, rehanging a stable door that had dropped on its lower hinge. I did accounts at the kitchen table, reset the two upstairs rooms that had been vacated, checked the stores for the evening meal, carried water, blacked the range, and started the stock for tomorrow's broth because if I did not start it today there would be no broth tomorrow. The ordinary grind. The work that was always there, that did not ask whether I wanted to do it, that simply required doing. I locked up alone, as I always did. The last guests were settled -- two in the upstairs rooms, one in the small chamber off the dining room who was leaving before dawn and had paid in advance. The taproom fire I raked down to coals and set the guard. The bar tallied. The cellar hatch locked. The kitchen closed, the range damped, the back door bolted. I went through the inn on my last circuit the way I had gone through it on my first: checking each room, each window, each latch. The taproom fire was low and steady behind its guard. The passage was dark. The cellar hatch was as I had left it. The stable yard was quiet when I stepped outside -- Jem had settled the horses and gone to his quarters above the tack room, and I could see no light from his window, which meant he was either asleep or sitting in the dark, which with Jem amounted to the same thing. On the second floor, a light behind Bridget's window. The shape of her, sitting in her chair, watching the yard below. I did not look long. She was there. She was watching. She was always there. I went back inside and bolted the yard door. My hands at the end of the day: reddened from cold water and cleaning, a small cut on the right index finger from the edge of the chandler's delivery note where I had folded it. I had not felt it happen. It was the invoice that had cut me. In the kitchen, on the table where I had left the morning's post to be sorted, the letter was waiting. I had not opened it when it arrived because I was serving the midday meal, and I had not opened it after because there had been the chandler's candles to put away and the soldier to feed and the taproom to manage, and because I had known what it was from the moment I saw it. Stainforth's seal on the envelope. His handwriting on the direction. I picked it up. I held it. I turned it over once, feeling the weight of the paper, which was heavier than the paper I used for my own correspondence because Stainforth could afford heavier paper and because men like Stainforth understood that the weight of a letter said something before the words inside it did. I did not open it. I set it on the table and looked at it lying there against the scrubbed wood, and I was aware, in the flat way I was aware of most things by this hour of the night, that I had known this was coming since the last rent quarter. Since before that. Since the chandler raised his prices the second time and I calculated the margin and understood that the margin was not the problem. The margin was a symptom. The problem was the man whose seal was on this envelope, and what he wanted, and the fact that what he wanted was something he had the power to take. I had been moving faster ever since. It had not been fast enough. The kitchen was empty. The fire was out. The inn was locked and the inn was quiet and there was no one to hear me and no reason to speak and I said it anyway, to the room, to no one. "Well then."

I checked the ledger twice because the first time I did not believe my own arithmetic. Six weeks. That was what remained between the White Hound and the next quarter's rent, and the column of figures I had tallied against what was owed did not leave the margin I wanted. I closed the book and pressed my palm flat on the cover as though I could hold the numbers still. The kitchen was cold. The fire had been banked overnight and I had built it up twenty minutes ago but the warmth had not yet reached the table where I sat. October had arrived like a creditor -- early, unwelcome, and disinclined to negotiate. The windows were rimed at their edges, a thin crust of white that would melt by nine and return by nightfall. I pulled my sleeves down over my wrists and stood. The kitchen first, always. Porridge on for the staff. Water drawn and set to heat. Then the taproom: I crossed the passage and knelt at the grate, raking out last night's ash and building the new fire with the efficiency of a woman who has done this perhaps four thousand times and does not intend to do it slowly on the four thousand and first. The taproom smelled of spilled ale and cold stone and the particular staleness of a room that has held too many bodies and released them all at once. I opened the small window behind the bar to let the air move. Bar stock checked. Two casks wanted tapping before the day's custom began. The gin was low. I made a note of it in the margin of yesterday's accounts, which I had left on the shelf behind the bar and which still wanted finishing. I finished them standing up, the pencil's scratch the only sound in the room. Fourpence out on the ale. Somewhere between Nan's reckoning and the cask there was fourpence I could not find, and I wrote it down because I wrote everything down, and moved on. The stairs needed the banister tightened at the second-floor turning. I had noticed it three days ago. It could wait another three but not much longer; a guest with a heavy grip would work the whole post loose. I tested it with my hand as I came back through. Still holding. I gave it a week. Outside, the yard was grey and sharp with frost. Jem was in the stable, which I knew because the stable door was open and because I could hear the particular rhythm of his work -- the scrape and shift of a man who has learned to do with one hand and a stump what most men cannot manage with two. He was straightening tack on the wall pegs, the bridles hung in the order I had asked for two years ago and which he had maintained without reminder since. He did not look up when I stood in the doorway. He did not need to. I checked the water trough -- not frozen, not yet -- and went back inside. Nan came through the kitchen door a few minutes later, pins in her hair not yet set, apron already on. She crossed to the bar and took her station behind it with the unhurried certainty of a woman who has stood in that exact spot six days a week for seven years. She ran a cloth along the counter without looking at it and reached beneath for the clean tankards. I did not greet her. She did not greet me. The morning did not require it. The London coach came in forty minutes late, which was not so unusual as to remark on but unusual enough that I had already adjusted the kitchen accordingly. When the horn sounded on the road I was at the front door, and when the thing lurched into the yard I saw at once that we were short: two passengers instead of the expected four, both climbing down with the stiff-backed displeasure of people who have been rattled for several hours longer than they bargained for. The first was a woman of fifty or so, well dressed, travelling with a trunk that had split its hinge sometime between Islington and here. She spotted the damage the moment Jem hauled it down and let out a sound that suggested the trunk had done her a personal injury. "The roads," she said to no one. "The roads are an absolute disgrace." The trunk was not my problem. I directed Jem toward it with a look, and he was already moving -- he had seen the hinge before she had finished her complaint. He tucked the trunk under his arm and carried it toward the inn, and the woman, who had been preparing a longer speech, found that its subject had been removed. She stared at me. I told her that her room was upstairs to the left and that breakfast would be ready in twenty minutes, and that the trunk would be mended by the time she had finished eating. She went. The second passenger was a man who said nothing and wanted only the dining room. I showed him in. The coachman was Tom Fletch, who drove the Thursday route and had opinions about everything between here and Barnet. He came to me while Jem was seeing to the horses. "North road's cut up bad past Hatfield," he said. "There's a stretch by the hill where the ruts are a foot deep. Axle-deep in wet." "I know. The parish won't mend it before spring." "Spring." He spat to the side. "I'll lose a wheel before spring." "You might try the lower road past the mill. It adds ten minutes but the surface holds." He looked at me for a moment in the way men sometimes did when I gave them an answer that was both practical and unwelcome, then nodded once and went to see about his horses. The yard cleared. The morning settled into its next shape. Nan had been wiping down the bar with one eye on the door through all of it, and I noted this without saying anything, because there was nothing to say. She was watching. She was always watching. It was one of the reasons I trusted her. At half past ten the chandler came. His name was Garvey, and he was a short, square man with clean hands and a leather apron and a cart that announced itself by the smell of tallow before it turned the corner. He carried the candles in himself -- three dozen, wrapped in paper, the usual monthly order -- and set them on the bar with the invoice on top. I looked at the invoice. I looked at him. "Eightpence a dozen," I said. "Tallow's gone up, Miss Dale. I can't help what tallow does." "Tallow hasn't gone up. It went up in March, and again in June, and the price you're paying Hatcher for it has not moved since July. I know because Hatcher drinks here on Tuesdays and is not careful with his conversation." Garvey's expression did not change, which told me he had expected this. "My costs are my own business." "They are. And my custom is mine. There are three other establishments between here and Baldock who would take a standing order at sevenpence, and you know it, because you supply two of them at that price already." He was quiet for a moment. I let him be quiet. Time was his weapon and mine; the difference was that his cart was in my yard and his candles were on my bar and he would have to take them away again if we could not agree, and no man likes to load a cart twice. "Sevenpence ha'penny," he said. "Sevenpence. Same as last quarter. And I'll take four dozen next month if you can manage it." He worked this over. Four dozen was more than I strictly needed, but the winter was coming and I would use them, and the promise of a larger order in November was worth more to him than the ha'penny he was trying to hold. "Sevenpence," he said. "Four dozen in November." "I'll have the payment for you Friday." He left. The candles stayed. I picked up the invoice and wrote the corrected figure in the margin, and the pencil point caught the edge of the paper and nicked the side of my index finger, a thin line of red that I did not notice until later. I stood behind the bar with the invoice in my hand and the numbers in my head -- the candles, the rent, the casks, the fourpence I could not find -- and for one moment the calculation I had made a hundred times before surfaced again, flat and unbidden. If I married, none of this would be mine to negotiate for. The lease, the stock, the right to stand in my own taproom and tell a chandler what I would and would not pay -- all of it transferred, by law, to a husband. It was not a feeling. It was arithmetic. I set the invoice on the shelf and went to check the kitchen. The soldier came around the back, as the sign asked. I saw him through the window before he knocked. Young -- not more than twenty, if that. Thin in the way of a body that has been working on too little food for too long, the flesh drawn tight over the bones of his face. His coat was army issue, worn through at the elbows. His boots were coming apart at the welt, the sole of the left one separating from the upper so that I could see the grey of his stocking underneath. He stood outside the kitchen door and did not knock immediately. He looked at the door, and at the yard, and at his own hands, and I understood that he was trying to determine whether he was welcome enough to ask. I opened the door. He straightened. "Ma'am." "Have you eaten?" A pause. He could have lied. He did not. "Not recently, ma'am." "Kitchen, not the taproom. Sit there." I pointed to the bench at the long table where the staff took their meals, and he came in and sat where I told him with the automatic obedience of a man who has spent recent years doing as he was told. I cut bread and ladled broth from the pot on the range -- it was yesterday's, reheated, but it was hot and it was food -- and set both in front of him without ceremony. He ate. Not fast, not desperately, but steadily and completely, the way a man eats who has learned that you finish what is in front of you because you do not know when the next meal is coming. I did not watch him. I went about the kitchen and let him eat. When he was finished he stood. "Thank you, ma'am. I'm grateful." "Where are you headed?" "North," he said. "There's work in the mills, I've been told." I had been told this too. I had also been told it was not true, or not true enough for all the men who were walking north to find it. I said nothing about this. He knew it already, or he would find out, and either way it was not mine to say. He went. I closed the door. I cleared the bowl and the bread plate and wiped the table where he had been sitting, and I did the next thing, which was checking the stock for the evening meal. In the taproom at half past two, a dispute. Two men -- guests from the morning coach and a local who had come in for his dinner -- and something about a chair, or a seat near the fire, the kind of nothing that becomes something when men have been sitting too long with too much ale. One of them had stood up. The other was deciding whether to. "Sit down," I said, from the doorway. "Both of you. Or finish your drinks and go." They sat. Nan, behind the bar, caught my eye and looked away. She had not needed to intervene. She would not have. But she had been ready. The afternoon opened into the gap between coaches -- the long quiet hours when the inn rested. Nan at the bar, polishing what did not need polishing. Jem in the yard. I worked through the accounts for the week, reset the rooms on the second floor where the beds wanted turning, and prepared the kitchen for the evening's supper. Mutton, potatoes, bread. The ordinary machinery of the day, grinding forward, each task leading to the next without interruption or event. I moved through it the way I always moved through it, by doing the thing that was in front of me and not looking past it. The evening came in early, as October evenings do -- the light failing by five, the lamps lit, the last of the day's custom settled or gone. Supper served, supper cleared. The kitchen cleaned down. Nan tallied the bar and brought me the figures, which were correct, and I sent her to bed. Jem had settled the horses an hour ago. The yard was quiet. I made my final circuit alone. Taproom fire banked down to coals, the grate swept, the chairs pushed in. The cellar hatch locked -- I checked it by pulling, which I did every night, because my mother had done it every night. The front door bolted, top and bottom. The passage to the kitchen swept. The stairs, where the banister gave its small movement under my hand and reminded me again that it wanted tending. Up to the second floor. The rooms were quiet. At the far end of the corridor, the window that looked out over the yard. I paused there. Below, the stable was dark. Jem's quarters above it were dark. Across the yard, the building where Bridget kept her rooms showed a lit shape at her window -- the pale glow of a single candle behind the glass, and the outline of a figure sitting in the chair where she always sat, watching the yard as she watched it every evening, as she had watched it for twenty-six years. I did not look long. She was there. She was always there. I came back down to the kitchen. My hands, I noticed now, were reddened from the cold water and the cleaning -- the hot water had run out an hour ago and I had finished with cold, as I usually did. The small cut on my index finger, from the edge of the chandler's invoice, had dried to a thin dark line. I had not felt it at the time. It was the paper that had done it. It was always paper that cut deepest when you were not attending. The letter was on the table. It had not been there this morning. Someone had brought it in -- Nan, probably, from the afternoon post -- and left it on the kitchen table where I would find it. The seal was dark red, pressed firm and clean. I knew the seal. I knew the handwriting on the direction: Miss D. Dale, The White Hound, Great North Road, Hertfordshire. Stainforth. I picked it up. I turned it over once. The paper was heavy, the kind of paper a man uses when he wants you to understand that he can afford good paper and you cannot. The seal held. I did not break it. I had known it was coming. I had known since the last quarter, since the chandler's first price increase, since the road got worse and the coaches got shorter and the rooms stood empty on nights they should have been full. I had been moving faster ever since -- the ledger, the negotiations, the accounts checked twice because once was not enough and because speed was the only answer I had to a problem that was not, in the end, a problem of speed. I set the letter down on the table, unopened, and stood in the kitchen with my hands at my sides. "Right then," I said. No one heard. The kitchen was empty, the inn was locked, and the night was cold, and tomorrow the fire would want building again.

Tallied the week's coal deliveries first, then moved back to the columns that mattered. Six weeks, give or take three days, before the next quarter's rent became a question she could not answer with what was in the strongbox. She had known this when she opened the ledger, and she knew it still when she closed it. The knowing did not change the number. The fire in the kitchen range was already built up -- she had done that before sitting down, carrying the first coal herself rather than waiting for Jem, because waiting for anyone was a habit she had never managed to form. The kitchen was cold despite the fire. October had come in with its hands in its pockets and its teeth showing, and the range needed another hour before the room would be anything but functional. She pulled her sleeves down over her wrists and went to check the taproom. The taproom fire was slower, banked from the night before. She worked the damper, set two fresh logs across the coals, and stood back. The room was empty in the way of spaces that had been full only hours ago -- the particular vacancy of cleared tables and swept flags and upended stools, everything back in its place and yet not quite at rest. The bar stock needed checking. She did it by touch as much as by sight: casks of mild and bitter, the bottles behind the bar in their proper order, the measure on its hook. The porter was getting low. She noted it. She found the slate where Nan kept the day's tally and read it in the grey light from the window. The glass had rimed at the edges during the night. Beyond it, the yard was a dark shape, the stable block a darker one, the road beyond the gate still invisible. She did not need to see the road. She knew its sounds. The accounts from the previous night she tallied at the bar counter, working quickly, her handwriting compressed and even in the column. The supper sittings had been decent -- two private parties, one family, the regular table of drovers who came through on Wednesdays. The room and board for three guests. Against that: Nan's wages, the kitchen provisions for the week, the last delivery of firewood that had come in two days ago at a price she had been intending to revisit with the supplier. She set the pen down, looked at the total for a moment, and moved on. The cellar hatch: locked, as she had left it. The kitchen: fire drawing properly now, the range beginning to tick with heat. She filled the kettle, set it on, and checked the larder against what she would need before the morning coach. Bread from yesterday, enough for the guests' breakfast. The remains of a joint. Eggs from the village girl who brought them on Tuesdays, which was today, which meant they would arrive before the coach and she should be ready for that before anything else. She set a pan on the range. Through the back window she could see the stable yard beginning to separate from the dark -- the shapes of it becoming specific: the pump, the gate, the mounting block with its stone cap white with frost. Jem was already there. She could make him out by the stable door, working at something -- the tack, by the set of his shoulders, the particular angle of concentration. She had not asked him to do it. The morning required it, and Jem knew what the morning required. She watched him for a moment without meaning to, then turned back to the eggs. Nan came through the kitchen door some minutes later. The pins in her hair were only half set, but her apron was already tied, and she moved to the bar without stopping -- took up the cloth from its hook, checked the state of the glasses on the shelf, set her back to the counter in the particular stance that meant she was ready before she appeared to be. Neither of them said anything. There was nothing that needed saying. The kettle came to a boil. Dinah made two cups and left one on the end of the bar for Nan without comment. --- The London coach was fifteen minutes late, which was not unusual, and short by two passengers, which was not unusual either in this season when the better-off found reasons not to travel north until they absolutely had to. The ones who had come -- a woman with an aggressively split trunk and a manufacturer from Royston who had been occupying the same corner of the coach since Barnet and had opinions about it -- both arrived in the yard with the specific ill temper of people who felt their discomfort should be somebody's fault. The woman with the trunk had already begun on the coachman by the time Dinah reached the yard. The coachman -- Grice, who ran the London to Baldock route on Tuesdays and Thursdays and had been doing so since before Dinah took over the inn -- was not listening. He was watching Dinah cross the yard with the expression of a man who had a specific thing to say and was calculating the fastest route to saying it. She got there before the woman with the trunk could redirect her complaints. "Grice." "Miss Dale." He pulled his hat brim briefly. "I need a word about the north road above Hatfield. The surface above the second milestone's breaking up bad -- flints lifting, a rut opening on the eastern verge that wants filling before the first hard frost. Near had a wheel in it yesterday." "Has it been reported to the surveyor?" "Reported to him in September. Man doesn't move unless his axle's personally at risk." He shifted his weight. "I'm telling you because your coaches run that road. If a wheel goes there in November it goes in the mud and it doesn't come out fast." "I'll write to the surveyor today and to the trustees by the end of the week. I can have Jem look at the worst of the flinting when he goes out with the hire trap tomorrow -- he'll give you his honest assessment and you'll have something in writing before Saturday's run." Grice looked at her for a moment, then nodded. "That'll do." "The trunk," she said, because the woman behind him was working up to something. "Hinge has gone." "Jem." She did not raise her voice. Jem was already crossing the yard, having assessed the situation through some ostler's instinct that she had long since stopped trying to explain. The woman with the trunk transferred her attention to him, which was its own kind of resolution. Grice gathered his lines. "There's also a matter of the ostler's--" "Same terms as last quarter," Dinah said. "I checked the records before you arrived." He had not argued. There was nothing to argue with. The yard began to clear. The manufacturer from Royston went inside without incident. The trunk went with Jem. Grice's horses were watered and the coach turned round in the time it usually took, the yard filling briefly with the noise of harness and instruction and then emptying again as completely as a bucket tipped over. The gate settled on its post. Nan was visible through the taproom window, wiping the bar counter with one eye on the yard. Dinah noted it and said nothing. --- Higgins the chandler arrived at half past ten with his cart and his revised invoice, and the particular expression of a man who has decided that what he is about to do is entirely reasonable and would prefer not to have to justify it at length. "Miss Dale." He had the invoice ready, which told her he had been rehearsing this. "Mr Higgins." He set it on the bar counter. She looked at it without touching it. "Tallow's up," he said. "Can't be helped. That's the price from Barnet market, and I'm not in a position to absorb it all myself." "You're showing me eleven shillings the dozen." "That's correct." "Last quarter you were at nine and six. Quarter before that, nine even." She looked up at him. "I also know what you charge the Three Crowns at Welwyn, Mr Higgins, and I know what you charge the Bell at Hatfield. I know what tallow costs at Barnet market because I check the market reports, and I know your margin well enough to understand that eleven shillings doesn't reflect your costs -- it reflects your preference." Higgins's expression shifted through several positions. "I have other customers to consider." "You have a cart that runs this road twice a month and a route that doesn't make sense unless the White Hound is on it.
