I'll take your standard delivery at ten shillings the dozen, and you'll write me that price on a fresh invoice, and you'll have what I owe you before your cart's hitched." She paused. "That's a better outcome than the alternative." He looked at her. She looked at him. "Right," he said at last. "Ten shillings." He wrote out the new invoice. She paid him. He took his money and left, and the candles stayed. She wrote the adjusted figure in the margin of the delivery note in her small, compressed hand, and set it with the other receipts in the order in which they would need to go into the ledger. Somewhere between tallying the adjustment and moving to the next thing, the thought came and went the way working thoughts do: if she married, there would be no negotiation, because there would be no inn to negotiate for. The lease would pass to a husband at the altar the same moment she said the words that were supposed to be about something else. Higgins's margin, the coal column, the six weeks in the ledger -- all of it would belong to someone else's account. She had done the arithmetic before, many times. It came out the same. She moved on. --- She saw him through the kitchen window before he knocked. He was standing outside the kitchen door at the edge of the yard, in the particular way of someone who is considering whether to knock and has not yet decided. Young -- younger than she had first thought, something about the way he stood making him seem older at a distance. His coat was too thin for October, though it was a serviceable cut once, army issue, the kind that had been good at the start and was now at the end of everything. His boots were the most honest thing about him: they were coming apart at the welt on the left side, the leather peeling from the sole in a careful, progressive way that said these boots had been resoled twice and would not survive a third. He stood for a moment looking at the door. He was not threatening. He was calculating the odds of a welcome, and he was not confident about them. Then he knocked. --- She opened the door. He was perhaps twenty, perhaps a year or two more. His face had the pared quality of someone who had not eaten reliably for longer than a few days. He held his hat with both hands, turning it slightly. "I saw the sign," he said. "Round the back." "You ate this morning?" He considered lying and decided against it. "Not today, miss. No." "Kitchen," she said. "Not the taproom. Come in." He came in. She set bread and a bowl of broth on the table -- the broth from the pot that was always on the range, the bread from yesterday's batch that would not last another day anyway. He sat where she indicated and ate without ceremony. She let him get through the worst of it before she moved to the other end of the kitchen to continue what she had been doing. When he had finished he sat with his hands around the empty bowl. "Where are you headed?" she asked. "North." A pause. "I've a cousin in Doncaster. I think." "The Great North Road goes past the gate," she said. "You'll be five miles on before noon if you go directly." "Yes, miss." He stood and picked up his hat. "Thank you." She nodded once. He went out through the same door he had come in by. She heard his steps cross the yard and go quiet at the gate. She cleared the bowl and the bread plate, set them in the washing basin, and her hands moved to the next task. --- The two men in the taproom had been sitting since before noon with nothing to do but develop a grievance about the seating arrangement that was, at its core, about something else entirely -- each of them with that particular stillness of men in a confined space who have been deciding whether to be unreasonable and have come to a conclusion. She dealt with it from the doorway. "Gentlemen. You're both keeping a seat at the fire and there are other guests waiting for a table. You'll settle up or move to the bench." They looked at her. She waited. They moved to the bench. Nan, behind the bar, continued wiping the counter. She had not needed to come round. --- The afternoon gap had its own particular quality: the inn in its between-rush state, the coaches gone and the evening not yet started. Dinah worked through it in the usual way -- the accounts, two rooms that needed resetting before the evening guests arrived, the kitchen provisions for supper, the cellar stock checked against what she had ordered. Jem was in the yard. Nan was at the bar with something she was reading when she thought Dinah wasn't looking. The ordinary grind of it had a texture like good plain cloth: no fineness, but no holes

I checked the ledger twice because the first time I did not like the answer. The kitchen was cold, the fire only just beginning to draw. I had built it up ten minutes before with the last of the split ash from the pile Jem kept stacked against the yard wall, and the flames were still working at the bark, not yet settled to the steady burn I needed for the range. My hands were stiff. I turned the page back and ran my finger down the column again. Tallow, flour, malt, coal. Farrier. The glazier's bill for the window in room nine, which a commercial traveller had put his elbow through in September and for which no payment had been received or would be. Candles. Soap. The quarterly rent was due the second week of December. Six weeks. The figures did not change on the second reading. I had not expected them to. I closed the ledger and set it on the shelf above the dresser where it belonged. The taproom fire needed building. I carried the bucket through and knelt on the stone hearth, raking out yesterday's ash and laying the new fire with the efficiency of a woman who has done this particular task perhaps four thousand times and has no opinion about it. The grate was cracked on the left side -- it had been cracked since July, and the ironmonger wanted more for a replacement than I would spend. I set the kindling, lit it, waited until it caught, then closed the draught to half. The room smelled of old ale and cold stone and the particular staleness that a taproom acquires overnight when the windows are shut. I opened the small casement two inches. Not more. The October air had a blade to it. Bar stock next. I counted the casks by feel in the half-dark of the cellar, tapping each one to judge what was left. Two barrels of ordinary, one nearly spent. The strong ale was adequate. The gin was low but would last the week. I made the tally in my head and came back up, pulling the hatch shut behind me and sliding the bolt. The last night's accounts were still on the bar where Nan had left them, written in her small, careful hand. I checked them against the takings in the cashbox. Correct. Nan was always correct. The windows along the front were rimed at their edges with the first real frost of the season. I could feel it through the glass when I passed close. I pulled my sleeves down over my wrists and went to unlock the front door. The yard was quiet. Jem was already there, which I expected, because Jem was always already there. He was at the tack rail outside the stable, straightening a bridle that had been hung crooked. He did not look up. His one hand worked the leather through the buckle with the practised economy of a man who has long since ceased to think about what is missing and thinks only about what is present and whether it is in order. The horses were moving in their stalls -- I could hear them shifting, the heavy shuffle of hooves on straw. The water trough had been filled. I noted this and went back inside. Nan came through the kitchen door a few minutes later. Her hair was pinned but not yet set, the front pieces escaping already as they always did. Her apron was on and tied. She crossed to the bar without a word and took up her position, running a cloth along the counter in a single practised sweep. She had done this before. We all had. Many times. The inn was awake. The London coach was late by thirty minutes, which was not unusual for late October when the road above Hatfield turned to a study in ruts and standing water. I heard it before I saw it -- the particular grinding note of iron-rimmed wheels on gravel that meant a heavy vehicle taking the turn into the yard too fast. I was at the front door by the time the coachman pulled up. Two passengers instead of the expected four. The first was a stout woman in a brown travelling cloak who descended with the rigid dignity of someone who has been rattled about for three hours and intends to hold everyone responsible. The second was a thin, silent man who went directly to the dining room without speaking to anyone, which suited me well. The woman had a trunk. The trunk had a split hinge, and she had a great deal to say about it. I directed Jem, who had appeared at the yard gate without being called. He took the trunk by its good handle and its bottom edge simultaneously, managing both with one arm and the stump braced against his hip, and carried it toward the stair. The woman watched this operation with her mouth open. I did not give her time to form a comment. "Room six is ready. Breakfast is laid in the dining room. If the trunk needs a strap to hold it, we have one." She blinked, closed her mouth, and followed Jem inside. The coachman had not yet gone. He was standing by his horses with the reins looped over the brake handle, and he had the particular expression of a man who has been composing a speech for the last several miles. "The road above Hatfield is out in two places," he said. "Properly out. The ruts will take an axle off if you're not careful." "The parish was meant to grade it last month." "Well, they haven't. I've lost twenty minutes on every run this week, and the mail coach behind me lost a wheel Tuesday." "I'll write to the surveyor again. I can't do more than that." "I know you can't. I'm only telling you so you know, because no one else on this road will bother." "Thank you, Mr Haddon." He touched his hat and climbed back up. I watched the coach pull out of the yard, the horses leaning into the traces with the resignation of animals who understand that the next stop is not theirs to decide. Then the yard was empty. The thin man was eating in the dining room. The stout woman was upstairs. Jem had gone back to the stable. Nan was behind the bar with one eye on the door, the way she always stood when the morning was still unsettled. I saw her watching and said nothing. The chandler arrived at half past ten. Mr Fenton was a square, unhurried man who delivered his tallow candles on the first Tuesday of every month and who had, over the course of the year, developed a habit of adjusting his price upward by small increments, the way a man tests a fence for weakness by leaning on it a little harder each time. He set the box of candles on the kitchen table and named his price. "Fourpence ha'penny the dozen." "It was fourpence last month." "Tallow's gone up." "Tallow has not gone up. I buy soap from the same supplier you buy your tallow from, and his price hasn't moved since August." Fenton looked at me with the steady expression of a man who has been caught out but does not consider this a reason to concede. "There's the carriage of it to consider." "Your carriage hasn't changed. You come this way for the Red Lion and the Woolpack regardless. I'm not an extra journey, Mr Fenton. I'm a stop you make on a route you're already travelling." A pause. He shifted his weight. "I'll go to fourpence farthing," he said. "That's fair." "Fourpence. Same as last month. Or you can take them to the Red Lion, who I happen to know has not paid you for September." The silence was brief. He left the candles. I counted them after he'd gone -- three dozen, as agreed -- and wrote the corrected price in the margin of the delivery note. Fourpence. The door closed behind him. The kitchen was quiet. I stood with the delivery note in my hand and the ledger's figures still precise in my memory. The rent, the chandler's fourpence, the cracked grate, the glazier's bill. All of it held in my name because my name was the one on the lease, and if I married, the name on the lease would change and the fourpence negotiation and every negotiation like it would no longer be mine to conduct. I set the delivery note down and went to check the dining room. He came around the back, as the notice on the gate asked. I saw him through the kitchen window before he knocked -- young, thin, standing on the flagstones outside the door with the particular stillness of a man who is deciding whether he is welcome enough to announce himself. His coat was too large for him and had been someone else's. His boots were coming apart where the sole met the upper, the stitching pulling away from the welt in a manner I recognized because I had seen it on a dozen pairs of boots this year. He was perhaps twenty. He looked at the door. He looked at his boots. He raised his hand and knocked. I opened the door. "Have you eaten?" He met my eyes. There was nothing sly in his face, nothing calculating. Only the careful honesty of a man who knows that a lie will cost him more than the truth. "Not since yesterday, ma'am." "Kitchen, not the taproom. Sit there." He sat at the table where I pointed, and I set before him what the kitchen had: bread from this morning's baking, a bowl of broth from the stockpot that had been on the range since dawn, a heel of cheese. He ate steadily, not fast, with the restraint of someone who has learned that eating too quickly on an empty stomach is its own punishment. I did not sit. I wiped down the work table and checked the stockpot and let him eat. When he finished he stood and brought the bowl to the basin without being asked. "Thank you, ma'am." "Where are you headed?" "North," he said. "My mother's people are near Biggleswade." He went out the way he had come. I cleared the bowl and the plate and the cup and washed them and set them on the rack. The bread knife needed wiping. I wiped it. In the taproom, two men who had been sitting too long over their second pints had found something to disagree about -- the source of the dispute was unclear and did not matter, because the volume was what mattered, and the volume was rising. "Gentlemen. You'll settle this outside or you'll settle it nowhere, but you will not settle it in my taproom." They settled it nowhere. Nan, behind the bar, had not moved. She did not need to. The afternoon opened into the gap between coaches -- that stretch of hours when the inn belonged to no one in particular and the work was the quiet kind. Nan polished glasses. Jem was outside; I could hear the rhythmic scrape of a hoof pick against stone, which meant he was cleaning the yard. I reset the two rooms that had been vacated, stripped the linen, checked the mattresses, laid new fires. In the kitchen I took stock of what remained for the evening meal and what would need to be stretched. The mutton would serve if I made a hash of it with potatoes and onion. There was enough bread. There was always enough bread if I baked twice. The light went early. By four o'clock the windows were dark and the candles were lit -- Mr Fenton's candles, at fourpence the dozen -- and the evening trade came and went with the reliable rhythm of a coaching inn in autumn. A pair of travellers wanting supper and beds. A local man for his evening pint. The curate from St Mary's, who drank one half of mild and spoke to no one and left a ha'penny on the bar as though it were a tithe. I closed the kitchen at nine. I banked the range fire and wiped the table and hung the cloth to dry. The taproom was empty. Nan had tallied the bar and gone up. I checked the front door -- Loss. Bolted. The cellar hatch -- locked. I moved through the ground floor in the dark, my hand finding each door and latch by memory. The taproom fire was down to coals, the grate ticking as the iron contracted. I stood for a moment in the doorway and listened to the sound the building made when it was done being used: the settling of old timber, the small complaints of a structure that has held its shape through many hundreds of nights like this one and will hold it through many more, God willing and the rent paid. I went out the back to check the yard. The stable was dark and quiet. Jem would have settled the horses and gone to his room above the tack store. The air was sharp and still, the frost already forming on the cobbles. I looked up, as I always did on the last circuit, at the second floor. The window at the far end was lit -- a candle or a lamp behind the glass, and the shape of Bridget sitting where she always sat, watching the yard below. I did not look long. Inside, I locked the kitchen door behind me. My hands were red from the cold water and the cleaning, and there was a small cut on my right index finger where the edge of Fenton's delivery note had caught the skin. I had not noticed it at the time. It was the invoice that had cut me. The letter was on the kitchen table. It had not been there at nine o'clock, which meant someone had pushed it under the front door after I had locked it, or it had been there all day and I had failed to see it, which I did not believe. The seal was dark red, pressed with a signet I knew. The handwriting on the direction was careful and measured and familiar. Mr Stainforth's hand. Miss D. Dale, The White Hound. I picked it up. I held it. I turned it over once and felt the weight of it -- a single sheet, perhaps two. I set it down on the table and placed my hand flat on top of it, as though it were something that might move if I did not hold it still. I did not open it. I had known it was coming since the last quarter day. I had known it in the ledger this morning and in the chandler's fourpence and in the cracked grate and the road that the parish would not mend. I had been moving faster ever since, as though speed and competence could outrun what was, in the end, only a man with a seal and a piece of paper and the law behind him. The kitchen was empty. The fire was low. The inn was locked and quiet and mine, for now, for tonight, for six more weeks or however many the letter in my hands intended to leave me. "Right, then," I said to no one.

His left knee was stiff, which meant the temperature had dropped overnight, which meant October was doing what October did along the Great North Road, and none of this would have been notable except that the left knee had been unreliable in cold weather since Torres Vedras in 1810, when he'd spent four nights in a stone room with no straw and a draught that found the gap under the door from dark to dawn and stayed.

He lay still for a moment, the way he'd learned to lie still in the first seconds of a morning with an uncertain perimeter, and assembled the pieces. Stable loft. Coaching inn, the Great North Road, twenty miles north of London. Late October, 1816. The war was finished.

He'd slept in worse. The billet outside Torres Vedras had been stone floor, no straw, and that same draught, and he'd called it serviceable, because serviceable was the word he applied to things that had not yet killed him and showed no urgent intention of doing so. This was serviceable and then some. He found this thought somewhat cheering, in the way of a thought he wasn't going to examine.

* * *

He got up.

The blanket he folded and set aside. The loft hatch he checked, found it closed and the latch seated, and then checked again, because his hands had already done it before the decision to do it had quite arrived. He noted that he'd checked it twice, which was once more than it required. He didn't examine why. He'd stopped examining why some time ago, which was, if a man wanted to put a name to it, its own kind of progress.

His father's watch was in the waistcoat pocket where he'd put it the night before. He wound it. The mechanism was familiar under his thumb, the resistance and the catch and the small certain ratchet sound it made in the quiet of the loft. He kept it wound. He rarely consulted it, which was a contradiction he hadn't resolved and wasn't presently intending to.

The trunk he'd positioned so that he could see both it and the hatch from the bedroll, not because he'd decided to but because that was where it was by the time he finished moving it. His Peninsular boots were beside it, the original stitching military, the soles resoled twice in two years of trying to wear them out. He hadn't managed it yet.

Sapper was pressed against his good knee, which was the warmer of the two available options, and opened one eye when Laurence moved. The eye surveyed the loft, the hatch, the state of the morning, and the degree to which the results met expectation, and then closed again. Sapper had, in Laurence's assessment, the expression of a creature who had accepted considerable professional responsibilities under conditions he'd never fully agreed to and was performing them under quiet but sustained protest.

"It's a stable loft," Laurence said.

The dog's ear moved once.

Sapper eventually stood, stretched with the full commitment of a creature with nowhere to be, went to the water bucket, drank, came back, and leaned against Laurence's good knee. Not affection, exactly. More the dog confirming that whatever the current arrangement was, he was prepared to continue it, under protest, indefinitely.

* * *

He came out into the yard when the light was still more suggestion than fact, the mud crusted over from the night's cold, the smell of horse and wet straw and woodsmoke from the kitchen chimney, which was already lit.

Jem was at the trough with the grey. He had the halter in his remaining hand, left stump braced against the trough edge for leverage, a leather loop on the gatepost doing the work of the second hand, and he was talking to the horse in a low and steady voice that had nothing to do with soothing and everything to do with management. The engineering of the whole arrangement, the loop and the angle of the stump and the way the horse had been positioned so the trough provided the third point of stability, was the solution of a man who'd converted a permanent problem into a finished procedure and required no comment on the outcome.

He read the missing arm the way he read everything about a new position: below the elbow, clean amputation, field surgery rather than infection, which said something about the unit the man had served with. The pinned sleeve was neat. Neat meant the man had standards. He moved through the yard as if it had been arranged specifically for his use, which, Laurence observed, it largely had.

He'd been assessed before, by men with considerably more authority and less patience. Sergeant Coyle at Vimeiro could reduce a junior officer to his component failures in the time it took to light a pipe and had done so frequently and without malice, which in Laurence's experience was the worst kind. Jem's version was quieter. It was not less thorough.

* * *

Jem looked up. Looked back at the horse.

Laurence came over.

"Your grey favors the near foreleg," he said.

"She does." Jem adjusted the buckle without looking at it. A pause, during which he did not look at Laurence either. "Where'd you serve."

"The Peninsula."

Jem's eyes moved, not to his face. Down and back up. "Officer's boots, those." He turned back to the horse, working the lead with the single hand. "And you've got your hands behind your back. Thinking position." He glanced at Laurence once more. "Man with those boots and that posture doesn't muck stalls. Why isn't he in a decent house?"

Laurence didn't answer. "What's her temperament near the gate latch," he said. "She startled last night."

Jem was quiet for a moment. The grey drank. He let her.

* * *

"She takes against it if you don't give her warning first," he said. "Slow approach. Let her hear you coming." He adjusted the loop on the gatepost with a practiced shift of his wrist. "Morning feed's at six. Evening at four. She doesn't take well to changes."

"I'll keep that in mind," Laurence said.

"See that you do." Jem looked at him again, the same unhurried assessment. "Your name."

"Ren Calvert."

* * *

Jem held this for a moment, in the way a man holds something he suspects is lighter than it's been represented as, without anger, just noting the weight. "Calvert," he said. "Right." He unlooped the halter from the gatepost, turned back to the horse, and the exchange was over. What Jem had decided about the gap between Ren Calvert and the rest of it, Laurence couldn't say. He'd find out from the man's actions, later. That was how it worked.

He watched Jem work: the one-armed efficiency, the system of braces and loops that had turned a limitation into a method, the way it read now as simply how the job was done. He'd served with men who'd lost more than an arm and carried what remained with considerably less composure. Jem carried it the way a good NCO carried every obstacle the army placed between him and his work, as part of the load, no more specific than that. It wasn't admiration, exactly, what Laurence felt in observing it. It was the flat recognition of one professional reading another at the appropriate distance.

He'd been in the yard an hour when she came out.

She came from the kitchen door already in motion, giving some instruction across the yard to the dark-haired girl (Nan, he'd gathered, from last night) without breaking stride, and adjusting a harness strap on the near stall doorpost as she passed it, a half-second correction her hands made before the rest of her had fully registered the problem. She had her hair pinned flat, her apron clean (which told him the day hadn't started yet for the apron, even though it clearly had for her), sleeves rolled to the forearm, and she moved through the yard with the ownership of someone who had known every square foot of it since before the cobblestones were worn smooth.

He'd assembled the approach and the posture and the speed of the adjustment and the shorthand between her and Nan, and was in the middle of filing it under competent operator, established management, when she reached the far stall's doorpost, stopped, and corrected a second buckle on her way past, a different buckle, with the same automatic competence as the first, and something in his analytical register stalled for a half-second he wasn't going to examine and filed under nothing.

Not desire. Something more like recognition, which was either better or more inconvenient. He filed that too.

Nan called something from across the yard. She answered without turning, two words, and Nan went inside. Then she was in front of him, looking at him the way someone looked at a new piece of equipment: not its appearance, its function.

* * *

"You're the man who wants work," she said.

"Yes," he said.

"Can you handle a nervous horse."

"The mare in the third stall," he said, "favors her left foreleg and startles at the gate latch if you don't announce yourself first. She'll take a slow approach and a voice." He waited a beat. "The roan two stalls down kicks from the near side when his feed runs late."

She looked at him. A small recalibration, visible if you were watching for it: a man who had already been working before she arrived. She was filing this.

* * *

"Can you lift a barrel."

"Yes."

"Do accounts."

"Yes."

"Have you references."

"No."

"Worked an inn before."

"No."

"Your name."

"Ren Calvert."

"Where are you from, Mr. Calvert."

"The road," he said.

She appeared to weigh whether this was an adequate answer. Provisionally, it appeared to be. "I'll put you on trial for a week," she said. "The loft above the near stable, which you've already found, which tells me something." She let that land without explaining what it told her. "Meals and a shilling at the end of the week, if I keep you. Those are the terms."

He could see her waiting for a counter. He didn't offer one.

He watched her file this too, the small revision behind her eyes: a man who didn't haggle was either desperate or didn't need the shilling, and neither reading simplified matters. It was, more or less, the same calculation Jem had been running since he'd come out of the stable, and Laurence was beginning to appreciate that this place was operated by people who watched first and concluded later and were very good at both.

"Those terms suit me," he said.

"Then start with the stalls," she said. "The roan first." She turned toward the far stable without another word, and the interview was over because she had work to do, which was, Laurence understood, as clear a welcome as this kind of place offered.

He found the mucking fork in the near stable, where it was supposed to be.

He mucked the stalls and filled the water buckets and resettled a partition in the far corner that had been working loose from its frame, doing all of it well because he couldn't do things poorly, not because of any decision but because the second son's instinct toward usefulness had predated the army by twenty years and the army had made it structural. He'd arrived here last night with no particular intention and by noon he'd catalogued three maintenance needs and corrected two of them.

He recognised the pattern. He always recognised the pattern. He found a place, made himself necessary, and did not examine what would happen when the place no longer needed him or he could no longer stay, the way other men did not look down from a height because it didn't improve the situation. The pattern might, he supposed, bear some examination, at some point.

Not today. The roan was due his evening feed at four.

The day used itself up. When he climbed to the loft, his body was tired in the way that physical labour made a body tired, which was different from the tiredness of marching and better in ways he'd have considered beneath comment two years ago and found genuinely restoring now.

He took out the book first: Arthur Young's Travels in France, the agricultural survey, read twice in Spain and presently being read a third time for reasons he didn't intend to examine. He set it on the trunk lid. Then the spare shirt, which he'd worn yesterday and folded, and which he placed beside the book. Beneath the shirt, the letter.

The address was in the solicitor's hand: Sir Laurence Calvert, Bart., of Thornfield Hall, Buckinghamshire. The seal was unbroken. It had been unbroken for six weeks, which was two weeks longer than he'd carried Corporal Hardie's effects at Badajoz: a wife in Cheshire, two daughters, a letter in the packet addressed to a woman named Mary in the careful handwriting of a man who'd learned to write in school and not had much occasion for it since. He still knew the daughters' names. He'd carried the packet for three weeks before the company clerk had found somewhere to forward it, and for those three weeks it had been the heaviest item in his kit, though it weighed less than a pound.

He'd gotten good at not opening letters. It was, on reflection, probably his most fully developed skill of the past year, the discipline he'd applied the most sustained effort to, the one genuine personal achievement he could point to since Waterloo. He'd have ranked it, on a short list of his accomplishments, above supply logistics and below the ability to assess a defensive position in under four minutes, which was not quite a joke but close enough to one that he allowed it passage.

He put the shirt back over the letter, and the book on top of the shirt. He wound the watch, the evening winding, the second of the day, and set it on the trunk lid where the candlelight found it.

The stable was quiet.

He noticed this the way he noticed things without choosing to: the weight of the quiet, the specific sounds inside it. Sapper's breathing. The straw settling. The distance from the bedroll to the hatch. He counted the knots in the nearest beam. Seven. He counted them again. Seven. The counting was nothing. It was what his attention did when the quiet became a particular kind of quiet, which was a thing he understood about himself without requiring its further examination.

Then a sound came from below.

The sound was the stable door. The stable door on its new pin, the one he'd reset himself the night before and knew the sound of exactly. That was what it was. His hands knew it too, perhaps half a second after the rest of him did.

He wound the watch a second time. He hadn't meant to. He set it back on the trunk lid and sat with his hand resting on Sapper's flank, listening to the watch tick and the stable settle into quiet around him, the dog's breath rising and falling steady under his palm. His hand rose and fell with it and was not quite still.

He didn't hear Jem come up. One moment the hatch was empty. Then Jem was there, leaning in the frame, and had clearly been there long enough to see what he'd come to see. He looked at Laurence. He looked at the dog. He looked at the trunk, at the book and the shirt on top of it. He nodded once. Then he went back down, and the latch settled, and the stable was quiet.

Tallied the night's takings first, because the morning fire could wait thirty seconds and the numbers could not. The coins were right -- I had counted them twice before setting them in the tin -- but the ledger column told a story the coins could not contradict. Six weeks, perhaps seven if the coaches ran full and the chandler held his price, before the next quarter's rent came due and the arithmetic stopped working in my favour. I closed the ledger. The tin went under the bar with the key on its hook beside it. I had made a habit of looking at those numbers before I let myself look at anything else. It was better to know. It was always better to know. The kitchen fire needed building up. The coals had banked through the night and there was still heat in them, enough to work from, and I laid the wood in the way my mother had shown me -- smallest pieces first, then the larger splits, the gaps left for air to move through. She had said: a fire choked is a fire wasted, and she had been right about that as she had been right about most things relating to the running of the inn. The flame caught and I fed it until it steadied. The cold this morning was particular, the kind that settled into stone flags and stayed regardless of what you did to the air above them. I pulled my sleeves down over my wrists. Taproom fire next. The grate was fully out and I rebuilt it from scratch, kneeling on the hearthrug and working quickly. The room was dark -- the shutters still closed -- and smelled of last night's ale and tallow and the particular damp-wool smell of men who had been travelling in October rain. I did not open the shutters. The fire first, then the light. My mother had done it in this order and I had never found reason to change it. Bar stock after that: I moved along the shelves checking levels, noting what wanted bringing up from the cellar before the morning trade, moving a bottle that had been put back in the wrong place by whoever had closed last night. Not Nan -- Nan put things back correctly. One of the guests, then, helping himself after hours, which was a problem I would address when I had evidence of who. I wrote nothing in the ledger about this. I held it in my head instead, where it joined the other things I held there: the cracked slate on the rear roof that Jem had patched but not replaced, the hinge on the dining room door that had been stiffening since August, the particular way the second step on the back stairs announced itself to anyone trying to move quietly at night, which had saved me more than once from a guest who thought the cellar door an interesting target. The windows were rimed at the corners where the glass met the wood. I noticed this without stopping. October coming in earnest, then. The north road would be harder going and the coaches would start running late if they were not already, which they were, frequently, for reasons the coachmen considered self-evident and I considered avoidable. There was a delivery of flour expected this morning. I hoped it arrived before the coach and not after, because Jem would need the yard clear. I went to the back door and looked out. Jem was already at the tack wall, working one-handed at the bridles with the same focused, unhurried attention he brought to everything in the stable yard. He had developed, over the years, a system for tasks that most men would have said required two hands, and the system worked, and he had never invited comment on it. I had long since stopped offering any. He did not look up when I opened the door. I did not call to him. The yard was still and grey and the horses were quiet in their stalls. I was back in the kitchen when Nan came through from the lane door, pins not yet set in her hair but her apron already on and tied, which was the correct order of priority. She moved to her place behind the bar without speaking. We had done this many times. There was nothing that required saying. The coach was late. I heard it before I saw it -- the particular sound of four horses on the north road, not the clean collected sound of a full team running well but something slightly ragged, one of the lead horses pulling unevenly. I was in the yard when it came through the gate, and Jem appeared from the stable door without being called. The coachman had the look of a man who had been arguing with the road for the last six miles and was prepared to continue the argument with whoever stood still long enough. He climbed down before the steps were fully set. "Miss Dale." "How late are you?" I asked him. "Twenty minutes, maybe more. It's the road above Hatfield -- the surface wants attention above the chalk cutting. There's a rut forming on the east edge that'll take a wheel off a lighter carriage before the month's out." "Write to the turnpike trustees," I said. "I'll give you paper if you haven't got it." "And they'll do nothing before Christmas." "Likely not," I agreed. "But then it's on record that they were told. How many passengers?" He said two, which was a short load, and both had emerged from the coach by then and were standing in the yard with the particular tight-shouldered displeasure of people who feel they have been inconvenienced and wish someone to acknowledge it. One of them had a trunk, an old piece of luggage, good leather once, now showing its age, and as Jem moved to take it I saw the hinge give way on the right side and the lid drop six inches before the strap caught it. The woman to whom the trunk belonged drew herself up in a way that promised a detailed accounting of its condition. I moved toward her. "I'll have that taken up to your room directly, ma'am. The hinge will need wrapping to hold it safe. If you'll follow Nan inside, there's a fire in the dining room and I'll bring tea." She looked at me. She had been preparing to say something at length. The offer of tea and fire landed before she could begin, and she found herself moving toward the door in Nan's wake without quite having decided to. The trunk went up with Jem's good hand and the crook of the other arm, steadily, as though the compromised hinge were not his concern. The coachman was still standing at the yard gate. I walked back to him. "The chalk cutting," I said. "East edge. I'll send word to Harrow at the toll house. He'll not fix it but he'll flag it, and a flagged rut is slower damage than an unmarked one." He looked at me with the tired assessment of a man deciding whether this answer was sufficient. He decided it was. He went. Nan had been watching the door all through this, I noticed, her cloth moving along the bar in the automatic way that meant her attention was elsewhere. I said nothing about it. Nan's attention was her own business, and the bar was clean. --- The chandler came at half past ten with his cart, which he always did on the last Monday of the month, and which I had spent seventeen of the last twenty-one months finding reasons to be present for. His name was Garbett and he was not an unreasonable man. He was, however, a businessman, and businessmen adjusted their prices when they thought the adjustment would hold. He set the first box on the kitchen table and named his price. I had been waiting for this. I let it sit in the air for a moment, not for effect, but because I had learned that speaking immediately made men think you had not heard them properly. "That's four pence up on September," I said. "Tallow's dearer." He said this comfortably, as a man says something he has prepared. "It is," I agreed. "I checked the market price on Friday. It's up three farthings the pound from September. Your price is up four pence a dozen. The discrepancy is somewhere in your margin, Mr Garbett, and I'm curious where." He looked at me. He was a broad man, red-faced, with the hands of someone who had been lifting things for forty years. He did not look embarrassed. He looked, instead, like a man recalculating. "I've my own costs to consider," he said. "The cart, the horse." "You have," I said. "As you had them in September. The horse has not grown more expensive in the last five weeks, or if he has, I'm glad not to be his owner." I picked up the nearest candle and held it in the way I held things I was considering returning. "Three pence up. That I can account for, given the tallow. Four pence is a different number." There was a pause. Garbett was looking at the box of candles, then at me, then at the door, the way a man looks when he is deciding whether the concession costs him less than the principle. "Three pence up, then," he said. "Since you've done your sums." I set the candle back in the box. "Thank you, Mr Garbett. Same time next month." He left. The door settled on its latch. I wrote the adjusted price in the margin of the delivery note -- three pence, not four, which was the difference between absorbing the increase and not absorbing it, which was the difference between six weeks and five. Somewhere between returning the delivery note to the shelf and the next task -- the flour had arrived and needed checking against the invoice -- there was a moment, flat and brief, in which the morning's arithmetic assembled itself into a single clear statement: the chandler's concession, the ledger figure, the rent. All of it added against the fact that if I married, the inn would not be mine to negotiate for. Not the candle price, not the flour delivery, not the coin in the tin under the bar. The lease would pass. The business my grandmother had built and my mother had kept would belong to whoever I married, and whatever he decided to do with it. I had made this calculation before. It came and went the way working thoughts came and went. I moved to the flour sacks. --- I saw him before he knocked. He was standing outside the kitchen door with his arms at his sides, looking at the step, not in the way of a man about to knock but in the way of a man deciding whether the knock was his to make. Young -- twenty, perhaps younger, the particular thinness of someone who has been walking and not eating in roughly equal measure. His coat was serviceable, once. His boots were coming apart at the welt on the left side, a split I could see from inside the window, the leather pulling away from the sole in the way that meant another week's walking would finish them entirely. He was not threatening. He was assessing whether he was welcome enough to knock. He knocked. I opened the door. "Have you eaten?" I said. He looked at me. He had the careful look of a man who has learned that the answer to this question determines what happens next, and who is not sure whether honesty is the safer choice. Then he said, "Not recently." "Kitchen," I said. "Not the taproom. Sit down." He came in without further discussion and sat at the far end of the table, out of the way, which told me he understood the terms. I brought him bread and broth -- the kitchen pot had been on since early morning and the broth was good, better than it needed to be for the purpose, but the pot did not know who it was feeding. He ate quickly, then more slowly, then with the particular careful attention of a man who knows that eating too fast after too long is its own problem. I was at the range when he finished. "Thank you," he said. "I didn't --- thank you." I came back to the table and took the bowl. "Which way are you headed?" He said north. He did not say more than that and I did not ask. Neither of us said anything about the war, which was present in the room anyway, in his coat and his boots and the careful way he had placed himself so as not to be in the way. He went. The door closed on him. I put the bowl in the tub and my hands moved to the next thing. --- It was mid-afternoon when the trouble started in the taproom -- not real trouble, only the particular friction of two men who had been sitting too long with nothing to do and had decided that a disagreement about the placement of a travelling bag was a matter worth raising voices over. I came through the taproom door. "If the bag is in the way," I said, looking at neither of them in particular, "move it. If it's your bag and you don't want it moved, put it under the bench. The floor between the tables is not storage." Both of them looked at me. The voice that had been rising subsided. The bag was moved. Nan, behind the bar, had not needed to set down her cloth. The afternoon gap came and went. Coaches due at half four; nothing between now and then but the ordinary business of the day. I did the room accounts, reset the corner room for a new arrival expected Thursday, checked the kitchen against the evening menu, spoke to no one unless spoken to, and was rarely spoken to. This was the character of the afternoon: no incident, only work, the inn running

Tallied the coal first, because coal mattered more than candles at this hour. I was already at the kitchen table when the first grey came into the east windows, the ledger open to October's column, my finger moving down the figures I had entered the night before by the last of the lamp. Six weeks, give or take, before the December quarter caught up with me. I had known this number since September. I did not become more comfortable with it by looking at it again. I closed the ledger. The kitchen fire had been banked overnight and needed building up. I did this: added coal, worked the bellows until the room stopped breathing cold at me, filled the kettle from the pump in the yard and set it over. The flagstones were cold through my boots. Frost had come in at the edges of the windows and left a fine rime along the leading, and the yard beyond was the particular shade of pale grey that meant the ground had hardened overnight. I noted this because hard ground slows the coaches, and slow coaches mean late arrivals, and late arrivals mean either wasted food held too long or guests irritable with hunger, and either outcome costs something. The taproom fire next. I carried coals from the kitchen scuttle, knowing the taproom scuttle would need filling before the afternoon. I noted it. I checked the bar stock: three casks of ordinary ale, one of the better stuff, a quantity of port that was running lower than I liked given the weather we were in, two bottles of brandy I was reserving for the dining room and would not move to the taproom regardless of who asked. I tallied last night's take in the margin of the day's sheet, crossed it against what I had drawn from the bar myself, found the figures agreed, and moved on. The dining room chairs were stacked on the tables. I did not set them down yet -- that could wait until Nan arrived. I checked the window latch on the far wall, which had been sticking since September and which I had asked about twice. It still stuck. I added it to the list I carry in my head of things that need attention and cannot yet have it. Back through to the kitchen. The kettle was not yet boiling. I stood at the table and looked at the open page of the ledger again, then shut it, because looking at it a second time would not change the numbers and I had work to do. This was the inn before anyone else was awake: mine entirely, cold and quiet, smelling of yesterday's cooking and the particular note of old stone and timber that never quite left the walls. The White Hound had smelled this way for as long as I had been alive. My mother said her mother had described the same smell, which meant it had been breathing itself out into the same rooms for at least sixty years. I found this neither comforting nor depressing. It was a fact of the place, the way the sticking window latch was a fact of the place. You knew it, you worked around it, you moved on. I was at the pump again, filling the bread bowl, when I heard Jem cross the stable yard. I did not need to look through the window to know it was him -- I knew his step, the slight economy of movement on the left side that came from managing without the arm, the particular way he set his weight down when the yard was icy. Through the glass I could see him moving along the stable block, checking the tack that hung under the overhang, straightening a bridle that had slipped from its peg. He had not been asked to do this. He was doing it because the morning required it, and Jem understood what mornings required. He did not look up toward the kitchen window. Nan came through the kitchen door perhaps ten minutes later, pins not quite set in her hair, apron already tied. She had the look she always had in the early part of the day, quick and slightly combustible, ready for the work before the work was ready for her. She went directly to the bar without a word, took up the cloth from under the counter, and began wiping down the wood. We had done this before. Many times. The silence between us was not the silence of two people with nothing to say. It was the silence of two people who had already said everything that needed saying across years of six o'clock mornings, and who understood that the morning's business was not conversation. The coach from London was twenty minutes late, which I had half-expected. I heard it before I saw it -- the particular weight of the wheels on the road above the village, then the change in the horse-sounds as they came under the inn sign and into the yard. I was already in the doorway before the coachman had pulled them up. Two passengers, where the booking had said four. The first, a woman of perhaps fifty in a dark travelling cloak, was halfway down the steps before the coachman had set the block. The second was a young man who descended with the careful deliberateness of someone nursing a headache. Neither looked pleased with the world, and I did not take this personally. The road between London and Hatfield had been uneven since August and the coaches announced this fact to everyone inside them. The woman had a trunk with a split hinge. She wanted me to know this immediately. "It has been split since Barnet," she said, "and no one has seen fit to do anything about it." "Jem," I said. Jem was already there, at the back of the boot, assessing the trunk with the same expression he applied to every problem that crossed the yard: careful, unhurried, certain he could manage it. I said to the woman, "He'll take it up. You won't lose anything." "I have glassware in that trunk." "Then he'll be careful with it." I met her eyes directly. "You can watch him carry it if you'd like, or you can come inside and I'll have hot water brought to your room inside ten minutes. The choice is yours, but you've been in a coach for three hours and the fire is going." She looked at me for a moment -- the look of someone recalibrating -- and then she went inside. This is what directness does, when it is applied without heat. It surprises people into cooperation, and cooperation is almost always what everyone actually wants. The coachman dropped down from the box. He was a man named Croft, who ran this route three days in seven, and I knew his face the way I knew the faces of all the regulars -- as information rather than acquaintance. "Road's bad above the hatfield junction," he said. "That stretch by the old quarry. Two ruts that'll take a wheel off if you come at them wrong." "I'll note it for the northbound driver this afternoon." "I've been noting it for a month. Noting it doesn't fill the ruts." "No," I said, "it doesn't. But I can't mend the King's road this morning and nor can you. Is there anything you need before you turn them out?" He wanted a word about the harness on the second horse, a loose cheekpiece he'd been nursing since St Albans. I sent him to Jem, who was coming back across the yard with the trunk already squared on his shoulder. Croft accepted this redirection with the fatalism of a man who has been on the road since before dawn and has learned to take his solutions where he can find them. The yard cleared. The passengers went upstairs. Jem led the horses toward the stable block without needing to be asked -- this was not Jem doing me a favour; this was Jem doing the job, which was what I paid him for, which was why I had kept paying him when the December quarter made me look twice at every shilling. Through the taproom window I could see Nan at the bar, cloth still moving in slow circles across the wood, one eye on the door. I noted that she was watching for something. I said nothing. The chandler arrived at half past ten, as he always did on the third Wednesday of the month, with his cart and his ledger and the particular expression of a man who is about to tell you something you will not like. His name was Pratt, and he had been delivering tallow candles to the White Hound since my mother's time. He was a compact, careful man with a red face and a habit of consulting his ledger before he spoke, as though his figures might have changed in the last thirty seconds if he didn't keep an eye on them. "Price is up," he said, when we were in the kitchen and the delivery was stacked on the table. "Tallow's gone up at source. Can't be helped." I looked at the figure he named. I looked at it for a moment without speaking. "That's the fourth time since March," I said. "Prices rise. Supply and demand, Miss Dale." "They do. Though I notice the Falcon at Hatfield is paying seven shillings for the same dozen, and the Marquis of Granby at Welwyn is paying six and ten." I had made it my business to know this. I had made it my business in March, when the price first moved. "You serve the same road I do, Mr Pratt. I know your rounds." He consulted his ledger. This was what he did when he needed time to think -- a habit so consistent it was almost comforting. "My costs have gone up," he said. "They have. But not by as much as your prices have, or you'd have raised your prices at the Falcon as well. You haven't, because you know Thompson at the Falcon would find another supplier. He's closer to Stevenage and he has more options." I folded my hands on the kitchen table. "I have fewer options. That's what you've been charging me for." He looked up from his ledger. "Seven shillings and sixpence," I said. "The same as the Falcon. Or I'll tell Thompson you've been undercutting him, which I expect he'd find interesting." Pratt looked at me for a long moment. Outside, Jem's voice carried across the yard to one of the stable lads -- brief, flat, directing. In the taproom, something was set down on the bar. "Seven and sixpence," Pratt said. "Thank you." He left the candles and went. I took the delivery note and wrote the corrected figure in the margin. The invoice had a sharp edge -- I had been holding it at the corner and it had caught the tip of my index finger, not enough to bleed, just enough to feel. I set the note down and moved on. Somewhere in the business of the midday -- between the last of the morning's rooms and the first preparation for the afternoon coach -- the ledger's numbers came back to me, arranged against each other in the way they sometimes did, without invitation. The chandler's concession. Pratt's margin. The rent at December. And underneath all of it, always, the same practical calculation I had run before and would run again: if I married, the ledger would not be mine to negotiate from. The lease, the inn, the candle price, all of it -- handed over to a husband at the moment of saying yes, as thoroughly and irrevocably as if I had signed it away myself. This was not an injustice I was capable of dwelling on. It was a term of existence, like the weather, like the ruts above the Hatfield junction. You knew it, you worked around it, and you kept moving. I was moving. He was standing outside the kitchen door at the back of the inn, around the side where the sign asked deliveries and tradesman's calls to come. I saw him through the window before he knocked -- which was to say he had not yet knocked, was still standing in that particular way of someone who is not sure of his welcome and is assessing whether the welcome is worth the risk of asking for it. He was young. Not so young as to be a boy, but not far from it -- perhaps twenty, perhaps a little less. His coat had started life as a soldier's greatcoat and was making a determined effort to continue in that role, though the effort was showing. His boots were coming apart at the welt on the left side, the leather splitting from the sole in the way that happened when boots had been wet and dried too many times without oil. He stood still in the cold October light and looked at the door. I opened it before he knocked. "Have you eaten?" I said. He answered honestly. The honesty was immediate, without calculation -- he hadn't, not since yesterday, and he had the look of a man who was not going to lie about this even if lying might have served him. I respected the honesty. I noted the boots. "Kitchen," I said. "Not the taproom." This was the term, and he understood it without explanation -- understood that the taproom was where guests went, that the kitchen was where he would not be a problem, and that both of these facts were simply facts and not an insult. He came inside and I put bread on the table and ladled broth from the pot that was always on the back of the range at this season. He ate. He had the discipline of a man who wanted very badly to eat quickly and was making himself slow down. When he was done, he said, "Thank you." The words were simple and he meant them. "Where

Tallied the coal order first, because the coal merchant had a habit of shorting the delivery by a quarter-hundredweight and writing it down as a rounding error. Three times in the past two months. The fourth time she would say something about it. The ledger was open on the kitchen table and the candle beside it was three hours old, guttered low in its holder and giving off a thin, uncertain light. Dinah drew the flame closer and ran her finger down the column of figures. Six weeks, roughly, before the quarter's rent became a problem. Possibly seven if the northern coaches ran full through November, which they would not. She knew this and she had always known this, the way she knew the sound of a wheel about to lose its pin or the particular quality of silence from the taproom that meant men were working themselves toward a fight. The numbers were not alarming. They were not comfortable, either. She closed the ledger. The kitchen fire had been built up but not yet given permission to take hold, and the room held the particular cold of a space that has not yet been inhabited by the day. She stirred the coals forward, set the damper, and moved to the taproom. The fire there wanted laying again -- Jem had banked it yesterday evening but the wood had been wet and the coals were mostly ash. She laid the wood herself, tinder first, and coaxed the flame with the small brass bellows that had been her mother's and before that her grandmother's and still bore the dent from some occasion no one had bothered to record. The taproom smelled of yesterday's ale and pipe smoke and cold stone. She opened the two windows a quarter-inch each to let the night air clear it and went to the bar. Bar stock: she counted glasses, checked the cask taps, noted that the second small cask of porter was running near its end and would need drawing off by noon or the lees would make the last pints undrinkable. She wrote this on the small slate hung beside the bar for exactly this purpose. Bottles on the shelf behind: brandy, gin, two of the better claret, three of the ordinary. The brandy was down by more than the evening's accounts explained. She looked at this for a moment without expression and wrote a second note on the slate. She moved back through the taproom and into the passage and checked the cellar hatch -- locked, as she had left it -- then went to the dining room and pushed the door open. Four tables, each set for the morning, the linen clean from yesterday and laid again last night by Nan, who was thorough about this and did not need to be asked. Two chairs had been pushed out of alignment. She straightened them. Back through the kitchen. She pulled her sleeves down over her wrists, because the morning was cold enough that her hands had gone stiff, and she needed them working. The windows above the sink were rimed at their edges, the frost making small fern shapes in the corners of the glass. October now, properly, and the year without summer had become a year without autumn -- the cold had come in before the harvest was properly finished and everyone on the road was saying the same thing about it: that the spring had been wrong and the summer had been wrong and the year itself was wrong and there was nothing to be done. Dinah had noted the coal prices in June and she had ordered double in August and she did not spend more time than this on the subject of what could not be controlled. She put water on to heat. The sound from the stable yard came at quarter to six: Jem's particular movement across the cobbles, unhurried, the weight of a man who knew exactly how much distance he needed to cover and had no interest in covering it faster than necessary. She did not go to look. He was in the yard because the morning required it, not because she had told him to be. By the time she heard him at the tack room she had the kitchen fire drawing properly and the first water near the boil. Nan came through the back door at ten past six, pins in her hair not yet settled, apron already tied. She went directly to the bar without a word, without stopping in the kitchen, and Dinah heard the familiar sequence: the bar cloth lifted from its hook, the tap checked, the glasses arranged. They had done this before. Enough times that doing it had become the shape of the day itself, the frame inside which everything else happened. Dinah made herself tea, drank half of it standing, and checked the clock above the kitchen mantelpiece. The London coach was due at half past eight. She had time. She did not have time. The coach came up the road at twenty minutes past eight, which should have been ten minutes early and a welcome thing, except that it came at the pace of something that had been pushed rather than merely driven, and when it turned into the yard she could see from the way the lead pair were moving that they had been worked harder than the conditions warranted. She was already in the yard when the coachman climbed down from the box. He was a short, weather-beaten man named Croft, whom she knew by sight from the Tuesdays and Fridays. He had an expression on him now that she recognized: the face of a man who has had a bad morning and intends to share the experience. "North road above Hatfield," he said, without greeting. "There's a section gone soft just past the second milestone. Not the road surface -- the verge. Something's given way underneath and the camber's dropped a good four inches over maybe thirty foot of road. If it keeps raining the way it's rained, that section will be impassable by the month's end." "How impassable," Dinah said. "Impassable enough that I'll be taking the old way through Woolmer Green, which adds forty minutes and costs me a passenger change at Welwyn that I cannot afford." "Have you reported it to the surveyor?" "I've reported it to every man I can think of who might do something about it, and not one of them has done anything, and so I'm reporting it to you, because you're on this road every day same as I am and because someone ought to know." "I'll write to the parish surveyor today," Dinah said. "There's a back coach from Stevenage at three and he'll know it already but I'll write anyway. That's what I can do." She nodded toward the inn. "Your passengers will want breakfast. The dining room is set." Croft looked at her for a moment with the expression of a man who had expected more and was recalculating what he had gotten. Then he nodded. "Right." He went in. Jem was already at the lead pair, not because she had signalled him but because he had been watching from the tack room door with the particular quality of attention of a man who assesses before he acts. He ran a hand down the near horse's leg, checked the hoof briefly, and led them toward the stable without comment. One of the passengers, a woman of around fifty in a travelling dress of good quality but evident hard use, emerged from the coach with the assistance of no one and the expression of a person arriving not at a destination but at a confrontation. "My trunk," she said, to the yard in general. The trunk was on the roof, and when Jem's boy came to lift it down it was immediately clear that one of the brass hinges on the near side had come half away from the leather, leaving the lid sitting at an angle and the contents visible to anyone who cared to look. The woman drew a breath that suggested she had been drawing this particular breath since somewhere south of Hatfield and had not yet found anyone deserving of what followed it. Dinah said, "We have a leather-worker in the village who does good work. If you're staying tonight I can send to him this morning and he'll have the hinge reset before you leave tomorrow." She looked at the trunk with the frank assessment of a woman accustomed to evaluating the practical state of objects. "If it's the wood that's cracked beneath the fitting we can have it stabilised with a brace. It'll hold the journey." The woman looked at her. The prepared speech went somewhere it would not be needed. "Tonight, then," she said. "I'll need the room with the southern aspect if it's available. My hip." "Room seven," Dinah said. "Nan will take you up." Nan had appeared at the inn door. Dinah did not need to check. The trunk went with Jem's boy, listing slightly to the left. The second passenger, a thin man with the look of a commercial traveller who had been having a mediocre year, went into the dining room and asked about the quality of the eggs. Nan told him they were good and brought him tea and he did not ask again. The yard cleared. The coach went to the stable for watering and the horses for their hay and the morning resumed its ordinary pace. Dinah went back to the kitchen to meet the first of the day's deliveries. The chandler was punctual, which was the best thing she could say for him at present. He arrived at ten o'clock with his cart and his ledger and his candles done up in paper bundles of a dozen, and he stood in the kitchen doorway with the air of a man who had prepared his opening position on the walk over. "Prices have moved again," he said. "Tallow's up a penny a pound from my supplier and I can't hold it back any longer, Miss Dale. It's sixpence the dozen now rather than five." Dinah looked at him. "It was fivepence in March," she said. "It was fivepence in June. It was fivepence in August." "And the tallow market has moved three times since March." "The tallow market moved in May," she said. "I know, because I buy tallow for the kitchen and I spoke to Mrs Hartley at the Rose and Crown in June and she told me you were still charging her fivepence. In October." She paused. "Sixpence is the price you charge the houses on the Stevenage road. I am not a house on the Stevenage road. I am taking eighty candles a month from you and I have been taking eighty candles a month from you for three years and the price was set at fivepence on the understanding that volume warranted it." The chandler had an expression she had seen before, on men who had come in with a settled figure and found the ground shifting. "Even so," he said. "My costs--" "Your costs allow you fivepence and a half and still leave your margin sound," Dinah said. "Which is what I'll pay. Five and a half, and I'll take ninety this month instead of eighty because I have guests through November." There was a pause. "Five and a half," the chandler said. "And I'll want them dry," she said, looking at the bundles on his cart. "The last order had two dozen that were soft at the base. I didn't make a fuss over it then. I'm making it now." He unloaded the candles. They did not part warmly. He left, and the door closed, and the kitchen was quiet again with the sound of the fire and the occasional movement from the stable yard. Dinah took the delivery note from the kitchen table and wrote the adjusted price in the margin, in ink, neatly. She put it in the ledger. She was at the stove, moving the soup stock forward on the heat, when the thought arrived as thoughts like this arrived: without drama, between other things, the way a stone sits in a path you have walked a hundred times. The ledger. The chandler's price. The rent in six weeks. All of it tallied against the same fact she had tallied it against before, which was that if she married, the inn would not be hers to stand in and argue over candle prices. The lease and the income and the building itself would belong to whoever she married, and she would belong to him, and the negotiation she had just conducted would be conducted on his behalf, not hers. She had done this arithmetic many times. She was onto the next task before she finished the thought. She was at the back window at half past eleven when she saw him. He was standing outside the kitchen door, and he had not knocked yet. Young -- younger than his posture suggested, or perhaps it was the other way round. Boots split at the welt on both feet, the upper leather pulling away from the sole. A coat that had been good once and had since served through conditions that a good coat should not have had to serve through. He was very thin. He stood with the particular quality of stillness of a person assessing whether the space in front of him was one that would receive him, and she could see in the angle of his shoulders that he had already calculated the odds and was not certain they favoured knocking. She opened the door before he raised his hand. He was perhaps twenty. A scar on the jaw, not recent. Brown eyes with something behind them she recognised without naming it, because naming it was not her business. "Have you eaten today," she said. He answered honestly. "Not since yesterday morning." "Kitchen," she said. "Not the taproom. Sit there." She indicated the bench beside the table. He

Tallied the last of the receipts, closed the ledger, and put it aside. Six weeks. Give or take a few days depending on what the north coach brought in and whether the chandler held his price, which he would not, but six weeks was the number she had to work with this morning, and she held it flat in her mind the way she held a hot pan--by the handle, away from her body, without flinching. She pushed back from the kitchen table. The fire wanted building. She built it. The wood was damp at the edges from last night's rain and she had to coax it with the bellows until the first decent flame caught and began to mean something. The kitchen came up around her slowly: the copper pots hanging in their row, the smell of yesterday's broth still in the air, the particular grey of early morning coming through the single window above the sink. She did not look at it as scenery. She looked at it as a list of tasks. Taproom next. The fire there had gone to ash and she raked it out before laying fresh wood, her hands clumsy with cold until the warmth reached them. She moved along the bar checking stock: the level in each bottle, the state of the tap fittings, the mugs stacked behind the counter. One was chipped at the lip. She set it aside and would not use it for a guest. She would use it herself at midday, because it poured just as well and she was not a guest. Back through to the kitchen to check the bar ledger against what she'd already counted. The tallies matched to within sixpence, which meant either Nan had been careful or the evening had been slow. She noted it either way. The windows were rimed at the edges--a thin crust of frost on the inner glass that told her the temperature had dropped further in the night than she'd expected. She pulled her sleeves down over her wrists, tugged them straight, and went to check the yard. Jem was already in the stable. She saw him through the gap in the door before she pushed it open: the orderly movement of a man working through his morning the same way she worked through hers, not because anyone had asked him to but because the morning required it. He was straightening tack with his one good hand, the leather dropping into place with the practised ease of long habit, his right sleeve pinned neatly below the elbow. He did not look up when she appeared in the doorway. She did not feel the need to speak. She looked over the stalls--four horses in at present, all quiet--and went back inside. Nan arrived through the kitchen door perhaps ten minutes later, her cap not yet pinned properly, her apron already on and tied. She went straight to the bar without a word. Dinah heard the sound of cloth on wood as Nan began wiping down the counter. They had done this before. They would do it again tomorrow. Dinah put water on for tea and began setting the dining room for the morning meal. --- The London coach was late, which was nothing unusual on the north road in October, and arrived with two passengers instead of four, which was more of a problem. The coachman brought it in tight to the yard gate and climbed down already composing his face into the expression of a man with a grievance he considers reasonable. Dinah was in the yard when he came through, and she saw the passengers before she heard what the coachman had to say. The first was a stout woman of fifty or so with an aggrieved expression and a trunk whose hinge had given way somewhere around Barnet. The trunk was being held closed by a length of cord that one of the outside men had tied with more goodwill than skill. The second passenger was a thin gentleman in a wool coat who moved through the yard with the determined invisibility of a man who wished to be in his room before anyone required anything of him. The woman with the trunk stopped in the middle of the yard and looked at Dinah. "The hinge," she said, as though this required no further elaboration. "I can see that." Dinah raised her voice across the yard. "Jem." He appeared without being called, which was what she'd counted on. She directed him to the trunk with a look. He took in the situation in a single glance and moved the woman gently aside before she could argue about it. Dinah turned to the coachman. He had the face of a man accustomed to delivering bad news about roads. "State of the surface above Hatfield," he said. "Third time this month I've brought it in on one of those ruts. It wants grading before winter's properly here or we'll be losing an axle." "I've written to the turnpike trust twice since September," she said. "Write again yourself. You carry more weight than I do with them." "I carry freight, Miss Dale. Not influence." "Then find a magistrate who uses this road and make him uncomfortable about it. That's how it gets done." She turned back toward the door. "There's tea in the dining room and the fire's been up an hour." He opened his mouth, looked at her face, and closed it. She had given him two minutes and spent them. --- The second passenger had already gone upstairs. The trunk woman--a Mrs Hodgson, as it emerged when Nan took her coat--settled into the dining room with her grievance somewhat deflated by the speed with which Jem had resecured the hinge. Jem had also, Dinah noticed, managed to make the repair look temporary enough that Mrs Hodgson felt she had suffered without making it look so poor that it would fail again before Bristol. This was a distinction of some subtlety and she did not take it for granted. The yard cleared. Nan moved from the bar to the dining room and back with the practiced economy of someone who can cover two rooms without appearing to rush. Dinah watched her settle the woman with tea and a plate of bread and did not say anything, because nothing needed saying. --- The chandler arrived at half past ten. He was a compact man named Farthing--a name he appeared to find no humour in--who made his monthly circuit of the coaching establishments between Hatfield and Stevenage and considered himself indispensable to all of them. He was not wrong about this, which was the essential problem. "Tallow's up," he said, before he had properly set down his box. "Sixpence on the dozen." "I know what tallow costs," Dinah said. "Sit down." He sat, which meant he was open to the conversation. If he had intended to hold the price firm he would have stayed standing. "That's the fourth increase in seven months," she said. "Supply and demand, Miss Dale. The manufacturers--" "The manufacturers supply Barkley's on the Baldock road at eleven and six the dozen. You've been supplying them at eleven and four. I want eleven and four." A brief silence. He picked up his pen and put it down again. "Barkley's takes a larger quantity." "Barkley's burns twice what I burn because they're heating three times the space and they leave the lights running in the dining room for effect. I burn what I need and not a candle more, and you know it, which is why it costs you less to supply me. Eleven and four." Farthing looked at his delivery sheet. He looked at the window. He looked back at his delivery sheet. "Eleven and five," he said. "Eleven and four." He wrote the number down with the precise, controlled gesture of a man who has conceded a point and intends to extract value from the memory of it. He stood, picked up his box, and went. The door closed. The inn was quiet for a moment in the way that a room is quiet after a negotiation--not peaceful, exactly, but settled. Dinah wrote the adjusted figure in the margin of the delivery note and moved the candles to the shelf. It was only later, transferring the number to the weekly account, that the thought arrived the way working thoughts do: sideways, complete, already on its way out again before it finished. The chandler would not have sat down for a woman with no standing to enforce the price. He sat down because the White Hound was hers and had been hers long enough that he knew she knew his margins. If she married, the standing would transfer with the lease and the ledger and everything else, and whoever sat across from Farthing next month would start the negotiation from the beginning. She moved to the next column. --- She saw him through the kitchen window before he knocked. He was young--younger than she'd expected, though she wasn't certain why she'd been expecting anyone--and thin in the way of someone who had recently been thinner, the flesh coming back slowly over bones that still showed their work. His coat had been good once. His boots were coming apart at the welt along the left side, the leather pulling away from the sole in a way that would let in water with the next rain.
