He stood at the edge of the kitchen step and looked at the door, and then he looked at the yard, and then he looked at the door again. He was deciding whether he was welcome enough to knock. He knocked. She opened the door. He was perhaps twenty, or looked it, though the eyes were older. He had the particular quality of stillness that she had learned to read--not calm, but controlled, the way a man holds himself when he is not certain of his reception. "Have you eaten?" she asked. "Not today." "Kitchen," she said. "Not the taproom. You can sit there." She stepped back to let him in. He understood the terms and did not argue them. She put bread on the table, ladled broth from the pot that had been keeping warm since morning, and set it in front of him without ceremony. He ate in the careful way of a man who is hungry and is making himself go slowly anyway, which told her more than she needed to know. She did not watch him eat. She wiped down the counter and checked the level in the soup pot and moved the bread to a cooler part of the shelf. When he had finished he set his spoon down neatly and said, "Thank you, miss." "Where are you headed?" she asked. "North," he said. "Somewhere north." She nodded. She did not say anything else, and he did not offer anything else, and neither of them said anything about the war. He thanked her once more at the door and went, and she bolted it after him, practical against the cold, and cleared the bowl. --- The trouble in the taproom was not serious and she did not treat it as such. Two men who had been sitting over their ale since the coach left and had run out of good nature by the time the afternoon turned toward evening. She heard the voices before she reached the door and she knew by the pitch of them--not quite there yet, but moving--exactly how much time she had. "That's enough," she said, from the doorway. Not loudly. "You'll finish your drinks or you'll leave them. I don't mind which." The larger of the two looked at her. The smaller one looked at the table. Nan, behind the bar, did not need to intervene. It was over in the time it took both men to remember they were in someone else's establishment and had nowhere better to be. --- The afternoon passed the way midafternoon passes in October when the light is already going by three: steadily, without distinction, one task giving way to the next. Dinah worked through the accounts for the upstairs rooms, reset the linen in two of them, checked the kitchen stock against tomorrow's expected arrivals, calculated what she would need from the butcher in the morning. Nan moved through the taproom and the dining room in her own orbit. Jem was outside. The inn breathed. --- By evening the last coach had come and gone, the guests had eaten, and the work of settling the house had begun. This was the part of the day she had done hundreds of times and would do hundreds more: the circuit of the inn moving from fire to fire, room to room, checking each one the way you check a living thing before you leave it for the night. Taproom fire down to coals--she set the guard and made sure the ash pan was clear. The bar tallied against what Nan had entered: it came out within threepence and she was not going to worry about threepence at this hour. The cellar hatch locked. She tried it twice out of habit. The kitchen banked, the pots moved off the heat, the doors checked. Out to the yard for the last time. The cold was proper now, not the thin cold of morning but the settled cold that meant the temperature would not recover before dawn. The stable was dark and quiet--Jem would have settled the horses before he turned in. She stood in the yard for a moment and did not call it rest; it was merely the pause between the last task finished and the walk back inside. The second floor showed a lit shape at Bridget's window. The curtain moved once, or perhaps it was the candle in a draught. Dinah did not look long. Back inside, she stood at the kitchen table. Her hands, she noticed, were reddened from the cold water and the afternoon's work--the scrubbing of the pots, the resetting of the rooms. There was a small cut along the side of her index finger where the edge of the chandler's delivery note had caught her without her noticing. It was the invoice that had done it, not the work. She looked at this without registering it as significant and looked away. The inn was quiet in the specific way of a place that has been used hard and is now resting. Not empty--the guests were upstairs, Bridget was at her window, Jem was in his room above the stable--but quiet in the way that belongs to the end of a full day, when everything that needed doing has been done and the space has settled back into itself. She stood in it. The letter was on the table. She had not put it there, which meant Nan had set it aside for her, probably when the afternoon post came and Dinah had been occupied with the linen. It was a letter of no particular size, addressed in a hand she recognised without needing to look closely, and sealed with a mark she also recognised. Stainforth's mark. She picked it up. She turned it once in her hands, feeling the weight of it--not heavy, letters were never as heavy as they ought to be--and looked at the direction written on the front. Her name, the inn's name, the road. His handwriting was measured and regular, the hand of a man who believed in legibility as a form of authority. She had known this was coming since the last quarter's rent. She had known it the way you know a storm is coming when the light changes in a particular direction and the horses shift in the stalls--not proof, but as certain as proof. She had been moving faster ever since. The ledger, the chandler's margin, the six weeks: all of it calibrated against the arrival of this letter. She set it on the table and did not open it. She would open it in the morning when she had slept and when the numbers were steady in her head and when she could read whatever was inside with the same flatness she had read the ledger this morning, the same flatness with which she had managed Farthing and the coachman and the men in the taproom and all the rest of it. She would read it in the morning and she would know what it said and she would decide what to do. Tonight she left it on the table. She pulled her sleeves down over her wrists. "Right," she said to the empty kitchen. "Right, then." The lamp burned. The inn settled around her. She put her hand to the door.

I checked the ledger twice, which was once more than the numbers deserved. Six weeks. Six weeks until the quarter's rent came due, and the column would not rearrange itself no matter how steadily I looked at it. The tallow was down to eleven candles. The ale account was current but only because I had shorted the last order by a firkin and Mr Gedge had not yet noticed, or had noticed and was saving the observation for a moment when it would cost me more. I closed the ledger and set my hand flat on the cover, as though the book might open itself again if left unattended. The kitchen was cold. I had built the fire twenty minutes ago but it was a modest fire, because coal was coal and October had weeks left in it, and I was not in the habit of warming rooms before they needed warming. The window glass had rimed at its edges overnight, a fine craquelure of ice that would melt by midmorning and leave the panes wet. I pulled my sleeves down over my wrists and went to check the bread. The loaves had gone in before I opened the ledger. They were not ready. I closed the oven door and crossed to the taproom. The fire there was dead. I cleaned the grate, set kindling, struck the flint. The taproom when it was empty had a particular smell -- old ale and tallow smoke and the wood of the bar itself, which was elm and had been elm since my grandmother's time and carried sixty years of spillage in its grain. I ran my hand along it as I passed. The surface was tacky in two places, which meant Nan had been called away before she finished wiping down last night. I noted it and moved on. The bar stock was where it should be. The gin was low. I would not replace it until the end of the week. I checked the dining room, which needed nothing. I checked the front door, which was bolted. I unbolted it. I checked the yard from the kitchen window. Jem was there already, moving between the stalls. He had the lamp lit and was straightening a harness on its hook, his one hand and the stump of his other arm working together in the economy of motion he had spent three years perfecting. He did not look toward the kitchen. He did not need to. The morning had a shape and he was inside it, same as me. I went back to the bread. Nan came through the kitchen door a few minutes later, pins half-set in her hair and apron already tied. She took one look at the fire, one look at me, and went through to the taproom without a word. I heard the cloth on the bar before I heard anything else. The two tacky spots would be gone in a minute. The London coach was late, which was not unusual for this stretch of the Great North Road in late October when the ruts north of Hatfield turned to soup after every rain. I heard it before I saw it -- the particular grinding complaint of iron rims on gravel that meant a heavy coach carrying fewer passengers than it should. When it swung into the yard I was at the door. Two passengers, not the four we expected. A woman with a trunk whose hinge had split and a man who looked as though the last twelve miles had personally offended him. The woman descended first and the trunk was handed down after her in a manner that did nothing for the hinge. "That trunk wants a strap," I said. "Jem -- " He was already there. He took the trunk's weight and bore it toward the door, and I turned to the woman, who had opened her mouth to begin what I could see would be a detailed accounting of the trunk's mistreatment. "We'll have it secured before you leave," I said. "Dining room is through and to the left. There's tea." She closed her mouth. She went. The coachman climbed down stiffly and walked his shoulders in a circle. He was a man I knew by sight -- Pardoe, out of the Bell Savage, who made this run three days a week and did not waste conversation on anyone who could not help him. "Road's gone above Hatfield," he said. "Carters have dug it to pieces this side of the hill. Another week of rain and you'll lose the afternoon service." "Has Gurnett not filled it?" "Gurnett fills it with chalk, and chalk washes out the first night. You want it held, it wants gravel and a drain cut to the ditch." "I'll write to the surveyor." "You've written to the surveyor." "Then I'll write again. Two minutes, Mr Pardoe. Nan will have your ale." He went. I did not have time to write to the surveyor, nor any confidence that a third letter would accomplish what two had not, but the road was the inn's livelihood and I noted it in the place in my mind where I kept the things that must be done before they became the things that had not been done in time. The yard cleared. The offended man had taken himself inside without requiring direction, which was the most convenient thing about offended men: they seldom wanted to be managed, only to sit somewhere and go on being offended in comfort. Jem had the horses. The trunk was inside. Nan was behind the bar with a cloth in her hand and one eye on the door through the whole of it, the way she always watched -- not anxiously, but with the attention of someone who catalogued arrivals the way other women catalogued weather. I noted it. Said nothing. The chandler came at half past ten with his cart and his list and his quarterly adjustment, which was what he called a price increase when he wished it to sound like the movement of celestial bodies -- inevitable, impersonal, beyond any one man's control. "Tallow's up again," said Mr Fleet, setting the crate on the kitchen table. "Tenpence ha'penny the dozen." I did not look at the crate. I looked at him. "It was ninepence in April." "And tallow was cheaper in April." "Tallow is not dearer now. Rayner's rendering yard has been running double since Michaelmas and you buy from Rayner at fourpence the pound, same as last year. You charge the King's Arms tenpence. You charge the George ninepence three-farthings. You may charge me ninepence ha'penny, Mr Fleet, which is what you charged me in July and which gives you a margin I consider generous for a cart ride of four miles." He looked at me with the flat, recalculating expression of a man whose figures had just been read back to him by someone who should not have known them. "Ninepence three-farthings," he said. "Ninepence ha'penny. And I'll take two dozen." A silence. He removed one dozen from the crate and set it beside the other on the table. Then he named the total, which was correct, and I paid him from the cashbox, and he wrote the receipt. I wrote the adjusted price in the margin of the delivery note while he was still in the room, so that he would see me do it. He left. The door closed behind him. I stood in the kitchen with the delivery note and the candles and the sound of the yard settling back into its ordinary business. If I married, none of this would be mine to negotiate. The ledger, the suppliers, the lease itself -- all of it would pass into a husband's hands the moment the register was signed, and no amount of knowing Mr Fleet's margins would matter if the man holding the cashbox was not me. I had done this arithmetic before. I set the candles in the store cupboard and went to check the dining room. He came around the back, which was where the sign directed anyone arriving on foot and without a horse. I saw him through the kitchen window before he knocked: young, very thin, standing two feet from the door with his weight shifted as though he had not yet committed to being there. His coat had been military once and was civilian now only by virtue of its condition. His boots were coming apart at the welt, the sole of the left one separating along the toe in a way that meant he had been walking in wet weather for days. He looked at the door. He looked at the yard. He raised his hand and then lowered it and stood a moment longer, measuring something I could not see. I opened the door. He startled, but only just. Up close he was perhaps twenty, perhaps younger. His face was weather-dark and there was nothing in his expression that asked for anything. "Have you eaten?" I said. He considered this as though the question required honest accounting. "Not since yesterday, ma'am. The day before that, I'm not certain." "In the kitchen. Not the taproom." "Yes, ma'am." I sat him at the table and set before him what there was -- bread from the morning's baking, broth from the stockpot that had been going since the previous night, a piece of hard cheese. He ate carefully, not fast, the way men eat when they are hungry enough that their stomachs have stopped expecting food and must be reminded how to receive it. I did not sit with him. I worked at the counter with my back half-turned so that he would not feel observed. When he had finished he stood and brought the bowl to the side of the basin without being asked. "Thank you, ma'am." "Which way are you headed?" "North," he said. "Leeds, or thereabouts." He went out the back the way he'd come. I closed the door. I cleared the bowl and wiped the table and put the bread away. In the taproom two men who had been sitting over their pints since late morning had found something to disagree about -- I could hear the pitch of it changing through the doorway, the particular upward tilt that meant a conversation was about to stop being a conversation and start being a problem. I went through. They were at the table nearest the window, leaning across their tankards in a posture I had seen a thousand times. "Gentlemen," I said. "Whatever the quarrel, it will keep until you are outside my house." They looked at me. They looked at each other. They sat back. Nan, behind the bar, set down the glass she'd been drying and picked up another one. She had not needed to intervene. She had not expected to. The afternoon stretched out in the way it did between coaches -- a long, quiet reach of hours in which the inn breathed and settled and required maintenance rather than management. Nan at the bar. Jem in the yard. The rooms on the first floor wanted airing. The kitchen wanted provisioning for the evening. I made a list, worked through it, started another. The light through the taproom windows shifted from grey-white to grey as the cloud cover thickened. I lit a candle at four o'clock, one of Mr Fleet's, and thought of ninepence ha'penny and nothing else. By evening the last guests had eaten, the last orders had been poured and settled, the fire was down to its embers in the taproom. I checked the cellar hatch: locked. I checked the front door: bolted. The kitchen fire I banked for the night, raking the coals forward and setting the guard. The dining room was clear. The bar was wiped. Nan had gone up twenty minutes before with a brief word and a briefer nod, and the inn was mine alone now, which was the same as saying it was itself alone -- released from the duty of being used and returned to the state of simply being a building. I made my last circuit. Taproom: coals settling, the elm bar dark and clean, the chairs pushed in. Front door: the bolt seated in its cradle. Stable yard through the window: quiet, Jem's lamp out, the horses settled. I could hear nothing from the stalls, which meant he had done his work and gone to his room above the tack store. The second floor of the inn: a light behind Bridget's window, the shape of her in the chair where she sat most evenings, watching the yard with the attention she gave to everything and the commentary she reserved for when it was least expected. I did not look long. My hands at the end of the day were what they always were. Reddened across the knuckles from cold water and the scrubbing of surfaces, rough at the fingertips where the lye in the cleaning soap ate at the skin. There was a small cut on my right index finger, a clean slice from the edge of the chandler's delivery note. I had not noticed it when it happened. I noticed it now only because it stung when I wiped my hands on my apron, and even then I looked at it only long enough to confirm that it was not bleeding. It was the invoice that had cut me. That seemed about right. In the kitchen I stopped. On the table, where I had not seen it before -- or perhaps where it had been placed in the afternoon while I was elsewhere in the house -- there was a letter. I did not need to pick it up to know the seal. Stainforth. His hand on the direction, the ink heavy and unhurried. I picked it up anyway. I held it. I turned it over once, felt the weight of the paper, which was good paper -- better paper than I used for my own accounts. The seal was unbroken. I set it down on the table and looked at it the way you look at something you have been expecting for long enough that its arrival is not a surprise but only a confirmation. I had known since the last quarter. I had been moving faster ever since -- the ledger, the suppliers, the gin I would not replace until the end of the week, the road I could not get repaired, the margin I had fought for on Mr Fleet's candles. All of it against this. I did not open it. Not tonight. I put my hand on the back of the chair and stood in the kitchen, which was dark except for the banked fire, and quiet except for the sound of the building settling around me the way it settled every night, timber and stone contracting in the cold. "Right, then," I said. No one heard it. I turned the lamp down and went to bed.

I checked the ledger twice because the first time the numbers were worse than I remembered, and the second time they were exactly the same. Six weeks. Perhaps seven, if the weather held and the coaches kept running full. The column on the right-hand side of the page -- what came in -- and the column on the left -- what went out -- had been drawing toward each other all autumn like two dogs circling in a yard, and I did not care for what would happen when they met. I closed the book and set my hand flat on its cover, as if the figures might try to crawl out while my back was turned. The kitchen fire had burned down to a dull orange crust in the night. I knelt and raked the ashes forward, laid kindling crosswise the way my mother had taught me, and blew until the flame caught. The floorboards were cold through my stockings. I had come downstairs without shoes -- a habit I ought to break, and would not. The kitchen was mine at this hour in a way it was not once the day began, and I did not wish to announce my arrival to it. I filled the kettle and hung it on the crane. Checked the bread bin: two loaves from yesterday, still good enough for toast, not good enough for the table. The butter crock was down by a third. I had noted this already in the ledger but I noted it again now, with my hands, pressing the cloth cover down and feeling the level beneath. My fingers were stiff. The windows were rimed at their edges, a thin crust of frost that caught no light because there was no light yet to catch. The taproom fire took longer. The grate had not been properly cleared the night before -- my own fault -- and I spent several minutes on my knees with the ash bucket, scraping out the cold mass of yesterday's fuel before I could lay a new one. The air in the taproom was thick and stale, carrying the ghost of pipe smoke and spilled ale. I opened the small window behind the bar and let the cold pour in. Better cold than foul. Bar stock: I counted bottles by feel in the dimness. The gin was lower than it ought to be, which meant I had poured more generously than I intended last night or someone had helped himself after hours. I set the bottle at the front of the shelf where I would see it clearly later and moved on. The cask of ordinary ale was two-thirds full. The strong ale, barely tapped. No one was drinking the strong ale this month. The price was not dear but people's pockets were, and they chose accordingly. I tallied the previous night's take against the chalkboard behind the bar. The figures matched. One evening's income would not have impressed anyone, but it was correct, and being correct was the only victory available at six o'clock in the morning. The stable yard was grey when I looked out the back door. Jem was there already, straightening a length of tack on the hook beside the mounting block. He did not look up. His one hand moved with the deliberate precision I had grown accustomed to, the stump of his left arm bracing the leather against the post while his right hand worked the buckle. I watched him for a moment -- not because he needed watching, but because the sight of a person doing useful work before being asked to do it was a comfort I had not learned to take for granted. I pulled the door shut and went back inside. Nan came through the kitchen a few minutes later, pins in her hair not yet properly set, her apron already tied. She took her place behind the bar without a word and began wiping down the counter with a rag she must have picked up from the shelf on her way through, because I had not put one out. We had done this before. Many hundreds of times. The London coach was late. This was not unusual. What was unusual was that it arrived carrying two passengers instead of the expected four, and both of them came through the front door with expressions that suggested the Great North Road had personally offended them. The first was a stout woman in a travelling cloak who carried a trunk with a split hinge. She set this down in the hallway with the air of a person presenting evidence at a trial. The second was a thin man in a brown coat who said nothing and went directly upstairs. "This has been like this since Barnet," the woman said, pointing at the trunk. "I cannot be expected to travel the length of England with my belongings falling into the road." "Jem," I said, and he was there -- I do not know how he heard from the yard, but he always did -- and took the trunk by its leather handles, tilting it so the broken hinge held shut under its own weight. He carried it toward the stairs without comment. The woman opened her mouth to say something further, reconsidered, and followed him. The coachman had climbed down and was standing in the yard, stamping his feet against the cold. He was a man I had seen many times -- Gibbs, or Gibbon -- the regular driver on the Tuesday route. "Road's breaking up past Hatfield," he said when I came out. "Ruts deep enough to lose a wheel. You'll want to warn anyone heading north this afternoon." "I will. How far past Hatfield?" "Mile and a half, perhaps two. Where the road crosses the stream bed. The rain's done for it." He blew into his hands. "If the trust doesn't send someone to relay gravel before the frost sets in hard, you'll be pulling coaches out of ditches all winter." "I'll send word to the surveyor. You've twenty minutes if you want something hot." He nodded and went inside. I could have given him more than twenty minutes. I could have given him an hour. But the coach had a schedule, and the schedule was not mine to adjust, and neither of us had time to pretend otherwise. The coachman went. The passengers had disappeared into their rooms or the dining room. Jem took the trunk upstairs and returned to the yard. The small bustle of arrival passed through the inn and out again like a breath, and the building settled. Nan was behind the bar, wiping the same surface she had already wiped, her eyes on the doorway through which the last of the morning's activity had gone. I noticed it. Said nothing. The chandler arrived at half past ten, which was his custom on Tuesdays. He was a man called Fitchett, narrow-shouldered, with a careful way of setting down his crate of candles that suggested he wanted full credit for the effort of carrying them. He had a ledger of his own, smaller than mine, which he consulted as he stood in the kitchen doorway, though I suspected he had memorized the figure before he left his shop. "Tallow per dozen, one and tenpence," he said. "It was one and seven last month." "Cost of tallow's gone up. Everyone's paying more." "Everyone on this stretch of road is paying one and seven, Mr Fitchett. I spoke to Mrs Beale at the Red Hart on Saturday and she confirmed it." A pause. He had not expected me to have spoken to Mrs Beale. He adjusted the crate on his hip. "One and nine." "One and seven." "Miss Dale, I cannot sell below my cost." "Your cost for rendered tallow is ninepence per dozen, and has been since August. I know because your carter told Jem when he was drunk, and Jem does not forget figures. One and seven gives you tenpence clear, which is exactly what you were making in June when you were cheerful enough about the arrangement." Fitchett stared at me. I stared back. The kitchen was very quiet. Somewhere behind me the kettle shifted on its crane with a small metallic sound. "One and eight," he said. "One and seven. And I'll take an extra two dozen this month, which you may tell anyone who asks." He set the crate down on the kitchen table. He made a note in his ledger. He did not look at me as he did it. "Pleasure doing business, Miss Dale." I did not reply. He let himself out through the kitchen door, and I listened to his boots on the yard stones until the sound of them was gone. I wrote the adjusted price in the margin of the delivery note. One and seven. The ink blurred slightly where my pen pressed too hard. I set the note aside and began counting candles into the storage box, and somewhere between the eighth and the ninth my mind did what it did when the numbers pressed in -- it ran the calculation I had made before, the one that sat beneath all the other calculations like the foundation beneath a floor. The lease was mine so long as I held it alone. If I married, everything transferred. The inn, the income, the forty years my family had poured into this place. One signature in a church register and it became some man's property, to run or ruin or sell as he chose. This was not a grievance. It was arithmetic. I set the ninth candle into the box and reached for the tenth. I saw him through the window before he knocked. Young -- younger than Jem, younger than the coachman -- with a thin face and a coat that had once been military issue, though someone had torn the insignia from the sleeve. His boots were coming apart where the upper met the sole, the welt splitting along both sides so that his stockings showed through. He stood outside the kitchen door with his hand raised, not yet touching the wood. He was looking at the door the way a man looks at a thing he is not certain he is permitted to approach. He knocked. I opened it. He had a narrow, weather-darkened face and hair that had been cut badly and not recently. His eyes moved past me to the kitchen -- the fire, the kettle, the table with its candles and ledger -- and came back to my face. "Have you eaten?" I said. "Not today." He paused, as if deciding whether more honesty would help or hurt. "Not yesterday, properly." "Kitchen, not taproom. Sit there." I pointed to the bench by the hearth. He came in and sat where I told him. He did not remove his coat. I cut bread from yesterday's loaf and ladled broth from the pot that had been on the fire since morning. I set both in front of him with a spoon and nothing else, because ceremony was not what he needed and I did not have it to spare. He ate. Not fast, which surprised me. Men who had not eaten in two days usually ate as though the food might be taken away. This one ate steadily, deliberately, like a man who was rationing his own hunger. When he finished he set the spoon across the bowl and sat with his hands on his knees. "Thank you," he said. His voice was quiet and had the particular flatness of someone who has recently stopped expecting things. "Where are you headed?" "North." He hesitated. "Huntingdon, if I can make it." I nodded. He stood, and I saw him glance at the bread on the table, and then stop himself from glancing at it. I cut a second piece and held it out. He took it. "The road's bad past Hatfield," I said. "Keep to the verge where you can." He went out through the kitchen door. I watched him cross the yard and turn north along the lane, the bread in his hand, the broken boots dark against the frost-hardened mud. I closed the door and took the bowl to the basin and washed it. I dried it. I set it back on the shelf. The afternoon was a slow one. In the taproom, two men who had been sitting since midday found something to disagree about -- I never learned what -- and their voices began to climb in the way that voices climb when men have been still too long and the ale has given their grievances a shape. "Gentlemen," I said from the doorway. "You'll settle it outside or you'll settle it now, but you will not settle it in my taproom." They settled it now. Nan watched from behind the bar with her cloth in her hand and did not need to move. The hours between the coach's departure and the evening meal were the hours that belonged to the building itself. No one arrived. No one left. Nan polished glasses. Jem was outside -- I could hear the rhythmic scrape of a rake on cobblestones. I sat in the kitchen with the ledger open and the candles counted and the evening menu worked out in my head: what we had, what would stretch, what could not be made to stretch no matter how I managed it. The kitchen fire cracked and settled. I closed the ledger and went upstairs to check the rooms. Room four wanted airing. Room seven had a cracked pane that would need boarding before the cold deepened. I noted both and moved on, running my hand along the banister as I came down the stairs -- a gesture that had no practical purpose but that I had been performing since I was tall enough to reach, and that my mother had performed before me, and that I was not willing to examine for what it meant. Evening came down quickly. The last of the light went grey, then blue, then dark. The guests who remained had eaten and retired. The fire in the taproom was low, the coals orange beneath a fine white skin of ash. I stood before it for a moment, then closed the grate. The bar was tallied. Nan had gone upstairs an hour ago. The kitchen was clean -- the pots on their hooks, the knives in the drawer, the range shut down, the kettle empty and cool. I moved through the closing routine the way I had done it every night for two years, and every other night for the ten years before that when my mother was alive and I was learning the sequence. Taproom fire: coals safe, no open flame. Cellar hatch: bolted. Front door: locked, key on the nail. Stable yard: quiet. I stepped outside for a moment and stood in the cold. Jem had settled the horses. I could hear one of them shifting in its stall, a slow heavy sound that carried in the stillness. On the second floor, a light burned behind Bridget's window. The faint shape of her behind the glass -- seated, watchful. I did not look long. She was there. She was always there. I went back inside and shut the door. My hands, when I looked at them in the kitchen lamplight, were reddened from cold water and cleaning. There was a small cut on my right index finger -- the edge of Fitchett's delivery note, which I had not noticed at the time. The paper had been coarser than I expected. I examined the cut without interest and let my hand drop. The letter was on the kitchen table. It had not been there when I cleared the table before dinner, which meant it had been delivered to the front door while I was upstairs checking rooms, and Nan had set it in the kitchen for me, and I had been moving through the building all evening with this thing sitting here, waiting. I picked it up. The seal was a dull red -- Stainforth's seal, the one he used for correspondence with tenants, different from his magistrate's seal. I knew both. His handwriting on the direction was small and slanted and very regular, the hand of a man who did not waste ink. I turned the envelope over once. The paper was heavy. Good paper. He did not economize on paper. I set the letter down on the table. I did not open it. I had known it was coming. I had known since the last quarter's rent was received without acknowledgment -- no note of receipt, no confirmation, just silence where there had always been at least a formal line. The silence had been louder than any letter. I had been moving faster ever since, though I could not have said toward what. The kitchen was empty. The fire was down to its last coal. The inn around me was silent with the particular silence of a place that has worked hard and is done. "Right, then," I said. No one heard me. I turned down the lamp.

I checked the ledger twice because the first time the figures were worse than I remembered, and I thought I had carried a sum wrong. I had not carried a sum wrong. Six weeks and two days until the quarter's rent came due, and the margin between what I had and what I owed was narrowing by a quantity I could now measure in candles. I closed the ledger and pressed my palms flat against the table until the wood bit into the callus beneath my ring finger. Then I stood up and got on with it. The kitchen fire first. I riddled the ash through the grate and built it back up from the overnight embers, laying the kindling in the pattern my mother had taught me -- small sticks crossed, larger ones angled so the draught pulled through from below. The iron of the range was cold enough that my fingers stiffened against it. I blew on them once, tucked them under my arms, and waited for the first crackle of the kindling catching before I moved on. Taproom fire next. The hearth in the taproom was larger and dirtier, and the chimney pulled badly when the wind sat in the northeast, which it had been doing for three days. I knelt on the stone, cleared the grate, rebuilt the fire, and checked the draw by holding my hand above the flame until I felt the pull. It was adequate. It was not good. The chimney wanted sweeping, and the sweep wanted paying, and the paying wanted money I did not have in the proportion I wanted to spend it. I pulled my sleeves down over my wrists and went to the bar. Stock: the small cask of porter was a third gone, which was about right for mid-week. The brandy was low. I made a note on the slate hung behind the bar. Last night's accounts were on the shelf where I had left them, and I checked them standing up because sitting down at this hour would be a concession I could not afford. The figures tallied. Eleven shillings and fourpence. Not a bad night, not a good one. The kind of night that kept you where you were without moving you anywhere. The windows were rimed at the edges with a thin crust of ice that caught the early grey light. October had no business being this cold. I could see my breath in the taproom when I turned back toward the kitchen, and I noted, as I passed it, that the hinge on the door between the two rooms had begun to sag again. It would need shimming. I added it to the list I kept in my head, which was longer than the list I kept on paper because the paper list was for things I could fix today and the head list was for things that would have to wait. No one was awake. The inn held its breath the way it always did at this hour -- the particular silence of a building that would be loud in three hours and was storing up its quiet against the coming noise. Twenty rooms above me, most of them empty. A dining room that wanted laying for breakfast. The cold at my wrists and the work ahead of me and the ledger shut in the kitchen, its numbers no different for being closed. I went out the back. Jem was in the stable yard. He had the bridles down from their hooks and was checking the stitching on the cheekpieces, running his thumb along each join with the steady attention of a man who did this not because he had been told to but because the morning required it. He did not look up. The yard was swept. The water trough had been broken and re-iced overnight, and a thin sheet of new ice was forming at the edges, which he had already cracked with the heel of his boot. I could see the dark starburst pattern in the ice where he had stamped it. The horses were quiet. Everything was in order. I went back inside. Nan arrived through the kitchen door a few minutes later, her hair still half-pinned, her apron already tied. She crossed the kitchen without a word, took the cloth from the hook beside the bar, and began wiping down the counter. I heard the soft drag of the cloth against the wood as I laid the dining room for breakfast, and neither of us spoke, because there was nothing to say that the work did not say for us. We had done this before. We had done this so many times that the doing of it had become its own language, fluent and requiring no translation. The coach was late. It was not unusual, but I stood at the front window of the taproom and watched the road anyway because a late coach could mean a broken axle, a thrown shoe, or a disagreement between the coachman and the guard, any of which would become my problem the moment the thing rolled into the yard. When it finally appeared around the curve beyond the elm, I could tell from the lean of it that it was running light. Two passengers instead of the expected four, and one of them was already audible before the door opened. She had a trunk with a split hinge, and she had opinions about the split hinge, and she had opinions about the road, and she had opinions about the temperature of the inn, which she had not yet entered. I received all of this on the threshold with my hands clasped at my waist and my face arranged in the expression that my mother had called the front door and I called the cost of doing business. "The hinge will be seen to," I said. "Jem." He had appeared at my shoulder without being called, which was his way. He took the trunk. The woman watched him lift it one-handed and went quiet for a moment, which was long enough for me to steer her toward the dining room and the promise of a hot breakfast. The second passenger was no trouble. He wanted a room and got one. Nan took him upstairs. The coachman wanted a word. "Road north of Hatfield's gone to ruin past the turnpike," he said, standing in the yard with the steam still rising off the horses. He was a broad, wind-scoured man, and he spoke with the compressed urgency of someone who had four minutes before his schedule carried him away. "Hole the size of a pig trough just past the milestone. Nearly threw us into the ditch." "Which side?" "Left. Twenty yards past the second gate." "I'll send word to the turnpike trust," I said. "They won't mend it this week, but if it's on record they'll have less excuse come the quarter sessions." He nodded, satisfied not with the answer but with the fact that there was one. He touched his hat, climbed back up, and was gone. The yard cleared. The sound of the wheels faded into the road noise, and then there was only the wind and Jem leading the post-horses back toward the stable and Nan at the bar with one eye on the door, the cloth still in her hand, watching everything and commenting on nothing. I noted it. Said nothing. The chandler arrived at half past ten. His name was Fitch, and he had been supplying the White Hound with tallow candles since before my mother died. He was a lean, careful man with clean fingernails and a habit of looking just past your left ear when he named a price, as though the number were written on the wall behind you and he were merely reading it aloud. "Tenpence the dozen," he said, setting the crate on the kitchen table. I did not pick up a candle. I did not need to inspect them. I knew his tallow and I knew his wicks and I knew that tenpence the dozen was a halfpenny more than last month, which had been a halfpenny more than the month before, which had been a halfpenny more than the month before that. "You were at eightpence in April," I said. "Tallow's gone up." "Tallow went up in June. It came back down in August. You buy from Jessop in Welwyn and he's been selling at the same price since July. I know because he supplies the Red Lion as well and Mrs. Harding talks." Fitch's gaze drifted from the wall behind me to the ceiling above me, which apparently also had numbers written on it. "There's transport to consider." "You drive the same cart the same nine miles you've always driven. Your horse eats the same oats. If your transport costs have risen, it's because you're charging yourself more for the journey, and I don't see why I should pay for your imagination." A silence. Fitch looked at the crate on the table and then at me, and I could see him calculating whether the White Hound's custom was worth more than the halfpenny, and we both knew it was. I was his largest single order on this stretch of road. If he walked, he'd be knocking on my door again inside a month, and we'd have this conversation in exactly the same way except that he'd have lost four weeks of my business and gained nothing but the exercise. "Ninepence," he said. "Eightpence ha'penny, same as last month." "Nine." "Eightpence ha'penny. And I'll take an extra two dozen at the same price, which is more than I need this month, but I'm sure you can do the arithmetic." He did the arithmetic. He did not like it. He took the money and left, and the candles stayed on the table, and the door closed behind him with the particular sound of a man who has lost a negotiation and knows it and is already composing the version of events in which he did not. I took the delivery note from the top of the crate and wrote the adjusted price in the margin. Eightpence ha'penny. The ink was still drying when the thought arrived, as it did every time I won one of these small battles: that none of it would matter if I married. The ledger, the candles, the nine miles of road between me and Fitch's tallow -- all of it would pass out of my hands and into the hands of a husband who might or might not know the difference between Jessop's prices in June and Jessop's prices in August. The law did not care whether he knew. The law cared only that he was a man, and I was not. I put the delivery note on the shelf and carried the candles to the storeroom. He came around the back, as the signs asked. I saw him through the kitchen window before he knocked: young, thin, standing on the step with the particular stillness of a man deciding whether he was welcome enough to announce himself. His coat was too large for him and had been too large for whoever owned it before him. His boots were coming apart at the welt, the left one worse than the right. He could not have been older than twenty. He was not threatening. He was not sure. He knocked. I opened the door. "Have you eaten?" I said. He looked at me with the careful eyes of someone who has learned to assess the terms of an offer before accepting it. "Not recent," he said. "Kitchen, not the taproom. Sit there." He sat. I put bread in front of him and a bowl of the broth that had been on the range since morning. He ate the way hungry men eat when they are trying not to look hungry, which is to say steadily and without pausing and with his arm curved around the bowl as though someone might take it. I did not watch him. I wiped down the table at the far end of the kitchen and let him finish. When the bowl was empty he stood. "I'm grateful," he said. "Where are you headed?" "North," he said. He did not name a town. He named a direction, and I understood that the direction was all he had. I opened the door for him. He went. I cleared the bowl and the bread plate and set them in the basin, and my hands were already reaching for the cloth to wipe down the table where he'd sat before the back door had fully closed. The afternoon brought the usual small disorders. Two men in the taproom -- commercial travellers, both of them, sitting too long over their second pints and discovering that they disagreed about something to do with the price of wool. The disagreement was finding its volume when I came through from the kitchen. "Gentlemen," I said. "You'll settle it outside or you'll settle your bills. I don't mind which." They settled it by going quiet, which was a third option I had not offered but was willing to accept. Nan watched from behind the bar with the cloth in her hands and the expression of a woman who has calculated the distance between herself and the nearest heavy object and found it satisfactory. The afternoon opened into the long gap between the morning coach and the evening trade, and I filled it the way I always filled it: with work. Rooms checked, linen counted, the kitchen provisioned for supper. Nan restocked the bar. Jem was somewhere in the stable yard doing whatever Jem did in the hours when no one required him, which was the same as what Jem did in the hours when everyone required him, only quieter. The inn breathed its mid-afternoon breath, the fires ticking, the wood settling, the particular creak of the second stair that I kept meaning to fix and kept not fixing because there were always twelve things ahead of it. I worked. The light moved across the taproom floor in its familiar arc, catching the dust I had not yet swept and the gouge in the floorboard near the bar where a guest had dropped an andiron three years ago. I noted it. I had noted it every day for three years. I moved on. Evening came in the way it does in late October, not gradually but with a kind of determined finality, the light pulling itself out of the rooms like a guest who has overstayed and knows it. I lit the candles -- Fitch's candles, eightpence ha'penny the dozen -- and served the evening's custom, which was thin, and cleared the evening's plates, which were few, and tallied the evening's accounts, which were sufficient and no more. The last guest went upstairs at half past nine. Nan had already gone. Jem was in the stable yard, and I could hear him through the back wall, the low murmur of his voice as he settled the horses for the night. He talked to horses the way some men talk to God -- quietly, without expecting an answer, but with the conviction that the talking itself was worth something. I closed the inn down the way I always closed it down. Taproom fire: I banked it to coals and set the guard. The cellar hatch: locked, the bolt thrown and checked with my hand. The dining room: chairs in, shutters drawn, the cloth on the long table brushed clear. The kitchen: range damped down, the kettle pulled off the heat, the last of the broth covered for morning. My hands at the end of it were reddened from the cold water and the cleaning, and there was a small cut on my index finger, a clean line that stung when I flexed the knuckle. The edge of Fitch's delivery note. I had not noticed it at the time. It is always the invoices that cut you. I went out the back door for the final circuit. The stable yard was quiet. Jem had gone. The air was sharp and smelled of frost and horse and the faint sweetness of hay from the loft. I looked up. The second floor: a lit shape at Bridget's window, the outline of her sitting where she always sat, watching the yard below. I did not look long. She was there. She was watching. I did not need to see more than that. I came back inside and locked the back door. The bolt was stiff and I leaned my weight into it until it caught, and the sound of it sliding home was the sound of the day ending, the inn sealed, everything accounted for. The letter was on the kitchen table. It had not been there at supper. Nan must have taken it from the afternoon post and set it down while I was serving, and I had not seen it, or I had seen it and not registered it, which amounted to the same thing. Stainforth's seal on the envelope, dark red and pressed with the signet I had seen on every quarterly communication for two years. His handwriting on the direction, the letters small and precise and slanting slightly to the right, as though even his penmanship leaned forward with the certainty of a man who has never doubted that the world will arrange itself to meet him. I picked it up. I held it. I turned it over once. The wax was smooth under my thumb, and the paper was good, heavier than anything I bought for the inn's correspondence. I could feel the fold of the sheet inside, a single page, which meant it was not an account and not a formal notice but something in between -- a letter that required only enough room to say what it intended to say. I did not open it. I set it down on the table beside the closed ledger, where the two documents sat next to each other like the terms of an argument I had been expecting and had no answer for. I already knew what it was about. I had known since the last quarter's rent, since the chandler's prices started climbing, since the conversation I'd had with the brewer in September when he'd mentioned, casually, that someone had been asking about his accounts with the local inns. I had been moving faster ever since -- tallying earlier, negotiating harder, counting the weeks with the precision of a woman who can see the edge of the cliff and is measuring her steps. Six weeks and two days. I stood alone in the kitchen with my reddened hands and the sealed letter and the banked fire ticking in the range. The inn was quiet around me in the way a building is quiet when it has done its work and is waiting to be told whether it will be allowed to do it again tomorrow. "Right, then," I said. No one heard it. I turned down the lamp and went upstairs.

I counted the ledger twice before I believed it. The numbers had not changed since last night, but I had half-hoped that sleep might improve them, the way sleep sometimes improved a sore back or a foul temper. Six weeks to the quarter. Forty-three pounds in the strongbox, twelve shillings and eightpence in loose coin behind the bar, and the Cooper account still unpaid because Cooper's wife had been ill and I was not yet the kind of woman who dunned a man whose wife was coughing blood. I would become that woman soon enough if the ledger kept speaking to me in this tone. I closed the book and set it on the shelf above the flour bin where it lived, spine to the wall. The kitchen was cold. The fire had been banked overnight and I'd built it up twenty minutes ago, but the heat had not yet reached the far wall where the damp came in every autumn and would not leave until April. I could feel it in the flagstones through my boots. I pulled my sleeves down over my wrists and went to check the flue. The taproom fire I laid from scratch. The grate had been cleaned last night--Nan's work, thorough as always--and I set the kindling in the pattern my mother had used, which was my grandmother's pattern before that: paper twist at the centre, sticks in a square around it, two splits of oak on top. It caught on the first match. I did not take pride in this. It was a fire. But if it had not caught I would have been annoyed with myself, and the annoyance would have cost me a full minute, and the morning did not have minutes to spare. Bar stock next. I counted the bottles on the shelf behind the taps--gin, brandy, the last of the decent port--and checked them against the tally sheet pinned to the beam. Two measures short on the brandy, which meant either I had miscounted last night or someone had helped themselves after I locked up. Neither explanation pleased me. I made a note in the margin of the tally sheet and moved on. The inn was quiet in the way that it was quiet only at this hour: not empty, but held. The building was resting. Twenty rooms above me, the dining room through the passage, the stable yard beyond the kitchen door, all of it still, all of it waiting for the day to require it. I knew every sound the White Hound made--the particular creak of the third stair, the rattle of the dining-room casement when the wind came from the north, the slow drip beneath the cellar hatch that worsened in rain--and this morning it made none of them. Just the fire. Just my boots on the flagstones. Just the ordinary silence of a building older than anyone alive in it. I went out the back. Jem was in the stable yard already. He had a saddle over the partition rail and was working the buckle of the girth strap with his one hand and the leather loop he'd rigged on the post for exactly this purpose. He did not look up. Behind him the horses shifted in their stalls, and the grey mare blew hard through her nose, which meant she'd not been fed yet, which meant Jem had come straight to the tack. He had his own order of operations and I had learned not to interfere with it. The yard was cold. Frost along the lip of the water trough and a thin rime on the cobbles where the shadow of the stable wall fell. I could see my breath. I noted the state of the north gate--the bottom hinge had shifted again--and went back inside. Nan came through the kitchen door a few minutes later, pins not yet set in her hair, apron already tied. She went straight behind the bar and began to sort the clean glasses from the shelf where they'd been drying overnight. She did not greet me. I did not greet her. The morning had its own language and pleasantries were not part of it. I heard the coach before I saw it. The particular grind of iron wheels on the road surface, the jangle of harness, the driver's voice carrying ahead of the horses. I stepped out to the front of the inn and looked north. The London coach was late by forty minutes, which was not unusual for this stretch of the road in late October, when the surface turned treacherous after rain and the light came slow. What was unusual was the coach's lightness--it was riding high on the springs, which meant fewer passengers than the four I'd expected. Two climbed down. The first was a woman of perhaps fifty, dressed for travel and furious about it. She had a trunk, and the trunk had a split hinge, and she had opinions about the split hinge which she delivered to me as though I had personally taken a chisel to it. "The case was perfectly sound when I left Islington," she said. "The coachman loaded it as though it were a sack of turnips." "Jem," I said. He had appeared at the edge of the yard without being called. I nodded toward the trunk. He came forward, assessed the hinge, and lifted the whole thing onto his shoulder with his good arm, steadying it against the post with his stump. The woman stared. Jem did not notice, or did not care, which with Jem amounted to the same thing. He carried the trunk inside. The second passenger was a thin man in a brown coat who said nothing, wanted nothing, and went directly to the dining room. I let him go. The coachman came down from the box with the stiffness of a man who had been holding reins since before dawn. He was red-faced, thickset, and familiar--Cartwright, who drove this route three times a week and knew the road better than most people knew their own kitchens. "North side of Hatfield's gone again," he said. "Ruts deep enough to lose a wheel. We came through sideways for thirty yards." "They were supposed to have laid gravel last month." "They did not." "I'll write to the turnpike trust." He looked at me as though I had offered to write to the Almighty. "Much good that'll do." "It's done more good than not writing. Go round back. Nan will see to you." He went. The yard cleared. The woman's trunk was inside. The thin man was somewhere in the dining room. Jem had gone back to the stables without a word. I stood in the front of the inn for a moment and looked at the road, which was empty in both directions, and at the sky, which was the colour of old pewter, and then I went inside because standing still was not something I could afford to do for long. Nan had not moved from behind the bar, but she had been watching the door through the whole of it. I saw the angle of her attention as I came in--not curious, not alarmed, just aware, the way she was always aware of what came through that door and what it might require. I said nothing. She said nothing. She went back to the glasses. The chandler arrived at half past ten. His name was Burrell. He was a sandy-haired man with a cart and a talent for incremental theft. He brought the tallow candles in a crate, set the crate on the bar as he always did, and named his price. "Eightpence the dozen," he said. "It was sevenpence last month." "Tallow's gone up." "Tallow has not gone up. Tallow has been steady since August. I buy from the same market reports you do, Mr Burrell." He looked at me with the expression of a man who has been caught out and is deciding whether to bluff. "There's the carriage, Miss Dale. Cost of bringing it out this far." "Your carriage cost has not changed since you started this route, and you deliver to the Lamb and the George on the same run, so the White Hound costs you nothing extra. Sevenpence." "Seven and a half." "Sevenpence, and I'll take four dozen instead of three." He worked this through. Four dozen at sevenpence was more than three dozen at eightpence, and he knew that I knew it, and he knew that if he walked the candles out the door he would have to sell them to someone else at a price no better than what I was offering and with the trouble of finding the buyer. "Sevenpence," he said. "Thank you, Mr Burrell." He left. The door shut behind him with a sound that was just a door shutting and nothing more. I stood at the bar and wrote the adjusted price in the margin of the delivery note: 4 doz tallow, 7d/doz, 2s 4d. Then I put the pen down. If I married, none of this would be mine to negotiate. The ledger, the lease, the daily margin between survival and failure--all of it would pass into the hands of a husband, because the law did not recognise a wife's right to hold property or conduct business in her own name. I had made this calculation before. It came and went like weather. I picked up the pen and wrote the date at the top of the next page in the delivery book. The afternoon was the afternoon. Coach gone, guests settled or departed, Nan behind the bar with a cloth and a novel she thought I didn't know about. Jem outside doing whatever Jem did in the hours between coaches, which involved the horses and the yard and a degree of private industry I respected enough not to inspect. I reset the rooms that needed resetting, changed linen, checked the kitchen stores against the evening menu, and tallied the afternoon's modest takings against the ledger's unyielding arithmetic. The work was not interesting. It did not need to be interesting. It needed to be done. I saw him through the kitchen window before he knocked. He had come around the back, as the sign on the gate asked. Young--twenty at most, probably less. Thin in the way that meant weeks, not constitution. His coat had been decent once and was decent no longer. His boots were coming apart at the welt, the left one held together with what looked like twine. He stood outside the kitchen door and did not knock. He looked at the door, then at the ground, then at the door again. He was deciding whether he was welcome enough to try. I opened the door. He looked up. His face was weathered and careful and not yet old enough for the caution in it. "Have you eaten?" I said. "Not recently, ma'am." "Kitchen. Not the taproom." He understood. He came in and sat where I pointed him, at the end of the table nearest the fire. I set bread in front of him, and broth from the pot that had been on the range since morning. He ate steadily and without haste, which told me he had been hungry long enough to know that eating fast would make him sick. When he was finished he stood and put his cap back on. "Thank you, ma'am." "Where are you headed?" "North," he said. "There's work, I'm told." He went out the way he had come in. I cleared the bowl and the plate and set them in the basin. The bread that was left I wrapped in cloth and put back in the crock. The broth I left on the range. There would be others before the week was out. There always were. In the taproom, late in the afternoon, two men who had been sitting too long over too little ale discovered a disagreement about something I neither heard clearly nor cared to identify. One of them stood. The other leaned forward in his chair in a way that promised he would stand next. "Sit down," I said. "Both of you. Or take it outside, pay your accounts first, and don't come back." They sat. Nan, behind the bar, did not look up from the glass she was polishing. She had not needed to. The evening came in the way evenings came in late October: early, without announcement, the light going grey and then going. I lit the lamps in the taproom and the dining room. The last guests ate. The last drinks were poured. Nan tallied the bar and brought me the figures, which were neither good nor bad. I checked them, nodded, and she went upstairs. I closed the inn alone. It was not a choice. It was the order of things--Nan to bed, Jem to the room above the stable, and me through every room one more time because no one else would do it and it could not be left undone. Taproom fire: down to coals, the grate safe, the guard in place. I checked it with my hand near the stones, feeling for heat that shouldn't be there. Cellar hatch: locked, the bolt thrown, the padlock tested. Bar: the takings in the strongbox, the strongbox in the cupboard, the cupboard locked. Dining room: cleared, the shutters fastened, the draught from the north window stopped with the folded cloth I kept on the sill for the purpose. I went through the kitchen and out the back door to the yard. The stable was quiet. Jem would have settled the horses. The north gate was shut. The sky was black and starless, and the cold had a weight to it that pressed against the skin. On the second floor, a lit shape at Bridget's window. The faint glow of a candle or a lamp behind the glass, and the suggestion of a figure--sitting, watching, or simply awake because old women did not always sleep when the world expected them to. I did not look long. Back inside. Kitchen door bolted. I stood at the table and looked at my hands in the lamplight. They were reddened from the cold water and the cleaning, the knuckles rough, and there was a small cut on my right index finger from the edge of the chandler's delivery note--the paper had been cheaply made and the edge was sharp, and I had not noticed it at the time because there had been nothing to notice. It was an invoice. It had cut me. These things were not related except in the way that everything at the end of a day was related: the accumulated evidence of hours spent in use. The letter was on the table. I had not seen it arrive. It must have come with the afternoon post, left by the carrier on the hall stand, brought through to the kitchen by Nan or by habit or by the natural movement of mail toward the person it concerned. It was there, on the table, beside the bread crock and the delivery book. I picked it up. The seal was dark red, pressed with an impression I knew without needing to examine it closely. The handwriting on the direction was small and precise. Mr Phineas Stainforth's hand, or his clerk's--it did not matter which. I turned the envelope over once. The paper was heavy. Good paper. The kind of paper a man used when he wished the recipient to understand that he could afford good paper and they could not. I set it down. I did not open it. I had known it was coming. Since the last quarter, since the chandler's prices began their upward drift, since the road worsened and the coaches thinned and the ledger spoke to me each morning in a voice that was patient and unyielding and would not be improved by sleep. I had been moving faster ever since--faster through the mornings, sharper with the suppliers, more careful with the accounts--as though speed and competence could outrun whatever was in that envelope. I looked at the letter on the table. I looked at the kitchen, which was clean, and dark, and mine. "Well then," I said. No one heard it. I put out the lamp.

I checked the ledger twice because the first time the numbers were worse than I remembered, and the second time I wanted to be certain I had not made an error in my own favour. I had not. Six weeks to the quarter, and the margin between what the inn held and what the rent required was a figure I could cover only if nothing broke, no deliveries came short, and every coach between now and December carried its full complement of paying passengers. I closed the ledger and set it on the shelf above the kitchen table, spine out, beside the flour crock. The kitchen fire had burned low overnight. I raked the ash forward, laid kindling from the box Jem kept stocked without being asked, and built the fire up until it caught properly. The flue drew well this morning. Some mornings it did not, and I would spend twenty minutes adjusting the damper and swearing into the smoke, but today the draught pulled clean and the heat began to spread across the hearthstones. I filled the kettle and hung it on the crane. The taproom fire next. I carried the coal scuttle through and knelt on the cold flagstones. The windows on the east side of the taproom were rimed at their edges, a thin crust of frost that caught the first grey light and held it. The room smelled of last night's ale and the tallow from the candles I had not yet replaced. I built the fire, checked the draught, and crossed to the bar. Stock: three casks of ordinary ale, one of the better brown, the gin bottle down to the last third. The wine I kept for guests who asked was adequate, two bottles remaining of the claret and one of a Madeira that had been sitting so long it might have improved or might have turned. I would open it and check before the evening. The rum was low. I made a note in my head and moved on. Last night's accounts were still on the bar where I had left them, the column of figures in my own hand, the total circled. I had been too tired to carry them through to the ledger. I tallied them now standing up, running my finger down the entries, subtracting the ale and the candle-ends and Nan's wages and the delivery of firewood that had come yesterday afternoon. The total was not enough and not disastrous. It was the kind of number that kept you exactly where you were. I pulled my sleeves down over my wrists. The cold had got into the stone of the building and would not come out until the fires had been burning for an hour at least. Outside the kitchen window the stable yard was still in half-dark. Jem was already there. I could see him by the near wall, straightening the tack that hung from its pegs beside the stable door. He worked one-handed with his usual economy, lifting each bridle off its hook, checking the leather for cracks, hanging it back square. The stump of his left arm braced against the post when he needed counter-pressure. He did not look toward the kitchen. He did not need to be told what the morning required. I went back to the kitchen and put bread on the table, cut the end off yesterday's loaf and set it aside for the broth pot. Nan came through the back door a few minutes later. Pins not yet set in her hair, apron already tied. She hung her shawl on the hook by the door and went through to the bar without a word. I heard her cloth on the wood, the steady back-and-forth of wiping down the surface. We had done this many hundreds of times. The sound was as much a part of the morning as the kettle. The London coach was late. I heard it before I saw it, the particular heavy rattle of a full team at pace on a bad road, but the rhythm was off. Too few hooves for a full complement, and the driver was pushing. When it came around the bend past the signpost I counted: four horses, not six. Two passengers climbing down instead of the four the schedule promised. The first was a woman of middle years with a trunk that had split its hinge. She announced this fact to the yard at volume before her second foot touched the ground. "The latch has been wrenched entirely off. I shall require someone to attend to it immediately." Jem appeared at the edge of the yard. I did not call him. He took the trunk from the boot without comment and carried it toward the inn, the broken hinge flapping against his forearm. The woman watched him, saw the pinned sleeve, and for a moment could not decide whether to be impressed or appalled. She settled for following him inside at speed. The second passenger, an older man in a brown coat, went straight through the front door without looking at anyone. I let him go. Some guests wanted nothing but a fire and a chair, and the best thing you could do for them was provide both and keep out of their way. The coachman climbed down last. He was a solid man with a wind-scoured face and the posture of someone who had been holding the reins since before dawn. He walked toward me rolling his shoulders. "Road's breaking up north of Hatfield," he said. "The stretch past the turnpike. Ruts deep enough to lose a wheel." "The parish surveyor was out last week. They've gravel ordered." "They'll need more than gravel. There's a hole the size of a horse trough where the drainage culvert's collapsed." "I'll send word to the turnpike trust this afternoon." He looked at me as if assessing whether this was a promise or a courtesy. I looked back at him. "Right," he said. He touched his hat and went to see about his team. The yard cleared. The passengers were inside, Jem had taken the trunk, and the coachman was in the stable conferring with Jem about a near-side leader that was favouring its off foreleg. I stood in the yard for a moment in the sudden quiet. Through the front window I could see Nan behind the bar, cloth still in her hand, her gaze on the door to the dining room where the woman with the trunk had gone. She had been watching the door through all of it. I noted this. Said nothing. The chandler arrived at half past ten, as he always did on the last Monday of the month. He came through the yard with his handcart and the particular unhurried manner of a man who believes he has something you need more than he needs your custom. His name was Garvey. He sold tallow candles to every inn and alehouse between Hatfield and Baldock, and he had been raising his price by a halfpenny per dozen every seven or eight weeks since the spring. This was the fourth increase. I knew because I had written each one in the margin of its delivery note. I met him in the kitchen doorway. He set the first box of candles on the step and named his price. "Tenpence ha'penny the dozen," he said. "It was tenpence three weeks ago." "Tallow's gone up." "Tallow's gone up a farthing the pound since August. You're charging a halfpenny more per dozen. That's not the same margin, Mr Garvey." He shifted his weight. "I've costs of my own, Miss Dale." "You have. And you've also got the Crown at Welwyn paying you ninepence the dozen on a standing order of eight gross a quarter, which I happen to know because Mrs Parrish told me so last week when I complimented her candles." Garvey's mouth opened and closed. "Tenpence," I said. "Same as last time. And I'll take four dozen now and four dozen on the fifteenth." "I can't do tenpence for split deliveries." "You can. Because the alternative is I buy from Jessop in Stevenage, and you lose this stop entirely. Jessop's candles are worse than yours, and we both know it, but they burn and that's all I require." He held his ground for perhaps three seconds. Then he unloaded the four dozen from his cart and set them inside the kitchen door. I counted them, signed the delivery note, and handed it back. "Fifteenth," I said. "Fifteenth." He took the note, turned his cart, and left through the yard gate. The gate banged shut behind him. I stood in the kitchen with the delivery note's carbon still in my hand. Tenpence the dozen. I wrote the adjusted figure in the margin beside the last entry and set the note with the others in the box on the shelf. The ledger, the rent, the candles. Every figure pressed against the same calculation, the one I had made so many times it hardly registered as thought anymore: that if I married, none of it would be mine to negotiate for. I wiped my hands on my apron and went through to check on the dining room. The midday trade was thin. Two local men took their ale in the taproom and ate the ordinary. Nan served. I stayed in the kitchen preparing the stock for the evening's broth and listening for trouble through the open door between kitchen and taproom. There was none. The afternoon was the kind that accumulated without event, one task following the next with the steady weight of hours that were neither good nor bad but simply used. I saw the soldier through the kitchen window before he knocked. He came around the back, as the board on the gate directed, and stood outside the kitchen door. Young -- twenty, perhaps, or a year either side -- thin in the particular way of someone who had been thin for some time, not just today. His coat had been army issue once, though the regiment markings were gone. His boots were coming apart at the welt, the sole of the left one separating from the upper in a line I could see from six feet away through glass. He stood there looking at the door. Not knocking. Assessing whether the kind of place this was would let him knock. I opened the door. He straightened. "Ma'am. I don't mean to trouble you." "Have you eaten today?" A pause. He was deciding whether to lie. "Not since yesterday." "Kitchen," I said. "Not the taproom. Sit there." I pointed to the bench against the wall. He came in and sat where I pointed, his hands on his knees. I cut bread from the loaf and ladled broth from the pot that had been simmering since morning. I set the bowl and bread in front of him and put a cup of water beside it. He ate the way hungry men eat, steadily and without pausing, but he did not snatch at it. Someone had taught him his manners, or the army had. When the bowl was empty he set his spoon down and looked at me. "Thank you, ma'am. That was kind." "Where are you headed?" "North," he said. "There's work at the brickyards past Biggleswade, I was told." I took the bowl from the table and set it in the washing-up basin. He stood, ducked his head, and went out through the kitchen door. I watched him cross the yard and pass through the gate. The broken sole of his left boot caught on the cobbles as he went. I turned the tap and rinsed the bowl. Then I dried my hands and went through to the taproom. The two local men had been joined by a third, a carter I knew by sight, and the conversation at the near table had turned loud. Not violent -- not yet -- but the kind of loud that precedes loud, the pitch rising as two men discovered they disagreed about something too small to matter and too large to abandon. I crossed the room and stood at the end of their table. "Gentlemen. If you want to continue this discussion, you're welcome to do it in the yard. If you want to continue drinking, you'll do it quietly." The carter looked at me. The other man looked at the carter. Nan, behind the bar, had her hand flat on the counter and her eyes on the table. She did not need to move. The noise settled. I went back to the kitchen. The afternoon wore on in the way of coaching inn afternoons when the road was quiet: stock to be checked again, rooms to be turned, the kitchen prepared for whatever evening trade came. Nan cleared the taproom. Jem was in the yard seeing to the horses. I worked the accounts at the kitchen table, corrected an error in the week's column, moved through to check the upstairs rooms, came back down and counted the remaining candles against what Garvey had delivered. The numbers held. They always held, just barely, for now. The evening came early, as October evenings did, the light going grey by five and dark by half past. The last guests settled -- the woman with the trunk had taken supper in the dining room and gone upstairs; the man in the brown coat had eaten nothing and retired before seven. I let the fires burn down to their banking height and moved through the closing routine. Taproom first. The chairs pushed in, the tables wiped, the fire raked and screened. The floor wanted sweeping, but it could wait until morning. I locked the cellar hatch and checked the bolt. The taproom fire was down to a low orange crawl in the grate. Through to the front. Door bolted, windows latched. The cold was sharper now, pressing against the glass from outside, and the frost had started again on the lower panes. I crossed the yard. The stable was quiet -- Jem would have settled the horses an hour ago, and I could hear nothing from inside but the occasional shift of weight and the sound of an animal breathing. Above the stable, on the second floor of the wing that looked over the yard, there was a lit shape at Bridget's window. The lamp behind the curtain, the outline of her sitting in the chair where she always sat. I did not look long. I turned and went back in through the kitchen door. The kitchen was dark except for the low fire. I moved through the last of it: banking the fire, checking the range, locking the back door. My hands at the end of the day were reddened from the cold water and the scrubbing, and there was a small cut on my index finger that I had not noticed getting. I looked at it. The edge of Garvey's delivery note, I realized. The paper had been rough-cut. It was the invoice that had done it. The letter was on the kitchen table.
