It had not been there when I left the kitchen twenty minutes ago, which meant Nan had brought it in from wherever the afternoon post had left it and set it here before she went up. The seal was dark red, pressed deep. Stainforth's seal, his initials in the wax. His handwriting on the direction, the same careful slanting hand I had seen on every quarterly communication for the past two years. I picked it up. Turned it over once. The wax was cold and slightly ridged under my thumb. I set it down on the table beside the ledger and did not open it. I had known it was coming. I had known since the last quarter, since the conversation at the gate when he had mentioned his nephew's name for the first time and I had understood what was being assembled behind the reasonable language and the measured voice. I had been moving faster ever since. The ledger, the margins, the chandler, the coach passengers counted and recounted -- all of it pressing against the same approaching wall. The kitchen was quiet. The fire ticked as it settled. Outside, the yard was dark and still. "Right, then," I said. No one heard it. I turned down the lamp and went upstairs.

I checked the ledger twice because the first time I did not believe my own arithmetic. The kitchen was dark except for the single tallow candle I had carried down. The fire was not yet built up. The flagstones drew the cold through the soles of my boots and held it there, patient and absolute, the way cold is in a stone building before anyone has fought it back for the day. I turned the page and ran my finger down the column again. Tallow, flour, coal, the chandler's account, the brewer's account, Jem's wages, Nan's wages, the farrier for the off-side leader's shoe last Tuesday. The figures were not wrong. They were simply not enough. Six weeks to the quarter, and the margin between what the inn would earn and what the rent required was a gap I could see but not yet close. I shut the ledger. The fire first. I riddled the grate, cleared the ash into the pail, and laid the new fire the way my mother taught me: paper, then kindling split fine, then two pieces of coal set at an angle so the draught could get beneath them. I struck the flint three times before the spark caught. The paper flared and the kindling took, and I stayed crouched there a moment with my hands held out, waiting for the heat to reach me. It did not, not yet. The fire was too young. I stood and left it to its business. The taproom fire next. The same procedure, though the grate was larger and the ash heavier, and the room smelled of last night's ale and the faint sourness of a tallow stub that had guttered into its dish. I checked the bar stock by touch as much as sight: the ale cask near the tap had perhaps two days left in it, the gin was low, the brandy sufficient. I lined up the clean tankards Nan had set out the night before and found one she had missed -- a smear of dried foam along the rim. I wiped it with my apron and set it back. The accounts from last night were on the shelf behind the bar where I always left them: three sheets of paper in my own hand, the figures small and tight. I did not check them now. I had checked them before I went up to bed, and they were correct then, and nothing would have altered in the night except my willingness to look at them again. The windows in the taproom were rimed at the lower edges. I pulled my sleeves down over my wrists and crossed to the front door to draw the bolt. The door opened onto a morning that was grey and still. The road was empty. The inn sign -- the white hound, painted by a man in Stevenage eleven years ago and touched up twice since -- hung motionless. No wind, which meant no coach dust, which was a mercy for the front windows. I closed the door and went out through the kitchen to the yard. Jem was already in the stable, which I expected. He had the big grey's bridle off its hook and was checking the bit, turning the metal joint between his hand and the flat of the leather pad he wore strapped over his stump. He did not look up when I appeared in the doorway. The hay had been forked into the stalls. The water buckets were full. The saddle Nan's sleeve by the time she reached the bar and took up her cloth and began to wipe down the wood. I watched her work for perhaps five seconds -- no more -- and then went back through the kitchen to check the bread delivery, which had not come, and the milk, which had. The morning settled into its shape. The London coach was late. This was not unusual. It was late more often than not since the roads north of Hatfield had worsened through the autumn, the parish having declined to repair a stretch of rutted ground near Welwyn that every coachman on the north road had a view about. By the time I heard the horn and the rattle of wheels on the approach, I had the front door open and Jem was in the yard. Two passengers instead of four. Both women, both in poor temper. The first descended from the coach unaided and stood in the yard with her mouth set in a line that promised complaint. The second required the coachman's hand and had opinions about the trunk he swung down after her, which had a split hinge and gaped like a broken jaw. I directed Jem to the trunk with a look. He was already moving. He took it by the handle and the base, bracing it against his hip with the ease of long practice, and carried it toward the door. The woman with the trunk opened her mouth to instruct him further, but I stepped into her line of sight. "The hinge can be mended before your departure. There's a fire laid in the dining room and breakfast in half an hour. Through the door and to your left." She blinked. She went. The second woman followed without prompting, which was a relief. The coachman -- a heavy man named Barlow whom I knew from his Wednesday route -- climbed down from the box and stretched his back with both hands pressed to his kidneys. "That stretch above Hatfield is getting worse," he said. "I know it." "Near broke the axle on a rut the size of a drainage ditch. Someone's going to lose a wheel before Christmas." "I'll mention it to the parish again. It won't do any good, but I'll mention it." He grunted. "You'll want to know the afternoon coach may run late as well. They're saying there's a blockage on the road south of Stevenage -- a carter overturned." "Thank you, Mr Barlow." He touched his hat and went to see about the horses, and I went inside. Two passengers instead of four meant half the coaching income for the morning stop. I did not calculate the figure. I did not need to. I already knew it. The passengers went upstairs. Jem took the trunk. The yard cleared. Through the doorway to the taproom I could see Nan at the bar, her cloth in one hand, her eyes on the front door. She had watched the whole arrival without moving from her position. I noted it. Said nothing. The inn has its rhythms, and Nan knows hers. The chandler arrived at half past ten. He was a neat, compact man named Slade who carried his delivery box on one shoulder and his revised prices in his head, and who had been nudging both upward since March. He set the box on the kitchen table and named his figure. "Fourpence ha'penny the dozen." I did not answer immediately. I took the lid off the box and counted the candles. Three dozen, as agreed. Tallow, not beeswax. The quality was adequate -- no better, no worse than the last delivery. "It was fourpence last month," I said. "Tallow's up. The renderer in Hatfield has raised his price." "The renderer in Hatfield charges you tuppence a pound for raw tallow, which is what he charged you in June. I buy soap from the same man. He talks." Slade's expression did not change, but he shifted the empty lid from one hand to the other. "There's the cost of carriage." "You carry your own deliveries. You are carrying them now. Fourpence the dozen, Mr Slade, which is what I paid last month, and the month before that, and which is more than the Red Lion pays you, because Mrs Calder told me so last Thursday when she thought she was complaining about your service." A pause. He looked at the candles in their box, and then at me, and then at the kitchen door through which he had come and through which he could leave, taking his candles with him. He would not. I knew he would not. I was his most reliable account on this stretch of road, and punctual with payment, and he knew that I knew he knew. "Fourpence," he said. "Fourpence." He left the candles and took the empty box. I heard the kitchen door close behind him. I picked up the delivery note he had left on the table, found the figure he had written, struck through it, and wrote the corrected price in the margin. The edge of the paper caught the side of my index finger as I set it down. A thin line of blood rose but did not drop. I entered the adjusted figure in the ledger and closed it. The candles needed putting away. The midday preparations needed starting. Between one task and the next, in the pause where my hands were still and my mind was not, the thought came as it always did: a plain calculation, flat and familiar. If I married, the lease would pass to my husband. The income. The negotiation. The right to say fourpence and have it be fourpence. All of it transferred, by law, to a man -- any man -- the moment I stood in a church and said the words. I had done the sum before. The answer had not changed. I picked up the candles and carried them to the storeroom. He came around the back, as the sign on the gate asked. I saw him through the kitchen window before he knocked -- standing outside the door with his arms at his sides, looking at the handle as though measuring whether he had earned the right to touch it. Young. My age or younger -- it was difficult to say. Thin through the coat, which was army wool, much worn. His boots were coming apart at the welt, the left one separating from the sole so that I could see a line of grey stocking beneath. He stood a moment longer, and then he raised his hand and knocked once. I opened the door. He was taller than he had looked through the window. His face had the scrubbed, wind-raw look of a man who has been walking in open weather for some time. "Have you eaten?" I said. He met my eyes. It took him a moment. "Not since yesterday, ma'am." "Kitchen, not taproom. Sit there." I pointed to the bench by the range. He came in and sat where I told him. I cut bread from the loaf and ladled broth from the pot that had been warming since morning. He ate without haste, which told me more about his hunger than speed would have. A man who bolts his food has been hungry for a day. A man who eats steadily, not looking up, not speaking, wrapping his hands around the bowl as though it might be taken -- that man has been hungry longer. When he had finished, he set the bowl down and looked at me. "Thank you, ma'am." "Where are you headed?" "North," he said. He did not elaborate. I held the door open for him. He went through it, and I watched him cross the yard and pass through the gate without turning back. His left boot flapped with each step. I closed the door and cleared the bowl and the plate, washed them, and set them on the rack to dry. The afternoon was quiet, and then it was not. Two men in the taproom -- commercial travellers who had been sitting since noon -- found a disagreement between them about something I did not trouble to identify. One of them had risen from his chair. The other had raised his voice. I crossed the room. "Sit down, or finish your drinks and leave. Those are the choices." They sat down. Nan, behind the bar, had not put down her cloth. The hours between the midday trade and the evening settled over the inn like dust. Nan at the bar, polishing what did not need polishing because idleness discomforted her. Jem in the yard -- I could hear the slow scrape of his rake over the cobbles. I worked through the afternoon's accounts at the kitchen table, then went upstairs to check the rooms the morning passengers had used, stripped the linens, brought them down, started the evening preparations. Turnips to peel. The remains of a mutton joint to be stretched into something that would serve. Coal to be brought in for the taproom fire. I carried, I chopped, I counted. The work was the work, and it did not require my opinion of it. Evening came on early as it does in late October. The light outside the kitchen window turned from grey to a darker grey, and then to the colour of old pewter, and then it was gone. The last guests settled or departed. The last fire I checked was the taproom's -- I raked the coals flat and set the guard before them. The bar was tallied, the figures entered, the cashbox locked and put away. Nan had gone upstairs an hour earlier, her good night a nod and nothing more. The kitchen I closed down by feel as much as by sight: the range banked, the surfaces wiped, the morning's bread set covered on the shelf. I stood in the taproom doorway and listened. The building made its settling sounds -- a beam contracting, a window frame ticking in its housing. From the cellar, the slow drip of the tap I would need to re-seat in the morning. I checked the cellar hatch: locked. The front door: bolted. The yard door: bolted. I went out through the kitchen door and stood in the yard. The cold was immediate and total. Jem had settled the horses -- I could hear nothing from the stable except the occasional shift of weight and the sound of an animal breathing. I looked up. The second-floor window above the yard had a light behind it: the thin, steady glow of a single candle seen through old glass. Bridget's shape was there, or the suggestion of it -- a stillness behind the pane. I did not look long. I went back inside and locked the kitchen door behind me. My hands, when I turned the key, were red from the cold water and the cleaning, and the cut on my index finger from the edge of Slade's delivery note had opened again and left a thin rust-coloured line along the side of the key. I wiped it on my apron. The letter was on the kitchen table. It had not been there before -- or it had, and I had not seen it, though I did not believe that. Someone had put it inside the door while I was in the yard, or it had come with the afternoon delivery and Nan had left it and said nothing because the seal on the envelope was not the sort of thing Nan would remark on without being asked. It was there. That was what mattered. The envelope was heavy paper, cream-coloured, the direction written in a hand I knew. The seal was dark red wax, pressed with an initial I did not need to hold to the candle to read. Stainforth. I picked it up. I held it. I turned it over once and felt the weight of it, which was slight -- a single sheet, most likely, folded twice. The wax was smooth under my thumb. I set it down on the table beside the ledger and did not open it. I had known it was coming since the last rent quarter. I had known it the way I knew when the ale cask was nearly empty -- not because I had checked, but because I had been counting without counting, tracking the weight of what remained. The chandler's prices. The short coaches. The gap in the ledger I had checked twice this morning because I did not believe my own arithmetic. I had been moving faster ever since, and here was the reason, sealed and addressed and sitting on my kitchen table at the end of a day in which I had done everything right and it had not been enough. I looked at the envelope. The candle on the table guttered once and steadied. "Well then," I said.

I checked the ledger twice because the first time the numbers were the same as the night before, and I did not believe them. Six weeks. Forty-three days, if I counted from yesterday, which I had. Forty-three days until the quarter rent came due and the figures in the credit column would need to outpace the figures in the debit column by eleven pounds, eight shillings, and fourpence. I knew the sum the way I knew the creak of the third stair and the draft beneath the taproom door--it was simply part of the building. I closed the ledger and set it on the shelf above the flour bin where it lived between uses. The kitchen was cold. The fire I had built ten minutes ago was still working its way through the kindling, not yet hot enough to take the chill off the flagstones. I could see my breath if I breathed hard enough, so I did not breathe hard. I pulled my sleeves down over my wrists and went to the range to check the draw. The flue was clear. I had cleaned it on Sunday and it would hold for another week. I set the kettle on the hob and crossed through the passageway to the taproom. The fire there was yesterday's ash. I knelt, raked it out, and built a new one--paper, kindling, two splits of beech from the stack Jem kept by the back wall. The beech was drier than the elm and caught faster. I did not waste elm on the morning fire. The taproom smelled of last night's ale and tallow and the cold particular smell of a room that had been shut up with its own breath overnight. I opened the small window at the far end two inches, no more. The October air came in sharp and clean and I let it work while I moved behind the bar to count bottles. Three of the good brandy. Seven of the ordinary. Gin adequate. Ale: two barrels tapped, one reserve in the cellar. The reserve would want broaching by Saturday if the coaching traffic held. The accounts from last night were where I had left them, weighed down with the smooth stone I used as a paperweight--a river stone, grey, that my mother had kept on this bar for as long as I could remember. I ran the column. Four shillings ninepence from the taproom. Six shillings eightpence from two dinners and the rooms. One shilling threepence from stabling. Total receipts for the day: twelve shillings eightpence. Against that, the coal delivery, the kitchen stores, and Nan's wages for the week, which I owed her tomorrow. I set the accounts aside. The window had done its work. I shut it. The passage to the yard was dark, and I went through it by feel--hand on the left wall, three steps, then the latch of the back door. Outside, the morning was grey-white. Frost rimmed the horse trough and the edges of the cobbles. Jem was in the stable yard already, his back to me, straightening a halter on its hook with his one hand. He did not turn around. The tack was in order because Jem's tack was always in order, and the stable doors were latched, and the yard was swept. I looked, confirmed what I expected to confirm, and went back inside. Nan arrived through the kitchen door a few minutes later, pins in her hair not yet set, apron already tied. She passed me without a word and took her place at the bar. I heard her lift the stone, check the accounts sheet, set it down again. Then the sound of a cloth being wrung into the bucket. She was wiping the bar top. We had done this before. Many hundreds of times. The London coach was late. This was not unusual, but I had been listening for it since half seven and it was nearly nine when I heard the change in sound from the road--the particular heavy rhythm of a full team at a trot slowing for the turn. I went to the yard. It was running short. Two passengers where the way-bill said four. The first was a woman of perhaps forty-five, well-dressed, furious about something, holding a trunk by its remaining handle while the split hinge gaped open like a broken jaw. The second was a man in a dark coat who wanted nothing but to get inside and be left alone, and who managed this without my help. The coachman climbed down stiffly and stood stretching his back while Jem appeared beside the horses without having been called. I directed Jem to the trunk. He took it with a glance at the hinge that communicated his opinion of whoever had packed it. The woman watched her trunk depart and turned her displeasure on me. "That hinge was perfectly sound when I left London," she said. "Your roads have shaken it apart." "The room is at the top of the stairs, first on the left," I said. "There is hot water. I will have the hinge seen to within the hour." She opened her mouth, reconsidered, and went inside. The coachman had not moved. He was a man of about fifty, heavy through the shoulders, with the permanently weathered complexion of a person who has spent thirty years facing into the wind. "The road above Hatfield," he said. "North stretch, past the toll." "The ruts." "It's worse than ruts. There's a section where the surface has broken through entirely. Six yards, maybe eight. I nearly lost a wheel." "Was it marked?" "A branch across the road. That was all." "I will send word to the turnpike trust this afternoon." He nodded. It was not gratitude--it was the acknowledgment of a man who had discharged his duty and could move on. "Will you eat?" I asked. "No time. I'm forty minutes behind as it is." He climbed back up. Jem had already changed the lead pair. The coach pulled out of the yard and the sound of it diminished down the road until there was nothing left but the ordinary morning quiet of the inn: Jem's boots on the cobbles, the settling of the horses, the creak of the sign above the front door turning in a wind I could not feel at ground level. Nan had been wiping the same stretch of bar through all of this. I saw the cloth pause when the yard was clear, then resume. I noted it. Said nothing. The chandler came at eleven. He was a broad man named Hadwell with clean hands and a careful way of placing his delivery crate on the kitchen table that was meant to communicate the value of its contents. Twelve dozen tallow candles, wrapped in paper, smelling of rendered fat. "Fourpence ha'penny the dozen this month," he said. I set down the knife I had been using to trim the wick of the kitchen lamp. "It was fourpence last month." "Tallow's gone up." "Tallow has gone up a farthing the pound since March. You have raised your price a ha'penny the dozen four times since then. That is twopence the dozen above what the cost warrants." He shifted the crate an inch to the left on the table, as though it might leave with him. "I've other customers on this road who pay what I ask and don't require an accounting lesson." "You have six customers on this stretch between Hatfield and Baldock. Three of them buy fewer than four dozen a month. The Angel at Baldock buys in quantity from a supplier in Hitchin. That leaves me and the Feathers at Welwyn, and Mrs. Pryce at the Feathers told me last Tuesday she is considering making her own." Hadwell looked at the crate. He looked at me. "Fourpence farthing," I said. "That's barely above my cost." "It is a farthing above your cost, and you know it, and I will take twelve dozen a month through the winter, which is more than the Feathers takes in a quarter." He stood there. I waited. The fire popped behind me. Somewhere in the stable yard, a horse stamped. "Fourpence farthing," he said. He left. The candles stayed. I wrote the adjusted price in the margin of the delivery note and set it with the accounts. The ink was not yet dry when I moved on to the kitchen stores, tallying what was needed for the evening meal against what was in the larder. The coverture calculation came then, the way it always came--not as a thought I chose to have but as a fact that arrived alongside the other facts: the candle price, the rent, the diminishing margin. If I married, the inn passed to my husband. The lease, the income, the negotiation I had just conducted and the ones that would follow it--all of it would belong to a man who might be shrewd or might be a fool and over whom I would have no authority whatever. The law was not complicated. I had understood it since I was old enough to understand the ledger. I moved on. He came around the back, as the sign asked. Young--twenty, perhaps, or less. Thin in the way of a man who has been thin for a long time, not the way of a man who was born to it. His coat was adequate. His boots were not. The welt of the left boot had separated from the sole along the outside edge, and the right was heading the same way. He stood outside the kitchen door without knocking. I saw him through the window--the pause, the assessment, the particular stillness of a person calculating whether he would be welcome enough to knock. I opened the door. He straightened. He did not speak first, which told me something about him. "Have you eaten?" I said. "Not since yesterday, ma'am. Morning." "Kitchen, not the taproom. Sit there." He came in and sat where I pointed, at the end of the kitchen table farthest from the door to the passageway. I cut bread and ladled broth from the pot on the range. It was yesterday's broth--mutton bone, turnip, onion--reheated, perfectly sound. I set it in front of him with a spoon. He ate. Not desperately, but steadily, the way a man eats who has learned to eat when food is available and not to waste the opportunity. He finished the bread. He finished the broth. He set the spoon down parallel to the edge of the bowl. "Thank you, ma'am." "Where are you headed?" "North," he said. "My mother's people are in Lincolnshire." He stood, and I saw his hand go briefly to the table edge to steady himself, and then he was upright and the moment passed. He went out through the kitchen door, and I watched him cross the yard to the road, and then I closed the door and took the bowl to the basin and washed it and set it with the others. The afternoon passed in the ordinary way of afternoons at the White Hound when no coach was due and the guests had settled or gone. Two men in the taproom had been sitting too long over their second pints, and something about the prior night's boxing match at Barnet had tipped from disagreement into the kind of noise that precedes breakage. I went in. "Gentlemen. You will lower your voices or you will settle your account and leave. Those are the options." They lowered their voices. Nan, behind the bar, set down the glass she had been holding and said nothing. The gap between the afternoon coach and the evening trade was the inn's dead time, and I used it as I always used it: accounts, provisions, the room on the second floor that wanted fresh linen, the kitchen prepared for supper. Nan at the bar. Jem outside. The work. I did not stop, because the work did not stop, and because the alternative to working was thinking, and thinking was a luxury that cost more than I could afford. I locked up alone, as I always did. The last guest settled by half nine. The taproom fire banked down to coals. I stood for a moment with my hand on the mantelpiece, watching the last colour leave the embers. Then the bar--cloth over the taps, glasses accounted for, the stone on the accounts. The kitchen: range damped, surfaces wiped, the morning's bread dough set to prove overnight under a cloth. The cellar hatch: locked, key on the hook by the passage door. I went out to the yard for the final circuit. The stable doors were latched. Jem had settled the horses--I could hear their breathing through the walls, the slow shuffle of hooves on straw. The night was cold and very clear. Above the roofline, more stars than I had attention for. On the second floor of the inn, a lit shape at Bridget's window. I did not look long. My hands at the end of the day: reddened from cold water and the cleaning, the knuckles rough, a small cut on the index finger where the edge of Hadwell's delivery note had caught the skin. It was the invoice that had cut me. I noticed this without stopping to account for it. I came back to the kitchen. The fire was down to almost nothing, and the room held that particular silence of a space that has been used hard and is now finished with its day. Everything in order. Everything accounted for. The ledger on its shelf. The accounts under the stone. The morning's tasks already assembling themselves in my mind--Jem would need to look at the loose board on the mounting block, and the flour was running lower than I liked, and the woman with the broken trunk would want breakfast early if she was catching the southbound coach. The letter was on the table. It had not been there at supper. Someone had brought it--Nan, most likely, who collected the post from the village--and set it down without remark. Stainforth's seal on the envelope. His handwriting on the direction, small and very even. Miss D. Dale, The White Hound, Great North Road. I picked it up. I held it. I turned it over once. I did not open it. I had known it was coming since the last quarter. I had known it the way I knew the draught beneath the taproom door and the creak of the third stair--it was part of the building, part of what the building cost, and I had been moving faster ever since, as though speed alone could outrun the arithmetic. I set the letter down on the table, seal up, and looked at it. "Well," I said to the room. "There it is." No one heard me. The fire ticked as it cooled. Outside, the sign creaked once in the wind, and then the inn was quiet.

I checked the ledger twice because the first time the numbers were worse than I remembered, and the second time they were exactly the same. Six weeks. Perhaps seven, if the weather held and the coaches kept running full. The column on the right-hand side of the page -- what came in -- and the column on the left -- what went out -- had been drawing toward each other all autumn like two dogs circling in a yard, and I did not care for what would happen when they met. I closed the book and set my hand flat on its cover, as if the figures might try to crawl out while my back was turned. The kitchen fire had burned down to a dull orange crust in the night. I knelt and raked the ashes forward, laid kindling crosswise the way my mother had taught me, and blew until the flame caught. The floorboards were cold through my stockings. I had come downstairs without shoes -- a habit I ought to break, and would not. The kitchen was mine at this hour in a way it was not once the day began, and I did not wish to announce my arrival to it. I filled the kettle and hung it on the crane. Checked the bread bin: two loaves from yesterday, still good enough for toast, not good enough for the table. The butter crock was down by a third. I had noted this already in the ledger but I noted it again now, with my hands, pressing the cloth cover down and feeling the level beneath. My fingers were stiff. The windows were rimed at their edges, a thin crust of frost that caught no light because there was no light yet to catch. The taproom fire took longer. The grate had not been properly cleared the night before -- my own fault -- and I spent several minutes on my knees with the ash bucket, scraping out the cold mass of yesterday's fuel before I could lay a new one. The air in the taproom was thick and stale, carrying the ghost of pipe smoke and spilled ale. I opened the small window behind the bar and let the cold pour in. Better cold than foul. Bar stock: I counted bottles by feel in the dimness. The gin was lower than it ought to be, which meant I had poured more generously than I intended last night or someone had helped himself after hours. I set the bottle at the front of the shelf where I would see it clearly later and moved on. The cask of ordinary ale was two-thirds full. The strong ale, barely tapped. No one was drinking the strong ale this month. The price was not dear but people's pockets were, and they chose accordingly. I tallied the previous night's take against the chalkboard behind the bar. The figures matched. One evening's income would not have impressed anyone, but it was correct, and being correct was the only victory available at six o'clock in the morning. The stable yard was grey when I looked out the back door. Jem was there already, straightening a length of tack on the hook beside the mounting block. He did not look up. His one hand moved with the deliberate precision I had grown accustomed to, the stump of his left arm bracing the leather against the post while his right hand worked the buckle. I watched him for a moment -- not because he needed watching, but because the sight of a person doing useful work before being asked to do it was a comfort I had not learned to take for granted. I pulled the door shut and went back inside. Nan came through the kitchen a few minutes later, pins in her hair not yet properly set, her apron already tied. She took her place behind the bar without a word and began wiping down the counter with a rag she must have picked up from the shelf on her way through, because I had not put one out. We had done this before. Many hundreds of times. The London coach was late. This was not unusual. What was unusual was that it arrived carrying two passengers instead of the expected four, and both of them came through the front door with expressions that suggested the Great North Road had personally offended them. The first was a stout woman in a travelling cloak who carried a trunk with a split hinge. She set this down in the hallway with the air of a person presenting evidence at a trial. The second was a thin man in a brown coat who said nothing and went directly upstairs. "This has been like this since Barnet," the woman said, pointing at the trunk. "I cannot be expected to travel the length of England with my belongings falling into the road." "Jem," I said, and he was there -- I do not know how he heard from the yard, but he always did -- and took the trunk by its leather handles, tilting it so the broken hinge held shut under its own weight. He carried it toward the stairs without comment. The woman opened her mouth to say something further, reconsidered, and followed him. The coachman had climbed down and was standing in the yard, stamping his feet against the cold. He was a man I had seen many times -- Gibbs, or Gibbon -- the regular driver on the Tuesday route. "Road's breaking up past Hatfield," he said when I came out. "Ruts deep enough to lose a wheel. You'll want to warn anyone heading north this afternoon." "I will. How far past Hatfield?" "Mile and a half, perhaps two. Where the road crosses the stream bed. The rain's done for it." He blew into his hands. "If the trust doesn't send someone to relay gravel before the frost sets in hard, you'll be pulling coaches out of ditches all winter." "I'll send word to the surveyor. You've twenty minutes if you want something hot." He nodded and went inside. I could have given him more than twenty minutes. I could have given him an hour. But the coach had a schedule, and the schedule was not mine to adjust, and neither of us had time to pretend otherwise. The coachman went. The passengers had disappeared into their rooms or the dining room. Jem took the trunk upstairs and returned to the yard. The small bustle of arrival passed through the inn and out again like a breath, and the building settled. Nan was behind the bar, wiping the same surface she had already wiped, her eyes on the doorway through which the last of the morning's activity had gone. I noticed it. Said nothing. The chandler arrived at half past ten, which was his custom on Tuesdays. He was a man called Fitchett, narrow-shouldered, with a careful way of setting down his crate of candles that suggested he wanted full credit for the effort of carrying them. He had a ledger of his own, smaller than mine, which he consulted as he stood in the kitchen doorway, though I suspected he had memorized the figure before he left his shop. "Tallow per dozen, one and tenpence," he said. "It was one and seven last month." "Cost of tallow's gone up. Everyone's paying more." "Everyone on this stretch of road is paying one and seven, Mr Fitchett. I spoke to Mrs Beale at the Red Hart on Saturday and she confirmed it." A pause. He had not expected me to have spoken to Mrs Beale. He adjusted the crate on his hip. "One and nine." "One and seven." "Miss Dale, I cannot sell below my cost." "Your cost for rendered tallow is ninepence per dozen, and has been since August. I know because your carter told Jem when he was drunk, and Jem does not forget figures. One and seven gives you tenpence clear, which is exactly what you were making in June when you were cheerful enough about the arrangement." Fitchett stared at me. I stared back. The kitchen was very quiet. Somewhere behind me the kettle shifted on its crane with a small metallic sound. "One and eight," he said. "One and seven. And I'll take an extra two dozen this month, which you may tell anyone who asks." He set the crate down on the kitchen table. He made a note in his ledger. He did not look at me as he did it. "Pleasure doing business, Miss Dale." I did not reply. He let himself out through the kitchen door, and I listened to his boots on the yard stones until the sound of them was gone. I wrote the adjusted price in the margin of the delivery note. One and seven. The ink blurred slightly where my pen pressed too hard. I set the note aside and began counting candles into the storage box, and somewhere between the eighth and the ninth my mind did what it did when the numbers pressed in -- it ran the calculation I had made before, the one that sat beneath all the other calculations like the foundation beneath a floor. The lease was mine so long as I held it alone. If I married, everything transferred. The inn, the income, the forty years my family had poured into this place. One signature in a church register and it became some man's property, to run or ruin or sell as he chose. This was not a grievance. It was arithmetic. I set the ninth candle into the box and reached for the tenth. I saw him through the window before he knocked. Young -- younger than Jem, younger than the coachman -- with a thin face and a coat that had once been military issue, though someone had torn the insignia from the sleeve. His boots were coming apart where the upper met the sole, the welt splitting along both sides so that his stockings showed through. He stood outside the kitchen door with his hand raised, not yet touching the wood. He was looking at the door the way a man looks at a thing he is not certain he is permitted to approach. He knocked. I opened it. He had a narrow, weather-darkened face and hair that had been cut badly and not recently. His eyes moved past me to the kitchen -- the fire, the kettle, the table with its candles and ledger -- and came back to my face. "Have you eaten?" I said. "Not today." He paused, as if deciding whether more honesty would help or hurt. "Not yesterday, properly." "Kitchen, not taproom. Sit there." I pointed to the bench by the hearth. He came in and sat where I told him. He did not remove his coat. I cut bread from yesterday's loaf and ladled broth from the pot that had been on the fire since morning. I set both in front of him with a spoon and nothing else, because ceremony was not what he needed and I did not have it to spare. He ate. Not fast, which surprised me. Men who had not eaten in two days usually ate as though the food might be taken away. This one ate steadily, deliberately, like a man who was rationing his own hunger. When he finished he set the spoon across the bowl and sat with his hands on his knees. "Thank you," he said. His voice was quiet and had the particular flatness of someone who has recently stopped expecting things. "Where are you headed?" "North." He hesitated. "Huntingdon, if I can make it." I nodded. He stood, and I saw him glance at the bread on the table, and then stop himself from glancing at it. I cut a second piece and held it out. He took it. "The road's bad past Hatfield," I said. "Keep to the verge where you can." He went out through the kitchen door. I watched him cross the yard and turn north along the lane, the bread in his hand, the broken boots dark against the frost-hardened mud. I closed the door and took the bowl to the basin and washed it. I dried it. I set it back on the shelf. The afternoon was a slow one. In the taproom, two men who had been sitting since midday found something to disagree about -- I never learned what -- and their voices began to climb in the way that voices climb when men have been still too long and the ale has given their grievances a shape. "Gentlemen," I said from the doorway. "You'll settle it outside or you'll settle it now, but you will not settle it in my taproom." They settled it now. Nan watched from behind the bar with her cloth in her hand and did not need to move. The hours between the coach's departure and the evening meal were the hours that belonged to the building itself. No one arrived. No one left. Nan polished glasses. Jem was outside -- I could hear the rhythmic scrape of a rake on cobblestones. I sat in the kitchen with the ledger open and the candles counted and the evening menu worked out in my head: what we had, what would stretch, what could not be made to stretch no matter how I managed it. The kitchen fire cracked and settled. I closed the ledger and went upstairs to check the rooms. Room four wanted airing. Room seven had a cracked pane that would need boarding before the cold deepened. I noted both and moved on, running my hand along the banister as I came down the stairs -- a gesture that had no practical purpose but that I had been performing since I was tall enough to reach, and that my mother had performed before me, and that I was not willing to examine for what it meant. Evening came down quickly. The last of the light went grey, then blue, then dark. The guests who remained had eaten and retired. The fire in the taproom was low, the coals orange beneath a fine white skin of ash. I stood before it for a moment, then closed the grate. The bar was tallied. Nan had gone upstairs an hour ago. The kitchen was clean -- the pots on their hooks, the knives in the drawer, the range shut down, the kettle empty and cool. I moved through the closing routine the way I had done it every night for two years, and every other night for the ten years before that when my mother was alive and I was learning the sequence. Taproom fire: coals safe, no open flame. Cellar hatch: bolted. Front door: locked, key on the nail. Stable yard: quiet. I stepped outside for a moment and stood in the cold. Jem had settled the horses. I could hear one of them shifting in its stall, a slow heavy sound that carried in the stillness. On the second floor, a light burned behind Bridget's window. The faint shape of her behind the glass -- seated, watchful. I did not look long. She was there. She was always there. I went back inside and shut the door. My hands, when I looked at them in the kitchen lamplight, were reddened from cold water and cleaning. There was a small cut on my right index finger -- the edge of Fitchett's delivery note, which I had not noticed at the time. The paper had been coarser than I expected. I examined the cut without interest and let my hand drop. The letter was on the kitchen table. It had not been there when I cleared the table before dinner, which meant it had been delivered to the front door while I was upstairs checking rooms, and Nan had set it in the kitchen for me, and I had been moving through the building all evening with this thing sitting here, waiting. I picked it up. The seal was a dull red -- Stainforth's seal, the one he used for correspondence with tenants, different from his magistrate's seal. I knew both. His handwriting on the direction was small and slanted and very regular, the hand of a man who did not waste ink. I turned the envelope over once. The paper was heavy. Good paper. He did not economize on paper. I set the letter down on the table. I did not open it. I had known it was coming. I had known since the last quarter's rent was received without acknowledgment -- no note of receipt, no confirmation, just silence where there had always been at least a formal line. The silence had been louder than any letter. I had been moving faster ever since, though I could not have said toward what. The kitchen was empty. The fire was down to its last coal. The inn around me was silent with the particular silence of a place that has worked hard and is done. "Right, then," I said. No one heard me. I turned down the lamp.

I checked the ledger twice because the first time I did not believe the numbers, and the second time I did. Six weeks. That was what remained between the White Hound and the next quarter's rent, and between the next quarter's rent and the conversation I did not intend to have with myself about what came after. I closed the book and set my hand flat on the cover as though the figures might shift if left unattended. They would not. Numbers did not rearrange themselves out of sympathy. I had learned this from my mother, who had learned it from hers, and the lesson had not improved with repetition. The kitchen was cold. The fire I had built twenty minutes earlier was still finding its courage, the coals ticking and settling beneath the first true flames. I could see my breath if I turned toward the window, where the glass was rimed at its edges with a fine, persistent frost that had been there every morning this week and would be there every morning for the next several months. October had no business being this cold. I pulled my sleeves down over my wrists and went to check the bar stock. The taproom was dark and smelled of last night's ale and the particular staleness of a room where men had sat too long. I opened the shutter on the nearest window to let in enough grey light to work by and ran my eye along the casks. The small beer was adequate. The porter was low -- I would need to speak to the brewer before Friday. The gin was untouched, which meant either that last night's guests had been unusually abstemious or that Nan had watered it again, and I suspected the latter, and I did not blame her. I tallied the previous evening's take against what was in the cashbox. The numbers agreed, which was a small mercy. I wiped down the counter where someone had left a sticky ring the size of a saucer, checked that the chairs were pushed in, and moved to the second fireplace. This one I built more carefully than the kitchen fire, because the taproom was the room that paid for everything else, and a cold taproom was an empty one. The wood was damp. I stacked it with the driest pieces on the inside and the greenest on the outside where the draught from the chimney would season them over the course of the morning, and I used the last of the kindling without thinking about it and then stood up and thought about it. I would need to send Jem to split more before the evening. I added this to the list I kept in my head, which was the only list I had ever found entirely reliable. The staircase boards were cold underfoot as I climbed to check the second-floor landing. Two rooms occupied, both doors shut, no sound from within. The linen on the unoccupied beds had been turned yesterday. The washstand in room four had a crack in the basin that I had noticed three days ago and not yet replaced, because the replacement basin cost one and fourpence and one and fourpence was one and fourpence. I noted it again and went back downstairs. Through the kitchen window I could see Jem in the stable yard. He was straightening tack on the wall hooks, working with his one hand and the stump of the other in the efficient, unhurried way that meant he had been up for some time and had already seen to the horses. He did not look toward the house. He did not need to. The morning required the tack to be straight and the horses fed and the yard swept, and Jem attended to what the morning required without being asked, which was the quality I valued most in him and the one I would never embarrass us both by naming. I went back to the kitchen and put the kettle on. Nan came through the back door a few minutes later with her pins not yet set and her apron already tied. She crossed to the bar without a word, took her place, and began laying out the breakfast things. We had done this before. We had done this six mornings out of seven for three years, and the rhythm of it was so fixed that any interruption would have been more conspicuous than the routine itself. I heard her set out the bread board, the butter crock, the knives. The small sounds of a house preparing to be of use. The London coach was late, which was not unusual. What was unusual was that it was also short -- two passengers instead of the expected four, and both of them in the kind of temper that comes from being rattled over bad road in a cold coach for the better part of three hours. The first was a man who said nothing and wanted only to be shown to a room. Nan took him. The second was a woman of perhaps fifty with a trunk that had a split hinge and a great deal to say about it. "The clasp gave way at Hatfield," she told me, holding the damaged trunk as though presenting evidence in a court of law. "The coachman offered no assistance whatsoever." "Jem," I said, and Jem was there, which he had been since the coach pulled into the yard, and he took the trunk from her hands with a nod that conveyed both competence and the absolute minimum of social engagement. The woman watched her trunk leave with the expression of someone who was not yet finished being aggrieved but had temporarily lost the object of her grievance. I directed her to the dining room, where Nan had already set out breakfast, and she went. The coachman had come down from the box and was stamping his boots in the yard. He was a man I knew by sight -- Fenton, who drove the Tuesday and Friday route -- and he wanted a word. "Road north of Hatfield is gone to pieces," he said. "Ruts past the axle in two places. I'll not answer for the springs if it's left another week." "The turnpike trust meets Thursday. I'll send word to Harding." "Harding won't do a damn thing." "Harding will do a damn thing if I tell him his coaches are losing passengers to the Baldock road. Which they are." I handed him a cup of ale, which was what he had actually come for. "Two passengers today, Fenton." He drank, wiped his mouth, and set the cup down. "Aye. Well." He touched his hat and went back to the coach, and that was the end of it. He had his route and I had mine, and neither of us had time for more. The yard cleared. The coach pulled away with a sound of wheels and harness that I could have identified blind -- the particular rattle of Fenton's rig, which wanted a new linchpin on the off side. Jem took the woman's trunk inside. Nan was behind the bar, wiping down the counter with one eye on the door through which the guests had gone. I noticed. I said nothing. The chandler arrived at half past ten with his cart and his monthly delivery and his new price. "Tenpence the dozen," he said, setting the first box of tallow candles on the kitchen table as though the matter were already settled. I did not pick up the box. "It was ninepence last month." "Tallow's gone up." "Tallow has gone up a farthing the pound since March. Your price has gone up a penny the dozen four times in seven months. That is not the same arithmetic." He looked at me with the expression of a man who had hoped this would be easier. I knew his expression well. I had been seeing it for seven months. "I've costs, Miss Dale." "You have costs. And I know what they are, Mr Flood, because you supply every establishment on this stretch of road and they all talk to one another. You pay fourpence halfpenny the pound for tallow from Barnet. You sell to the Red Lion at eightpence the dozen. You sell to the George at eightpence the dozen. You would like to sell to me at tenpence the dozen because I am one woman with one inn and you believe I have no other source. But I do have another source. I can buy direct from Barnet and make my own, and I have done it before, and the only reason I have not done it recently is that your candles are better than mine and we both know it." He said nothing for a moment. Then: "Ninepence ha'penny." "Ninepence. Same as last month. Same as next month." He exhaled through his nose. He left the candles. He took his money. He went. The door closed behind him. I stood in the kitchen with the delivery note in my hand and wrote the adjusted price in the margin: ninepence. The pencil point caught the edge of the paper and I felt it but did not look at my hand. There was the afternoon to manage and the evening after that, and the chandler's ninepence was ninepence I would not have to find somewhere else, and that was the whole of what it meant. I carried the candles to the storeroom. Somewhere between the storeroom and the kitchen -- between one task and the next, in the gap where thought sometimes slipped in uninvited -- it came to me as it always did. The ledger. The rent. The chandler's price edging up and my margin edging down, and all of it weighed against the fact that none of it would be mine to fight for if I were any man's wife. The lease was in my name. The law was not. I had done this calculation before. I did not dwell on it. I went to check the dining room. He came around the back, as the sign on the gate asked. I saw him through the kitchen window before he knocked -- young, thin, standing outside the door with the particular stillness of a man who was deciding whether he was welcome enough to try. His coat was too large for him and had been too large for whoever owned it before him. His boots were coming apart at the welt, the left sole separating from the upper in a way that meant he had been walking a long time on wet ground. He was perhaps twenty. He was not threatening. He was not sure. He raised his hand to knock and I opened the door before his knuckles reached the wood. "Have you eaten?" I said. He looked at me. His eyes were older than his face. "Not recent." "Kitchen. Not the taproom." I stepped back and he understood and came in and sat where I pointed, which was the bench nearest the range. I set out what the kitchen had: bread from this morning, broth from last night reheated, a piece of cheese that was harder than I would have served to a paying guest but was food and he was hungry. He ate without speaking and without haste, which told me he had been hungry long enough to know that eating too fast would make him sick. When the bowl was empty he set down the spoon and looked at the table. "Thank you, ma'am." "Where are you headed?" "North," he said. He did not name a town. I cleared the bowl and the plate and he stood and went out through the kitchen door and I closed it behind him. The latch caught. I washed the bowl and set it on the rack and wiped the table where he had sat and went to see what Nan needed for the evening. In the taproom, two men who had been sitting since midday had found something to disagree about. I could hear the pitch of it rising as I came through the doorway -- not yet a fight, but the particular frequency that preceded one, when voices go tight and chairs begin to scrape. "Gentlemen," I said. "You'll settle it outside or you'll settle it nowhere, but you will not settle it in my house." They settled it nowhere. Nan, behind the bar, had not moved. She did not need to. The afternoon passed the way afternoons at the White Hound passed when there was no coach due and no crisis to manage -- which is to say, it passed in work. I reset the rooms that needed resetting. I checked the kitchen stores against what we would need for the evening meal and made a list that was shorter than I wanted and longer than I could afford. Nan polished glasses. Jem split kindling. The light outside went from grey to the colour of old pewter and then, slowly, to the colour of nothing at all. I locked up alone. This was not unusual. This was every night. The last guests settled or gone, the last fire checked, the bar tallied, the kitchen closed down. I moved through the inn's closing routine the way I had moved through it hundreds of times -- taproom first, where the fire was down to coals and the chairs were pushed in and the floor wanted sweeping but would get it in the morning. The cellar hatch: locked. The front door: bolted. The dining room: cleared, the shutters drawn. I checked the kitchen range and closed the damper halfway for the night. The stable yard was quiet. Jem would have settled the horses. I could see the line of the stable roof against what was left of the sky, and beyond it the second-floor windows of the inn. A light behind the glass in Bridget's room -- the shape of her, sitting at the window, watching the yard below as she watched it every evening. I did not look long. She was there. She was watching. I had seen it too many times for it to need anything from me. I came back inside and stood in the kitchen. My hands at the end of the day were reddened from the cold water and the cleaning, and there was a small cut on my index finger where the edge of the chandler's delivery note had caught the skin. I noticed it the way I noticed everything at this hour -- with the flat attention of a body that had been in use since before dawn and was nearly finished. The letter was on the table. It had not been there this morning. Someone -- Nan, or the afternoon post -- had left it beside the ledger, and I had not seen it until now, or I had seen it and had not let myself know that I had seen it. Stainforth's seal on the envelope. His handwriting on the direction. I picked it up. I held it. I turned it over once and felt the weight of it, which was the weight of good paper and sealing wax and nothing more. I did not open it. I had known it was coming since the last rent quarter. I had known it the way I knew when the porter was running low or the chimney wanted sweeping -- not because anyone told me, but because I paid attention, and the signs were there if you were willing to read them. I had been moving faster ever since. The chandler's price. The short coaches. The six weeks in the ledger. All of it the same current, and this letter the place where it surfaced. I set it down on the table beside the closed ledger. I stood in the kitchen with my cut hand and my reddened fingers and the inn locked and quiet around me. "Right, then," I said. No one heard it. There was no one to hear.

I checked the ledger twice because the first time the numbers were worse than I remembered and I wanted to be certain I had not carried a figure wrong. I had not. Six weeks and two days to the quarter, and the margin between what I held in reserve and what Mr Stainforth's agent would expect was narrowing to something I could feel in my teeth. I wrote the total at the foot of the column, set down the pen, and closed the book. The kitchen was cold. The fire had been banked overnight and I had built it up twenty minutes ago, but the chimney drew badly when the wind came from the north and this morning the wind was doing exactly that. I could hear it testing the casements in the dining room, that particular low moan that meant the putty on the northeast window had cracked again. I would deal with it after breakfast. I pulled my sleeves down over my wrists and went to check the taproom. The taproom fire I built from scratch. The grate had been cleaned -- Nan had done it before she went up last night, I could tell from the way the ash bucket sat, pushed flush against the wall instead of left crooked the way Piers left it. I laid the fire with the efficiency of a woman who has laid ten thousand fires and does not think about the method any longer: paper, kindling, two splits of oak, the draught plate adjusted. It caught on the first match. I stood a moment to be sure, then moved behind the bar. Stock: three casks of ale, the second nearly kicked. Two bottles of brandy, one opened. Port, adequate. Gin, more than adequate because no one had been ordering gin this month, which told me something about the quality of our custom or the season or both. I made a note in my head and checked the till. Last night's take was where I had left it, counted and recorded. Fourteen shillings, eightpence. A Tuesday in late October. I wiped down the bar with a cloth that wanted replacing, rinsed it in the bucket, wrung it out, and hung it on the hook. The windows along the front were rimed at their edges with a fine crust of ice that caught the early light and made the glass look older than it was. Through them the yard was empty, the cobbles dark with overnight damp. Jem was in the stable yard when I checked out the back. He had the tack rail down and was straightening the harness, working through the bridles one-handed with the unhurried precision that meant he had been at it for some time. He did not look up. The horses were quiet in their stalls. I could hear one of them shifting its weight, the soft thud of a shod hoof on straw, and beneath that the steady sound of Jem's breathing as he worked a buckle free with his remaining hand and the stump braced against the rail. I went back inside. Nan came through the kitchen door a few minutes later. Pins not yet set, apron already on. She crossed to the bar without a word and began checking the glasses, holding each one to the grey light from the window and setting aside the two that were clouded. We had done this before. We had done it so many times that the silence between us was not silence at all but a kind of language, fluent and undramatic, that said the day has started and we are here and there is work. The London coach was late. I heard it before I saw it -- the particular grind of iron rims on the road surface that meant a heavy vehicle moving slower than it should. When it came into view through the yard gate it was listing faintly to the left, which meant the rear spring on that side was going again, and the coachman's face as he pulled up confirmed everything the sound had already told me. Two passengers instead of the expected four. The first was a stout woman in a travelling cloak the colour of mud who descended the coach step with the compressed fury of someone who has been uncomfortable for a long time and intends to make others pay for it. She had a trunk. The trunk had a split hinge, and she had opinions about this which she began to share before her second foot touched the cobbles. "The hinge will want a strap until it can be mended properly," I said. "Jem." He was already there. He took the trunk without comment, assessed the hinge with a glance, and carried it toward the inn with the split side braced against his hip. The woman watched him, opened her mouth, reconsidered, and followed. The second passenger was a thin clergyman who wanted nothing except to be shown to a room where he could lie down, and I directed him upstairs with a minimum of ceremony. The coachman climbed down stiffly. He was a man I knew by sight -- Aldridge, who had been running the London-to-Biggleswade route for three years and who treated the Great North Road as a personal grievance. "The surface above Hatfield is gone to pieces," he said. "Ruts a foot deep past the Wrestler's. I've put this to the company twice." "Have you put it to the turnpike trust?" "The trust." He said the word the way a man says the name of a creditor he has given up on. "I'd sooner write to the moon." "Write to Alderman Jessop at the Hatfield vestry. He chairs the trust committee and he keeps a carriage. If the road is damaging his own springs he'll act. If you write to the company they'll write to the trust and the trust will write back and nothing will be mended before Christmas." He looked at me with the particular expression of a man who has been given practical advice by a woman and is deciding whether to be grateful or affronted. He chose grateful, or near enough. He touched his hat, asked for ale, and I sent him to Nan. The yard cleared. Jem had taken the trunk inside. The coachman was at the bar. The clergyman was upstairs. The stout woman was in the dining room, where I could hear her settling herself with the effortful sighing of a woman arranging cushions. Nan had been wiping down the bar with one eye on the door through all of it. I noted this. Said nothing. The chandler arrived at half past ten. His name was Fenton and he had a cart and a boy and forty-eight tallow candles in a crate, and he had adjusted his price upward. "Tenpence the dozen," he said, setting the crate on the kitchen table as though the matter were already settled. "It was eightpence in April." "Tallow's dear this autumn. The renderers--" "The renderers are charging you what they charged you in April, Mr Fenton, because I spoke to Samuel Hart at the Baldock market last Thursday and he told me his price has not moved since Lady Day." Fenton's face did something complicated. "There's the cartage to consider." "Your cartage has not changed. Your route has not changed. You deliver to the Red Lion and the George before you reach me, and neither of them is paying tenpence because Mrs Adkins at the George would have told me so and enjoyed doing it." He was quiet for a moment. The boy on the cart outside was picking his teeth. "Eightpence ha'penny," I said. "And I'll take sixty next month if you can supply them, which will make your journey more worth the candle, if you'll forgive the expression." He did not smile, but he took the price. He marked the delivery note in pencil and pushed it across the table to me. I signed it and he left. The door closed behind him. The kitchen was quiet. I stood at the table and wrote the adjusted figure in the margin of the delivery note: eightpence ha'penny the dozen, sixty ordered for November. The pencil was blunt. I sharpened it with the kitchen knife, brushed the shavings into my palm, and tipped them into the fire. The figures from this morning were still in my head -- not the specific numbers now but the shape of them, the way they pressed against each other. The chandler's half-penny mattered. Every half-penny mattered, because every half-penny was mine to negotiate for only as long as I remained unmarried and the lease remained in my name. I had done this calculation before. I did not need to do it again. I picked up the crate and carried the candles to the store cupboard. The afternoon was ordinary. I reset two of the upstairs rooms, stripped the bed in the third where the sheets had not been changed since Saturday, carried the linen down to the copper. Nan served the stout woman a late dinner and reported that she had complained about the mutton, which was not surprising because the mutton was not good. I made a note to speak to the butcher. Jem reshod one of the post horses in the yard, the ring of his hammer carrying through the kitchen window in a steady rhythm that marked the hours more reliably than the clock. I was in the kitchen when I saw the soldier. He came around the back, as the sign on the front gate directed. Young -- younger than Piers, or perhaps only thinner, which made him look younger. His coat had been a uniform coat once and someone had removed the facings, leaving pale lines where the coloured wool had been. His boots were coming apart at the welt, the left one held together with a twist of cord. He stood outside the kitchen door without knocking. I watched him through the window. He was looking at the door the way a man looks at a thing he is not certain he is permitted to want. He was assessing whether he was welcome enough to knock. I opened the door before he decided. He took a half-step back. Up close his face was weathered past his years, the skin drawn tight across the cheekbones the way it goes when a body has been feeding on itself for some time. "Have you eaten?" I said. "Not since yesterday, ma'am. I had some bread." "Kitchen, not the taproom. Sit there." He sat at the table where the chandler's crate had been. I cut bread, ladled broth from the pot that had been on the range since morning, and set both in front of him. He ate the way hungry men eat -- not fast, not slow, but with a concentration that left no room for anything else. I did not watch him. I went about the kitchen and let him finish. When the bowl was empty he stood. "Thank you, ma'am." "Where are you headed?" He named a direction -- north, toward Baldock, and then perhaps further, toward somewhere a cousin might or might not still be living. He was not certain. He thanked me again and went out through the yard and I did not see which way he turned at the gate. I cleared the bowl and the plate. I washed them, dried them, and put them back on the shelf. I wiped the table. In the taproom after four o'clock two men who had been sitting too long over too little ale began a dispute about something I did not trouble to identify. I heard the pitch of it change -- that particular shift from irritation to intention -- and went through. "Not in my house," I said. "Settle it outside or settle it quietly, but you will not settle it at volume." They settled it quietly. Nan, behind the bar, had not moved. She caught my eye as I turned and I gave her the smallest nod, which meant nothing had happened and nothing would. The inn in the late afternoon was the inn between uses. Coach gone, guests settled or departed, the dining room empty, the taproom holding two old men and their silence. Nan restocked the bar. Jem was somewhere in the yard -- I could hear him, or rather I could hear the absence of the sounds that meant something was wrong, which was its own kind of hearing. I sat at the kitchen table with the accounts book and worked through the week's figures, then went upstairs to check the rooms that would be needed for the evening coach, then came down and started the preparations for supper. Potatoes, the last of the mutton, carrots from the cellar that were still sound. The ordinary work. The work that did not end and did not need to end because it was the thing that held everything else in place. Evening came early, the way it does in late October. The light went out of the yard by half past four and by five the windows were black. I lit the lamps in the taproom, the dining room, the hallway. The evening coach brought three passengers and no difficulties. Nan served supper. I ate standing in the kitchen, a plate of what I had made, not tasting it particularly. Jem came in for his supper and ate it at the table without conversation and went back out to settle the horses for the night. The last guests went up by nine. I tallied the bar with Nan, who handed me the take and said good night and went upstairs. I heard her door close. Then the inn was mine in the way it was only mine at this hour -- not as property, not as obligation, but as a thing I knew so completely that moving through it in the dark was no different from moving through it in the light. I checked the taproom fire. Down to coals, the grate still warm, safe to leave. I locked the cellar hatch. I checked the stable yard from the back door -- quiet, the horses settled, Jem's lantern out. The air was sharp and smelled of frost and woodsmoke and the faint sweetness of hay. I looked up at the second floor. A lit shape at Bridget's window -- the candle, the edge of her curtain, the suggestion of a figure behind the glass. She was watching. She watched every night. I did not look long. I came back inside and bolted the back door. I moved through the hallway to the front, checked the lock, checked the bolt. My hands at the end of the day were reddened from cold water and cleaning, the knuckles rough, and there was a small cut on my right index finger from the edge of Fenton's delivery note that I had not noticed until now. It was the invoice that had cut me. I looked at it for a moment and then I did not look at it. The kitchen. The range banked, the table wiped, the pots on their hooks, the floor swept. Everything accounted for. Nothing wasted. I had done this hundreds of times and each time the inn settled around me like a held breath released, and I stood in the middle of it and felt the specific quiet of a place that has been used hard and is now resting. The letter was on the shelf by the door where the post was left. I had not seen it arrive. It must have come with the evening coach, or Nan had taken it from someone during supper and set it there without mentioning it, which she would have done because she would have seen the seal and known that mentioning it would not improve my evening. Stainforth's seal on the envelope. His handwriting on the direction. Miss D. Dale, The White Hound, Great North Road, Hertfordshire. I picked it up. I held it. I turned it over once. The wax was dark red, pressed with a signet I had seen on three previous letters, each one more pointed than the last. The paper was good -- heavier than anything I used, smooth under my thumb. I did not open it. I had known it was coming since the last rent quarter. I had known it the way you know weather from the colour of the sky at morning -- not the specifics, not the hour, but the certainty that it is on its way and that nothing you do between now and its arrival will change its nature. I had been moving faster ever since. The chandler's half-penny. The extra candles for November. The short margin in the ledger that I checked twice this morning because the first time the numbers were worse than I remembered. I set the letter on the table. I looked at it. The kitchen was empty and the inn was quiet and no one was listening. "Right, then," I said.

Tallied the week's coal deliveries first, then moved back to the columns that mattered. Six weeks, give or take three days, before the next quarter's rent became a question she could not answer with what was in the strongbox. She had known this when she opened the ledger, and she knew it still when she closed it. The knowing did not change the number. The fire in the kitchen range was already built up -- she had done that before sitting down, carrying the first coal herself rather than waiting for Jem, because waiting for anyone was a habit she had never managed to form. The kitchen was cold despite the fire. October had come in with its hands in its pockets and its teeth showing, and the range needed another hour before the room would be anything but functional. She pulled her sleeves down over her wrists and went to check the taproom. The taproom fire was slower, banked from the night before. She worked the damper, set two fresh logs across the coals, and stood back. The room was empty in the way of spaces that had been full only hours ago -- the particular vacancy of cleared tables and swept flags and upended stools, everything back in its place and yet not quite at rest. The bar stock needed checking. She did it by touch as much as by sight: casks of mild and bitter, the bottles behind the bar in their proper order, the measure on its hook. The porter was getting low. She noted it. She found the slate where Nan kept the day's tally and read it in the grey light from the window. The glass had rimed at the edges during the night. Beyond it, the yard was a dark shape, the stable block a darker one, the road beyond the gate still invisible. She did not need to see the road. She knew its sounds. The accounts from the previous night she tallied at the bar counter, working quickly, her handwriting compressed and even in the column. The supper sittings had been decent -- two private parties, one family, the regular table of drovers who came through on Wednesdays. The room and board for three guests. Against that: Nan's wages, the kitchen provisions for the week, the last delivery of firewood that had come in two days ago at a price she had been intending to revisit with the supplier. She set the pen down, looked at the total for a moment, and moved on. The cellar hatch: locked, as she had left it. The kitchen: fire drawing properly now, the range beginning to tick with heat. She filled the kettle, set it on, and checked the larder against what she would need before the morning coach. Bread from yesterday, enough for the guests' breakfast. The remains of a joint. Eggs from the village girl who brought them on Tuesdays, which was today, which meant they would arrive before the coach and she should be ready for that before anything else. She set a pan on the range. Through the back window she could see the stable yard beginning to separate from the dark -- the shapes of it becoming specific: the pump, the gate, the mounting block with its stone cap white with frost. Jem was already there. She could make him out by the stable door, working at something -- the tack, by the set of his shoulders, the particular angle of concentration. She had not asked him to do it. The morning required it, and Jem knew what the morning required. She watched him for a moment without meaning to, then turned back to the eggs. Nan came through the kitchen door some minutes later. The pins in her hair were only half set, but her apron was already tied, and she moved to the bar without stopping -- took up the cloth from its hook, checked the state of the glasses on the shelf, set her back to the counter in the particular stance that meant she was ready before she appeared to be. Neither of them said anything. There was nothing that needed saying. The kettle came to a boil. Dinah made two cups and left one on the end of the bar for Nan without comment. --- The London coach was fifteen minutes late, which was not unusual, and short by two passengers, which was not unusual either in this season when the better-off found reasons not to travel north until they absolutely had to. The ones who had come -- a woman with an aggressively split trunk and a manufacturer from Royston who had been occupying the same corner of the coach since Barnet and had opinions about it -- both arrived in the yard with the specific ill temper of people who felt their discomfort should be somebody's fault. The woman with the trunk had already begun on the coachman by the time Dinah reached the yard. The coachman -- Grice, who ran the London to Baldock route on Tuesdays and Thursdays and had been doing so since before Dinah took over the inn -- was not listening. He was watching Dinah cross the yard with the expression of a man who had a specific thing to say and was calculating the fastest route to saying it. She got there before the woman with the trunk could redirect her complaints. "Grice." "Miss Dale." He pulled his hat brim briefly. "I need a word about the north road above Hatfield. The surface above the second milestone's breaking up bad -- flints lifting, a rut opening on the eastern verge that wants filling before the first hard frost. Near had a wheel in it yesterday." "Has it been reported to the surveyor?" "Reported to him in September. Man doesn't move unless his axle's personally at risk." He shifted his weight. "I'm telling you because your coaches run that road. If a wheel goes there in November it goes in the mud and it doesn't come out fast." "I'll write to the surveyor today and to the trustees by the end of the week. I can have Jem look at the worst of the flinting when he goes out with the hire trap tomorrow -- he'll give you his honest assessment and you'll have something in writing before Saturday's run." Grice looked at her for a moment, then nodded. "That'll do." "The trunk," she said, because the woman behind him was working up to something. "Hinge has gone." "Jem." She did not raise her voice. Jem was already crossing the yard, having assessed the situation through some ostler's instinct that she had long since stopped trying to explain. The woman with the trunk transferred her attention to him, which was its own kind of resolution. Grice gathered his lines. "There's also a matter of the ostler's--" "Same terms as last quarter," Dinah said. "I checked the records before you arrived." He had not argued. There was nothing to argue with. The yard began to clear. The manufacturer from Royston went inside without incident. The trunk went with Jem.
