Grice's horses were watered and the coach turned round in the time it usually took, the yard filling briefly with the noise of harness and instruction and then emptying again as completely as a bucket tipped over. The gate settled on its post. Nan was visible through the taproom window, wiping the bar counter with one eye on the yard. Dinah noted it and said nothing. --- Higgins the chandler arrived at half past ten with his cart and his revised invoice, and the particular expression of a man who has decided that what he is about to do is entirely reasonable and would prefer not to have to justify it at length. "Miss Dale." He had the invoice ready, which told her he had been rehearsing this. "Mr Higgins." He set it on the bar counter. She looked at it without touching it. "Tallow's up," he said. "Can't be helped. That's the price from Barnet market, and I'm not in a position to absorb it all myself." "You're showing me eleven shillings the dozen." "That's correct." "Last quarter you were at nine and six. Quarter before that, nine even." She looked up at him. "I also know what you charge the Three Crowns at Welwyn, Mr Higgins, and I know what you charge the Bell at Hatfield. I know what tallow costs at Barnet market because I check the market reports, and I know your margin well enough to understand that eleven shillings doesn't reflect your costs -- it reflects your preference." Higgins's expression shifted through several positions. "I have other customers to consider." "You have a cart that runs this road twice a month and a route that doesn't make sense unless the White Hound is on it. I'll take your standard delivery at ten shillings the dozen, and you'll write me that price on a fresh invoice, and you'll have what I owe you before your cart's hitched." She paused. "That's a better outcome than the alternative." He looked at her. She looked at him. "Right," he said at last. "Ten shillings." He wrote out the new invoice. She paid him. He took his money and left, and the candles stayed. She wrote the adjusted figure in the margin of the delivery note in her small, compressed hand, and set it with the other receipts in the order in which they would need to go into the ledger. Somewhere between tallying the adjustment and moving to the next thing, the thought came and went the way working thoughts do: if she married, there would be no negotiation, because there would be no inn to negotiate for. The lease would pass to a husband at the altar the same moment she said the words that were supposed to be about something else. Higgins's margin, the coal column, the six weeks in the ledger -- all of it would belong to someone else's account. She had done the arithmetic before, many times. It came out the same. She moved on. --- She saw him through the kitchen window before he knocked. He was standing outside the kitchen door at the edge of the yard, in the particular way of someone who is considering whether to knock and has not yet decided. Young -- younger than she had first thought, something about the way he stood making him seem older at a distance. His coat was too thin for October, though it was a serviceable cut once, army issue, the kind that had been good at the start and was now at the end of everything. His boots were the most honest thing about him: they were coming apart at the welt on the left side, the leather peeling from the sole in a careful, progressive way that said these boots had been resoled twice and would not survive a third. He stood for a moment looking at the door. He was not threatening. He was calculating the odds of a welcome, and he was not confident about them. Then he knocked. --- She opened the door. He was perhaps twenty, perhaps a year or two more. His face had the pared quality of someone who had not eaten reliably for longer than a few days. He held his hat with both hands, turning it slightly. "I saw the sign," he said. "Round the back." "You ate this morning?" He considered lying and decided against it. "Not today, miss. No." "Kitchen," she said. "Not the taproom. Come in." He came in. She set bread and a bowl of broth on the table -- the broth from the pot that was always on the range, the bread from yesterday's batch that would not last another day anyway. He sat where she indicated and ate without ceremony. She let him get through the worst of it before she moved to the other end of the kitchen to continue what she had been doing. When he had finished he sat with his hands around the empty bowl. "Where are you headed?" she asked. "North." A pause. "I've a cousin in Doncaster. I think." "The Great North Road goes past the gate," she said. "You'll be five miles on before noon if you go directly." "Yes, miss." He stood and picked up his hat. "Thank you." She nodded once. He went out through the same door he had come in by. She heard his steps cross the yard and go quiet at the gate. She cleared the bowl and the bread plate, set them in the washing basin, and her hands moved to the next task. --- The two men in the taproom had been sitting since before noon with nothing to do but develop a grievance about the seating arrangement that was, at its core, about something else entirely -- each of them with that particular stillness of men in a confined space who have been deciding whether to be unreasonable and have come to a conclusion. She dealt with it from the doorway. "Gentlemen. You're both keeping a seat at the fire and there are other guests waiting for a table. You'll settle up or move to the bench." They looked at her. She waited. They moved to the bench. Nan, behind the bar, continued wiping the counter. She had not needed to come round. --- The afternoon gap had its own particular quality: the inn in its between-rush state, the coaches gone and the evening not yet started. Dinah worked through it in the usual way -- the accounts, two rooms that needed resetting before the evening guests arrived, the kitchen provisions for supper, the cellar stock checked against what she had ordered. Jem was in the yard. Nan was at the bar with something she was reading when she thought Dinah wasn't looking. The ordinary grind of it had a texture like good plain cloth: no fineness, but no holes

I checked the ledger twice because the first time the numbers were worse than I remembered, and the second time they were the same. Six weeks to quarter day. I wrote the figure at the bottom of the column, then wrote it again beneath the first because I had pressed too lightly and the ink had not taken. The candle on the kitchen table was a stub from last night, already guttering, and the fire I had built twenty minutes earlier had not yet thrown enough heat to take the chill from the room. My breath showed when I leaned back. October had come in cold and stayed cold, and the mornings at the White Hound started in the dark and stayed dark until the kitchen fire caught properly and the windows gave back something other than your own face. I closed the ledger and set the inkwell on top of it to keep it shut. There was no profit in staring at the page. The numbers would not improve for being looked at. The kitchen fire first, then the taproom. I riddled the grate and built the fire up with the smaller logs, saving the good oak for the evening when the room would need to hold its heat. The taproom hearth took longer -- the flue had been drawing badly since September, and I made a note, again, that the chimney wanted sweeping before the real cold set in. I checked the bar stock: three casks of ale, one nearly spent, the gin at half, the brandy adequate. I tallied the previous night's accounts against the coin in the box and found them short by fourpence, which meant someone had poured a measure without marking it or I had miscounted. I wrote the discrepancy in the margin of the accounts book. It was more likely my error. Nan did not make mistakes with coin. The windows along the front were rimed at the lower edges where the putty had shrunk away from the glass. I could feel the draught from three feet off. I pulled my sleeves down over my wrists and moved on to the dining room, where the cloths from last night's supper were still on the tables because I had been too tired to clear them. I cleared them now. Folded each one, checked for stains that would need soaking, stacked them on the sideboard. One chair had a loose spindle. I turned it over, tested the joint, set it aside for repair. The dining room smelled of cold tallow and the ghost of mutton broth. I went through the kitchen and out the back door to check the yard. Jem was already there. He had the big gelding's bridle across his knee and was working at the cheekpiece buckle with his one hand and the blunt hook he'd rigged to the end of his stump. The leather was stiff with cold. He did not look up when I came out, and I did not speak. The tack was straight on its pegs, the straw fresh in the nearest stalls. He had been up before me, or he had not gone to bed. With Jem it was not always possible to tell. I went back inside. A few minutes later Nan came through the kitchen door with her pins half-set and her apron already tied. She crossed to the bar and began laying out the glasses for the morning, wiping each one with the cloth she kept tucked at her waist. She did not say good morning and neither did I. We had done this before. Many hundreds of times. The London coach was late. This was not unusual -- the road north of Hatfield had been in a poor state since the summer rains, and the coaches lost time on the hill above Welwyn -- but it put the morning behind by nearly an hour, which meant the midday work would be compressed and the kitchen would suffer for it. When the coach finally came into the yard I was at the front door and Jem was at the horses before the wheels had stopped. Two passengers instead of the expected four. The first was a stout woman in a travelling cloak who descended with a trunk that had a split hinge and a great deal to say about both the trunk and the road and the coach and the temperature. The second was a thin clergyman who went directly to the dining room without speaking to anyone, which was the most sensible thing I had seen all morning. The woman's trunk was listing open where the hinge had given way. I caught Jem's eye and he was already moving toward it, tucking his hook under the strap to steady the lid while he worked out how to carry it one-handed up the stairs. I turned to the woman. "The hinge will hold if you leave the trunk latched. I'll have it mended by this afternoon. Your room is the second on the left at the top of the stairs, and there's hot water in the jug." She opened her mouth, reconsidered, and went upstairs. The coachman was waiting by the lead horse. He was a man I knew slightly -- Harding, who ran the Tuesday route -- and he looked as though the road had used him badly. "The turnpike above Hatfield wants regrading," he said. "There's a rut on the eastern side deep enough to take a wheel off. I've reported it twice." "Report it to the trust again and put it in writing this time. If you leave the letter with me I can have it witnessed and sent with the mail." "Will that do any good?" "It will do more good than telling me about it in my yard." He almost smiled. He touched his hat and climbed back up. The yard cleared. The coach pulled away. Jem led the change horses back into the stable. The stout woman's trunk sat at the foot of the stairs where Jem had left it, its broken hinge gaping like a split lip. I would mend it myself. Jem had enough to do. Nan had been wiping down the bar through all of this with one eye on the front door. I noticed. I said nothing. The chandler came at half past eleven, as he always did on the last Tuesday of the month. He was a neat, deliberate man named Farrow who carried his wares in a handcart and conducted his business as though each transaction were a small legal proceeding. He set the box of tallow candles on the kitchen table and named his price. "Tenpence the dozen," he said. I looked at the candles. I looked at him. "It was ninepence last month." "Tallow's gone up. The renderers in Hitchin have raised their price and I must pass it on." "The renderers in Hitchin are charging you what they charged you in August. I know because Mrs Pratt at the George buys from the same man, and she told me what she pays." Farrow's expression did not change. He was not a man who showed surprise easily, but his hands went still on the edge of the box. "Mrs Pratt's arrangement is her own business." "It is. And mine is mine. Ninepence the dozen, Mr Farrow, which is what you charged me in July and August and September, and which gives you a margin of threepence on every dozen, which is more than fair for a delivery you make on a route you would travel regardless." "I have other customers on this road who would take the stock at tenpence." "Then you must offer it to them. But you will make the delivery again next month, and you will find me here, and the price will still be ninepence." He held my gaze for a moment longer than was comfortable. Then he lifted his hands from the box and stepped back. "Ninepence," he said. "Thank you, Mr Farrow." He left. The door closed behind him. I stood in the kitchen with the box of candles on the table and wrote the adjusted price in the margin of the delivery note. Ninepence. The ink was barely dry before I was adding the figure to the column in my head -- candles, coal, flour, the farrier's bill for the bay mare's shoe, the quarterly rent that sat at the bottom of every calculation like a stone at the bottom of a well. If I married, the well would belong to someone else. The lease, the accounts, the right to stand in my own kitchen and argue with a chandler over a penny a dozen -- all of it transferred by law to a husband the moment the register was signed. I had done the arithmetic before. I did not do it again now. There was stock to count for the evening. He came around the back, as the sign on the gate asked. I saw him through the kitchen window before he knocked -- young, thin, standing a few feet from the door with his weight on his heels, as though he had not yet decided whether he was welcome enough to step forward. His coat was too large for him. His boots were coming apart at the welt, the sole of the left one separating from the upper so that I could see a line of grey stocking beneath. He was perhaps twenty, perhaps younger. It was difficult to tell with the ones who had been soldiering. They came back older in the face than they had any right to be. He stood there for several seconds, looking at the door. Then he raised his hand and knocked once. I opened the door. "Have you eaten today?" He looked at me directly. His eyes were pale and steady and not quite focused, as though he had been walking for a long time without a fixed point to aim for. "Not since yesterday, ma'am. Morning." "Kitchen, not taproom. Sit there." He sat at the table without removing his coat. I put bread in front of him and a bowl of the broth that had been on the range since morning. He ate carefully, not fast, holding the bread in both hands. When he had finished he set the spoon down and aligned it with the edge of the bowl. "Thank you, ma'am." "Where are you headed?" "North," he said. "Stamford way. There might be work." He stood, touched his forehead, and went out through the kitchen door. I cleared the bowl and the plate and wiped the table where he had sat. The broth pot wanted topping up. I added water and set it back on the range. In the taproom two men who had been sitting too long over their second pints had begun to disagree about something -- the state of the roads, or the price of grain, or whatever it was that men found to quarrel about when the afternoon stretched empty before them. One of them had pushed his chair back. The other was leaning forward with his jaw set. "Gentlemen. You will lower your voices or you will settle your accounts and continue this discussion in the road." They looked at me. The one who had been leaning forward picked up his glass and drank from it. The other pulled his chair back to the table. Nan was watching from behind the bar with her cloth in her hand. She did not need to move. The afternoon passed in the ordinary way. The coach was gone, the guests settled or departed. Nan polished glasses and restocked the bar. Jem was in the yard -- I could hear him working, the ring of a hammer on something, a low word to one of the horses. I reset the dining room for the evening, checked the kitchen stores, made a list of what would be needed from the village before the week was out. Flour. Salt. A sack of potatoes if they could be had at a decent price. The mutton from the butcher would last one more day if I was careful with the portions. I was always careful with the portions. The evening came in early. By five the light was gone and the candles were lit -- Farrow's candles, ninepence the dozen -- and the taproom had a small custom of local men who came for their pint and their half-hour of company before going home to their suppers. I served them, cleared their glasses, wiped their tables. The fire drew better in the evening when the flue had warmed through. The room smelled of woodsmoke and ale and damp wool, which was what the White Hound smelled of every evening from October to March and had done for as long as I could remember. The last guest went up at half past nine. I locked the front door, drew the bolt, and began the circuit. Taproom fire down to coals -- I spread them with the poker and set the guard. The cellar hatch: locked, the padlock cold under my hand. The bar: tallied, the coin box in the cupboard, the cloth folded on the counter where Nan had left it. The kitchen: range banked, surfaces wiped, the broth pot set to the side to cool. I went out the back door into the yard. The air was sharp and still. Jem had settled the horses -- I could hear them shifting in their stalls, the heavy sound of an animal at rest. The stable door was closed. The yard was quiet. On the second floor, above the kitchen, there was a lit shape at Bridget's window. I could see the faint glow of her candle and the outline of her sitting in her chair, angled toward the glass. I did not look long. I went back inside and locked the kitchen door behind me. My hands were reddened from the cold water and the work of the day, the skin across the knuckles tight and rough. There was a small cut on my right index finger, a thin line where the edge of Farrow's delivery note had caught the skin when I folded it. I had not noticed it at the time. It was the invoice that had cut me. The letter was on the kitchen table. It had not been there when I left to do the circuit, which meant it had come with the evening delivery and Nan had set it out before she went up. I knew the seal before I picked it up -- Stainforth's seal, pressed deep into dark wax -- and I knew the handwriting on the direction, the careful, unhurried hand of a man who had never needed to rush a letter in his life. I picked it up. I turned it over once. The wax was smooth and cold under my thumb. I set the letter down on the table beside the ledger and the stub of the morning candle. I did not open it. I had known it was coming since the last quarter day. I had known it the way you know a draught is coming through a window -- not because you can see it, but because the room has already begun to cool. I stood in the kitchen with my hands at my sides. "Right, then," I said. No one heard me. The inn was quiet, and the letter lay where I had put it, and the fire in the range ticked as it cooled.

I checked the ledger twice because the first time the numbers were worse than I remembered, and I wanted to be sure I had not carried a figure wrong. I had not carried it wrong. Six weeks to the quarter, and the margin between what came in and what went out was narrowing in the way a road narrows toward a bridge -- still passable, but with less and less room to correct if a wheel slipped. I closed the book and set my hand flat on its cover as though I could press the numbers into better order by force. The kitchen was cold. The fire I had built twenty minutes ago was still finding its temper, the coals not yet white enough to throw real heat. I pulled my sleeves down over my wrists and went to check the taproom. The taproom fire needed raking and rebuilding. I did it. The ash pan was heavier than yesterday's, which meant Nan had let the fire burn longer than she should have after I went up. I would not mention it. Nan knew what coal cost. She would not have done it without reason, and if the reason was that a guest had lingered, then the guest had been drinking, and the margin on ale was better than the margin on coal. I set the fire, swept the hearth, and checked the bar stock. Two casks of ordinary needed tapping before the morning coach. The gin was low. The brandy was adequate, but it was the cheap brandy, and the last bottle of the good had gone three days ago to a man who paid without complaint and left without tipping. I noted the gin on the edge of last night's tally sheet and moved on. The accounts from the previous evening balanced. They always balanced. My mother had taught me to tally at the end of every night and again at the start of every morning, and in thirteen years I had found a discrepancy exactly twice. Both times it had been my own error. I did not find this reassuring. It meant there was nothing to recover, no mistake to correct. The numbers were simply what they were. The windows in the taproom were rimed at the lower edges where the cold crept in along the sashes. The putty on the east-facing panes had cracked over the summer and I had not yet had it seen to. I pressed my thumb against the worst of it and felt the draught needle through. Another task for the list, another cost I would put off until I could not. I went out through the kitchen to the yard. Jem was in the stable already. I could see him through the open half-door, moving along the line of tack with his one hand working a bridle buckle loose while his stump braced the leather against the post. He did not look up. The horses were quiet, fed and watered -- he would have done that first, before the tack, because Jem understood the order of need the way a good sergeant understands the order of march. I had never had to tell him this. I stood for a moment in the cold yard, watching the steam of my breath, and went back inside. Nan came through the kitchen door a few minutes later. Her cap pins were not yet set, the edges of her hair escaping in the reddish wisps she would tuck away before the first guest came down. Her apron was already on and tied. She passed me without a word, took her place behind the bar, and began wiping the counter with the cloth she kept folded on the shelf beneath. I heard the small, efficient sound of it -- the damp fabric on wood -- as I returned to the kitchen to set the kettle on. We had done this before. Many hundreds of times. The London coach was late. It came in forty minutes past its time, which was not unusual for late October when the road above Hatfield turned to heavy mud after three days' rain, but it was running short -- two passengers instead of the expected four, and both of them already sour with the journey before they'd set foot in the yard. The first was a woman of middle years with a trunk that had split its hinge. She announced this fact before the coachman had finished handing her down, and she announced it again to Jem, and she was preparing to announce it a third time to the yard at large when I stepped forward and directed her inside. Jem appeared at the trunk without being called, assessed the hinge, tucked the loose strap end under his arm, and hauled it toward the door. The woman watched his one-armed handling of her luggage with an expression that moved from alarm to reluctant fascination, and by the time she reached the hallway she had stopped talking about the hinge. The second passenger went straight to the dining room and asked for coffee, which Nan was already preparing. The coachman stood by the lead horse, flexing his hands in the cold. He was a man I knew by sight -- Burrell, who ran the northern route twice a week and had opinions about everything between here and York. "The road past the Hatfield turning," he said. "It's gone, Mrs Dale. Properly gone. Three ruts deep enough to take a wheel off." "It was bad last month." "It's worse. I'll be writing to the turnpike trust, for what good that does. You might tell your people coming from the north to take the lower fork if they've any sense." "I'll mention it to anyone who asks." He looked at me as though he wanted to say more -- about the trust, about the state of things generally -- but I had the morning still ahead of me and he had the road to York, and neither of us had time for a conversation we had already finished. He touched his hat and climbed back up. The coach pulled out. The second passenger finished his coffee and went upstairs. Jem carried the woman's trunk to her room and came back down without comment. The yard cleared. Through the doorway to the bar I could see Nan wiping down the counter with one eye on the front door, the cloth moving in the same steady rhythm it always moved. I noted it. I said nothing. The chandler arrived at half past ten with his cart and his monthly delivery. His name was Fitch, and he had been supplying the White Hound with tallow candles since my mother's time, and for the fourth time in seven months he had adjusted his price upward. "Tenpence the dozen," he said, setting the first box on the kitchen table. "It was ninepence last month." "Tallow's gone up. You'll have heard." "Tallow's gone up a farthing the pound since July. That's not a penny a dozen on the finished candle, Mr Fitch, and you know it as well as I do." He set the second box down with slightly more care than the first. "I've costs beyond the tallow, Mrs Dale." "You have. And I know what they are, because you supply every inn and public house between here and Welwyn, and not one of them is paying tenpence." He looked at me. I looked at him. The kitchen was quiet except for the fire. "Ninepence halfpenny," he said. "Ninepence. Same as last month. Or I'll place my next order with Greaves in Knebworth and you can explain to your other customers on this road why you've lost the White Hound." The silence held for the space of two breaths. Then Fitch picked up his delivery note from the table and made a small, tight mark on it with his pencil. "Ninepence," he said. "Thank you, Mr Fitch." He left. The door closed behind him. I stood at the kitchen table and wrote the adjusted price in the margin of the delivery note, and the small scratch of the pen was the only sound in the room. The candles went into the store cupboard. The delivery note went into the ledger. The ledger went back to its place on the shelf, and as I slid it into position my hand rested there a moment longer than the task required. The rent, the chandler's price, the narrowing margin -- all of it pressed against the same calculation I had made before and would make again: that if I married, the lease would pass out of my hands and into his, whoever he was, and every negotiation I had ever conducted at this table would become someone else's right to conduct or abandon as he chose. It was not a new thought. I did not dwell on it. I took my hand off the ledger and went to check the bar stock I had noted that morning. I saw him through the kitchen window before he knocked. He had come around the back, as the sign on the front gate asked, and he was standing outside the kitchen door with the particular stillness of a man who is deciding whether he is welcome enough to try. Young -- not more than twenty, perhaps less. Thin in the way of men who have missed meals steadily rather than all at once. His coat was too large for him and had been too large for whoever owned it before him. His boots were coming apart at the welt, the left sole separating from the upper in a way that meant he had been walking on wet ground for longer than the leather could bear. He raised his hand to knock, then paused, and in that pause I saw the whole of it -- the calculation, the uncertainty, the effort of asking. I opened the door. He straightened. He was taller than I had thought, or would be when he was not hunching against the cold. "Have you eaten?" I said. He looked at me directly for the first time. His eyes were steady, older than the rest of his face. "Not recently, ma'am." "Kitchen, not the taproom. Sit there." I pointed to the bench by the fire. He understood. He came in and sat where I told him and did not look around the room the way a man looks when he is assessing what might be taken. I set bread in front of him, and broth from the pot that had been on since morning, and he ate with the controlled steadiness of someone who has learned that eating too fast on an empty stomach is a mistake you only make once. He finished. He set the spoon down and looked at the bowl as though he wanted to thank it separately. "Thank you, ma'am." "Where are you headed?" "North," he said. Then, after a pause that carried the weight of everything the word did not contain: "There's family. Somewhere past Biggleswade." I nodded. He stood, and I opened the door for him, and he went out into the yard and across it and through the gate without looking back. I closed the door. I cleared the bowl and the bread plate and set them in the basin, and my hands were in the water before the gate had finished swinging shut. The afternoon brought the usual small disputes. Two men in the taproom -- commercial travellers, both of them, with too much time between coaches and not enough to occupy it -- fell into a disagreement over something I did not trouble to identify. The pitch of it changed, the way a kettle changes note before it boils, and I crossed the room. "Gentlemen. You'll settle it outside or you'll settle it nowhere, but you will not settle it in my taproom." They settled it nowhere. Nan watched from behind the bar with her cloth in her hand and did not need to move. The hours between the afternoon coach and the evening were the hours the inn breathed. No arrivals, no departures. Nan restocked the bar. Jem was outside -- I could hear the rhythmic scrape of a hoof pick against stone, which meant he was cleaning hooves, which meant the horses were in good order and he was working ahead of tomorrow's need. I reset the two rooms that had been used that morning, checked the kitchen stores against what I would need for the evening meal, and wrote out the order for the brewery that was due by Friday. The work was not interesting. It was not meant to be interesting. It was meant to be done, and I did it, and the doing of it carried me from three o'clock to six the way it always did, without incident and without rest. I locked up alone. The last guest had gone to his room by nine. The last fire I checked was the taproom -- down to coals, the grate still warm, the ash pan half full. I would empty it in the morning. The bar was tallied, the figures written in Nan's hand because I had asked her to do it tonight, and her figures matched mine when I checked them against the stock. The kitchen was closed down: the range banked, the surfaces wiped, the morning's bread dough set to prove under a cloth near the warmth of the chimney wall. The cellar hatch was locked. I tested it with my hand on the bolt, the way I tested it every night, feeling for the catch. The stable yard was quiet. Jem would have settled the horses and gone to his room above the tack store. I could see the line of the stable roof against the sky, which had cleared enough to show a few stars and the thin edge of a moon. On the second floor, above the kitchen, a lit shape showed at Bridget's window -- the glow of her candle behind the glass, and the outline of her sitting where she always sat, watching the yard below with the patience of a woman who has outlived the need to be anywhere else. I did not look long. I came back inside and bolted the yard door. My hands at the end of the day were reddened from the cold water and the cleaning, the knuckles rough, a small cut on my right index finger where the edge of Fitch's delivery note had caught the skin when I folded it into the ledger. I noticed it without interest. It was the invoice that had cut me. The kitchen was empty and warm from the banked range. I stood at the table for a moment, my hands flat on the wood, not because I was tired -- though I was tired -- but because the table was solid and the wood was smooth and there was nothing left to do, and the absence of tasks was the one thing I had never learned to fill. The letter was on the table. It had not been there when I cleared the surfaces after dinner, which meant it had come with the evening post, which meant Nan had taken it in and left it for me without comment. The seal was dark red, pressed deep into the wax with the impression I knew without needing to look closely. Stainforth's seal. His handwriting on the direction, the letters small and even and slanting to the right, the way a careful man's handwriting slants when he has taken time over what he is saying. I picked it up. I held it. I turned it over once and felt the weight of it -- not heavy, a single sheet at most, which meant he had not troubled himself with preamble. I set it down on the table with the seal facing up. I did not open it. I had known it was coming since the last rent quarter. I had known it the way you know a draught is coming through a cracked window -- not because you can see it, but because the room has been getting colder for weeks and there is only one place the cold is coming from. I had been moving faster ever since. The ledger, the chandler, the margins, the coal -- all of it had been the work of a woman trying to outpace something she could not outpace, because it was not coming from outside. It was already here. The letter sat on the table. The seal caught the low light from the range. "Right, then," I said to the empty room. No one answered. I put my hand on the letter once more, briefly, the way you put your hand on a door before you open it -- not to test whether it is locked, but to steady yourself against what is on the other side. Then I took my hand away and left it there, and went upstairs to bed.

I checked the ledger twice because the first time the numbers were worse than I remembered, and I wanted to be certain I had not carried a figure wrong. I had not carried it wrong. Six weeks to the quarter, and the margin between what was in hand and what would be owed was thinner than the rind on a block of hard cheese. I closed the book and set my palm flat on the cover for a moment, as though the pressure of my hand could push the figures into better order. It could not. I stood and put the ledger back on the shelf above the kitchen dresser, spine out, aligned with the others. The kitchen fire needed building up. I riddled the grate, cleared the ash into the pail, and laid the new fire the way my mother taught me: paper twist, then kindling split fine, then two pieces of coal placed just so at the back where the draught would catch them. I did not think about my mother while I did this. I thought about the coal, and how much was left in the bin, and what it would cost to have more delivered before the month turned. The kitchen was cold. The windows were rimed at the edges where the damp had crept in overnight and frozen, a thin crust of white that would melt by mid-morning and leave the sills wet. I pulled my sleeves down over my wrists and tucked the cuffs into my palms. The taproom fire next. I carried the ash pail through and repeated the business -- grate, ash, paper, kindling, coal -- then checked the bar stock while the fire caught. Three casks of ale in the cellar, one broached, two sealed. Gin at a quarter bottle. The port was adequate. I counted the clean glasses on the shelf behind the bar and noted that two were chipped. I set those aside. A chipped glass costs nothing if you catch it before a guest cuts his lip; it costs a good deal more after. Last night's accounts were on the bar in Nan's hand, which was neater than mine. I tallied them standing, running the figures against what I expected. They matched. I folded the paper and put it in my apron pocket. The inn was quiet in the way it is quiet only before six o'clock: not peaceful, not restful, but held. Waiting. Every board and beam and hinge in the place was simply waiting for the day to begin, and so was I, and neither of us had the luxury of taking our time about it. I went out the back. The yard was grey and still, the cobbles dark with overnight damp. Jem was in the stable already. I could see him through the open half-door, straightening tack on the hooks along the wall -- bridles first, then the driving reins, each one checked for wear and hung in its place. He did not look up. He did not need to. He knew I was there the way he knew most things: by the sound of the door and the weight of the step and whatever instinct the army had put into him that he had never troubled to explain. I went back inside. A few minutes later Nan came through the kitchen door, pins in her hair not yet set, apron already on. She passed me without a word and took her place behind the bar. She wiped down the surface with a cloth, set the clean glasses to one side, and began arranging the bottles in the order she preferred, which was not the order I preferred but which I had stopped correcting two years ago because hers was better. The London coach was late. This was not unusual. What was unusual was that when it finally arrived -- I heard it on the road before I saw it, the particular heavy rhythm of a coach running with fewer passengers than its springs expected -- it carried only two instead of the four I had been told to prepare for. The first was a woman of perhaps fifty with a travelling cloak and a trunk whose hinge had split clean through, so that the lid gaped open on one side like a broken jaw. She had opinions about this. She shared them with the coachman, with the yard, with the October air, and finally with me, when I came out to meet them. I directed Jem to the trunk before the woman could describe its sufferings a fourth time. He appeared at my shoulder without being called, took one look at the hinge, and carried the trunk inside with his one hand braced under the base and the stump of his other arm steadying the lid. The woman watched him and went quiet, which was the first useful thing that had happened all morning. The second passenger was a man in a brown coat who wanted nothing but his room and said so. I gave him his key. The coachman wanted a word. He was a large, red-faced man I had seen a dozen times before, and he stood in the yard with his hands on his hips and the horses steaming behind him. "The road's gone to pieces above Hatfield," he said. "Ruts deep enough to lose a wheel in. Someone ought to speak to the turnpike trust." "Someone has," I said. "Twice. They've promised gravel before the first frost." "They promised gravel last year." "They did. I'll write again. Is there anything else you need before you go?" He looked at me for a moment as though he wanted to say more -- about the road, or the trust, or the general state of things -- and then he shook his head and climbed back up. I watched the coach pull out of the yard. The sound of it changed as it hit the road, the iron-shod wheels finding their rhythm on the harder surface, and then it was gone and the yard was quiet again. Jem had dealt with the trunk. The woman with the opinions had been shown upstairs. The man in the brown coat was already behind his door. Nan was behind the bar with one eye on the doorway through to the hall, the cloth still in her hand though there was nothing left to wipe. I saw her watching. I said nothing. The chandler arrived at half past ten. He was a narrow man named Burrell, and he came with his cart and his boy and his monthly delivery of tallow candles, price per dozen. He set the crate on the kitchen table and named his figure. "That's fourpence more than last month," I said. "Tallow's gone up." "Tallow's gone up a halfpenny the pound since March. You've raised your price fourpence the dozen four times in seven months. That's arithmetic I can do, Mr Burrell." He folded his arms. He had a habit of looking past my left shoulder when we came to this part of the conversation, as though someone more reasonable might be standing behind me. No one ever was. "I've costs of my own, Miss Dale." "You have. And I know what you charge the Woolpack and the Red Lion for the same candles, and it is not what you are charging me." "The Woolpack orders twice the quantity." "The Woolpack also pays you late. I pay on delivery. I have always paid on delivery." He said nothing for a moment. His boy shifted on his feet behind him. "I can take the candles elsewhere," Burrell said. "You can. And you'll sell them for less than what I'm offering, because there is no other inn between here and Baldock that will take your full monthly order and pay you the same day. You know this. I know you know this." The silence held for five seconds. I counted them. "Twopence more," he said. "Not four." "Done." He unloaded the crate. His boy carried it to the storeroom. Burrell left without pleasantries, and I did not offer any. The door closed behind him. I stood in the kitchen with the delivery note in my hand and wrote the adjusted price in the margin, the nib of the pen catching on the cheap paper. The candles. The coal. The ale. The quarterly rent. All of it weighed against the same plain fact -- that the inn was mine only so long as I remained unmarried, and that the law did not distinguish between a woman who chose not to marry and a woman who could not afford to. If I married, the lease would pass to my husband. The ledger, the negotiations, the whole of it -- his. I had done this calculation before. I set down the pen and went to check the dining room. I saw the soldier through the kitchen window before he knocked. He had come around the back, as the signs asked. He was young -- not a boy, but not far past it -- thin in the particular way of men who have been underfed for a long time, as though the body has been consuming itself from the inside. His coat was army issue, or had been. His boots were coming apart at the welt, the sole of the left one separating from the upper so that I could see a strip of grey stocking underneath. He stood outside the kitchen door without knocking. He looked at the door, then at the yard, then at the door again. He was not deciding whether to knock. He was deciding whether he was welcome enough to try. I opened the door. He looked up. His face was weathered and wary, and he held his cap in both hands. "Have you eaten today?" I said. "Not since yesterday, ma'am. The morning." "Kitchen, not taproom. Sit there." He sat at the table where the chandler's crate had been ten minutes before. I cut bread, ladled broth from the pot on the range, and set both in front of him. He ate without haste but without pause, the way men eat when they have learned that food may not be offered twice. I did not sit. I wiped down the range and checked the stockpot while he finished. He set the spoon down and looked at me. "Thank you, ma'am." "Where are you headed?" "North," he said. "There's work in the mills, I'm told." He put his cap back on and went. I closed the door behind him, cleared the bowl and the bread board, and washed them in the basin. The afternoon was the ordinary grind. Two men in the taproom got loud over something trivial -- a disputed card hand, or a remark about someone's horse, or whatever small friction passes for drama when men have been sitting too long with too much ale and too little purpose. I heard the pitch of their voices change from the kitchen and went through. "That's enough," I said. "Settle it outside or settle it quietly. Those are your choices." They settled it quietly. Nan was watching from behind the bar with the cloth in her hand. She did not need to move. The hours between the afternoon coach and the evening meal were the hours I used for everything else. Accounts in the kitchen. Two rooms to reset upstairs -- beds stripped, aired, made up again. The store cupboard checked and a list made for the next market day. Nan restocked the bar. Jem was in the yard; I could hear the steady sound of him working, the particular rhythm of a man who does not stop because stopping would require him to think about something he has no intention of thinking about. Evening came early, the way it does in late October -- not a dramatic darkening but a slow withdrawal of light, the grey sky simply deepening until the windows gave back nothing but the reflection of the room inside. I lit candles. The new ones, Burrell's twopence-more candles, burned clean and steady. The last guests settled. The last fire checked -- taproom coals banked, the iron screen set in front. The bar tallied, the figures written in Nan's hand and left on the counter for me. The kitchen closed down: range damped, surfaces wiped, floor swept, the stockpot moved to the cold shelf for tomorrow. I moved through the inn's closing routine alone. I had done this hundreds of times. I checked the cellar hatch and found it locked. The stable yard was quiet; Jem would have settled the horses and gone to his quarters above the tack room. I stood in the yard for a moment and looked up at the second floor. There was a lit shape at Bridget's window -- the faint glow of a candle behind glass, and the outline of her sitting where she always sat, watching the yard below. I did not look long. I went back inside and locked the kitchen door. The bolt was stiff. I worked it into its housing with the heel of my hand. My hands at the end of the day were red from cold water and cleaning. There was a small cut on my index finger, a thin line across the pad where the edge of Burrell's delivery note had caught the skin. I had not noticed it at the time. It was the invoice that had cut me. I turned from the door and saw the letter on the kitchen table. It had not been there at midday. Someone -- Nan, most likely -- had placed it there while I was upstairs. It sat alone on the scrubbed wood, pale and precise, as though it had been waiting longer than the few hours since its arrival. I knew the seal. I knew the handwriting on the direction. Stainforth. The letters were small and even, the hand of a man who did not waste ink. I picked it up. I held it. I turned it over once, feeling the weight of the paper, which was heavier than what I used -- good stock, expensive. The seal was unbroken, a circle of dark red wax pressed flat and clean. I set it down on the table and put my hand over it, the way I had put my hand on the ledger that morning, as though pressure could change what was written inside. I did not open it. Not tonight. I stood in the kitchen with my reddened hands and the single candle burning on the dresser and the letter under my palm. The inn was quiet around me -- the particular quiet of a place that has been used hard and is now resting, every beam and board and hinge settling into its own weight. "Well then," I said. No one heard it. The kitchen held the words and let them go, the way it held everything -- the heat, the steam, the smell of broth, the days -- and gave nothing back.

I checked the ledger twice because the first time the numbers had refused to make sense, and the second time they made sense and were worse. Six weeks. That was what the column gave me when I subtracted what we held from what we owed, divided by what we earned on an honest week, and allowed nothing for spoilage, breakage, a lame horse, or God's general enthusiasm for testing the deserving. Six weeks to the quarter's rent, and the margin between us and short was the width of a pencil line. I closed the book. The kitchen was cold. The fire I had built twenty minutes ago was still finding its confidence, the coals ticking and settling beneath the first true flames. I could see my breath if I angled my head toward the window, where the glass was rimed at its edges with a fine white crust that would not clear until midmorning. October had no business being this cold. I pulled my sleeves down over my wrists and set the ledger on the shelf where it lived, spine out, between the flour crock and the vinegar bottle, and went to fill the kettle. The taproom fire I built next, moving through the dim room with the grate brush and the coal scuttle and the long match. Last night's tallow had guttered to a crust in the wall sconce nearest the door; I scraped it out with my thumbnail and set a fresh stub in its place. The bar stock I checked by touch and count. Two casks of ordinary, one of the better ale that the Baldock brewer delivered on Thursdays, gin at the level I had marked on the bottle with a scratch of my nail. Someone had moved the pewter measures. I put them back. The accounts from last night were on the bar in Nan's hand, which was tidier than mine. Three shillings eightpence. A Tuesday in late October. I could have guessed the figure before I looked. I wiped down the bar with the rag that lived on its hook beneath the counter, wrung it, hung it back. Checked the hinge on the cellar hatch, which had been catching for a week and would need Jem's attention before it seized entirely. Noted the draught from the window seat where the putty had cracked along the sash. Moved on. Every morning was the same sequence, and I did not vary it, because the sequence was how the inn told me what it needed. If I listened with my hands and my feet and the particular quality of silence in each room, the building spoke plainly enough. A door that stuck had swelled overnight: damp getting in somewhere. A floorboard that gave more than yesterday meant the joist beneath wanted looking at. The smell of the taproom told me whether the chimney was drawing clean or whether soot had built up in the throat. This morning it drew clean. One less thing. Outside, the yard was grey and still. Jem was already there. He had the big gelding's harness across the half-door of the end stall and was working at a buckle with his one hand braced against the leather and his teeth holding the strap taut. He did not look up when I came to the back door. He did not need to. I had checked, and he was where he would be, and the yard was in order, and that was the whole of our conversation. I went back inside. Nan came through the kitchen door a few minutes later, pins in her hair not yet set, apron already tied. She took her place behind the bar without a word, pulled a cloth from beneath the counter, and began on the glasses. We had done this before. We had done it so many times that the doing had worn a groove in the morning, smooth and familiar as the rut a cartwheel cuts in a soft road, and there was comfort in the groove even on a morning when the ledger had said six weeks and meant it. The London coach was late. This was not unusual. What was unusual was that it was also short -- two passengers instead of the expected four, and both of them out of temper before the wheels had stopped turning. The first was a stout woman in a travelling cloak who descended from the coach with the compressed fury of someone who has been personally inconvenienced by the entirety of Hertfordshire. She had a trunk, and the trunk had a split hinge, and she had opinions about the split hinge that she began to express before her second foot touched the ground. I directed her inside. Jem had appeared at the coach door without being called, and I left the trunk to him. He would see the hinge. He would know whether it wanted a nail or a strap or simply a word of reassurance to the woman that her belongings were not about to scatter themselves across the Great North Road. The second passenger -- a thin man in a brown coat who looked as though he had been clenching his jaw since Hatfield -- went upstairs without speaking, which suited everyone. The coachman wanted a word. He climbed down from the box with the stiffness of a man who has been holding the same position for too long and not enjoying any of it. "North road's gone to pieces above the Hatfield turnpike," he said. "Ruts deep enough to break an axle. I've reported it twice." "The turnpike trust meets next week," I said. "I'll send word to Mr Hadley. He's the surveyor." "Hadley couldn't survey his own breakfast table." "He's the surveyor we have. I'll send word." He looked at me a moment, the way coachmen do when they have more to say and no time to say it, and then he touched his hat and went to see about his horses. I let him go. He had a schedule, and I had a morning, and neither of us had minutes to spare on the state of a road we could not mend ourselves. The coach left. The passengers settled or went upstairs. Jem took the trunk around to the back. The yard cleared, and the particular silence that follows a coach's departure filled the space -- not quiet, exactly, but emptied, the way a room feels after a crowd has gone and the air is still adjusting to the absence. Nan had been wiping down the bar with one eye on the door through all of it. I noted this. Said nothing. The chandler arrived at half past ten with his cart and his monthly delivery and his new price. "Tenpence the dozen," he said, setting the first box of tallow candles on the kitchen table as though the number were simply the weather and not a thing he had chosen. I did not pick up the box. "It was ninepence last month. And eightpence the month before that." "Tallow's dear. You know that as well as I do." "I know what you pay Burrell for your tallow, Mr Fitch, and I know it has not moved since August. I also know what the Cross Keys pays you, and the George at Baldock, and neither of them is paying tenpence." He looked at me. I looked at him. The kitchen fire cracked behind me. Outside in the yard I could hear Jem moving a bucket. "Ninepence ha'penny," he said. "Ninepence. Which is what I paid you last month and what you were content to take last month." "I wasn't content. I was accommodating." "Then accommodate me again. You have four other stops on this road, and I am the only one who pays on delivery and does not keep you waiting for her accounts. Ninepence." He breathed out through his nose. He set the second box on the table beside the first. "Ninepence," he said. He left. The door closed behind him. I stood at the table and wrote the adjusted price in the margin of the delivery note, the pencil pressing hard enough to dent the paper. Ninepence. Ninepence times the number of dozens we would burn between now and the quarter, subtracted from the figure in the ledger, added to the coal and the flour and the brewer's bill and the ostler's wages and Nan's wages and the hundred smaller things that kept the building standing and the doors open and the coaches stopping instead of passing through. If I married, none of it would be mine to calculate. The thought came and went the way it always did -- a stone dropped into moving water, sinking before the surface had time to register the disturbance. I picked up the candle boxes and carried them to the storeroom. I saw him through the kitchen window before he knocked. Young -- twenty, perhaps, or a hard-worn eighteen. Thin in the way of men who have been thin for long enough that the body has started to consume its own architecture. His coat was adequate. His boots were not. The sole of the left one had parted from the upper at the welt, and he was standing with that foot angled slightly, as though favouring the seam. He stood outside the kitchen door without raising his hand, looking at the door the way a man looks at something he is not certain he has the right to approach. I opened it. He straightened. His eyes were pale and steady, and his face had the particular blankness of someone who has learned to expect nothing so that nothing will be a disappointment. "Have you eaten?" I said. "Not recently, ma'am." "Kitchen. Not the taproom." He nodded once and came inside. I sat him at the table and put in front of him what the kitchen had: bread from yesterday, broth from the stockpot that had been on since morning, a piece of cheese that was harder than I liked but would do. He ate without haste, which told me he had been hungry long enough to know that eating too fast would bring it back up. His hands on the bread were clean. He had washed them somewhere before he came to the door, or perhaps on the way. I did not ask. When he had finished he set the spoon down and looked at me directly. "Thank you, ma'am." "Where are you headed?" "North," he said. Then, after a pause that was not quite long enough to be a hesitation: "There's a sister in Nottingham." I said nothing to that. He stood, and I opened the door for him, and he went out into the yard and across it and through the gate without looking back. I closed the door. I cleared the bowl and the plate and the spoon, and I wiped the table where he had been sitting, and I put the cloth in the basin and went to see what Nan needed. The afternoon was two men in the taproom whose disagreement about a coach fare had begun to acquire the particular density of feeling that comes when men have been sitting too long with too little to do and too much ale to do it with. I heard the pitch change from the kitchen, the way you hear a kettle before it boils -- the note shifting upward, tightening. I went through. "That's enough," I said. "Settle it outside or settle it quietly. I will not have the furniture moved." They settled it quietly. Nan, behind the bar, had not moved from her position and did not need to. The rest of the afternoon passed in the ordinary way of afternoons at the White Hound when no coach was expected and the weather kept sensible people indoors or on the road and not stopping. I reset the two rooms that had been used that morning. I went through the kitchen stores and planned the evening meal against what we had, which was enough but not generous. Nan polished glasses and restocked the bar and swept the dining room floor. Jem was outside. I could hear him intermittently -- a hammer, a gate latch, the low sound he made to the horses that was not quite speech and not quite singing. The inn in the midafternoon was a held breath. The morning's work done, the evening's not yet begun, and between them this strange, suspended hour when the building rested on its bones and I moved through it checking, adjusting, keeping the machinery in order. It was not peace. It was maintenance. But it was mine, and I knew every board and beam and draught of it, and that knowledge was the closest thing to peace I had. Evening came the way it comes in late October -- not gradually but as a decision, the light collapsing in on itself as though it had been holding on by will alone and had finally let go. I lit the lamps. I served the evening's custom, which was thin: two regulars in the taproom, a commercial traveller who wanted supper and a bed and was not interested in conversation. Nan and I managed it between us. By nine the taproom was empty. By half past, the traveller had gone upstairs. I sent Nan home. Then I closed the inn. The taproom fire I let die to coals. The grate would hold until morning. I checked the bar -- locked the gin away, covered the casks, hung the clean cloths on their hooks. The cellar hatch: locked, the bolt seated firmly this time, though the hinge still caught. The kitchen I shut down in order: stove banked, surfaces wiped, the stockpot moved to the cold shelf in the larder. I checked the front door, the side door, the lock on the storeroom. The stable yard was quiet when I stepped outside for the last circuit. Jem had settled the horses; I could hear their breathing from the near stalls, the slow heavy sound of animals at rest. The air was sharp and still and smelled of frost coming. Above the yard, on the second floor, there was a lit shape at Bridget's window -- the faint amber glow of a candle behind glass, and behind that the outline of a figure sitting, watching, as she watched every evening from that chair by the window. I did not look long. I went back inside and locked the yard door behind me. The kitchen was empty. The fire was down to its last colour, a deep red that gave almost no light but enough warmth to keep the room from freezing overnight. My hands, when I looked at them, were reddened from the cold water and the work. There was a small cut on my right index finger, a thin line just below the knuckle, from the edge of the chandler's delivery note. I had not felt it when it happened. It was the invoice that had cut me. The letter was on the kitchen table. It had not been there when I served supper. Someone had brought it -- slipped it through, or left it at the front door where I had missed it on my earlier round. It lay on the scrubbed wood with the proprietary stillness of a thing that knows it will be attended to. I recognized the seal before I touched it. I recognized the handwriting on the direction -- the neat, measured script of a man who did not raise his voice because he did not need to. I picked it up. I held it. I turned it over once, feeling the weight of the paper, which was good paper, heavier than anything I used. The seal was unbroken. The red wax caught the last of the firelight and held it. I set the letter down on the table. I did not open it. I had known it was coming. I had known since the last quarter, since the chandler's prices began to climb, since the coaches started running short. I had been moving faster ever since -- tallying closer, cutting finer, checking every cask and candle and sack of flour as though the checking itself could hold back what was coming. It could not. The letter was here. I stood alone in the kitchen with my hands at my sides and the letter on the table between me and the banked fire. "Well then," I said.

I checked the ledger twice because the first time the numbers were worse than I remembered, and the second time I wanted to be certain they were not worse than that. Six weeks. Perhaps seven if the weather held and the coaches kept running full, but I had stopped building plans on perhaps somewhere around the age of fifteen, and the habit had served me well enough since. The candle on the kitchen table guttered in a draught from under the door. I turned the page back, ran my finger down the column of last week's takings, and closed the book. The kitchen fire needed building up. I knelt on the stone flags and raked the ash forward, set the kindling in the grate, and fed it until it caught. The cold had got into the walls overnight. I could feel it through my sleeves when I leaned against the mantel to blow the flame steady, and again when I crossed to the taproom and laid the second fire there. October had no business being this bitter. The windows were rimed at their edges, a crust of ice in the corners of each pane that would not shift until midmorning. I checked the bar stock. Two casks of ale, one nearly spent. The gin was adequate. The brandy was not, but brandy had been dear since the summer, and I had stopped offering it by the glass to anyone who did not ask. I wiped down the counter, tallied the last of the previous night's accounts against the chits Nan had left on the spike, and noted the discrepancy -- threepence, likely the drover who had sworn he'd settle in the morning and had not. I wrote it off. There was no profit in chasing threepence down the Great North Road. The taproom smelled of last night's fire and spilled ale and the particular staleness of a room that had held too many bodies and not enough air. I opened the window an inch and left it. The draught would clear it before the first coach arrived. Jem was already in the stable yard when I looked out through the kitchen door. He had the big bay's harness over the rail and was working at a buckle with his one hand and the stump of the other braced against the leather, holding it steady with a pressure that looked like nothing and had taken him months to master. He did not look up. The yard was swept. The water trough was full. He had been out here since before I came down, which meant he had been out here in the dark, which meant his arm was troubling him again and he could not sleep. I said nothing. There was nothing to say that he would welcome. Nan came through the kitchen door a few minutes after I had gone back in. Her hair was half-pinned, the rest of it escaping down her neck in the way it always did, but her apron was already tied and she went straight to the bar and began setting out the glasses. She did not speak. I did not speak. We had done this before. We had done it so many times that speech would have been an interruption rather than a courtesy, and neither of us had any use for those. The London coach was late. I heard it before I saw it -- the particular grinding note of iron rims on frost-hardened road, and then the horn, and then the clatter into the yard twenty minutes past the hour. I was already at the front door. Two passengers instead of four. The first was a stout woman in a brown travelling cloak who descended from the coach with the compressed fury of someone who has been personally offended by every mile since Barnet. Behind her, a thin man in a clerk's coat who looked as though he had been trying not to exist for the duration of the journey. The coachman swung down from his box and stamped his boots on the cobbles. The woman's trunk had a split hinge. She announced this before she announced herself. "That hinge was sound when I left London," she said, "and if your man damages it further I shall expect compensation." "Jem," I said. He was already there, lifting the trunk by its base with the practised indifference of a man who had handled worse. I turned back to the woman. "There's a fire in the dining room and breakfast will be ten minutes. The trunk will be in your room when you go up. We'll see to the hinge." She opened her mouth, reconsidered, and went inside. The coachman wanted a word. He was a big man called Harker whom I had known for three years, and he did not waste time any more than I did. "Road's breaking up north of Hatfield," he said. "There's a stretch past the toll where the surface has gone to mud clear across. Lost half an hour on it this morning." "How far past the toll?" "Quarter-mile. Maybe more. It wants gravel and it won't get it before the frost sets in." "I'll send word to the turnpike trust," I said. "They won't do anything." "No," he agreed. "But you'll have sent word." He took his two minutes, drank the ale Nan brought out to the yard, and climbed back up to his box. The coach pulled out. The yard settled. The thin clerk had gone upstairs without a word. The woman with the trunk was in the dining room, where I could hear her telling Nan about the state of the roads as though Nan had personally laid them. Jem carried the trunk up the stairs, his heavy tread even and unhurried. The yard was empty. Nan had been wiping down the bar with one eye on the door through all of it. I noted this and said nothing. The chandler arrived at half past ten. His name was Burrell, and he came on the same day each month with the same cart and the same expression of mild grievance, as though the act of delivering candles to a coaching inn were a favour he had not been adequately thanked for. He set the crate on the kitchen table and named his price. "Fourpence ha'penny the dozen," he said. "Tallow's gone up." "Tallow has not gone up since August," I said. "You're paying Jessop the same rate you paid him in the summer. I know because Jessop told me, and Jessop has no reason to lie about what he charges for rendered fat." Burrell's mouth thinned. "There's the cartage to consider." "Your cartage has not changed. You pass this door on your way to the Red Lion and the Woolpack, and you have done so every month for four years. You are not making a special journey." "Fourpence, then." "Three and three-farthings," I said. "Which is what I paid you last month and the month before that, and which gives you a margin of a penny a dozen over your costs, and you know it." He looked at me. I looked at him. The kitchen fire cracked behind us. "Three and three-farthings," he said. He unloaded the candles, wrote out the delivery note, and left. The door closed behind him. I picked up the note and wrote the adjusted price in the margin, my pencil pressing hard enough to dent the paper. The chandler's price, the ledger's numbers, the rent coming due in six weeks. All of it tallied against the same quiet fact I had tallied before and would tally again: that if I married, none of this would be mine to negotiate for. The lease, the accounts, the inn itself -- all of it would pass to a husband as surely as water runs downhill, because the law said so and the law did not care whether the husband could tell tallow from beeswax. I had made this calculation. I moved on. The afternoon was ordinary. I cleared the breakfast things, checked the rooms the passengers had used, stripped a bed where the linen needed changing, and carried it down to the copper. The kitchen wanted provisioning for the evening -- there was a joint of mutton to be seen to, potatoes, the end of yesterday's bread to be cut for toast. I worked through it the way I worked through everything, which was steadily, without stopping, because stopping was when the thinking crept in, and the thinking was never as useful as the work. I saw him through the kitchen window before he knocked. Young -- not more than twenty, perhaps less. Thin in the way that is not natural thinness but the kind that comes from weeks of not eating enough. His coat had been decent once. His boots were coming apart at the welt, the sole separating from the upper on the left foot so that I could see a dark line of stocking through the gap. He stood outside the kitchen door with his hand raised and then lowered it and stood there a moment longer, looking at the door as though measuring whether he was welcome enough to touch it. I opened it before he knocked. He startled, then steadied himself. He had a soldier's face -- not scarred, not visibly damaged, but held in a way that was too still for a boy his age. He looked at me and waited. "Have you eaten?" I said. "Not since yesterday, ma'am. Morning." "Kitchen," I said. "Not the taproom. Sit there." He sat. I cut bread, ladled broth from the pot that had been on the range since morning, and set it in front of him. He ate the way hungry men eat -- quickly, without looking up, one hand curled around the edge of the bowl as though someone might take it from him. I went back to the mutton. When I turned around again the bowl was empty and the bread was gone and he was sitting with his hands flat on the table, looking at them. "Thank you, ma'am." "Where are you headed?" He named a direction -- north, toward the Midlands -- in a tone that suggested the direction was all he had. Not a destination. A bearing. He stood, thanked me again, and went. I cleared the bowl and the plate and wiped the table where he had sat. My hands were in the wash water before the yard gate closed behind him. Two men in the taproom got loud over something -- a chair, a slight, the particular grievance of men who have been sitting in one place too long with too little to occupy them. I heard the pitch change through the kitchen wall and went through. "That's enough," I said. "If you want to shout, the road is outside and it is free of charge." They stopped. Nan was watching from behind the bar with her cloth in her hand and did not need to move. The afternoon wore itself out. The coach was gone, the guests were settled or departed, and the inn entered the dead hours between the midday rush and the evening trade. Nan polished glasses. Jem was in the yard -- I could hear the rhythmic scrape of a hoof pick against stone. I sat at the kitchen table with the accounts and went through the week's figures again, then set the rooms for the evening and checked the mutton and peeled potatoes until my hands smelled of nothing but cold water and starch. Evening came on early. By five the light was gone and the candles were lit -- Burrell's candles, at three and three-farthings the dozen -- and the taproom filled with its small custom: a local farmer, two men from the Hatfield road, a woman travelling alone who took a private parlour and supper on a tray. I served, I cleared, I tallied. Nan handled the bar. Jem settled the horses for the night. The work did not end so much as thin out, like a stream narrowing toward its source, until there was nothing left but the last tasks and the quiet. I moved through the closing alone. I had done this hundreds of times and my body knew the sequence without consulting my mind: taproom fire raked down to coals, the guard set in front of the grate. Counter wiped, till counted, the take locked in the strongbox under the bar. Kitchen: range damped, surfaces cleared, the morning's bread dough set to prove under a cloth. Cellar hatch locked. I checked the bolt twice because I always checked the bolt twice. The stable yard was dark and quiet. Jem would have settled the horses -- I could hear nothing from the stalls, which meant they were fed and watered and content. The air was sharp and smelled of frost and hay and the faint iron tang of the water pump. I stood for a moment in the yard with my arms folded against the cold. Above, on the second floor, a lit shape at Bridget's window. The faint glow of a candle behind the glass, and the outline of her sitting where she always sat, watching the yard below. I did not look long. I went back inside. The kitchen door bolted behind me. My hands at the end of the day: reddened from cold water and cleaning, a small cut on my right index finger where the edge of Burrell's delivery note had caught the skin when I turned it over. The paper had done it. The invoice. The letter was on the kitchen table. It had not been there this morning -- someone had brought it in during the day, slipped it among the ordinary post, and I had not seen it until now. Stainforth's seal on the envelope, dark red, pressed deep. His handwriting on the direction, small and precise. I picked it up. I held it. I turned it over once. I did not open it. I set it down on the table beside the ledger, the two documents side by side -- the accounts I kept and the summons I had been expecting since the last quarter's rent came due. I had known it was coming. I had known it the way you know a draught is coming before the door opens, by the change in pressure, by the faint shift in the air. I had been moving faster ever since. My hand rested on the envelope. The seal was smooth and slightly raised under my fingertip. "Right, then," I said to the empty kitchen. No one answered. The fire ticked in the grate. I left the letter where it lay and blew out the candle.

I checked the ledger twice because the first time I did not believe the numbers, and the second time I did. Six weeks. That was what the column gave me when I subtracted what we owed from what we held, divided by what we spent in a day, and allowed nothing for surprise. Six weeks until the quarter's rent came due and the margin between the White Hound and insolvency became a matter of arithmetic rather than effort. I closed the book and set my hand flat on the cover as though it might try to open itself again. The kitchen was cold. The fire had been banked overnight and I had built it up twenty minutes ago, but the heat had not yet reached the table where I sat, nor the flagstones under my feet, nor the windows where the rime crept in along the edges of the glass. October had come in hard this year. I pulled my sleeves down over my wrists and stood. The taproom fire next. I carried the coal scuttle through and knelt on the stone hearth and laid the fire the way my mother taught me: paper first, then kindling crossed, then coal in a ridge along the back so the draught would pull through. I did not think about my mother while I did this. I thought about the coal, which was low, and the kindling, which Jem had split yesterday, and the draught, which meant the flue wanted sweeping before November or we would lose heat up the chimney like money through a hole in a pocket. The bar stock I checked by touch in the half-dark: three casks of ale, one nearly spent; the gin at the level I had marked with a pencil line on the label last night; the bottles of port and claret ranged along the shelf behind, none missing, none moved. Someone had left a glass on the near end of the bar. I picked it up, smelled it -- ale, not spirits -- and carried it back to the kitchen to wash. Last night's accounts were already tallied. I had done them before bed, sitting at this same table with one candle and the stub of a pencil, because if I left them until morning I would find a discrepancy and it would eat at me through the first hour of the day. The figures were clean. The figures were always clean. It was the sum they produced that was the trouble. I dried the glass and set it on the shelf and wiped the table where the ledger had been and hung the cloth on its nail. The kitchen was beginning to warm. The windows still held their frost at the edges but the air near the fire had shifted from bitter to merely cold, and the kettle I had set on the hob was starting to whisper. I poured water into the teapot and left it to steep and went out the back door into the yard. Jem was in the stable already. I could hear him before I saw him -- the particular sound of a man working one-handed with tack, the clink of a buckle drawn through leather, the soft grunt as he braced the harness against his hip to free his hand for the next strap. He had the bay mare's bridle over the half-door and was straightening the cheekpiece. He did not look up when I came out. He did not need to. I checked the water trough -- full -- and the muck heap -- cleared -- and the gate latch, which had been sticking since last week and which Jem had, at some point between yesterday evening and this morning, planed down with a rasp so that it moved cleanly. I tested it twice. It held. I went back inside. Nan arrived a few minutes later through the kitchen door, pins not yet set in her hair, apron already tied. She took her place behind the bar without a word and began wiping it down with the cloth she kept in her apron pocket, the one she washed every night and wrung out and folded into a square so precise you would think she had been taught by a military man. I poured her tea and set it at the end of the bar where she could reach it. She did not look up either. We had done this before. Many hundreds of times. The London coach was late. This was not unusual. The London coach was late more often than it was on time, owing to the state of the road above Hatfield, the unreliability of the team changes at Barnet, and the fact that the coaching company employed drivers who considered a published schedule to be a suggestion rather than a contract. I stood in the yard and waited because there was nothing else to be done about it, and because I could hear it coming -- the particular rattle of a coach running light, fewer passengers than the springs were set for, the horses moving faster than they should because the driver was trying to make up time. It came in hard and pulled up badly. Two passengers instead of the expected four.
