The first was a stout woman in a brown travelling cloak who descended with the help of the coachman's arm and immediately began to speak. "The trunk," she said. "The hinge is split. I told the man at Barnet it wanted securing and he did nothing, nothing at all, and now the lid will not stay shut and my things are --" "Jem," I said. He was already there. He took the trunk from the boot without waiting for the coachman and carried it inside on his shoulder, the split hinge facing upward so the lid would not swing. The woman watched him go and then turned to me with the particular expression of a person who has been preparing a longer speech and has been deprived of its occasion. "We'll have it mended within the hour," I said. "The dining room is through the hall on your left. There's tea and bread and cold meat if you want it." She went. The second passenger, a thin man in a grey coat, had already gone in without speaking. The coachman climbed down and stood by the lead horse and looked at me. "Road's breaking up past the Hatfield turning," he said. "There's a stretch of a quarter-mile where the surface has gone to pieces. Ruts deep enough to catch a wheel." "The turnpike trust was supposed to have it mended last month." "Well, they haven't. I've reported it twice and I'll report it again, but if you've guests expecting the afternoon coach from the north, tell them it may be late." "It may be late regardless," I said. He almost smiled. "Aye. But today it'll have a reason." He watered his horses and went. The passengers went upstairs or to the dining room. Jem took the trunk to the back passage where he kept his tools. The yard cleared. Through the window I could see Nan behind the bar, wiping down the same surface she had already wiped, her eyes on the door that led to the hall. She had been watching through the whole of it. I noted this. Said nothing. The chandler came at half past ten, as he always did on the last Tuesday of the month. He was a square man named Hatch with clean fingernails and a cart that smelled of tallow and the particular brand of self-satisfaction that comes from being the only supplier of a necessary commodity within four miles. He set the crate of candles on the kitchen table and named his price. "Fourpence ha'penny the dozen," he said. "It was fourpence last month." "Tallow's gone up." "Tallow has not gone up. Tallow is the same price it was in August, which I know because I asked Mr Ridley at the Baldock market what he was paying, and Mr Ridley has no reason to lie to me." Hatch shifted his weight. "There's the cartage to consider." "Your cartage has not increased. You make this same journey every month and the road has not grown longer." "I've other establishments that'll pay the price without complaint." "You have the Red Lion at Welwyn, who buys half what I buy, and the George at Baldock, who makes his own dips and takes your tallow candles only when his wife is too ill to render. You may take your fourpence ha'penny to either of them and see how far it gets you." He looked at me. I looked at him. The crate of candles sat between us on the table, smelling of mutton fat and commerce. "Fourpence a quarter," he said. "Fourpence. Same as last month." He held for a moment. Then he picked up his hat from the table and put it on. "Fourpence." "I'll mark the delivery note." He left. The door closed behind him. I stood at the table and wrote the adjusted price in the margin of the note -- fourpence, same as before, no change, no ground given -- and the silence of the kitchen settled back around me. The candles. The coal. The ale. The quarterly rent. All of it weighed against the same calculation I had made before and would make again: that if I married, the ledger would not be mine to keep. The lease would pass. The inn would pass. Every negotiation I had ever won at this table would be rendered meaningless by a signature in a church register. I did not dwell on it. There was stock to move and the dining room wanted clearing. I saw him through the window before he knocked. He had come around the back, as the sign on the front gate asked. Young -- not a boy, but not far past it, twenty perhaps, with the thinness of a man who has been walking on too little food for too many days. His coat was army issue, or had been once. His boots were coming apart at the welt, the sole of the left one separating from the upper in a way that meant he had been walking on wet ground and the stitching had rotted through. He stood outside the kitchen door without knocking. His hand was raised but he held it there, not quite touching the wood, and I understood what he was doing. He was deciding whether he was welcome enough to ask. I opened the door. He dropped his hand. Up close he looked older than I had thought -- not in his face, which was young and weather-roughened, but in his eyes, which were not. "Have you eaten?" I said. He considered lying. I watched him consider it. Then he said, "Not recently, ma'am." "Kitchen, not taproom. Sit there." He sat at the bench by the fire without argument. I cut bread and ladled broth from the pot that had been on the hob since morning and set both in front of him. He ate the way hungry men eat when they are trying not to show it -- steadily, without stopping, but with his mouth closed and his hands below the level of the table between bites, as though good manners were the last thing left to him and he would not surrender them. I let him finish. He set the spoon down and looked at the empty bowl and then at me. "Thank you, ma'am." "Where are you headed?" He named a direction -- north, into the Midlands -- with the vagueness of a man who did not have a destination so much as a bearing. I did not ask anything else. He stood and touched his forehead and went out through the kitchen door and across the yard and through the gate, and I watched him go until he was past the stable block and out of sight. Then I cleared the bowl and the bread board and wiped the table and set the cloth back on its nail. The afternoon turned on its wheel. Two men in the taproom got loud over something -- a dispute about a coach fare, or a horse, or nothing at all, the kind of friction that builds when men have been sitting too long with too little to do and too much ale in them. "Gentlemen," I said from the doorway. "You'll settle it or you'll settle your accounts and leave. I don't mind which." They settled it. Nan watched from behind the bar with one hand on the cloth and the other flat on the counter. She did not need to move. The inn between rushes was the inn I knew best. Coach gone, guests settled or departed, the taproom half-empty, the kitchen turning over toward the evening meal. Nan restocked the bar. Jem was outside -- I could hear him in the yard, the steady rhythm of a broom on cobbles. I sat at the kitchen table with the accounts and went through the week's figures again, not because I expected them to have changed but because the work of checking was a thing my hands could do while my mind ran ahead to what needed ordering, what needed mending, what needed watching. The hinge on the woman's trunk: Jem would have it done by now. The room on the second floor where the window catch had broken last week: I would see to it tomorrow. The evening meal: rabbit, potatoes, the last of the carrots from the kitchen garden. I wrote the menu on the slate that hung by the dining room door and went upstairs to check the rooms. Evening came down fast. By five o'clock the light was gone and the candles were lit -- Hatch's candles, fourpence the dozen, burning with the steady yellow flame of tallow that has not been cut with anything cheaper -- and the dining room was full and the taproom was full and Nan was moving behind the bar with the efficiency of a woman who has poured ten thousand pints and intends to pour ten thousand more. I served. I cleared. I settled a bill and refused credit to a man who had been refused credit before and would be refused credit again. I checked the kitchen, checked the fires, checked the cellar hatch. The evening passed the way evenings pass in a coaching inn: in a procession of small demands, each one ordinary, each one urgent, none of them remarkable and all of them necessary. The last guest went up at half past nine. I bolted the front door. Nan finished behind the bar and hung her cloth on the hook and said goodnight and went up the back stairs. I heard her door close on the landing above. I moved through the inn alone. Taproom fire: down to coals, the grate cleared, the guard set. Cellar hatch: locked, the bolt thrown and checked. The bar: tallied, the cloth over the taps, the glasses clean and ranked on the shelf. The kitchen: range damped, surfaces wiped, the morning's bread set to prove under a cloth near the banked fire. The back door: bolted. The stable yard through the window: quiet, dark, Jem's lantern extinguished. He would have settled the horses. I looked up at the second floor. A faint light behind Bridget's window. The shape of her there, sitting or standing, watching the yard below. I did not look long. She was there. She was watching. That was all. My hands at the end of the day were what they always were: reddened from cold water and the rough side of the cleaning cloth, the knuckles dry, a small cut on the index finger where the edge of Hatch's delivery note had caught the skin when I turned it over to write in the margin. I noticed it without interest. It was the invoice that had cut me. It would heal by morning. I came back through to the kitchen. The fire was low. The room was quiet in the way a room is quiet when it has been used hard all day and is now resting, the warmth still in the walls but fading, the smell of broth and bread and tallow smoke settling into the wood and the stone. The letter was on the table. It had not been there this morning. Someone had brought it -- left it with Nan, perhaps, or pushed it under the front door while the coach was in -- and it had found its way here, to the kitchen table, where I would find it last, after everything else was done. I knew the seal. I knew the handwriting on the direction. Stainforth. The letters of his name formed in a precise, unhurried hand, the ink dark, the paper heavy and cream-coloured, the kind of paper that costs more per sheet than I spend on candles in a week. I picked it up. I turned it over once. The seal was unbroken. The weight of it was nothing -- a single sheet, folded twice -- and everything. I set it down on the table. I did not open it. I already knew what it was about. I had known it was coming since the last quarter's rent, since the chandler started raising his prices, since the road began to break up and the coaches began to run light and the numbers in the ledger began their slow, implacable decline toward a figure I could not meet. I had been moving faster ever since. It had not been fast enough. The kitchen was empty. The inn was locked. The fire was almost out. "Right, then," I said. No one heard it. I put my hand on the letter and left it where it lay and went upstairs to bed.

I checked the ledger twice because the first time the figures made me angry and the second time I wanted to be sure the anger was warranted. It was. Six weeks to quarter day, and the column would not stretch. I knew what each line meant without reading the descriptions beside them -- malt, tallow, hay, the farrier's last invoice -- and I knew the sum at the bottom before my eye reached it, the way you know the last step of a staircase in a house where you have lived your whole life. I closed the book and set my hand flat on the cover. The kitchen was cold. The fire had been banked overnight and I had built it up twenty minutes ago, but the heat had not yet reached the table. October had come in hard this year. The windows were rimed at their edges, a thin crust of ice in the corners of each pane that would not melt until midmorning. I pulled my sleeves down over my wrists and went to check the flue. The taproom fire I laid from scratch. Kindling first, then the split logs Jem had stacked beside the hearth two days ago, then the draught adjusted until the flame drew clean and steady. The room smelled of last night's ale and tallow smoke. I opened the small window behind the bar to let the air move through, counted the bottles on the shelf -- gin, brandy, the local perry nobody much wanted but which cost me nothing because the farmer's wife traded it for stabling -- and noted that the port was lower than it should have been. I would speak to Nan. Or I would not speak to Nan, and she would know I had noticed, which amounted to the same thing. The bar top wanted wiping. I wiped it. The brass taps wanted polishing but that was Nan's work and she would do it when she came down. The hinge on the cellar hatch had begun to sing when lifted; I made a note to have Jem look at it, then made a second note that I could do it myself with a half-pennyworth of tallow, and struck through the first. Last night's accounts I had tallied before bed. Seven dinners served, four overnight guests, the private parlour let to a wool merchant from Bradford who drank a bottle of claret and complained about the fire. The fire had been adequate. The claret had been good. I did not write this in the ledger. The morning was the morning. I moved through it the way water moves through a channel it has cut for itself -- not thinking about the route, only following. Every door checked. Every lock sound. The kitchen stores assessed: flour enough for four days, butter for two, the bacon nearly gone. I would need to send to the butcher before Friday. I pulled my sleeves down again. The cold sat in the bones of the building like something that had been there longer than I had. Out in the stable yard the air was sharper, carrying the smell of frost and horse and hay. Jem was at the far end by the tack room, straightening a bridle on its peg with his one hand, the stump of the other arm braced against the post for balance. He did not look up. The yard was swept. The water trough had been broken through where ice had formed overnight. The horses were quiet in their stalls, their breath clouding in the grey light. I noted all of this and went back inside. Nan came through the kitchen door a few minutes later, pins not yet set in her hair, apron already tied. She took her place behind the bar without a word and reached for the polishing cloth. I heard the small sound of brass under her hands as I passed through to check the dining room. We had done this before. Many hundreds of times. The London coach was late, which was not unusual. What was unusual was the shortness of its cargo -- two passengers instead of the expected four, and both of them unhappy about something before they had even cleared the step. The first was a stout woman in a brown travelling cloak whose trunk had a split hinge and who wished everyone within hearing to understand that the roads north of Hatfield were a disgrace to the nation. The second was a thin clergyman who said nothing but looked as though he agreed with her on principle. I directed Jem to the trunk before the woman had finished her first sentence. He appeared at the side of the coach without being called -- he had heard the hinge from the yard, or he had seen the angle of the lid, or he simply knew, the way Jem knew most things, by attending to the world with the blunt patience of a man who had learned that surprises were seldom pleasant. He took the trunk on his shoulder and carried it inside. The woman watched him go with her mouth half open, her complaint stalled by the efficiency of its resolution. I settled her with a room number, a time for dinner, and a tone that did not invite further discussion. She went upstairs. The clergyman followed without being asked. The coachman found me in the yard. He was a heavyset man named Burden whom I had known for three years, and he had the look of someone who had been arguing with his horses since Hatfield. "The road's gone again past the Bell," he said. "Whole stretch of it. Mud to the axle." "The turnpike trust was meant to have it graded last month." "Well, they haven't. I lost twenty minutes and near enough a wheel. Someone ought to write to them." "Someone has. Twice." I handed him the ale Nan had drawn without being asked. "You'll want to water your team before you go on. Jem will see to it." He drank, nodded, and said nothing further. There was nothing further to say. He had a route to finish and I had an inn to run and the road would be mended or it would not, and neither of us had time to stand in a cold yard and wish it otherwise. The coach went. The passengers were settled or gone. Jem took the last of the baggage. The yard cleared and the particular silence of a coaching inn between arrivals settled over the place like dust after a cart has passed. Nan was behind the bar with one eye on the door. I noted it. Said nothing. The chandler arrived at half past ten with his cart and his monthly delivery and his new price. I had been expecting the new price. It was the fourth increase in seven months, and each time the increment was small enough to seem reasonable and large enough to matter. "Tallow's gone up," he said, setting the crate of candles on the kitchen table. "Sixpence more the dozen. Nothing I can do about it." "Tallow hasn't gone up. I buy tallow myself for the kitchen and I know what it costs." He shifted his weight. "It's the transport. The roads --" "You deliver to the Red Lion and the George and the Swan, all on this stretch, and you charge them fourpence less than what you're asking me. You have done since March." A silence. He looked at the crate, then at me, then at the crate again. "I'll take them at last month's price plus a penny," I said. "That's fair and you know it, and it saves you carting them back and finding someone else who'll buy forty dozen at once." He worked his jaw for a moment. "Tuppence." "A penny. And I'll settle today instead of on account." He took it. He had no choice that would cost him less, and we both knew it, and there was no profit in pretending otherwise. He counted the candles out, I counted the coin, and he left. The door closed behind him. The kitchen was quiet. I wrote the adjusted price in the margin of the delivery note and set it on top of the ledger. It was the ledger that caught me. The rent, the chandler's price, the column that would not stretch -- all of it pressed against the same fact, and the fact was this: if I married, the ledger would not be mine. The inn would not be mine. The negotiation I had just won would be conducted by someone else, or not conducted at all. This was not a grievance. It was arithmetic. I had done it before. I set down the pen and went to see about the kitchen stores. I saw him through the window before he knocked. Young -- not more than twenty, perhaps less. Thin in the way of men who have been thin for a long time, not the leanness of constitution but the drawn look of insufficient food over insufficient months. His coat was too large for him, an army coat that had belonged to someone broader. His boots were coming apart at the welt, the left sole separating from the upper so that I could see a line of grey stocking beneath. He stood outside the kitchen door with his hand raised and then lowered it. He looked at the door. He looked at the yard. He was deciding whether he was welcome enough to knock. I opened the door. He startled, then steadied. His eyes were pale and careful. "Have you eaten?" I said. "Not recently, ma'am. No." "Kitchen, not taproom. Sit there." He came in and sat where I pointed, at the end of the table nearest the fire. I set out bread, the end of yesterday's loaf, and a bowl of the broth that had been on the range since morning. He ate without rushing, which told me he had been hungry long enough to have learned that eating too fast would make him sick. His hands around the bowl were red with cold. He did not look up until the bread was gone. "Thank you, ma'am." "Where are you headed?" "North," he said. Then, as though that were not enough, "There's a cousin. Near Nottingham." He stood, and I saw him hesitate -- the brief calculation of a man who knows he should offer something and has nothing to offer. I did not help him through it. He nodded once, put on his cap, and went out. The door closed. I cleared the bowl and the crumbs from the table and set the cloth to soak. The broth wanted more water. I added it and moved the pot to the hotter side of the range. In the taproom, midafternoon, two men who had been sitting too long over too little ale found a disagreement about something -- I did not hear what, and it did not matter what. "Gentlemen. Not in my house." They looked at me. The louder of the two opened his mouth. "Not in my house," I said again. He closed it. They drank. Nan watched from behind the bar and did not need to move. The hours between the afternoon coach and the evening meal were the hours I used for everything that could not be done while guests were watching. Rooms checked and reset. The linen counted -- we were short two sheets, which meant washing tomorrow regardless of the weather. The kitchen set for the evening: mutton, potatoes, the last of the carrots from the garden. Nan restocked the bar. Jem was outside; I could hear the steady sound of his hammer against something, a fence post or a hinge, the particular rhythm of a man who works with one arm and compensates so thoroughly that you forget, until you watch closely, that anything is missing. The inn between rushes was the inn I knew best. Not the performance of hospitality but the machinery of it -- the unseen work that made the visible work possible. I moved through it without hurry because hurrying was wasteful, and without pause because pausing was worse. Evening came the way it always did, not all at once but in degrees -- the light thinning in the yard, the fires needing building up, the lamps lit one by one until the taproom had the particular amber warmth that made men feel they had arrived somewhere worth stopping. Four dinners served. The wool merchant from Bradford ate in the private parlour again and did not complain about the fire. Nan cleared the dining room. I tallied the bar. The last guest went up at half past nine and the inn began its slow descent into quiet. I locked up alone. This was mine, this final circuit -- the taproom fire raked down to coals, the guard set before the hearth. The cellar hatch: locked, the new singing in the hinge noted again and again deferred. The front door: bolted, top and bottom. The kitchen: range damped, surfaces wiped, the morning's kindling laid ready in the box beside the hearth. I checked the stable yard from the back door. Quiet. Jem would have settled the horses an hour ago. The cold came in off the yard in a single clean blade of air and I stood in it for a moment, letting it press against my face. On the second floor, a lit shape at Bridget's window -- the faint glow of a candle behind glass, and behind the candle, the outline of her sitting in the chair where she always sat, watching the yard below. I did not look long. My hands, when I turned back to the kitchen, were reddened from the cold water and the cleaning and the day. There was a small cut on my right index finger, a thin line where the edge of the chandler's delivery note had caught the skin. I had not felt it at the time. It was the invoice that had cut me. The letter was on the table. It had not been there this morning. Someone -- Nan, or Jem, or the afternoon post -- had set it on the ledger while I was occupied with the evening, and I had not seen it until now. The seal was dark red, stamped with an impression I knew. The handwriting on the direction was small and even and unhurried. Mr Phineas Stainforth's hand. My name, the inn's name, written out in full as though there might be some confusion about where I was or who I was. I picked it up. I held it. I turned it over once, feeling the weight of the paper, which was good paper, heavier than anything I used for my own correspondence. The seal was unbroken. I set it back on the table. I had known it was coming. I had known since the last quarter day, since the chandler's prices began to climb, since the small pressures that were not small at all had begun to accumulate with the steady patience of a man who did not need to be dramatic because time and law and custom were already doing his work for him. I had been moving faster ever since. It had not been fast enough. The kitchen was empty. The fire was down. The inn was locked and the day was done and there was no one to hear me and nothing to be done about any of it tonight. "Well," I said. "There it is."

I checked the ledger twice because the first time I did not like the answer. The kitchen was cold, the fire only just beginning to draw. I had built it up ten minutes before with the last of the split ash from the pile Jem kept stacked against the yard wall, and the flames were still working at the bark, not yet settled to the steady burn I needed for the range. My hands were stiff. I turned the page back and ran my finger down the column again. Tallow, flour, malt, coal. Farrier. The glazier's bill for the window in room nine, which a commercial traveller had put his elbow through in September and for which no payment had been received or would be. Candles. Soap. The quarterly rent was due the second week of December. Six weeks. The figures did not change on the second reading. I had not expected them to. I closed the ledger and set it on the shelf above the dresser where it belonged. The taproom fire needed building. I carried the bucket through and knelt on the stone hearth, raking out yesterday's ash and laying the new fire with the efficiency of a woman who has done this particular task perhaps four thousand times and has no opinion about it. The grate was cracked on the left side -- it had been cracked since July, and the ironmonger wanted more for a replacement than I would spend. I set the kindling, lit it, waited until it caught, then closed the draught to half. The room smelled of old ale and cold stone and the particular staleness that a taproom acquires overnight when the windows are shut. I opened the small casement two inches. Not more. The October air had a blade to it. Bar stock next. I counted the casks by feel in the half-dark of the cellar, tapping each one to judge what was left. Two barrels of ordinary, one nearly spent. The strong ale was adequate. The gin was low but would last the week. I made the tally in my head and came back up, pulling the hatch shut behind me and sliding the bolt. The last night's accounts were still on the bar where Nan had left them, written in her small, careful hand. I checked them against the takings in the cashbox. Correct. Nan was always correct. The windows along the front were rimed at their edges with the first real frost of the season. I could feel it through the glass when I passed close. I pulled my sleeves down over my wrists and went to unlock the front door. The yard was quiet. Jem was already there, which I expected, because Jem was always already there. He was at the tack rail outside the stable, straightening a bridle that had been hung crooked. He did not look up. His one hand worked the leather through the buckle with the practised economy of a man who has long since ceased to think about what is missing and thinks only about what is present and whether it is in order. The horses were moving in their stalls -- I could hear them shifting, the heavy shuffle of hooves on straw. The water trough had been filled. I noted this and went back inside. Nan came through the kitchen door a few minutes later. Her hair was pinned but not yet set, the front pieces escaping already as they always did. Her apron was on and tied. She crossed to the bar without a word and took up her position, running a cloth along the counter in a single practised sweep. She had done this before. We all had. Many times. The inn was awake. The London coach was late by thirty minutes, which was not unusual for late October when the road above Hatfield turned to a study in ruts and standing water. I heard it before I saw it -- the particular grinding note of iron-rimmed wheels on gravel that meant a heavy vehicle taking the turn into the yard too fast. I was at the front door by the time the coachman pulled up. Two passengers instead of the expected four. The first was a stout woman in a brown travelling cloak who descended with the rigid dignity of someone who has been rattled about for three hours and intends to hold everyone responsible. The second was a thin, silent man who went directly to the dining room without speaking to anyone, which suited me well. The woman had a trunk. The trunk had a split hinge, and she had a great deal to say about it. I directed Jem, who had appeared at the yard gate without being called. He took the trunk by its good handle and its bottom edge simultaneously, managing both with one arm and the stump braced against his hip, and carried it toward the stair. The woman watched this operation with her mouth open. I did not give her time to form a comment. "Room six is ready. Breakfast is laid in the dining room. If the trunk needs a strap to hold it, we have one." She blinked, closed her mouth, and followed Jem inside. The coachman had not yet gone. He was standing by his horses with the reins looped over the brake handle, and he had the particular expression of a man who has been composing a speech for the last several miles. "The road above Hatfield is out in two places," he said. "Properly out. The ruts will take an axle off if you're not careful." "The parish was meant to grade it last month." "Well, they haven't. I've lost twenty minutes on every run this week, and the mail coach behind me lost a wheel Tuesday." "I'll write to the surveyor again. I can't do more than that." "I know you can't. I'm only telling you so you know, because no one else on this road will bother." "Thank you, Mr Haddon." He touched his hat and climbed back up. I watched the coach pull out of the yard, the horses leaning into the traces with the resignation of animals who understand that the next stop is not theirs to decide. Then the yard was empty. The thin man was eating in the dining room. The stout woman was upstairs. Jem had gone back to the stable. Nan was behind the bar with one eye on the door, the way she always stood when the morning was still unsettled. I saw her watching and said nothing. The chandler arrived at half past ten. Mr Fenton was a square, unhurried man who delivered his tallow candles on the first Tuesday of every month and who had, over the course of the year, developed a habit of adjusting his price upward by small increments, the way a man tests a fence for weakness by leaning on it a little harder each time. He set the box of candles on the kitchen table and named his price. "Fourpence ha'penny the dozen." "It was fourpence last month." "Tallow's gone up." "Tallow has not gone up. I buy soap from the same supplier you buy your tallow from, and his price hasn't moved since August." Fenton looked at me with the steady expression of a man who has been caught out but does not consider this a reason to concede. "There's the carriage of it to consider." "Your carriage hasn't changed. You come this way for the Red Lion and the Woolpack regardless. I'm not an extra journey, Mr Fenton. I'm a stop you make on a route you're already travelling." A pause. He shifted his weight. "I'll go to fourpence farthing," he said. "That's fair." "Fourpence. Same as last month. Or you can take them to the Red Lion, who I happen to know has not paid you for September." The silence was brief. He left the candles. I counted them after he'd gone -- three dozen, as agreed -- and wrote the corrected price in the margin of the delivery note. Fourpence. The door closed behind him. The kitchen was quiet. I stood with the delivery note in my hand and the ledger's figures still precise in my memory. The rent, the chandler's fourpence, the cracked grate, the glazier's bill. All of it held in my name because my name was the one on the lease, and if I married, the name on the lease would change and the fourpence negotiation and every negotiation like it would no longer be mine to conduct. I set the delivery note down and went to check the dining room. He came around the back, as the notice on the gate asked. I saw him through the kitchen window before he knocked -- young, thin, standing on the flagstones outside the door with the particular stillness of a man who is deciding whether he is welcome enough to announce himself. His coat was too large for him and had been someone else's. His boots were coming apart where the sole met the upper, the stitching pulling away from the welt in a manner I recognized because I had seen it on a dozen pairs of boots this year. He was perhaps twenty. He looked at the door. He looked at his boots. He raised his hand and knocked. I opened the door. "Have you eaten?" He met my eyes. There was nothing sly in his face, nothing calculating. Only the careful honesty of a man who knows that a lie will cost him more than the truth. "Not since yesterday, ma'am." "Kitchen, not the taproom. Sit there." He sat at the table where I pointed, and I set before him what the kitchen had: bread from this morning's baking, a bowl of broth from the stockpot that had been on the range since dawn, a heel of cheese. He ate steadily, not fast, with the restraint of someone who has learned that eating too quickly on an empty stomach is its own punishment. I did not sit. I wiped down the work table and checked the stockpot and let him eat. When he finished he stood and brought the bowl to the basin without being asked. "Thank you, ma'am." "Where are you headed?" "North," he said. "My mother's people are near Biggleswade." He went out the way he had come. I cleared the bowl and the plate and the cup and washed them and set them on the rack. The bread knife needed wiping. I wiped it. In the taproom, two men who had been sitting too long over their second pints had found something to disagree about -- the source of the dispute was unclear and did not matter, because the volume was what mattered, and the volume was rising. "Gentlemen. You'll settle this outside or you'll settle it nowhere, but you will not settle it in my taproom." They settled it nowhere. Nan, behind the bar, had not moved. She did not need to. The afternoon opened into the gap between coaches -- that stretch of hours when the inn belonged to no one in particular and the work was the quiet kind. Nan polished glasses. Jem was outside; I could hear the rhythmic scrape of a hoof pick against stone, which meant he was cleaning the yard. I reset the two rooms that had been vacated, stripped the linen, checked the mattresses, laid new fires. In the kitchen I took stock of what remained for the evening meal and what would need to be stretched. The mutton would serve if I made a hash of it with potatoes and onion. There was enough bread. There was always enough bread if I baked twice. The light went early. By four o'clock the windows were dark and the candles were lit -- Mr Fenton's candles, at fourpence the dozen -- and the evening trade came and went with the reliable rhythm of a coaching inn in autumn. A pair of travellers wanting supper and beds. A local man for his evening pint. The curate from St Mary's, who drank one half of mild and spoke to no one and left a ha'penny on the bar as though it were a tithe. I closed the kitchen at nine. I banked the range fire and wiped the table and hung the cloth to dry. The taproom was empty. Nan had tallied the bar and gone up. I checked the front door -- Loss. Bolted. The cellar hatch -- locked. I moved through the ground floor in the dark, my hand finding each door and latch by memory. The taproom fire was down to coals, the grate ticking as the iron contracted. I stood for a moment in the doorway and listened to the sound the building made when it was done being used: the settling of old timber, the small complaints of a structure that has held its shape through many hundreds of nights like this one and will hold it through many more, God willing and the rent paid. I went out the back to check the yard. The stable was dark and quiet. Jem would have settled the horses and gone to his room above the tack store. The air was sharp and still, the frost already forming on the cobbles. I looked up, as I always did on the last circuit, at the second floor. The window at the far end was lit -- a candle or a lamp behind the glass, and the shape of Bridget sitting where she always sat, watching the yard below. I did not look long. Inside, I locked the kitchen door behind me. My hands were red from the cold water and the cleaning, and there was a small cut on my right index finger where the edge of Fenton's delivery note had caught the skin. I had not noticed it at the time. It was the invoice that had cut me. The letter was on the kitchen table. It had not been there at nine o'clock, which meant someone had pushed it under the front door after I had locked it, or it had been there all day and I had failed to see it, which I did not believe. The seal was dark red, pressed with a signet I knew. The handwriting on the direction was careful and measured and familiar. Mr Stainforth's hand. Miss D. Dale, The White Hound. I picked it up. I held it. I turned it over once and felt the weight of it -- a single sheet, perhaps two. I set it down on the table and placed my hand flat on top of it, as though it were something that might move if I did not hold it still. I did not open it. I had known it was coming since the last quarter day. I had known it in the ledger this morning and in the chandler's fourpence and in the cracked grate and the road that the parish would not mend. I had been moving faster ever since, as though speed and competence could outrun what was, in the end, only a man with a seal and a piece of paper and the law behind him. The kitchen was empty. The fire was low. The inn was locked and quiet and mine, for now, for tonight, for six more weeks or however many the letter in my hands intended to leave me. "Right, then," I said to no one.

The ink had dried on the last column before I noticed my fingers were numb. I flexed them over the ledger--eight pounds, fourteen shillings, and a handful of coppers that would not add up to anything resembling comfort no matter how many times I tallied them. Six weeks to quarter day. Six weeks for the roof to hold, the beer to pour, the coaches to keep stopping, and for none of the dozen small disasters that could swallow a margin like ours to bother showing up. I closed the book and pressed my palms flat against the kitchen table, feeling the grain of it, the familiar grooves where my mother's knife had scored the wood ten thousand times. The kitchen fire was still finding its legs. I'd built it up twenty minutes ago, but the October cold had got into the stones overnight, and the chimney drew reluctantly, as though the whole building needed convincing. I pulled my sleeves down over my wrists and went to check the taproom. The taproom hearth was dead ash. I knelt, cleared it, laid the fire fresh--kindling first, then the split logs Jem had stacked by the door yesterday. The match caught on the second strike. I watched the flame lick upward until I was satisfied it would hold, then stood and wiped my hands on my apron. Bar stock next. I counted bottles by touch in the half-dark: the gin was low, the brandy adequate, the ale casks in the cellar sound enough when I'd checked them last night. I pulled the cloth off the bar top and ran my hand along the surface. Sticky near the far end, where someone had set down a glass without a word to Nan. I fetched the rag and the vinegar and cleaned it myself, because it was five o'clock in the morning and there was no one else to do it, and because a sticky bar at opening was the kind of thing my mother would have risen from the dead to correct. Last night's accounts were still on the shelf behind the bar. I pulled them down and checked Nan's figures against the till. She'd been off by tuppence--in our favour, which meant she'd shortchanged someone or miscounted, and either way I'd need to mention it. Later. When the day had enough hours in it for small corrections. I wanted--just for a moment, standing behind the bar in the grey almost-light with the fire beginning to take--I wanted someone to walk in and tell me the roof would last the winter. Not fix it. Not pay for it. Just tell me, with the kind of certainty I could not manufacture for myself, that the next six weeks would not undo everything. Nobody walked in. I set the accounts back on the shelf and went to check the yard. Jem was already there. He had his back to me, working a length of harness through his one hand and the hook he'd rigged to the stable post, checking the stitching by feel. The leather moved through his grip with a rhythm that said he'd been at it awhile. He didn't turn. I didn't call out. The morning required it, and he was doing it, and there was nothing more to say about that. I went back inside. Nan arrived a few minutes later through the kitchen door, pins half-set, apron already tied. She took her place behind the bar without a word and began laying out the glasses for the morning. We had done this before. Many hundreds of times. The London coach was late. This was not unusual--the road north of Hatfield had been worsening since August, and every coachman who stopped at the Hound had an opinion about whose fault it was--but late coaches meant cold passengers, and cold passengers meant complaints, and complaints meant a taproom full of people who wanted everything at once and would judge the whole establishment by the temperature of the first cup of tea. When the coach finally clattered into the yard, it was running short: two passengers instead of four, and both of them in the kind of temper that suggested the missing two had been the agreeable ones. The first was a stout woman in a brown pelisse who descended the step with her mouth already open. The second was a thin man in a greatcoat who said nothing and went straight for the dining room. The woman had a trunk. The trunk had a split hinge. "This has been banging about since Barnet," she announced to the yard at large, "and not a soul has troubled to secure it, and if my things are damaged I shall want to know who is responsible." I directed Jem with a look. He was already moving. "The hinge will be mended within the hour, ma'am," I said. "Your room is the second on the left at the top of the stairs. Nan will bring tea directly. If anything in the trunk has suffered, you may tell me when you've had a chance to look, and we will settle it." The woman blinked. She had been prepared for an argument, and I had not given her one, and this left her with nothing to do but go upstairs. She went. The coachman was waiting by the horses, stamping his feet against the cold. He was a regular--Dawson, who ran the Tuesday and Friday route--and he had the tight jaw of a man who'd been holding his patience for thirty miles. "The stretch above Hatfield," he said, not bothering with a greeting. "It's gone to mud. Axle-deep in places. If it freezes before they mend it, you'll lose the Friday coach altogether--no driver with any sense will risk it." "They won't mend it before spring. You know that." "Aye, I know it. But someone ought to be writing to the turnpike trust." "Someone has. Twice." I handed him the cup of ale Nan had brought to the door. "Take it up with the trustees if you've got an hour to spare. I haven't." He drank, handed back the cup, and climbed up to the box. The coach pulled out. The yard cleared. Jem took the split-hinged trunk into the stable to work on it. Nan was behind the bar again, wiping down the counter with one eye on the door through all of it. I noticed. Said nothing. The chandler came at half past ten, as he always did on the last Tuesday of the month. Mr. Pollard was a thin man with an expression of habitual injury, as though the world had been specifically designed to undervalue tallow. He set the crate of candles on the kitchen table and named his price. "One and four the dozen," he said. "It was one and two last month." "Tallow's gone up." "Tallow has not gone up. Tallow is exactly where it was in September, because I asked Mr. Harker at the chandlery in Welwyn what he was paying, and he told me, and he had no reason to lie about it since I'm not his customer." Pollard's face did something complicated. "There's the cost of carriage." "Your carriage cost has not changed since April. You are on the same route you have always been on, Mr. Pollard, with the same horse and the same cart, and unless the horse has developed expensive tastes, I cannot see where the increase arises." A silence. He looked at the crate. He looked at me. "One and threepence," he said. "One and two. As it was. As it will remain until the price of tallow actually changes, at which point you are welcome to show me the invoice and we will discuss it like reasonable people." He took the one and two. He left the candles. He went. I stood in the kitchen after the door closed and allowed myself one small, private moment of satisfaction. Not because I enjoyed the negotiation--I did not, particularly--but because I had won it, and winning it meant the candles cost what they should cost, and that was one less thing eating at the margin between now and quarter day. I was good at this. It was worth remembering, on mornings when the ledger made my stomach clench, that I was good at this. The thought arrived the way it always did--not as philosophy, but as arithmetic. The chandler's price, the rent, the six weeks in the ledger: every number sat on the same foundation, which was that the lease was in my name and the decisions were mine to make. If I married, the arithmetic would not change, but the name on it would. The lease, the income, the negotiations--all of it would pass to a husband as surely as water runs downhill, and whether he was kind or competent or neither would be entirely beside the point, because the law did not ask. I had refused two proposals for this reason, and I did not regret the refusals. I regretted that they had been necessary. I wiped down the table where Pollard's crate had left a ring of dust and went to check the dining room. The afternoon was half gone when I saw him through the kitchen window. He'd come around the back, as the signs asked. Young--younger than Jem, younger than me. Thin in the way that said weeks, not days. His coat had been a soldier's coat once; the dye had faded to a colour that was not quite brown and not quite anything else. His boots were coming apart at the welt, the leather cracked and separating from the sole on the left foot. He stood outside the kitchen door without knocking. He was looking at the door the way a man looks at a thing he is not certain he is allowed to want. I opened it before he knocked. He startled. Steadied himself. He was perhaps twenty, with a face that had not yet decided what shape it would settle into. "Have you eaten?" I said. A pause. He was calculating whether honesty would help or hurt. "Not since yesterday, ma'am." "Kitchen, not taproom. Sit there." I pointed to the bench by the hearth. He came in and sat where I told him. I cut bread, ladled broth from the pot that had been on since morning, set both in front of him. He ate carefully, not fast--the way a man eats when he is hungry enough to bolt it and disciplined enough not to. He held the spoon the way Jem held tools: precisely, as though it mattered. When he'd finished, he stood. "Thank you, ma'am. I'm obliged." "Where are you headed?" "North," he said. And then, with the faintest suggestion that this was not a plan so much as a direction: "There's family. In Lincolnshire." I nodded. He left by the same door he'd come in. I watched him cross the yard and pass through the gate without looking back, and I stood there a moment longer than I needed to, because he was somebody's son and his boots would not last to Lincolnshire, and there was nothing I could do about either of those things. I cleared his bowl and washed it and put it back on the shelf. The afternoon passed in the ordinary way--rooms to check, the kitchen to manage for the evening meal, a candle to replace in the upstairs corridor where the draught always killed them. Two men in the taproom got loud over something trivial, the way men do when they've been sitting too long and the ale is working faster than their sense. I stepped into the doorway. "Gentlemen. You'll settle it quietly or you'll settle it outside, and outside is cold." They settled it quietly. Nan watched from behind the bar, cloth in hand, and did not need to move. The rest of the afternoon was work. Not the kind that makes a story--the kind that makes an inn. Nan at the bar. Jem in the yard. Me between the kitchen and the accounts and the dozen tasks that filled the space between one coach and the next. No incidents. No dramas. Only the ordinary grinding machinery of a day that earned just enough to justify the next one. Evening came on slowly, the way it does in late October--not a darkness so much as a thickening, the light draining out of the yard by degrees until the windows gave back reflections instead of views. The last guests were settled. The last fire checked. I tallied the bar, closed down the kitchen, and began the circuit I had made every night for two years, and my mother had made every night for fourteen years before that. Taproom fire down to coals. Good. Cellar hatch locked. The stable yard was quiet--Jem would have settled the horses. I crossed the yard in the dark, feeling the cold air against the places where my sleeves had ridden up. My hands were raw, reddened from cold water and the vinegar I'd used on the bar at dawn. There was a small cut on my index finger, right along the pad, where the edge of Pollard's delivery invoice had caught me when I'd folded it. I had not noticed it at the time. I noticed it now, in the particular way you notice small injuries only when the day has stopped requiring you to ignore them. The second floor. A lit shape at Bridget's window--the glow of a candle and the outline of her sitting where she always sat, watching the yard. I did not look long. I bolted the front door. I set the bar. I checked the kitchen door and turned the key. The inn was closed, and I was alone in it, and for one breath I let myself know it--the specific quality of an empty kitchen at the end of a day that had asked everything and offered nothing in return except the right to do it again tomorrow. The letter was on the kitchen table. It had not been there at supper. Someone--Nan, probably, collecting the last of the post from the box by the front door--had placed it squarely in the centre of the table where I would find it. The seal was Stainforth's: a neat impression in dark red wax, the same device I had seen on every piece of correspondence from his office since my mother's death. His handwriting on the direction, precise and unhurried. I picked it up. Turned it over once. Broke the seal. The letter was short. Stainforth did not waste words any more than I did, though his economy had a different quality--the compression of a man who expects to be obeyed and sees no reason to be persuasive about it. He had a buyer for the property. A gentleman of means--his nephew, though he did not say so here--who wished to acquire the freehold and the coaching licence and develop the premises into a more suitable establishment. The current lease would be reviewed at quarter day. He invited me to meet with him and his solicitor at his offices in Stevenage on the fourteenth of November to discuss terms for the surrender of the lease. He expressed his confidence that I would see the wisdom of a timely resolution. He remained my obedient servant. I set the letter down on the table. The cut on my finger was stinging where I'd gripped the paper. Six weeks. He'd given me exactly the time I already knew I had, which meant he knew the numbers as well as I did, which meant this had been coming longer than the letter suggested. The chandler's rising prices. The pressure on the suppliers. The quiet, patient work of a man who held the structural advantage and had only ever needed to wait. I looked at the letter on the table. I looked at the kitchen--my kitchen, my mother's kitchen, my grandmother's kitchen--and I said, to no one, to the empty room, to the walls that had heard three generations of women refuse to be moved: "You'll need a better offer than that, Mr. Stainforth."

I checked the ledger twice because the first time the numbers had refused to make sense, and the second time they made too much. Six weeks. That was what remained before the quarter's rent came due, and between now and then the inn would need to earn what it had not earned in the previous eight, which was enough. I closed the book and pressed my palms flat against the kitchen table, feeling the grain of the oak through the roughness of my skin. The fire I had built twenty minutes ago was only now beginning to push back against the cold. October had come in mean this year, a thin bitter wind from the north that found every gap in the plaster and every ill-fitted window frame. I could feel it at my ankles. I stood and moved to the taproom. The hearth there was dead, last night's ashes a grey crust that needed clearing before anything could be laid. I knelt and raked them out, set the kindling, struck the flint. The tinder caught on the second strike. I fed it carefully, because kindling cost money and patience was free. The bar stock I checked by touch as much as sight: the level of each cask told by the pitch of the knock, the bottles counted by running my fingers along the shelf. Two bottles of the good brandy left. Seven of the ordinary claret, which would not last the week if the coaches ran full. I noted it, moved on. Last night's accounts were still on the slip of paper I had left tucked inside the pewter tankard by the till. I unfolded it, read the figures, and added them to the running column in my head. The sum was adequate. Adequate was not enough, but it was what I had. The windows at the front of the taproom were rimed at their edges, a delicate frost that would melt by midmorning and leave the glass streaked. I pulled my sleeves down over my wrists and went to check the cellar hatch. Everything accounted for. Nothing wasted. That was the way my mother had run this place, and her mother before her, and it was the way I ran it now, not because I had inherited some philosophy of thrift but because there was no other way to run an inn on the Great North Road with the margins we had. You checked, you counted, you adjusted. You did it again tomorrow. The hinge on the cellar hatch was stiffening. I would need tallow for it, or lard if the tallow was dear. --- When I stepped into the yard, Jem was already there. He had a bridle over his one arm and was working the buckle with his hand and the stump of the other, his back to me, his attention wholly on the task. The tack was straight on the rail. The water trough had been filled. He did not look up. I went back inside. A few minutes later Nan came through the kitchen door with her pins half-set and her apron already tied. She crossed to the bar and took her place behind it, running her cloth once along the surface in a motion so habitual it was barely conscious. She did not speak. I did not speak. We had done this before. We had done it hundreds of times, and the doing was the language. --- The London coach was late by forty minutes, which was not unusual for late October when the roads north of Hatfield turned to soup after a night's rain. What was unusual was the load: two passengers where four had been expected, and neither of them pleased about anything. The first was a stout woman in a brown travelling cloak who descended from the coach already talking. Her trunk had a split hinge and she wished everyone within earshot to understand that this was not her fault, that the coach company was responsible, that she had packed carefully and the hinge had been sound when she left London. I directed Jem with a look. He appeared at the woman's elbow before she had finished her second sentence, took the trunk by its good handle, and bore it toward the door with the unhurried competence of a man who had carried heavier things under worse conditions. The woman stopped talking, which was the desired effect. The second passenger went upstairs without comment. I turned to the coachman, who had climbed down and was stamping mud from his boots with the grim energy of a man who had been doing this since Barnet. "The road above Hatfield," he said. "It's gone, Mrs. Dale. Half a mile past the toll, there's a rut deep enough to break an axle. Someone's put a branch across it but that won't hold past Tuesday." "I'll send word to the turnpike trust." "You sent word last month." "Then I'll send it again, and louder." I handed him a mug of ale that Nan had drawn without being asked. "You've twenty minutes if you want food. The ordinary's not ready but there's bread and cold beef." He took the ale and the twenty minutes and said nothing more about the road, because there was nothing more to say. The road was the turnpike trust's business, and the turnpike trust answered to no one with any urgency, and we both knew it. --- The coach went. The passengers settled into their rooms or drifted toward the dining room. Jem took the split-hinged trunk somewhere it could be mended. The yard cleared, and the particular silence of a coaching inn between arrivals settled over the place like dust. Nan was wiping down the bar with one eye on the door. I noted it. I said nothing. --- Mr. Fenton, the chandler, arrived at half past eleven with his cart and his delivery note and his expression of mild apology, which I had learned to recognize as the face he wore when he intended to charge me more. "Morning, Mrs. Dale." "Miss Dale. Morning, Mr. Fenton." He set the crate of candles on the kitchen table and placed the delivery note beside it. I looked at the figure. Tallow candles, four dozen, at a price per dozen that was threepence higher than last month. "You've adjusted again," I said. "Tallow's gone up. The renderers in Hitchin--" "The renderers in Hitchin are charging you what they charged you in August. I know because Mr. Hadley at the Red Lion told me his price hasn't moved, and you supply us both from the same stock." Fenton's expression shifted from mild apology to something more guarded. He was not a dishonest man, precisely. He was a man who tested his margins wherever he thought the testing would go unnoticed. "There's transport to consider," he said. "The roads being what they are." "The roads are what they were last month, and the month before that. The Red Lion is a mile farther from your shop than I am." I let that sit for a moment. "I'll take them at last month's price, Mr. Fenton. Or you can load them back on your cart and sell them to someone who doesn't check." He looked at me. I looked at him. It was not a long negotiation. He knew his margins and I knew his margins, and the distance between those two facts was where the price was set. "Last month's price," he said. "Thank you." He left. The candles stayed. I took up the delivery note and wrote the corrected figure in the margin, small and precise, and set it on the stack with the others. --- I stood for a moment in the kitchen with the delivery note still in my hand. The ledger on the table. The rent. The chandler's price, and every other price that had crept upward since the summer while the coaches ran shorter and the roads worsened. All of it weighed against the one fact I had calculated so many times it had worn a groove in my thinking: if I married, the lease would pass to my husband, and the inn would not be mine to negotiate for. I set the note down and went to check the dining room. --- I saw him through the kitchen window before he knocked. Young--twenty, perhaps, or not yet twenty. Thin in the way of men who have been thin for a long time, not the leanness of youth but the drawn look of insufficient food over months. His coat was army issue, or had been. His boots were coming apart at the welt, the sole separating from the upper on the left foot so that I could see a stripe of grey stocking beneath. He stood outside the kitchen door without knocking. He was looking at the door, then at the yard, then at the door again, and I understood the calculation he was making: whether he was welcome enough to knock. Whether this was the kind of place that would answer. I opened the door before he decided. He straightened. Up close he was younger than I had thought. The bones of his face were sharp under the skin. "Have you eaten?" I said. He considered lying. I could see the consideration pass across his face, the brief flicker of pride that wanted to say he was managing. Then it passed. "Not since yesterday, ma'am. Morning." "Kitchen," I said. "Not the taproom." He understood. He came in and sat where I pointed, at the far end of the table, and I set before him what the kitchen had: bread, the heel of yesterday's loaf, and broth from the stockpot that had been simmering since dawn. He ate carefully, not fast, with the particular restraint of a man who knew that eating too quickly on an empty stomach would make him ill. I did not watch him eat. I went about the kitchen work and let him be. When he had finished he stood and brought the bowl to the side of the basin. "Thank you, ma'am." "Where are you headed?" He paused. "North," he said. "There's a cousin in Nottingham. Might be work." I nodded. He went out through the kitchen door and crossed the yard and turned onto the road, and I cleared the bowl and wiped the table where he had sat and moved to the next thing. --- In the taproom, two men who had been sitting too long over too little ale had found something to disagree about. I could hear the pitch of it rising as I came through the doorway--not yet shouting, but heading there, the particular note of boredom tipping into belligerence that I had learned to identify at twenty paces. "Gentlemen," I said. "You'll settle it outside or you'll settle it nowhere, but you will not settle it in my taproom." They looked at me. They settled it nowhere. Nan, behind the bar, had not moved. She did not need to. --- The afternoon opened into the long gap between coaches. Nan at the bar, polishing glasses that were already clean because her hands needed occupation. Jem outside, doing whatever Jem did in the hours when the yard was empty--mending harness, I expected, or checking the stable roof where the tiles had slipped after the last wind. I worked through the accounts for the week, reset the rooms that needed resetting, checked the kitchen stores against what the evening meal would require. Ordinary work. The ordinary grind of a coaching inn on a cold afternoon in October, when the road was quiet and the light was already failing by four o'clock. No incidents. No crises. The work, and nothing but the work, and the particular steadiness that came from doing it well and knowing it was not enough. --- By evening the last guests were settled, the last fire checked, the bar tallied and the till locked. I moved through the inn's closing routine alone, as I had done every night since my mother died and most nights before that. Taproom fire down to coals, the guard set before it. Cellar hatch locked, the bolt thrown and tested with a pull. Kitchen closed down: the stockpot moved off the heat, the knives cleaned and laid in their cloth, the morning's bread dough set to prove in the coolest corner where it would rise slowly through the night. I stepped into the yard. The stable was quiet. Jem would have settled the horses an hour ago. The cold had deepened since sundown and the sky was clear, stars sharp overhead in a way that meant frost by morning. On the second floor, a lit shape at Bridget's window. The faint glow of a candle behind the glass, and the outline of her sitting where she always sat, watching the yard below. I did not look long. I came back inside and bolted the kitchen door. My hands, when I looked at them in the lamplight, were reddened from the cold water and the cleaning, the skin rough across the knuckles. There was a small cut on my index finger, a thin line where the edge of Fenton's delivery note had caught me when I turned it over. I had not noticed it at the time. It was the invoice that had cut me. That seemed about right. I set the lamp on the table and saw the letter. It was on the far end, where the morning's post would have been left--by Nan, probably, or Jem, whoever had taken it from the carrier. I had not noticed it earlier, or I had noticed it and put off the noticing, which amounted to the same thing. The seal was dark red, pressed deep. I knew the seal. I knew the handwriting on the direction: careful, slanted slightly to the left, the letters formed with the precision of a man who believed that penmanship reflected character. Mr. Phineas Stainforth, who owned the land beneath my feet and the walls around me and the roof above my head, and who had been waiting, with the patience of a man who held the structural advantage, for exactly this. I picked the letter up. I turned it over once. The wax was smooth under my thumb. I did not open it. I set it down on the table, beside the ledger, beside the delivery notes, beside the accounts that would not add up to enough no matter how many times I ran them. I knew what it was about. I had known it was coming since the last quarter, since before the last quarter, since the morning I had opened the ledger and seen the numbers refuse to make sense. I had been moving faster ever since, as though speed could outrun the arithmetic. The kitchen was empty. The inn was quiet. The fire had burned down to a faint orange glow that barely reached the table. "Right, then," I said. No one heard it. I turned down the lamp and went upstairs.

The ledger did not balance, and I had known it would not balance before I opened it. I sat at the kitchen table with the book flat under both hands, the column of figures marching down the right side of the page in my own careful ink. Forty-seven days until the next quarter's rent came due. Forty-seven days and roughly six pounds short, assuming no disaster between now and then, which was assuming a great deal. I closed the ledger and set my palm flat on its cover, as though the numbers might crawl out and cause further trouble if left unsupervised. The kitchen fire needed building up. I'd banked it the night before and it had gone sulky, as kitchen fires will when they sense you need them. I knelt on the stone floor and worked the bellows until the coals caught, then fed in kindling and two split logs from the basket Jem had filled yesterday. The wood was not as dry as I'd have liked. Nothing was as dry as I'd have liked. October had been wet and mean, and the damp had got into everything -- the wood, the walls, the hinges on the cellar hatch, my knees when I knelt too long. The inn was quiet in the way it only ever was at this hour, before the day arrived to fill it. I could hear the building itself: the tick of cooling plaster, the faint complaint of the sign bracket when the wind caught it, the particular creak of the second stair that I had been meaning to have seen to since July. I knew every sound this place made. I knew which door stuck in wet weather and which window let in a draught no matter how firmly you latched it. I knew the precise rattle of a full coach pulling into the yard versus a half-empty one, and I could tell by the sound of boots on the taproom floor whether a man intended to pay his bill or argue about it. What I wanted -- just once, just for one morning -- was to open that ledger and find a number I had not expected. A surplus. A margin wide enough that I might stop counting days. But wanting was a poor use of a Tuesday, so I got up off my knees and went to work. The taproom fire next. I swept the grate, laid the fire, lit it. The room smelled of last night's ale and tallow smoke, and the windows were rimed with cold at their edges, a thin lace of frost that would melt by midmorning and leave the glass streaked. I wiped down the bar, checked the casks -- the brown ale was running low, the small beer adequate, the cider untouched because nobody wanted cider in late October -- and tallied the previous night's take against what Nan had left in the cashbox. Nan's figures were exact. They were always exact. I pulled my sleeves down over my wrists against the chill and moved on. Bar stock. Candle supply. The hinge on the dining room door that had begun to grind. I noted each item in the running list I kept in my head, the one that never closed, the one that woke me sometimes at three in the morning with an addition I had not asked for. Through the back window I could see Jem in the stable yard, a dark shape moving between the stalls. He was straightening tack on the hooks outside the nearest box -- bridles hung evenly, reins looped and secured, everything in its place. He had not been asked. The morning required it, and Jem did what the morning required, and that was the whole of our understanding. I was back in the kitchen when Nan came through the door. Pins half-set in her hair, apron already tied, eyes not fully open but her hands already reaching for the bar cloth she kept on the hook by the passage. She crossed through the kitchen without a word and took her place behind the bar. I heard the cloth hit the wood, the small efficient sounds of a woman settling into the shape of her day. We had done this before. Hundreds of times. The machinery of the morning did not require conversation, only presence, and they were present, and so was I, and the White Hound stirred itself awake around us like a creature remembering what its limbs were for. The London coach was late. This was not unusual -- the London coach was late more often than it was punctual, owing to the road, the weather, the horses, and whatever other excuse the driver had assembled during the final miles. What was unusual was that when it finally clattered into the yard, steam rising from the team and the coachman's face set in a particular expression I recognized as a man composing a grievance, it carried only two passengers instead of the expected four. Two passengers and one trunk with a split hinge, which the owner -- a woman of perhaps fifty in a travelling cloak that had seen considerably better decades -- was already loudly lamenting before the coach had fully stopped. I directed Jem to the trunk with a look. He appeared at the coach door without being called, took the damaged luggage under his one good arm, and carried it inside as though split hinges were a personal affront he intended to remedy. The woman watched him go with the startled silence of a person whose complaint has been handled before she finished making it. The second passenger went to the dining room without fuss. I turned to the coachman, who had climbed down and was standing in the yard with his arms folded and his hat pushed back, which meant he wanted a word and intended to have one whether I had time for it or not. "Road's gone to pieces north of Hatfield," he said. "Ruts deep enough to lose a wheel in. Someone's got to speak to the turnpike trust." "Someone has. Twice. They've promised gravel before the frost sets." "Gravel won't answer. It wants proper drainage. I near lost a horse in that stretch last Thursday." "Then you'll want to take the Welwyn fork until it's mended. It adds twenty minutes and saves you the axle." He opened his mouth, reconsidered, and closed it. "Twenty minutes." "At most. And you'll still make Baldock by four if you don't dawdle here." He gave me the look men give when they have been managed by a woman and are deciding whether to mind. He decided not to. He touched his hat and went to see about the horses, and I went back inside, because the morning was not going to run itself and I had already given him his two minutes. The yard cleared. The passengers settled -- the woman with the trunk had been installed in room six, where Jem had already done something competent to the hinge, and the quiet gentleman was eating bread and cheese in the dining room as though bread and cheese were all he had ever wanted from the world. Nan stood behind the bar with the cloth in her hand and one eye on the door to the passage, watching the rhythm of the morning resume. I saw her watching. I said nothing. There was nothing to say. We both knew what we were looking at: a coach that should have been full arriving half-empty, and what that meant for the week's take. Mr. Fenton, the chandler, arrived at half past ten with his cart and his monthly delivery and his particular way of smiling that meant he was about to make my morning worse. "Miss Dale." He set the crate of tallow candles on the kitchen table with the careful placement of a man presenting a gift. "Fourteen dozen, as agreed." "At what price?" "Tuppence ha'penny the dozen." I looked at the candles. I looked at Mr. Fenton. He had a broad, well-fed face and the permanently hopeful expression of a man who believed that if he said a number with enough confidence, it would become true. "They were tuppence the dozen in March, Mr. Fenton." "Tallow's gone up." "Tallow's gone up a farthing the pound since March. That does not account for a ha'penny on the dozen." "There's cartage to consider." "Your cartage has not changed. You pass this door on your way to the Bell at Welwyn every month regardless. I am not additional cartage. I am a stop you would make anyway." The smile held, but it thinned at the edges. "Miss Dale, I have other customers who'd be glad of the supply." "You do. And they pay tuppence farthing, which is what Mrs. Pratt at the Bell told me last week when I asked, because I make it my business to ask." I folded my arms. "Tuppence farthing, Mr. Fenton. The same as last month and the month before. Or you may load them back on your cart and explain to your other customers why you've arrived late and short." A silence. Mr. Fenton looked at his candles. He looked at me. He performed a small, private calculation behind his eyes that I could follow as clearly as if he'd written it on the wall. "Tuppence farthing," he said. "Thank you. Nan will see you out." He took his coin and left. The candles stayed. I stood in the kitchen with the crate on the table and allowed myself one moment -- just one -- of the quiet, sharp satisfaction that comes from knowing exactly what a thing costs and refusing to pay more. It was a small victory. Most of mine were. But the inn ran on small victories, and I had learned to collect them the way other women collected compliments. The thought arrived without invitation, as it always did, somewhere between the chandler's departure and the next task. The ledger, the rent, the candle price -- all of it balanced against the single fact that governed every calculation I made: if I married, none of this would be mine to fight for. The lease, the accounts, the right to tell Mr. Fenton what his candles were worth -- all of it would pass to a husband the moment I spoke my vows. I had refused two proposals already, for exactly this reason. The refusals had not been difficult. The proposals had not tempted me. What was difficult was the thing I did not let myself examine too closely afterward -- the brief, unwelcome awareness that I had chosen the inn over the possibility of being chosen, and that the inn would never, in return, choose me back. I wiped down the table where the crate had been and went to see about the midday bread. I noticed him through the kitchen window before he knocked. He had come around the back, as the signs asked. Young -- twenty at most, possibly less. Thin in the way that means weeks, not constitution. His coat had been military once, though whatever regiment it belonged to had been worn past recognition. His boots were the worst of it: the leather split along the welt of the left one, the sole of the right separating at the toe. He stood outside the kitchen door with his hand half-raised and did not knock. He was looking at the door the way a man looks at something he is not certain he is permitted to want. I opened it before he had decided. He startled. Up close he was even younger than I'd thought -- fair-haired, hollow-cheeked, with a stillness about the eyes that I had seen before in men who had come back from the war and not entirely arrived. "Have you eaten today?" He considered lying. I watched him consider it. "Not since yesterday, ma'am." "Kitchen, not taproom. Sit there." He sat. I put bread in front of him, and broth from the pot that had been on since morning, and he ate with the steady, methodical attention of someone who has learned not to eat too fast after going without. He held the spoon carefully, almost formally, as though someone had once taught him table manners and the lesson had survived everything else. When he had finished, he stood and thanked me with a brevity that was not rudeness but economy -- he did not have words to spare any more than he had food. "Where are you headed?" "North, ma'am. There's work in the mills, I'm told." I did not say what I thought about the mills, or the likelihood of work, or the roads between here and there in boots that would not last another week. I said, "The Great North Road forks past Baldock. Take the left at the post -- it's shorter and the turnpike's free for foot traffic." He nodded once, put on his cap, and went. I stood in the kitchen after the door closed and looked at the bowl he had left on the table, wiped nearly clean. He had held the spoon the way a child holds a pencil when first learning to write -- with the whole of his concentration, as though the act itself were something to be got right. He was somebody's son. Somewhere, a woman was waiting for him, or had stopped waiting, and either way the bread and broth I'd given him would not be enough, and I knew it, and I gave it anyway, and that was all I could do. I washed the bowl and put it back on the shelf. The afternoon passed in the ordinary way of afternoons at the White Hound -- which is to say, in work. The coach had gone, the passengers with it or settled into their rooms, and the inn entered the long, quiet stretch between the morning rush and the evening trade. Nan polished glasses behind the bar. Jem was in the yard, doing something to a harness that involved a great deal of leather and very little conversation. I did accounts at the kitchen table, reset the rooms that needed resetting, checked the stores for the evening meal, and mended a tear in the curtain of room four that had been bothering me for three days. Two men in the taproom got loud over something -- I heard the pitch of it change from argument to performance, the particular note that means an audience is being played to rather than a point being made. I went in. "Gentlemen. You'll settle this outside or you'll settle it quietly, but you will not settle it in my taproom." They settled it quietly. Nan, behind the bar, caught my eye and did not need to say anything. I went back to the kitchen. The light was going by five.
