I heard the soft, rhythmic sound of it from the kitchen -- back and forth, back and forth -- and felt the inn settle into its working shape around me, the way a body settles when it draws its first proper breath of the day. The London coach was late. This was not unusual -- the London coach was late with the reliable frequency of a church bell rung by a drunk sexton -- but it was also running short. Two passengers instead of the expected four, and both of them had opinions. The first was a stout woman in a travelling cloak who had a trunk with a split hinge and a conviction that this was somehow my fault. I directed her to the dining room, directed Jem to the trunk, and directed my attention to the coachman, who had climbed down from the box with the particular heaviness of a man who has been arguing with the road for thirty miles. "North road's gone to pieces above Hatfield," he said, stamping mud from his boots on my clean step. "Ruts deep enough to lose a wheel. Someone needs to speak to the turnpike trust." "Someone has. Twice this month." "And?" "And the trust has a meeting in November, at which point the road will be frozen and the question academic. You'll want to take the lower fork past the mill if it's rained. The ruts are shallower." He considered this. "The lower fork adds twenty minutes." "The lower fork adds twenty minutes. A broken axle adds two days. I can offer you coffee before you go, but I cannot offer you a better answer than that." He took the coffee. The yard cleared. The stout woman was settled in the dining room with toast and the particular silence of a person who has run out of complaints and is recalibrating. Jem had the trunk and was carrying it upstairs with an ease that made the split hinge look like a personal failing on the trunk's part rather than a structural one. Nan stood behind the bar with her cloth folded over one arm, watching the door the way she always watched it -- not anxious, not expectant, just ready. I noted it. Said nothing. Mr Fenton, the chandler, arrived at half past ten with his cart and his monthly delivery and his newest price. "Tuppence ha'penny the dozen," he said, setting the first crate of tallow candles on the kitchen table as though he were presenting me with a gift. I did not pick up a candle. I did not need to. I had been buying Mr Fenton's candles for three years, and they had not improved. "They were tuppence last month, Mr Fenton." "Tallow's gone up. The renderers in Barnet have raised their price, and I'm obliged to pass the cost along." "The halfpenny is not your costs, Mr Fenton. It is your hope that I am too busy to check. The renderers in Barnet raised their price in June. You passed the cost along in July. And again in September. That is a great deal of passing for a single increase." He shifted his weight. "There are other considerations. Transport. Labour." "Your transport is the same cart and the same horse you have driven for six years, and your labour is yourself. Unless you have recently hired an assistant, in which case I congratulate you, but I do not intend to pay his wages." Mr Fenton's mouth opened, then closed. He was not a dishonest man. He was an optimistic one, which in a chandler amounted to much the same thing. "Tuppence farthing," he said. "Tuppence. Which is what I paid last month and what every inn between here and Baldock pays, as you know perfectly well, because Mrs Hewitt at the George told me her price when I saw her at the Stevenage market." A silence. Mr Fenton looked at his candles. His candles did not look back. "Tuppence," he said. "Thank you, Mr Fenton. I'll take four dozen." He unloaded the candles, took his money, and left without the pleasantries that sometimes padded the end of our transactions. I did not mind. The door closed behind him, and I allowed myself one small, private moment of satisfaction -- the kind that comes from knowing exactly what a thing costs and refusing to pay a farthing more. It was not a grand victory. But the inn was built on small ones, and I had learned to take them where I found them. I carried the candles to the storeroom and came back to the kitchen, where the ledger sat on the table like a conscience. Forty-three pounds. Six weeks. Every tuppence mattered, and every tuppence I saved on candles was a tuppence I did not have to find elsewhere -- and all of it added against the fact that sat beneath every calculation I made, quiet and immovable as bedrock. If I married, the lease would pass to my husband. The inn, the income, the business my grandmother had built and my mother had held -- all of it would belong to a man the moment I spoke two words in a church. I had refused two proposals on the strength of that arithmetic, and I did not regret either one. I wiped the table, hung the cloth, and went to check on the dining room. I saw him through the kitchen window before he knocked. Young -- twenty, perhaps, or younger, with the thin, unfinished look of a boy who had stopped growing when the food ran out. His coat was too large for him, army-issue by the cut, and his boots were coming apart at the welt, the leather separating from the sole in a way that meant he had been walking a long time on wet roads. He stood outside the kitchen door with his hand raised but not yet touching the wood, and I recognised the pause. He was not deciding whether to knock. He was deciding whether he was welcome enough to try. I opened the door before he could finish the calculation. He looked at me with the startled expression of a man caught between hope and the expectation of refusal. Up close, he was thinner than I'd thought. His cheekbones stood out sharp beneath skin that had gone sallow, and his eyes had that flat, careful quality I had learned to recognise in the men who came through -- not empty, exactly, but guarded against something I could not see. "Have you eaten?" I said. He hesitated. "Not recently, ma'am." "Kitchen, not taproom. Sit there." I pointed to the bench by the range. "I'll bring you something." He sat. He did not argue or thank me or attempt to explain himself, which told me he had done this before and knew the terms. I cut bread from the loaf, ladled broth from the pot that had been simmering since morning, and set both in front of him. He ate quickly, with his head down, one arm curled around the bowl as though someone might take it from him. He held the spoon with a steadiness that cost him something. I could see the effort in his wrist. I busied myself at the range and did not watch him. When the bowl was empty, he set down the spoon and sat very still. "Thank you, ma'am." "Where are you headed?" He named a direction -- north, toward the midlands -- in a voice that suggested the destination mattered less than the motion. I nodded. He stood, touched his forehead, and went out through the kitchen door into the yard. I watched him cross to the road and turn north, his broken boots dark against the pale mud, and I stood there longer than I needed to. He was somebody's son. He held the spoon the way a child holds it, with his whole fist, as though he had forgotten there was any other way. I turned back to the kitchen and wiped the place where he had sat. The afternoon brought the usual small frictions. Two men in the taproom had been sitting too long over their ale and had found something to disagree about -- the price of barley, or the state of the turnpike, or whatever thin excuse men used when what they really wanted was to raise their voices at someone. "Gentlemen." I did not raise mine. "If you wish to continue this conversation, you may do so in the yard. If you wish to continue drinking, you will do so quietly." They continued drinking. Nan, behind the bar, caught my eye and went back to her work. The hours between the afternoon coach and the evening trade were the inn's dead time -- the lull when the building exhaled and the work became invisible. Nan polished glasses. Jem was outside, doing whatever Jem did when the yard was empty, which generally involved improving something no one had asked him to improve. I reset the two upstairs rooms that had been used, checked the linen, reviewed the kitchen stores for the evening meal, and wrote out the next day's orders in the small, tight hand my mother had taught me, because paper cost money and margins were not for wasting. The work was ordinary. It was also everything. It was the thing that kept the doors open and the fires lit and the coaches stopping, and if it was not enough -- if it was never quite enough -- it was mine, and I did it well, and on most days that was sufficient. Evening came down early, the way it did in late October, the light draining from the sky as though someone had pulled a stopper. The last guests were settled by eight. I tallied the bar with Nan, checked the kitchen stores against tomorrow's needs, and sent her home. The dining room was cleared, the fires checked, the cellar hatch locked. I made my final circuit alone. Taproom fire down to coals, the grate ticking as the metal cooled. Front door bolted. The stable yard was quiet, and through the dark I could see the faint shape of the stable block where Jem would have settled the horses for the night. I looked up. On the second floor, a lit shape at Bridget's window -- her silhouette behind the glass, watching the yard as she always watched it, steady and unsleeping. I did not look long. Back in the kitchen, I banked the range and washed my hands in the basin by the door. The cold water stung, and I looked down at them -- reddened across the knuckles, rough at the fingertips, and on my right index finger a thin cut from the edge of Mr Fenton's delivery note, which I had not noticed until now. A small injury. The kind that accumulated without announcement and healed without attention and marked the difference between a woman who worked and a woman who did not. I dried my hands on the cloth and stood in the kitchen doorway. The inn was quiet in the way of a place that has been used hard and is now resting -- not peaceful, exactly, but spent. The bolt was set. The doors were locked. And I was alone, which was not the same thing as lonely except on the nights when it was, and tonight it was, and I let myself know it for the space of a single breath before I turned back to the table. The letter was where Nan had left the afternoon post, half-hidden beneath a bill from the farrier. I had seen it earlier and set it aside, because I knew the handwriting on the direction and I knew the seal, and there are some things you do not open until the day's work is done and your hands are steady. Stainforth's seal. Stainforth's hand. I picked the letter up and turned it over once. The paper was heavy, cream-coloured, the kind that announced its cost before you read a word. I broke the seal. The letter was brief. Mr Stainforth wrote the way he spoke -- measured, courteous, and perfectly clear. He had received an offer for the White Hound property from a Mr Josiah Campion of London, who wished to acquire the lease and convert the premises into a posting house of a more modern character. Mr Stainforth considered the offer a sound one. He was prepared to discuss terms with me, as the current leaseholder, as a matter of courtesy, but he wished me to understand that the decision was ultimately his to make. He proposed a meeting at his house on the fourteenth of November. If I did not attend, he would take my absence as consent to proceed. Six weeks. The same six weeks I had counted in the ledger this morning, the same margin I had been calculating and defending and scraping together tuppence by tuppence -- and none of it mattered, because Stainforth was not raising the rent. He was taking the ground out from under it. I set the letter down on the kitchen table. I looked at it for a long moment, at the careful handwriting and the courteous phrasing and the word *consent*, which was doing a great deal of work in that final sentence and doing none of it honestly. "The fourteenth, then," I said to the empty room. "I'll be there early."

The letter sat on the kitchen table where I had left it at four in the morning, and the kitchen was cold because I had not yet laid the fire. Stainforth's hand was a copperplate so even it might have been ruled. He wrote the way men wrote who had never had cause to hurry. He wished to discuss the terms. He hoped the hour of his call would not inconvenience me. He remained, with every assurance of regard, my most obliged servant. I knew what the letter meant in pounds and shillings before I had finished reading it the first time. The next quarter's rent was due in six weeks. I had, after the malt bill and the coal and Nan's wages and the farrier, eleven pounds four. The rent was twenty-three. Coaches were lighter this autumn because the harvest had failed and the half-pay officers were drinking small beer at home, and there was no honest arithmetic by which eleven became twenty-three in six weeks unless I emptied the cellar, which I would not, or sold the grey mare, which I would not yet. Stainforth wished to discuss the terms. I had seen his hand on a writ once and recognised the slope of the H. He wrote the way he stood. Pleasantly. The cut on my index finger had opened again in the night and stuck to the linen. I peeled it loose without looking at it and put the kettle on the hook. The ledger was where I had set it down. I did not open it. I knew the numbers, and a number does not change because a woman stares at it. I laid the fire. I wiped the table. I put the letter inside the lid of the tea caddy because I did not want Nan to read it before I had decided what to tell her, and then I went into the taproom and swept a floor that had been swept the night before. By eight I had the bar wiped, the brass on the ale taps polished, the cellar checked, the windows in the passage opened and shut again because the air carried rain. Nan came down with her hair half-pinned and her sleeves up and looked at me once, and looked at the polished brass, and did not say anything. She went to the back to fetch the bread. I heard the carriage on the road before it turned in. There is a sound a coach makes when the springs are good and the horses are matched, a sound entirely unlike the post coaches and the carrier carts. It came up the lane with the unhurried weight of a man who knew the door would open whether he knocked or not. He stepped down himself rather than wait for the boy. His coat was a heavy double-breasted wool of the colour they call iron, cut close at the waist, and the smell of it as he came through the door was the clean, oily smell of wool that has never been mended. His gloves were York tan. He drew them off finger by finger and folded them in his hand. "Miss Dale. Forgive the early hour. I was anxious not to keep you from the day." "Mr Stainforth." "I trust your aunt is well." "Well enough, sir. Thank you." "And the road, I see, has spared you the worst of last night's weather. We had it badly at Chitterley. Trees down at the lower lodge." "I'm sorry to hear it." He smiled. It was a courteous smile that asked nothing of his eyes. "You will think me presumptuous, Miss Dale, but I so rarely have the chance to see the place properly. Might I beg a turn through it before we sit down? I am a bad correspondent at desks. I think better when I have walked a building." I stepped back from the doorway and let him in. There was nothing else to do, and we both knew it. He walked the taproom slowly. He set his gloves on the bar and ran one finger along the brass tap of the porter cask, then along the rail. He looked up at the beams. He looked down at the floor where the boards had darkened in front of the fire. He did not say what he was looking for. He did not need to. I had stood at that bar for sixteen years, and I knew the shape of an inventory when I saw one. "How many rooms let in an average week, Miss Dale?" "Sixteen, sir. Twenty in the season." "And the coaches?" "Four north and four south daily. Five on the Royal Mail nights." "You service the Highflyer?" "And the Wellington." "Mr Pickford is content?" "Mr Pickford is content." He nodded once, as if filing each of these into a column whose total he would not show me. He moved on. I followed two paces behind, the way Patience had taught me to follow tradesmen who had come in to argue prices. Give them the number and the door, she had said, and not the explanation. The explanation is what they came for. The cellar door was at the back of the passage. He paused with his hand on the latch and ran his thumb along the hinge. He lifted the door an inch and let it settle, listening to the way it sat on the jamb. Then he opened it and went down. I followed with a candle. He looked at the casks. He looked at the drainage trough cut into the flag at the foot of the stair. He bent and put his palm flat on the stone. "It is dry." "It is." "Your mother had it relaid, I think. I remember." "Eight years ago." "A handsome piece of work." "Yes." He straightened and turned in the small space and looked at me, and there was a moment in which he might have spoken his purpose, and did not, because not speaking it was the purpose. He went up the stair ahead of me, and I came after him with the candle, and I shut the cellar door behind us and slid the bolt because the bolt was mine and the door was mine and I needed to put my hand on something that was. He went out through the back into the stable yard. The rain had stopped and the air was sharp with hay and wet stone. The yard was a long rectangle bound by the stables on one side and the wash-house on the other, with the fence running down the open end toward the lane. The post nearest the gate had gone crooked in the autumn wet, and the rail above it sat low. I had meant to have Jem set it the day before and had spent the day instead with the malt-man. Stainforth walked the length of the yard with his gloves still folded in his hand. He stopped at the fence and put two fingers to the crooked post and gave it the smallest sideways pressure, watching the rail dip. He did not push hard. He did not need to. "A pity," he said. "It will go in the winter if it is not seen to." "It will be seen to." "Of course." He let his hand fall. "Miss Dale, I should not like you to think this visit anything other than friendly. I have a great regard for what your family has done at the White Hound. Three generations is no small thing." "Thank you, sir." "It is, however, a great deal of work for one person." "It always was." "Your mother had your father, in the first years." "My father drank." He did not blink. "She had, at any rate, the appearance of a household." There it was. He had laid it down as gently as a man laying down a glass on a polished table, and he watched to see whether I would pick it up. I did not. I stood where I was and let the silence do what silence does between two people who both understand it. "An establishment of this size," he said, after a moment, "might benefit from"—and he let the sentence sit, the way he had let his thumb sit on the hinge. "From?" He smiled, and inclined his head, and did not finish. I had run the arithmetic in the kitchen at four in the morning, and I ran it again now while I looked at him. Eleven and four. Twenty-three. Six weeks. A man with a lease in his pocket and a nephew at his elbow did not need to threaten a woman with the law of coverture; he needed only to wait until she could not pay, and then he could be courteous about what came next. The courtesy was the knife. The lease was the hand on the hilt. "Mr Stainforth," I said, and I heard the edge in my own voice and did not soften it, "if you have business to put to me, put it. I can answer business. I cannot answer a sentence you will not finish." He absorbed it. That was the worst of it. He did not flinch and he did not flush. He nodded once, as if I had handed him a useful piece of information about the temper of the inn, and then he picked his gloves up off the rail where he had laid them. "You misunderstand me, Miss Dale. There is no business this morning. I came only to see the house and to pay my respects." He drew on one glove. "You keep it remarkably well. Truly. The cellar is a credit to you." "Thank you, sir." "We shall speak again before the quarter." "As you wish." He drew on the second glove. He looked once more along the line of the fence, and the look was the kind a man gives a field he has already walked. Then he bowed, very correctly, and went round to the front, and his coachman was already there with the step down. The carriage turned in the lane and went south, and I stood in the yard until I could no longer hear the wheels. I unclenched my jaw. I had not known it was clenched. The back of my neck ached as though I had carried a yoke. I went into the kitchen and stood at the table with both hands flat on the wood until my hands stopped shaking, and then I poured the water from the kettle into the basin and washed my finger and bound it with a fresh strip of linen, because the cut had opened again and I had bled into my cuff without noticing. Then I went upstairs. Bridget's door was on the latch, as it always was when she had seen something from her window. The good china was already out on the small table by the fire, two cups and the pot with the chipped lid she would not let me replace. The room smelled of lavender and tea and the wool of the blanket she kept across her knees, and the fire was high because Bridget kept her fire high and we did not argue about the coal. "Sit down, child." I sat. My shoulders dropped without my permission, the way a horse drops its head when the harness comes off. Bridget poured. Her hands were knotted at the knuckles and slow at the spout, and she watched the tea, not me. "He stayed forty minutes," she said. "Forty-three." "He is very tidy." "He is." She passed me my cup. The china was warm. I held it in both hands. "Your mother saw off Mr Greville over the stabling, you remember. He wanted four more stalls along the south side. He had drawn the plans himself. He brought them in a leather case." "I remember the case." "She told him no. She told him the drainage on that side would not bear the weight of horses on top of what was already there. He said she did not understand the matter. She told him she had grown up with her ear to that drain and he had not, and would he like another cup of tea." Bridget sipped. "He did not." "She was right about the drain." "She was right about the drain. She was also right that he would come back the next year with the plans redrawn and the same case, and she was ready for him. She held that yard for thirty years because she knew where it could bear weight and where it could not." Bridget set her cup down. "Holding on and holding back are not the same thing, Dinah." I reached for the pot. "Will you have more?" "In a moment." "The tea is good. Is it the new caddy?" "It is the same caddy it has been for ten years." "Mm." She looked at me, and she let me look back, and she did not say it again. Bridget had never been a woman who said a thing twice. She had said it once and put it on the table between us with the sugar and the milk, and what I did with it was my business. I drank my tea. I asked after her hands. She said her hands were her hands. I said I would send Nan up with the dinner tray, and she said she would prefer the broth if there was broth, and I kissed the top of her head where her hair was thin and warm, and I went down the stair and shut the kitchen door behind me. I did not think about what she had said. I had work to do. The day went on. The Wellington came through at three with a full inside and two outside passengers who wanted hot rum. A carrier broke an axle on the lane and brought his load into the yard to be stowed until morning. Nan got the supper up, the fire wanted feeding twice, a man in the snug argued with his wife about a hat and I told him quietly to take the argument outside, and he did, because men generally do when a woman does not raise her voice. By ten the taproom was empty. By half past, I had banked the fire and counted the till and bolted the front door, and I was standing in the passage with my hand on the bolt when the knock came. It was not a loud knock. It was the knock of a man who had walked a long way and did not wish to be more trouble than he had to be. I drew the bolt back. He was tall. The lamp behind me threw him into outline before it caught his face. A long coat, the wool wet through at the shoulders. Boots that had been good leather once and were now worn down at the heel until the seam showed. A dog at his knee, lean and grey-brindled, with the long legs and tucked belly of a lurcher, sitting where it had been told to sit and watching me with the patient worry of an animal that had learned not to expect supper. The man took off his hat. "Forgive the hour, ma'am. I was told in the village there might be work." His voice was quiet and used the words sparingly. It was a voice that did not have to be raised to be heard, and I noted that, and I noted the boots, and I noted that the dog had not moved. I had read a hundred men at this door at this hour. He was not drunk. He was not dangerous. He was tired in the way that took weeks to put down and could not be put down tonight. "What sort of work." "Any sort. Stable, yard, fetching. I can mend most things. I do not ask wages tonight. A corner of the stable for the dog and me, and we will speak in the morning if it suits you." "It suits me. The end stall on the left is dry. There is straw. There is a horse blanket on the peg by the door. The pump is at the back. Breakfast is at six." "Thank you, ma'am." "Dale. Miss Dale." "Miss Dale." He did not give me his name. I did not ask. I shut the door behind him and slid the bolt. I went into the kitchen because I went into the kitchen every night before bed, to see that the range was banked and the cat was in and the back door was on its chain. The window over the sink looked out into the yard. I lifted the muslin. He had set his pack down by the stable door. The dog had laid itself flat across the threshold, nose on its paws, eyes on him. He was on one knee at the door, with his hand on the lower hinge, lifting the door an inch and letting it settle, listening to the way it sat on the jamb. Then he took something from his pack, a small thing, I could not see, and he tightened the hinge pin, and the door sat true. He stood. He put a hand on the dog's head as he passed it. He went down the yard to the fence. He stopped at the crooked post. He did not touch it the way Stainforth had touched it. He set his shoulder to it and lifted, and worked it upright in the wet earth with the heel of his boot, and went round behind it and braced it with a stone that he carried over from the wall, and tested the rail with his hand. The rail sat level. He did not look pleased with himself. He looked at the post the way a man looks at a small thing crossed off a list, and he picked up his pack and went back to the stable and the dog rose and followed him in. I watched his hands until he was out of the lamp's reach. Then I let the muslin fall, and I turned the key in the kitchen door, and I went up to bed.

I tallied the last column twice before I trusted it. The numbers were not good, but they were the same both times, which was a species of comfort. Six weeks and three days until the quarter's rent came due, and between now and then, the gap in the ledger sat open like a mouth. I closed the book and laid my hand flat on the cover, as though the figures might shift if left unwatched. The kitchen was cold. The fire had been built up an hour ago but had not yet done its work on the room, and the flagstones drew the warmth out through the soles of my boots. I had already swept the hearth and set the kettle before I opened the ledger, because there is no profit in studying ruin on an empty stomach, and no sense studying it on cold feet if the alternative is to build the fire first. But the fire wanted time, and the figures would not wait, and so I had sat in the chill with my sleeves pulled down over my wrists and added the columns while my breath showed faintly in the air. I put the ledger in the drawer beneath the kitchen dresser and turned the key. The drawer stuck, as it always did in damp weather. I worked it shut with the heel of my hand and moved on. The taproom fire next. I carried the coal scuttle through from the passage and knelt at the grate, raking out last night's ash into the pail. The chimney drew well, which was the single reliable mercy of the taproom. The rest of it wanted attention I could not yet afford to give--the settle nearest the window had a cracked arm, the plaster above the serving hatch was stained where the roof had let in rain last March, and the floorboards near the cellar hatch had gone soft enough that I tested them each morning with my heel to be sure they would hold. They held today. I built the fire, checked the draught, and crossed to the bar. Three barrels on the stillage: the porter nearly out, the ale half full, the small beer adequate for two days if trade stayed as it was. I made a note in my head--porter by Thursday--and checked the spirit bottles. Gin at a third. Brandy enough. I wiped the bar with a rag that wanted replacing and set the morning's glasses out. The front windows were rimed at their edges, fine crystals of ice tracing the corners of each pane. October had come in hard this year. I could feel it in the walls, in the way the plaster held the damp, in the particular quality of silence that a cold building has before the first voices fill it. I pulled my sleeves down again and went to check the yard. Jem was in the stable, straightening tack on the wall pegs. He did not look up when I appeared in the doorway, nor did I expect him to. The harness leather was oiled and hung in order, the yard swept, the water trough filled. He had been at it longer than I had, by the look of things. I noted the neat line of bridles, the clean straw in the nearest stall, and went back inside without a word. Nan came through the kitchen door a few minutes later, pins not yet set in her hair, apron already tied. She passed me in the passage without greeting and took her place behind the bar, running one hand along its surface to check that I had wiped it, which I had. She found a glass I had missed--not dirty, but spotted--and held it up to the window light, breathed on it, polished it with her cloth, and set it down. We had done this before. Many hundreds of times. The efficiency was the thing itself and needed no comment. I heard the coach before I saw it. The sound carried differently in cold air--the iron rims sharper on the road, the horses' hooves harder and more distinct. I was in the passage between the kitchen and the front hall when it came, and I had the door open before the coachman had pulled up. Late. Not unusually so, but enough to put the schedule off by twenty minutes, which meant the northbound change would be tight. Two passengers instead of four, both women. One was already halfway down the coach step before the coachman could hand her out, and her face told me everything I needed to know about the state of the journey. The second carried a trunk with a split hinge, held shut by a strap that had been improvised from what looked like a stocking. The trunk would go first. I caught Jem's eye across the yard--he had appeared at the stable door without being called--and nodded toward it. He crossed, took the trunk's weight one-handed, and bore it inside before the woman with the stocking-strap had finished telling me whose fault the hinge was. 'The road surface is to blame, and the coachman, and the boy who loaded it at the Bell in Holborn, and also, I suspect, the manufacturer of the trunk itself, who is no longer in business, which tells you everything.' I directed her to the dining room with a firmness that surprised her into silence, settled the second passenger with less difficulty, and went back out to the yard, where the coachman was standing by the lead horse with his arms folded. 'North road above Hatfield,' he said. 'Ruts a foot deep past the turning. Council hasn't touched it since August.' 'I know. It floods in the dip past the milestone.' 'Floods?' He spat. 'It's a river. I nearly lost a wheel.' 'Take the upper track past the Wrestlers. It adds ten minutes but the surface holds.' He looked at me the way coachmen do when a woman gives them directions--half sceptical, half calculating whether the advice was worth the insult of having received it. He chose the advice. 'Upper track,' he said. 'Right.' 'You'll want to water the horses before you go. Jem will see to it.' He went. The passengers went upstairs or to the dining room. Jem took charge of the team and the yard cleared, the bustle of arrival collapsing back into the ordinary quiet of a morning that had no further coaches due until half past one. Nan had watched the whole of it from behind the bar, cloth in hand, one eye on the door. I saw her see me notice. Neither of us said anything. The chandler arrived at half past ten, as he did on the last Tuesday of each month. Mr Fenton. A solid man with a cart, a ledger of his own, and a settled conviction that prices moved in only one direction. He brought the tallow candles in a crate and set them on the kitchen table with the delivery note on top. I read the note. I read it again. 'Sevenpence ha'penny the dozen,' I said. 'Cost of tallow's gone up.' 'It has not gone up since August, and in August you charged me sixpence three farthings.' He shifted his weight. 'Transport costs.' 'Your transport costs are the same horse and cart you have used for three years, Mr Fenton, and the distance between your shop and this inn has not increased.' 'There's other establishments I supply who don't question the price.' 'There are other establishments you supply who do not buy nine dozen a month and pay on delivery. Name me one other customer on this road who does both.' He said nothing. I waited. Waiting is the cheapest weapon I own and the most reliable. 'Sixpence three farthings is August's price,' I said. 'I will pay sevenpence. That is my acknowledgment that the world has not stood still. It is also my final offer.' He looked at the crate on the table. He looked at me. He took the delivery note, crossed out the figure, wrote the new one, and handed it back. I signed it. He left. The candles stayed. I stood alone in the kitchen for a moment, the delivery note in my hand, the new figure drying. I took the note to the ledger drawer, unlocked it, and wrote the adjusted price in the margin beside the month's candle entry. Sevenpence. Fourpence more per month than last year. It was not the candles that would break us. It was the candles and the coal and the porter and the meat and the roof repairs and the six weeks and three days, all of it weighed against the single fact I had carried since my mother died: that the inn was mine only so long as I remained unmarried, and the law had no interest in distinguishing between a woman who chose solitude and a woman who had merely not yet found a man willing to take what was hers. I locked the drawer and went to see about the kitchen stores. He came around the back, as the sign asked. I saw him through the window before he knocked--standing just outside the kitchen door with his hands at his sides, not moving. Young. Twenty, perhaps, or younger and aged past it. A coat that had been military issue before it was patched. Boots coming apart where the upper met the sole, the welt splitting in a line I could see from six feet away through glass. He was not deciding whether to knock. He was deciding whether he was welcome enough to try. I opened the door. He looked at me. Brown eyes, steady, a little hollow beneath. He did not speak first. 'Have you eaten?' I said. 'Not since yesterday, ma'am.' 'Kitchen, not taproom. Sit there.' He sat. I cut bread from yesterday's loaf, ladled broth from the pot that had been on the range since morning, and set both in front of him. He ate the way hungry men eat when they are trying not to show it--steadily, without looking up, one hand curled around the edge of the bowl as though someone might take it. I let him finish. I did not sit down. When he was done he stood and touched his forehead. 'Thank you, ma'am.' 'Where are you headed?' 'North,' he said. 'Peterborough way.' He went out through the yard. I watched him cross to the road and turn north, the broken boots visible for a long time against the pale mud of the track. I closed the door and took the bowl to the basin. The afternoon brought the usual friction. Two men at the corner table in the taproom had been sitting since the morning coach deposited them--some dispute over the settlement of a debt, or the memory of one, the details irrelevant and the volume rising. I heard it from the passage and was through the door before Nan had to set down her cloth. 'Gentlemen. You will settle your business quietly or you will settle it on the road. Those are your choices and I will not offer them twice.' They chose quietly. Nan's hand, I noticed, had not moved toward the cudgel beneath the bar, which meant she had judged the situation as I had: noise, not danger. We did not discuss it. The hours between two and five moved the way they always did--slowly, and full of work that had no audience. I checked the rooms on the first floor: beds made, water jugs filled, chamber pots emptied. The window in room four would not latch properly; I wedged it with a folded card and added it to the list of things that wanted fixing when there was money for fixing things. The kitchen needed setting for the evening meal: I peeled turnips, put a mutton joint on to roast, and counted plates. Nan restocked the bar. Jem was somewhere in the stable yard, doing whatever Jem did in the long afternoons, which was everything that needed doing and nothing that needed supervision. The light went early. By five the windows were dark, and by six the last of the evening's guests had been fed, settled, or sent on their way. I moved through the closing routine the way water moves through a channel it has cut for itself--taproom fire raked down to coals, the grate swept, the fender set. Bar tallied: Nan had already done it, but I checked her figures because I check everything, and her figures were correct, as they always were. Cellar hatch locked. Kitchen fire banked. The passage swept, the front door bolted, the key turned. I went out to the yard for the last circuit. The air was sharp and still, the kind of cold that comes before frost. Jem would have settled the horses; I could hear nothing from the stable but the low sound of an animal shifting its weight. The yard was dark except for the lamp I carried and one other light. Bridget's window on the second floor. The shape of her behind the glass, watching the yard as she watched it every evening, a dark figure against the faint glow of her candle. I did not look long. She was there. She was watching. I had seen it too many times for it to require acknowledgment. I went back inside and locked the yard door behind me. In the kitchen, by the light of the lamp, I saw my hands clearly for the first time all day. Reddened from cold water and from wringing cloths and from every surface they had touched since five o'clock that morning. A small cut on the index finger of my right hand, a clean line, shallow--the edge of Fenton's delivery note, I thought, though I had not felt it at the time. The invoice had cut me and I had not noticed. I set the lamp on the table and saw the letter. It sat beside the salt cellar where someone--Nan, probably, or Jem--had placed it during the afternoon. I had not seen it until now, which meant I had been in and out of this kitchen four times without looking at the table properly, which was unlike me, or else it had been placed there recently, which changed nothing about what it was. Stainforth's seal on the envelope. His handwriting on the direction, small and level. I picked it up. I turned it over once. The wax was a dark red, almost brown, pressed with an insignia I knew well enough to identify by touch. The paper was heavy. The letter had weight. I held it in both hands and stood in the kitchen with the lamp burning beside me and the inn locked and silent around me, and I did not open it. I already knew what it was about. I had known it was coming since the last quarter's rent, since the chandler's prices began to climb, since the morning I opened the ledger and saw the gap. I had been moving faster ever since, and the letter had arrived anyway. I set it down on the table, seal up. I looked at it. 'Right, then,' I said to the empty room. No one answered. The fire ticked in the grate. I turned down the lamp and went upstairs.

The ink had frozen in the ledger's gutter overnight, a thin black rime along the spine where I'd left the pen uncapped like a fool. I breathed on it, worked the nib between my fingers until the last clot dissolved, and wrote the figure I'd been carrying in my head since before I opened my eyes: four pounds, eleven shillings, threepence. That was the distance between what the White Hound held in ready coin and what the quarter's rent would demand in six weeks' time. Not a disaster. Not yet. But the kind of number that sits on your chest at four in the morning and will not be reasoned with. I closed the ledger and set the pen in its rest, properly this time. The kitchen was dark except for the glow from the range, where last night's banked coals had done their duty and held. I knelt and fed the fire -- first the kindling I'd split the evening before, then two good logs from the stack Jem kept just inside the scullery door. The flue drew well. It always drew well; my grandmother had insisted on the chimney being rebuilt in 1779, and it was the soundest piece of masonry in the building. The rest of the inn was cold and silent, which was how I preferred it at this hour. Guests would not stir for another forty minutes. The London coach was due at half seven, assuming it was not late, which it usually was. I had perhaps an hour of solitude, and I meant to spend it usefully. I carried a candle through to the taproom and knelt at the hearth there, building a second fire. The windows were rimed at their edges, October frost tracing its lace along the glass. I checked the bar stock by touch as much as sight: the ale cask was a third full, which would see us through the day; the gin was adequate; the brandy was low, but brandy was always low, and replacing it at current prices required the kind of optimism I could not presently afford. I pulled my sleeves down over my wrists and tucked the cuffs into my palms. Last night's accounts were already tallied -- I'd done them before bed, because a woman who leaves her accounts until morning is a woman who has already begun to lose her grip on the numbers. The totals were honest and unspectacular. We had taken enough to cover provisions and wages. We had not taken enough to build the margin I needed against that figure in the ledger. I wanted -- and this was the kind of wanting I permitted myself only in the early dark, when no one could see it in my face -- I wanted one month without a crisis. One month in which the road was good, the coaches full, the suppliers honest, and nothing broke that could not be mended with what we had. I wanted to open the ledger and see a number that did not make my throat tighten. Just once. The fire caught in the taproom grate, and I stood and wiped my hands on my apron and went to unlock the back door. Jem was already in the stable yard. I could see him through the grey half-light, moving between the stalls with his particular economy -- one arm doing the work of two, his system of hooks and loops so practiced that the effort was invisible unless you knew what you were looking at. He was straightening tack on the wall pegs, working left to right, the empty sleeve pinned neat at the elbow. He did not look up. He did not need to. We had done this six hundred mornings and would do it six hundred more, God willing and the lease permitting. I went back inside. A few minutes later Nan came through the kitchen door with her pins half-set and her apron already tied, which told me she'd dressed in the dark and decided the pins could wait. She passed me without a word, took her place behind the bar, and began wiping down the counter with the cloth she kept folded on the shelf below. I heard the faint, rhythmic sound of it as I returned to the kitchen to set the water on for tea. We had done this before. Many times. The London coach arrived at a quarter to eight, which was late but not remarkably so. It came in short -- two passengers instead of the expected four -- and both of them were in foul temper, which I attributed to the cold and the road and the general condition of October. The first was a stout woman of middle years with a trunk whose hinge had split clean through, spilling the corner of a petticoat onto the cobbles. She had opinions about this, and she shared them with me, with Jem, and with the coachman, who was not listening. I directed Jem to the trunk with a look. He appeared at the woman's side before she'd finished her third sentence, took the handle in his one good hand, braced the split corner against his hip, and carried it inside with no more ceremony than if it had been a sack of oats. The woman stopped mid-complaint and stared after him. I settled her with a room number, a promise that the fire would be lit within ten minutes, and a tone of voice that left no space for further grievance. She went. The coachman wanted a word. "North road's gone to pieces above Hatfield," he said, stamping his boots on the cobbles. He was a red-faced man with hands like hams and the permanently aggrieved expression of someone who spends his life arguing with horses. "Ruts deep enough to snap an axle. I've reported it twice and nothing's done." "The parish surveyor was out a fortnight ago," I said. "They've patched the worst of it between the Hatfield turn and Welwyn." "Patched it with what? Prayers?" "Gravel and clay. It won't last the winter, but it'll hold until the frost sets in properly. After that you'll want to swing wide at the dip past Coleman's farm -- the drainage ditch has silted up and the road floods there first." He looked at me with the particular expression men reserve for women who know things about roads. I did not have time to enjoy it. "I'll need the horses changed inside twenty minutes," I said. "Nan will have bread and beer for you at the bar if you're quick." He went. The second passenger -- a thin, silent man who had not troubled anyone -- went upstairs without requiring direction. Jem took the luggage. The yard cleared. Through the doorway I could see Nan behind the bar, her cloth moving in steady passes across the counter, one eye on the front door. She'd been watching the whole exchange. I said nothing. There was nothing to say. The chandler arrived at half past ten with his cart and his monthly delivery and his air of mild apology, which I had learned to distrust. Mr Fenton was a lean, sandy-haired man who smiled too much and kept his prices written on slips of paper in his waistcoat pocket, as though the numbers might change if exposed to open air. "Morning, Miss Dale." He set the first crate of tallow candles on the kitchen table. "Sixteen dozen, as agreed. I've had to adjust the price a touch -- tenpence ha'penny the dozen." "It was ninepence three weeks ago." "Tallow's gone up. The renderers in Hitchin have raised their price to the whole trade." "The renderers in Hitchin are charging you fourpence a pound, same as they charged you in July. I buy soap from the same tallow, Mr Fenton. I know what you pay for it." The smile held, but the eyes behind it recalculated. "There's cartage to consider. And the cost of wicking has --" "Ninepence ha'penny," I said. "Which is more than the Bull at Hatfield pays you per dozen, and they order half what I do." A pause. He reached into his waistcoat pocket and studied one of his slips of paper as though it contained information he had not previously noticed. "I suppose I could manage ninepence ha'penny. As a courtesy." "As a courtesy," I agreed. He unloaded the rest of the crate, counted the candles twice -- he always counted twice, which I respected, since I would have done the same -- and departed with his cart and his smile and his slips of paper, leaving sixteen dozen tallow candles on my kitchen table at a price I could absorb. I stood in the kitchen after the door closed and allowed myself one moment of private satisfaction. It was a small victory -- pennies, not pounds -- but it was mine, and I had earned it by knowing his margins better than he'd have liked. My mother would have done it faster and with less preamble. But my mother had had thirty years of practice, and I had had two, and the candles were on the table at the right price, and that was enough. I put the candles away and returned to the ledger, not to write in it but to look at the page. The number had not changed. Four pounds, eleven shillings, threepence -- and now the chandler's bill settled, which was something, but not the something I needed. The rent, the suppliers, the coal, the wages: every shilling accounted for, every margin defended, and all of it resting on a lease that was mine only so long as I remained precisely what I was. Unmarried. Unpossessed. Alone in the eyes of the law and therefore permitted to possess things in my own name. I had refused two proposals already for this exact, practical reason, and I did not regret either refusal. What I regretted -- if regret was the word -- was that the calculation had to be made at all. I closed the ledger and went to check on the dining room. I saw the soldier through the kitchen window before he knocked. He'd come around the back, as the signs asked, and he was standing outside the door with the particular stillness of a man deciding whether he was welcome enough to announce himself. Young -- twenty, perhaps, or a hard-used eighteen. Thin in the way that means weeks, not days. His coat had been military once; it was not military now. His boots were coming apart at the welt, the sole of the left one separating from the upper in a gap wide enough to let in every puddle between here and London. He stood there for perhaps ten seconds, looking at the door. Then he raised his hand and knocked, twice, quietly. I opened the door. He took his hat off. His hair was cropped close, still growing out from the Army cut, and his face had the raw, wind-chapped look of someone who'd been sleeping rough. "Have you eaten?" I asked. He met my eyes, then looked away. "Not recently, ma'am. No." "Kitchen, not taproom. Sit there." I pointed to the bench by the range. He understood. He sat where I directed and put his hat on his knee and said nothing further while I cut bread and ladled broth from the pot that had been simmering since morning. I set it in front of him with a spoon and a cup of small beer and went back to my work at the counter, because watching a hungry man eat is a kind of theft -- you take his dignity along with his privacy. He ate steadily, not fast. He held the spoon the way men hold things when they are trying not to show how much they need them -- carefully, with the grip too tight and the movements too controlled. When he'd finished, he stood. "Thank you, ma'am. I'm grateful." "Where are you headed?" "North," he said. "My mother's in Huntingdon." I nodded. He put his hat back on and went out through the kitchen door and across the yard and through the gate, and I watched him go until he turned onto the road and the hedgerow took him. His boots would not last to Huntingdon. I knew that, and he knew that, and neither of us had mentioned it because there was nothing either of us could do about boots. He was somebody's son, walking north on shoes held together by habit, and I had fed him because a hungry man near a coaching inn is a practical problem, and because I could. I wiped the bowl and the spoon and put them back, and I went on with the day. In the taproom, just after noon, two men who'd been sitting too long over too little ale found something to disagree about -- the particulars escaped me, though it seemed to involve a wager and a horse and someone's disputed recollection of the Newmarket results. The dispute was edging toward the kind of volume that unsettles other guests. "Gentlemen," I said from the doorway. "You'll settle it outside or you'll settle your bill. I don't mind which." They settled it by going quiet, which was the third option and the one I'd counted on. Nan, behind the bar, caught my eye and returned to her work without comment. The afternoon stretched out in the way coaching-inn afternoons do when the road is quiet and the weather discourages casual travel. The coach had gone. The two morning passengers were settled -- the woman with the damaged trunk had come down for tea and found nothing further to complain about, which I considered an achievement. Jem was in the yard, doing whatever Jem did in the hours between coaches, which was everything that needed doing and several things that didn't but would need doing eventually. Nan polished glasses. I worked through the kitchen accounts, reset the upstairs rooms, checked the stores against the evening's requirements. The ordinary grind. No incidents, no revelations. The work, which is its own argument and its own reward, and which does not leave you, and which does not die. Evening came early, as it does in late October, and with it the particular heaviness of an inn preparing to close. The last guests were settled by eight. I checked the dining room -- cleared, the cloth shaken out and folded, the candles trimmed for morning. The taproom fire was down to coals, and I left it; it would burn itself out safely. The cellar hatch was locked. I tested it, as I tested it every night, because my mother had tested it every night, and her mother before her, and a woman who assumes a lock is set because she set it yesterday is a woman who will find her cellar open in the morning. The stable yard was quiet. Jem had settled the horses; I could hear nothing from the stalls, which meant they were fed and watered and content, because Jem's horses were always fed and watered and content, and this was one of the things I did not need to worry about and was grateful for. I looked up at the second floor. There was a lit shape at Bridget's window -- the amber glow of her candle, and behind it, the dark outline of her figure in the chair where she always sat. She was watching the yard, or the road, or the particular middle distance that old women watch when the day is finished and the night has not yet offered them sleep. I did not look long. Back inside. The kitchen, last of all. I checked the range, banked the fire for morning, hung the kettle where it would be ready. My hands at the end of the day were what they always were: reddened from cold water, roughened from work, with a small cut on my right index finger where the edge of Fenton's delivery note had caught the skin when I'd unfolded it. I hadn't noticed it at the time. I noticed it now, in the way you notice things when the work stops and the body remembers it has been used. I turned the key in the back door. I set the bolt. The inn was locked, the fires tended, the accounts as balanced as they were going to be, and I was standing in my kitchen in the quiet that comes after a house has been used hard and is now resting. I was alone. I noticed it the way I noticed the cut -- only when I was still enough for it to register, and too tired to look away from it. The letter was on the table where Nan must have put it sometime during the afternoon, half-hidden beneath the chandler's receipt. I had not seen it before. I saw it now. I knew the handwriting on the direction, and I knew the seal -- dark red wax, stamped with the Stainforth crest, pressed down with the particular firmness of a man who considers his correspondence a form of authority. I picked it up. I turned it over once. The paper was heavy, the kind a man buys to remind you that he can afford good paper and you cannot. I broke the seal. The letter was brief. Stainforth did not waste words any more than I did, though for different reasons. He wrote that he had received an enquiry from a gentleman -- he named no name -- regarding the purchase of the White Hound's lease. He wrote that the terms discussed were favourable and that he was, as the freeholder, disposed to entertain the offer. He wrote that he wished to extend to me, as the current leaseholder, the courtesy of a meeting to discuss the matter, and that he expected my reply within a fortnight. *The courtesy of a meeting.* As though he were offering me a chair at his table rather than pulling the ground from beneath my feet. A fortnight. He had given me a fortnight to respond to the possibility of losing everything my grandmother had built, everything my mother had held, everything I had kept alive with my own hands for two years of early mornings and late nights and ledger pages that would not add up to enough. I set the letter down on the table. I pressed my palm flat against it, feeling the weight of the paper under my skin, the cut on my finger stinging where it met the edge. Then I said, to the empty kitchen, to the banked fire and the clean counter and the locked doors and the silence: "A fortnight. Right. Then I'd best start tomorrow." I left the letter on the table where I could see it first thing, and I blew out the candle, and I went upstairs to sleep -- because a woman who means to fight needs her rest, and I had a great deal of fighting to do.

# Chapter 1: The White Hound

The grate was dead. I rebuilt it. Whoever banked it last night had done it wrong, and I'd have words with them if I could spare the breath.

Forty years of coaching traffic had worn the flagstones uneven. Forty years had smoked the ceiling beams to the colour of old bone. Forty years, and the place still stood, which was more than I could say for the hinge on the cellar door. I tested it with my boot. The whole thing shifted in the frame. Another job for the list that would never shorten. The shelves behind the bar wanted reinforcing where the weight of the ledgers had bowed them. The damp patch above the third window had spread another handspan since September. The bar top I'd sanded myself last winter when the snow kept the coaches away, working the grain until it ran smooth as water under my palm. My one improvement in two years of running the place alone.

The lease hung on its nail beside the gin bottles, Stainforth's name at the top in his clerk's tidy script. Mine appeared nowhere on it. Three generations of women keeping this inn alive, and not one of our names on that paper. I turned my back on it and checked the till.

"Mail coach lost a wheel at Hatfield." Nan was already tying her cap strings, reaching for her apron from the hook. "They'll be an hour late, bringing passengers."

"How many?"

"Eight inside, four up top. Driver's in a mood about it. Blames the Hatfield turnpike." Her hands never stopped moving. Cap secured, apron tied, already reaching for the coffee pot. "They'll want feeding."

"The kitchen's not—"

"I'll manage." She was already halfway there, and I knew better than to finish the sentence. Seven years of working together meant she knew the state of the kitchen fire as well as I did.

"Jem's in the yard with fresh horses and the stable doors thrown wide and the morning hay already down and three of the stalls mucked out." I counted off the preparations on my fingers, watching the yard through the window. The ostler had his back to me, checking the harness with his one good hand braced against the leather.

"Left rear axle needs work."

"The Hatfield coach?"

He nodded. We understood each other in the economy of morning.

The coach arrived in the expected chaos. Passengers spilled out cramped and querulous, the coachman red-faced and shouting about time lost, luggage tumbling from the roof. I counted twelve passengers, not twelve. Someone had climbed aboard at the last stop without paying. The coachman hefted a trunk that clearly wasn't his.

"That goes to Stevenage," he announced, dropping it hard enough to crack a corner. "Not my fault if it's damaged. Should have been transferred proper at Barnet."

"Mr. Welch at the Crown takes complaints every Tuesday." I named the agent, the day, the inn where he'd be found. "He's very particular about damaged goods, especially when the damage happens after the transfer should have occurred."

The coachman looked at me, at the trunk, at me again. He picked it up, gentler this time, and set it properly with the other luggage.

"I'll want compensation for the delay," the trunk's owner announced from the coach door. "This is intolerable."

I found the right amount of sympathy to offer someone who thought an hour's delay was the worst fate that could befall them.

"The candles are the same price they were last month." The chandler had arrived while I was settling the coach passengers, and now he stood in my front room with his sample box, trying not to meet my eyes.

His hands worried at the box catches. His gaze fixed somewhere past my left shoulder. He cleared his throat twice before answering.

"Tallow's gone dear, Miss Dale. Everyone's feeling it."

I opened the ledger, ran my finger down last month's column. "Three shillings the dozen last month. Three and six you're asking now. That's a steep rise for one month, Mr. Hammond."

"Like I said, the costs—"

"Baines in Baldock sells at three and threepence."

"Baldock's a mile further."

"I'd travel a mile to save threepence a dozen when I'm buying six dozen a week."

He shifted the sample box to his other hand, and I knew what he wasn't saying. Baines wouldn't extend credit. Baines didn't have Stainforth breathing down his neck about which establishments he supplied. Hammond cleared his throat again. "Three and fourpence. Best I can do."

The ledger went back on the shelf. I felt the anger rise and set it aside. Thursday's delivery would need checking, the new girl hadn't learned to count past twelve.

Two men in the taproom had been into their third pint before most folk had finished their first. Ex-soldiers by the look of them, the drink hitting harder than they'd calculated. One shoved the other over a spilled drop, voices rising.

"Sit down."

They did. The one who'd done the shoving muttered something I chose not to hear, but his knuckles unclenched and his companion settled back on the bench. Jem had positioned himself by the door, not intervening but ready. We'd done this dance before. We looked at each other. The frequency of it, the reason why, all understood in that glance.

The soldier at the kitchen door stood with his weight on his left leg, favouring the right. His coat had been blue once, before sun and weather faded it to grey. The boots were down at heel but carefully mended, the kind of repair work a man does himself by firelight.

He did not ask for charity. He asked if there was work.

I put him to stacking wood while I heated the broth. He worked steady, not rushing to prove his worth, not dragging to make the job last. I nodded him to the table. He sat carefully.

"Road was frozen past Stevenage," he said, after a while.

"Always is, this time of year."

The broth disappeared steady. While he ate, I covered the bread for morning, not watching him but aware of every spoonful. He'd been hungry long enough that his body had forgotten how to manage food. I could see it in the careful way he swallowed, the pause between bites.

"Thank you," he said when the bowl was empty.

"Pump's in the yard if you need it."

He finished the bread and thanked me and went. Through the window I watched him work the pump handle, drink deep, straighten his coat. The boots moved north, steady despite the worn heels. I went back to work.

The last coach departed at nine. I barred the front door, tested the shutters on the taproom windows where the latch wanted oil, turned the key on the spirit cabinet though I trusted my people, counted the till though I knew the sum. Each coin in its pile, the small weight of the day's taking. The strongbox closed with its particular click. My mother's fingers had worn the brass smooth where mine touched it now. Twenty years she'd done these same tasks in this same order, and her mother twenty before that.

I wiped down the bar though Nan had already done it, the cloth catching on a gouge in the wood near the tap. Could have been anything. Keg corner, chair leg, boot heel during some forgotten fight. Keg, I decided. Thursday's delivery. Whether Jem could manage the largest cask alone or if I'd need to help. The cloth needed wringing. I walked the length of the bar, checking for sticky patches, though I knew there were none.

The lease was in Stainforth's name. Three women keeping this place alive on a man's sufferance. His father had held it over my mother. His grandfather over my grandmother. Each generation of Stainforth men finding new ways to remind us that we existed at their pleasure.

I wrung out the cloth, hung it on the hook. The hook was one my mother put in. I could see her hands, the night before she died. Quick and certain, everything in its place, not knowing. Always moving, those hands, until they weren't.

The letter waited on the shelf where Nan had left it that afternoon. Stainforth's seal in the wax, heavy. I'd known what it was before I saw the direction. The weight of it, the timing. I set it beside the ledger, unopened. Tomorrow would be soon enough for whatever new terms he'd devised.

I took the candle and went to bed.

The ledger said six weeks, and I did not like what it said. I had the book open on the kitchen table, my fingers still on the column where the numbers ran out of patience with me. Tallow, oats, the quarterly coal delivery, Nan's wages, Jem's wages, the farrier's account that I had been stretching since August. I added them again. They came out the same. They always came out the same, which was the single dependable quality of bad arithmetic. Six weeks until the rent was due, and between now and then, a gap the width of my thumb on the page and rather wider in my stomach. I closed the book. The kitchen fire needed building up. I had laid it an hour ago, when the dark was still total and the flagstones bit through my stockings, but it had not yet taken the edge off the room. October had turned mean this year. The windows were rimed at their edges, a crust of ice so fine it looked like salt, and the draught under the back door had a personal quality to it, as if it had come looking specifically for me. I pulled my sleeves down over my wrists and went to the grate. I wanted one morning, just one, to come down and find the fire already lit and the water already hot and someone else's hand on the kettle. I wanted to sit at that table with a cup of tea and no ledger and nothing to calculate. I wanted ten minutes of not being the first person awake in a building that required me to be the first person awake. I wanted this so precisely and so pointlessly that I almost laughed. I did not laugh. I raked the coals and fed the fire and put the kettle on, because that is what there was to do, and I was the one who did it. The taproom fire next. I carried the ash pail through and knelt on the hearthstones, which were colder than the kitchen's and had the particular chill of a room that had held too many bodies the night before and now held none. The air smelled of old ale and tallow smoke. I laid the fire, struck it, watched it catch. The bar stock I checked by habit: three casks sound, one near its end, the gin bottle down to a knuckle's depth. I made a note in my head. I made all my notes in my head before I committed them to paper, because paper cost money and my head was free. The accounts from last night I had tallied before bed, sitting on the stairs with a candle and the cashbox balanced on my knees. We had taken eleven shillings and fourpence. Enough. Barely enough, but enough, if nothing broke and no one left and the coaches kept stopping. If. Outside, the yard was still dark. I checked from the back door and saw Jem's lamp already moving in the stable block, a warm yellow point swinging as he crossed from one stall to the next. He was straightening tack on its hooks. I could see his one hand working, the stump of the other arm braced against the post for leverage. He had taught himself every motion twice, once for the hand he had and once for the hand he did not, and the result was a fluency that looked almost careless if you were not paying attention. I was always paying attention. He did not look up. He did not need to know I was watching, and I did not need him to know. The work was the acknowledgment. It was enough. I went back inside. Nan came through the kitchen door a few minutes later, pins not yet set in her hair, apron already tied. She took her place behind the bar without a word, cloth in hand, and began wiping down the counter. We had done this so many mornings that the routine had its own silence, comfortable and complete, and neither of us disturbed it. The London coach was late, which surprised no one, and short, which surprised me. Two passengers instead of four. The first was a man in a brown coat who wanted his breakfast and said nothing else. The second was a woman with a trunk whose hinge had split, and she had a great deal to say about it. "The roads are a disgrace," she told me, as though I had laid them personally. "A positive disgrace. This trunk was perfectly sound when I left Islington." "Jem," I said, without raising my voice, because Jem was already in the yard and already walking toward us. He took the trunk from the coachman's hands and examined the hinge with his thumb. "I can pin it," he said. "Twenty minutes." "There," I said to the woman. "Twenty minutes, and your trunk will hold to Baldock and beyond. Breakfast is through the door on the left, and there's a fire." She opened her mouth. She closed it. She went to breakfast. The coachman, Hutton, wanted a word. He was a broad man with a red face and opinions about road surfaces that he shared with anyone who stood still long enough. I stood still for two minutes because two minutes was what I had. "The stretch above Hatfield is going to pieces," he said. "Ruts a foot deep after the rain. I lost a shoe off the near leader yesterday." "You'll want to report that to the turnpike trust." "I'm reporting it to you, Miss Dale, because you're the only person on this road who listens." "I listen, Mr Hutton. I cannot, however, resurface a highway." "Aye, well." He rubbed the back of his neck. "Someone ought to." "Someone ought to," I agreed, and that was the end of it, because the horses needed changing and Hutton had twenty miles to Biggleswade and I had a chandler arriving within the hour. I sent him off with a nod and turned back to the inn. The coach dispersed. The brown-coated man went to the dining room. The woman with the trunk went upstairs. Jem took the trunk to the workbench. The yard cleared, and the morning settled back into its ordinary shape. Nan had been wiping the same stretch of bar through all of this, one eye on the door the entire time. I noticed. I said nothing. Mr Oakes arrived at half past ten with his cart and his candles and his new price. "Tuppence ha'penny the dozen," he said, setting the first crate on the kitchen table as though the matter were already concluded. I looked at the crate. I looked at him. "It was tuppence last month, Mr Oakes." "Tallow's gone up." "Tallow has gone up a farthing the pound since March. You've raised your price a halfpenny the dozen four times in seven months. That is not the same ratio." He had the grace to pause. Oakes was not a stupid man. He was a man who assumed the person across the table would not check his margins, which is a different kind of stupidity and one I had less patience for. "I know what you pay Grigson for your tallow," I said. "I know what you charge the King's Head and what you charge the George at Welwyn, because Mrs Phipps at the George and I had a very informative conversation last Tuesday. You are making three farthings more per dozen off me than off either of them. I would like to know why." Oakes looked at his crate. He looked at the door, as though calculating the distance. "Tuppence farthing," I said. "Same as the George." "Miss Dale—" "Tuppence farthing. Or you may load your cart and try the Bull at Stevenage, though I believe Mrs Cranmer makes her own." A silence. Oakes was not a man who enjoyed silence, which was precisely why I let it sit. "Tuppence farthing," he said. "Thank you, Mr Oakes. Leave them on the table. Nan will count them." He left. The candles stayed. I stood alone in the kitchen for a moment after the door closed, my hand still on the edge of the crate, and allowed myself one private breath of satisfaction. I was good at this. I was very good at this, and no one was going to tell me otherwise, least of all a man who thought a halfpenny here and there would slip past me because I wore an apron. The thought came, as it always did, on the heels of the small victory. The ledger, the rent, the chandler's price: all of it weighed against the one fact I could not negotiate around. If I married, the inn stopped being mine. The lease, the accounts, the right to stand in my own kitchen and tell a chandler his margins, all of it transferred to a husband the moment I said the words. Two men had asked me. I had refused them both, and this was why. I picked up the crate and carried it to the storeroom. He came around the back, as the sign asked. I saw him through the kitchen window before he knocked. Young, thin. A coat that had been decent once and was now held together more by habit than by thread. His boots were coming apart at the welt, the sole of the left one separating from the upper in a gap wide enough to let in every puddle between here and London. He stood outside the door with his hand raised and did not knock. He looked at the step. He looked at the door. He was deciding whether he was welcome enough to try. I opened the door before he could decide. He was perhaps twenty, perhaps younger. It was hard to tell with the ones who had been soldiering. His face had the thinness of someone who had been hungry for longer than a few days, the kind of thinness that settles into the bones and changes the shape of a face. "Have you eaten?" I said. He met my eyes. "Not recently, ma'am." "Kitchen," I said. "There's bread and broth. Sit where I put you." He understood. Kitchen, not taproom. He nodded once and came in and sat on the bench by the fire without touching anything until I set the bowl in front of him. Bread beside it, a thick slice, and a heel of cheese because the cheese was going dry and would not last the week. He ate. Not fast, the way starving men sometimes do, bolting it as though someone might take it away. Carefully. He held the spoon with both hands, the way my father had the winter he was ill, as though the business of lifting it required all of him. I went back to the stockpot. I did not watch him eat, because being watched while you eat when you are that hungry is its own kind of cruelty, and I had been raised by a woman who understood the difference between charity and kindness. When the bowl was empty he set the spoon down and sat for a moment. "Thank you, ma'am." "Where are you headed?" "North," he said. "Yorkshire, if I can get there." I nodded. Yorkshire was a long walk in boots like those. I did not say so. He knew it better than I did. He left through the back door, the way he had come, and I stood at the window and watched him cross the yard and turn onto the road. He was somebody's son. He had held that spoon with both hands, and I could not stop seeing it, and I did not try. I went back to the stockpot and stirred it and put the lid on. The afternoon brought the usual small disasters. Two men in the taproom, travellers who had been sitting too long over too little ale, found something to disagree about. I heard the voices rising from the kitchen and was through the door before Nan had to move. "Gentlemen," I said. "This is a coaching inn, not a boxing ring. If you wish to continue, you may do so in the yard, where the ground is softer. If you wish to stay, you will lower your voices." They lowered their voices. I went back to the kitchen. The rest of the afternoon passed without incident. The inn between coaches was a quiet place, and I filled it the way I always did: accounts, rooms reset, the kitchen prepared for the evening meal. Nan polished glasses. Jem mended the trunk hinge and then a bridle and then a section of fence rail that had been listing since September. The building held us in its routine, and the routine held. I locked up alone, as I always did. The last guests settled. The last fire checked, the coals raked down and the guard set. The bar tallied, the cashbox locked, the kitchen closed. I moved through the inn on the same circuit I had walked every night for two years, and for ten years before that alongside my mother, and the building received me the way it always did, with silence and the smell of woodsmoke and the faint tick of cooling stone. Taproom fire: down to coals, safe. Cellar hatch: locked. Stable yard: quiet. Jem would have settled the horses. I could see the stable block from the back door, dark now, everything in its place. I looked at my hands in the lamplight. Reddened from the cold water and the cleaning, a small cut on my right index finger from the edge of Oakes's delivery invoice. I had not noticed it at the time. The paper had been rough-cut, and I had been too busy winning the argument to register the sting. I noticed it now. A thin line, already closed, the blood dried to a dark seam. The kind of cut you collect at the end of a day without remembering how. I crossed the yard toward the back door. The second floor: a lit shape behind glass, and Bridget's silhouette in the window. She was sitting in her chair. She was watching the yard. I did not look long. I set the bolt on the back door. The last bolt of the day, the one that meant the inn was mine for the night, sealed and accounted for. I stood in the kitchen with the lamp on the table and the building quiet around me and let myself notice, just for a breath, that I was alone. That I was always alone at this hour, in this kitchen, with the bolt thrown and the fire down and no one to hear me if I spoke. I did not speak. I turned to bank the kitchen fire, and that is when I saw the letter. It was on the table, half-hidden beneath the cashbox where Nan must have placed it when the afternoon post came. I had missed it. I had been in the storeroom counting candles, or upstairs turning down beds, or somewhere else in this building that required me every minute of every hour, and I had missed it. I picked it up. I knew the handwriting. I knew the seal. Stainforth. I turned the letter over once. The paper was heavy, cream-coloured, the kind a man buys when he wants you to understand that he can afford good paper. The seal was pressed deep into dark red wax. I broke it. The letter was brief.
