Stainforth's hand was neat and angular, each word set down with the precision of a man who weighs his sentences before he commits them. *Miss Dale, I write to inform you that I have received a serious offer of purchase for the freehold of the White Hound and its associated lands. The prospective buyer intends to take possession at the next quarter-day. I am prepared to discuss terms with you before any agreement is concluded, as a courtesy to your family's long association with the property. I shall expect you at Morden Hall on Thursday the seventh of November, at eleven o'clock. I trust you will appreciate that this matter does not admit of delay.* I set the letter down on the table. The next quarter-day. Lady Day was months off, but the Christmas quarter was eight weeks away. Six weeks until the rent came due, and now this: a buyer, a meeting, a deadline. Stainforth had not named the buyer. He did not need to. The point was the offer, the freehold, the word *possession* sitting in the middle of the page like a stone in a shoe. I read it again. *As a courtesy to your family's long association with the property.* A courtesy. He was selling the ground beneath my feet and calling it a courtesy. My hands were steady. The cut on my finger had opened again where I had pressed the seal, and a thin line of blood sat on the edge of the paper. I looked at it. I looked at his signature, that careful angular hand. "Well, Mr Stainforth," I said to the empty kitchen. "I suppose you had better meet the woman who holds your lease, and not the one you were expecting." I folded the letter and put it in my apron pocket, and I blew out the lamp.

The ink had bled through the third column again, and I pressed my thumb against the page to stop it spreading before I remembered that blotting paper existed for precisely this purpose and my thumb did not. Half past five by the kitchen clock, and the numbers in the ledger were the same ones I had tallied at midnight. Forty-three pounds, seven shillings, and ninepence stood between me and the quarter's rent. Six weeks to find the difference. I could do it if the coaches ran full and the weather held and nothing broke that cost more than a shilling to mend -- three conditions that had never, in the recorded history of the White Hound, aligned at the same time. I closed the ledger and set my hands flat on the table. The kitchen was cold. The fire I'd built twenty minutes ago was still finding its courage, the coals ticking and shifting beneath the first true flames. Outside, the yard was dark and perfectly still, and the windows had that fine white rime at their edges that meant the night had been harder than I'd expected. I pulled my sleeves down over my wrists and went to work. The kitchen fire first, fed and coaxed until it would hold. Then the taproom, where last night's ashes needed clearing before the grate could be relit. I knelt on the hearthstones and raked them out, the ash fine enough to ghost up into my hair and settle on my lips. The taproom smelled of old ale and cold stone and the particular staleness that accumulated overnight when the windows stayed shut. I opened one, just a crack, and the October air bit through. Bar stock next. I counted bottles by touch in the dim light -- gin, brandy, the rough cider that the coach drivers preferred. Three bottles of claret left from a case that should have lasted the month. Someone had been generous with the measures, and I had a reasonable suspicion who. I would speak to Nan. I checked the cellar hatch, tested the lock, and climbed back up to tally the previous night's accounts against the cashbox. The figures matched. They always matched, because I was the one who kept them, and I did not make errors with money. With other things, perhaps. With money, never. By the time I'd finished, the kitchen fire had steadied into something useful, and the first grey light was pressing at the windows. I set the kettle on the range and stood with my back to the warmth, letting it soak through the wool of my dress. There was a moment -- just one, small and foolish -- when I let myself want something other than the numbers to come right. Something I could not have named if pressed, only that the kitchen felt very large for one person, and the morning very quiet, and I was twenty-seven years old and tired in a way that sleep did not touch. Then the kettle hissed, and I had work to do. Jem was already in the stable yard when I looked out the back door. He had the big bay's harness across the mounting rail and was working at a stiff buckle with his one hand and the hook he'd rigged to his belt, his breath clouding in the cold air. He did not look up. He did not need to. He knew I checked, and I knew he'd be there, and neither of us required the other to say so. Nan came through the kitchen door a few minutes after, pins half-set in her reddish hair, apron already tied. She took her place behind the bar without a word and began wiping down the counter with the cloth she kept folded in her apron pocket. I heard the soft, rhythmic sound of it from the kitchen -- back and forth, back and forth -- and felt the inn settle into its working shape around me, the way a body settles when it draws its first proper breath of the day. The London coach was late. This was not unusual -- the London coach was late with the reliable frequency of a church bell rung by a drunk sexton -- but it was also running short. Two passengers instead of the expected four, and both of them had opinions. The first was a stout woman in a travelling cloak who had a trunk with a split hinge and a conviction that this was somehow my fault. I directed her to the dining room, directed Jem to the trunk, and directed my attention to the coachman, who had climbed down from the box with the particular heaviness of a man who has been arguing with the road for thirty miles. "North road's gone to pieces above Hatfield," he said, stamping mud from his boots on my clean step. "Ruts deep enough to lose a wheel. Someone needs to speak to the turnpike trust." "Someone has. Twice this month." "And?" "And the trust has a meeting in November, at which point the road will be frozen and the question academic. You'll want to take the lower fork past the mill if it's rained. The ruts are shallower." He considered this. "The lower fork adds twenty minutes." "The lower fork adds twenty minutes. A broken axle adds two days. I can offer you coffee before you go, but I cannot offer you a better answer than that." He took the coffee. The yard cleared. The stout woman was settled in the dining room with toast and the particular silence of a person who has run out of complaints and is recalibrating. Jem had the trunk and was carrying it upstairs with an ease that made the split hinge look like a personal failing on the trunk's part rather than a structural one. Nan stood behind the bar with her cloth folded over one arm, watching the door the way she always watched it -- not anxious, not expectant, just ready. I noted it. Said nothing. Mr Fenton, the chandler, arrived at half past ten with his cart and his monthly delivery and his newest price. "Tuppence ha'penny the dozen," he said, setting the first crate of tallow candles on the kitchen table as though he were presenting me with a gift. I did not pick up a candle. I did not need to. I had been buying Mr Fenton's candles for three years, and they had not improved. "They were tuppence last month, Mr Fenton." "Tallow's gone up. The renderers in Barnet have raised their price, and I'm obliged to pass the cost along." "The renderers in Barnet raised their price in June. You passed the cost along in July. And again in September. That is a great deal of passing for a single increase." He shifted his weight. "There are other considerations. Transport. Labour." "Your transport is the same cart and the same horse you have driven for six years, and your labour is yourself. Unless you have recently hired an assistant, in which case I congratulate you, but I do not intend to pay his wages." Mr Fenton's mouth opened, then closed. He was not a dishonest man. He was an optimistic one, which in a chandler amounted to much the same thing. "Tuppence farthing," he said. "Tuppence. Which is what I paid last month and what every inn between here and Baldock pays, as you know perfectly well, because Mrs Hewitt at the George told me her price when I saw her at the Stevenage market." A silence. Mr Fenton looked at his candles. His candles did not look back. "Tuppence," he said. "Thank you, Mr Fenton. I'll take four dozen." He unloaded the candles, took his money, and left without the pleasantries that sometimes padded the end of our transactions. I did not mind. The door closed behind him, and I allowed myself one small, private moment of satisfaction -- the kind that comes from knowing exactly what a thing costs and refusing to pay a farthing more. It was not a grand victory. But the inn was built on small ones, and I had learned to take them where I found them. I carried the candles to the storeroom and came back to the kitchen, where the ledger sat on the table like a conscience. Forty-three pounds. Six weeks. Every tuppence mattered, and every tuppence I saved on candles was a tuppence I did not have to find elsewhere -- and all of it added against the fact that sat beneath every calculation I made, quiet and immovable as bedrock. If I married, the lease would pass to my husband. The inn, the income, the business my grandmother had built and my mother had held -- all of it would belong to a man the moment I spoke two words in a church. I had refused two proposals on the strength of that arithmetic, and I did not regret either one. I wiped the table, hung the cloth, and went to check on the dining room. I saw him through the kitchen window before he knocked. Young -- twenty, perhaps, or younger, with the thin, unfinished look of a boy who had stopped growing when the food ran out. His coat was too large for him, army-issue by the cut, and his boots were coming apart at the welt, the leather separating from the sole in a way that meant he had been walking a long time on wet roads. He stood outside the kitchen door with his hand raised but not yet touching the wood, and I recognised the pause. He was not deciding whether to knock. He was deciding whether he was welcome enough to try. I opened the door before he could finish the calculation. He looked at me with the startled expression of a man caught between hope and the expectation of refusal. Up close, he was thinner than I'd thought. His cheekbones stood out sharp beneath skin that had gone sallow, and his eyes had that flat, careful quality I had learned to recognise in the men who came through -- not empty, exactly, but guarded against something I could not see. "Have you eaten?" I said. He hesitated. "Not recently, ma'am." "Kitchen, not taproom. Sit there." I pointed to the bench by the range. "I'll bring you something." He sat. He did not argue or thank me or attempt to explain himself, which told me he had done this before and knew the terms. I cut bread from the loaf, ladled broth from the pot that had been simmering since morning, and set both in front of him. He ate the way hungry men eat -- quickly, with his head down, one arm curled around the bowl as though someone might take it from him. I busied myself at the range and did not watch him. When the bowl was empty, he set down the spoon and sat very still. "Thank you, ma'am." "Where are you headed?" He named a direction -- north, toward the midlands -- in a voice that suggested the destination mattered less than the motion. I nodded. He stood, touched his forehead, and went out through the kitchen door into the yard. I watched him cross to the road and turn north, his broken boots dark against the pale mud, and I stood there longer than I needed to. He was somebody's son. He held the spoon the way a child holds it, with his whole fist, as though he had forgotten there was any other way. I turned back to the kitchen and wiped the place where he had sat. The afternoon brought the usual small frictions. Two men in the taproom had been sitting too long over their ale and had found something to disagree about -- the price of barley, or the state of the turnpike, or whatever thin excuse men used when what they really wanted was to raise their voices at someone. "Gentlemen." I did not raise mine. "If you wish to continue this conversation, you may do so in the yard. If you wish to continue drinking, you will do so quietly." They continued drinking. Nan, behind the bar, caught my eye and went back to her work. The hours between the afternoon coach and the evening trade were the inn's dead time -- the lull when the building exhaled and the work became invisible. Nan polished glasses. Jem was outside, doing whatever Jem did when the yard was empty, which generally involved improving something no one had asked him to improve. I reset the two upstairs rooms that had been used, checked the linen, reviewed the kitchen stores for the evening meal, and wrote out the next day's orders in the small, tight hand my mother had taught me, because paper cost money and margins were not for wasting. The work was ordinary. It was also everything. It was the thing that kept the doors open and the fires lit and the coaches stopping, and if it was not enough -- if it was never quite enough -- it was mine, and I did it well, and on most days that was sufficient. Evening came down early, the way it did in late October, the light draining from the sky as though someone had pulled a stopper. The last guests were settled by eight. I tallied the bar with Nan, checked the kitchen stores against tomorrow's needs, and sent her home. The dining room was cleared, the fires checked, the cellar hatch locked. I made my final circuit alone. Taproom fire down to coals, the grate ticking as the metal cooled. Front door bolted. The stable yard was quiet, and through the dark I could see the faint shape of the stable block where Jem would have settled the horses for the night. I looked up. On the second floor, a lit shape at Bridget's window -- her silhouette behind the glass, watching the yard as she always watched it, steady and unsleeping. I did not look long. Back in the kitchen, I banked the range and washed my hands in the basin by the door. The cold water stung, and I looked down at them -- reddened across the knuckles, rough at the fingertips, and on my right index finger a thin cut from the edge of Mr Fenton's delivery note, which I had not noticed until now. A small injury. The kind that accumulated without announcement and healed without attention and marked the difference between a woman who worked and a woman who did not. I dried my hands on the cloth and stood in the kitchen doorway. The inn was quiet in the way of a place that has been used hard and is now resting -- not peaceful, exactly, but spent. The bolt was set. The doors were locked. And I was alone, which was not the same thing as lonely except on the nights when it was, and tonight it was, and I let myself know it for the space of a single breath before I turned back to the table. The letter was where Nan had left the afternoon post, half-hidden beneath a bill from the farrier. I had seen it earlier and set it aside, because I knew the handwriting on the direction and I knew the seal, and there are some things you do not open until the day's work is done and your hands are steady. Stainforth's seal. Stainforth's hand. I picked the letter up and turned it over once. The paper was heavy, cream-coloured, the kind that announced its cost before you read a word. I broke the seal. The letter was brief. Mr Stainforth wrote the way he spoke -- measured, courteous, and perfectly clear. He had received an offer for the White Hound property from a Mr Josiah Campion of London, who wished to acquire the lease and convert the premises into a posting house of a more modern character. Mr Stainforth considered the offer a sound one. He was prepared to discuss terms with me, as the current leaseholder, as a matter of courtesy, but he wished me to understand that the decision was ultimately his to make. He proposed a meeting at his house on the fourteenth of November. If I did not attend, he would take my absence as consent to proceed. Six weeks. The same six weeks I had counted in the ledger this morning, the same margin I had been calculating and defending and scraping together tuppence by tuppence -- and none of it mattered, because Stainforth was not raising the rent. He was taking the ground out from under it. I set the letter down on the kitchen table. I looked at it for a long moment, at the careful handwriting and the courteous phrasing and the word *consent*, which was doing a great deal of work in that final sentence and doing none of it honestly. "The fourteenth, then," I said to the empty room. "I'll be there early."

# Chapter 1: The White Hound

The fire had gone cold again. I crouched before the grate in the predawn darkness, poking at the ash with more force than was strictly necessary. Whoever had banked it last night, and that would be Nan, since she's the only one besides myself who bothers with such details, had done a poor job of it. The coals were scattered, the ash too thick, and now I'd have to start from scratch while the kitchen held all the warmth of a tomb.

I built the fire the way my mother taught me: tinder first, then kindling laid just so, then the larger pieces balanced to let the air through. The flames caught and began their work, and I stood, brushing my hands on my apron. Another day at the White Hound. Another sixteen hours of managing what other people could not manage themselves.

I moved through the inn, sweeping these floors since I was tall enough to hold a broom. The taproom first, where I straightened chairs and checked that the pewter was properly arranged behind the bar. The hinge on the cellar door stuck when I tested it. It had been sticking since September, and I'd been meaning to oil it for a month. Above the third window, the damp patch had reappeared. It did this every October, regular as clockwork, no matter how many times I sealed the frame. I'd sealed it myself after Mother died, along with sanding and finishing the bar top when grief made me too restless to sit still.

Behind the bar, hanging on its nail next to the ledger, was the lease. Twenty-one years it had hung there, through my mother's tenure and now mine. The name on it was Stainforth's, not ours, had always been Stainforth's, would always be Stainforth's unless some miracle occurred that I was too practical to expect. I flicked the paper with my finger as I passed. It rustled like dry leaves.

The inn was mine in every way that mattered. I knew every creak in its floors, every draft that whispered through its walls, every guest who had ever broken a washbasin or stolen a towel. But mine in no way that signified under the law. A woman could run a business. A woman could not own one. Not if she wanted to marry, anyway, and even if she didn't, not unless some man allowed it.

"Morning," said Nan, appearing in the doorway with her arms full of linens and her cap already sliding sideways. "The Northcote boy's been sick in room seven. All over the carpet."

I looked at her. "How sick?"

"Sick enough that I've had to strip the bed and scrub the floor, and sick enough that we'll need Mrs. Hartwell's carbolic before anyone decent will sleep in there again."

"What did he eat?"

Nan shrugged, already moving toward the kitchen to heat water. "Same as everyone else. But he also had a bottle of port to himself before bed, and his complexion suggested he wasn't accustomed to it."

"Did he pay for the port?"

"He did. Though I suspect his father will have words about it when the bill arrives in London."

She was halfway to the kitchen before I thought to ask, "Is he still here?"

"Left on the first coach. Green as grass and trying to pretend he felt splendid." Nan paused in the doorway, cloth in hand. "I told him he might want to sit outside, considering."

I could always rely on Nan to think ahead. She had been managing the inn's small disasters and diplomatic emergencies since she was fourteen years old, and at twenty-one she could handle anything from a choleric guest to a drunken coachman. She brought the same brisk efficiency to turning mattresses. She was considerably more intelligent than her position suggested, though she was careful not to let it show around the guests.

The morning coach arrived at half past seven, announced by the particular blast of the horn that told me it was the Baldock route before I saw it. I stepped into the yard as the driver pulled his team to a halt, already counting passengers by habit. Four inside, two on the roof, all looking tired. They'd been traveling since before dawn.

The yard became a system. Jem appeared from the stable, moving toward the horses. He never wasted motion. Nan emerged from the inn with her arms full of clean linens for the coach. I positioned myself where I could supervise the luggage, speak to the coachman about his schedule, and keep an eye on the passengers simultaneously.

The horses were blowing hard. I could see that without Jem having to tell me, though he caught my eye and nodded toward the lead pair. They'd been pushed harder than usual between here and the last stage.

"Need twenty minutes, at least," he said, taking the nearest horse's head. "Left leader's favoring his off fore."

I nodded. Twenty minutes meant tea and bread for the passengers, and a chance to inspect their luggage for anything that might want special handling. It also meant dealing with whatever complaint the coachman was working up to. He had the look of a man with a grievance.

"Miss Dale," he said, climbing down from the box and stretching his shoulders. "I'll be wanting compensation for the delay yesterday. Cost me an hour, it did, and the passengers were none too pleased."

I looked at him. "What delay?"

"The trunk that wasn't ready when I arrived. Had to wait while your girl fetched it from upstairs, and then it wouldn't fit properly in the boot." He gestured toward a large leather trunk that was currently sitting in the yard. "Cost me my schedule, and my schedule's worth money."

"That's between you and the coaching company," I said. "I don't set your timetable or pay your wages. If you want compensation, you'll need to speak to Mr. Hartwell when he's at the Swan on Thursday. I can write you a note, if you like, explaining the circumstances."

The coachman opened his mouth, then closed it. He knew as well as I did that Mr. Hartwell would not be sympathetic to a claim based on a trunk that was difficult to load. More to the point, he knew that I knew Mr. Hartwell's schedule and his temperament. That put me on firmer ground than he'd expected.

"I'll manage the trunk myself," he muttered, bending to lift it.

The woman who owned the trunk approached me to lodge her own complaint about the delay. She was a thin creature in expensive black silk. I had little patience for her self-importance. She spoke about the coaching schedule and the hour's delay. I listened with half my attention and made appropriate noises, then watched her climb back into the coach. I would not have to see her again.

The chandler arrived as the coach was pulling away, and I knew from the way he set down his sample case that he'd come to raise his prices. He avoided meeting my eye while he arranged his wares on the table, fussing over the arrangement longer than was necessary.

"Fine morning," he said.

I waited. Men who opened with weather had usually come to deliver bad news.

"Business has been good, I trust?"

Still waiting.

"The thing is, Miss Dale," he said finally, "costs have been rising something fierce this quarter. The tallow, you see, and the wick. Even the molds are dearer now than they were at Michaelmas. I'm afraid I'll have to ask two and sixpence per dozen for the best candles."

I opened the ledger and turned it so he could see the page. "Last quarter you charged one and ninepence. The quarter before, one and eightpence." I looked up at him. "What's changed about tallow between September and October that accounts for ninepence per dozen?"

He shifted his weight. "It's the suppliers, you see. They've all raised their prices, and—"

"Mr. Greenwood in Baldock is still charging one and tenpence," I said. "I could have Jem ride over on Thursday and fetch what we need from him, if that would be more convenient."

The chandler's hands stilled on his sample case. We both knew that Mr. Greenwood in Baldock would charge me one and elevenpence if I actually appeared at his door, but we also both knew that wasn't the point. The point was that I had alternatives, and he knew I wasn't afraid to use them.

"Perhaps," he said slowly, "we could settle on two shillings. As a courtesy to an old customer."

I considered this. Two shillings was still higher than I wanted to pay, but it was low enough that I could work it into the month's accounts without making adjustments elsewhere. More importantly, something in his manner suggested that the price increase wasn't entirely his idea. There were other forces at work. Forces that might make Mr. Greenwood's prices rise just as mysteriously if I actually tried to shift my custom to Baldock.

"Two shillings," I agreed.

He packed his samples and apologized twice for the inconvenience. That told me everything I needed to know about whose idea the price increase had actually been.

I closed the ledger and allowed myself a moment of controlled fury. Then I went back to work.

By midday, the taproom held its usual collection of local farmers and traveling merchants, most of them nursing ale and speaking in the low murmur that meant business was being conducted. I was refilling pewter mugs when raised voices at the corner table caught my attention.

"—told you twice, that's my place—"

"Your place? Been sitting here since ten, I have, and—"

The argument was about a seat. A good seat, to be fair. Near the fire, with a view of the road. But hardly worth the volume they were putting into it. Both men had been drinking, though neither was what I'd call dangerous. Just stupid enough to make noise about something that could be settled with a word.

I approached their table. "Gentlemen."

They both looked up. One was red-faced and sweating, the other pale and tense around the eyes. Neither was a regular, but I'd seen them both before. Traders, probably, who stopped at the inn when business brought them through.

"The seat by the window is available," I said. "And the table near the bar. Both have excellent views and are closer to the fire than either of you are now."

The red-faced man opened his mouth to argue, then caught sight of Jem. He had appeared in the doorway between the taproom and the yard. Jem wasn't doing anything in particular, just standing there, waiting. But his presence had a way of settling disputes before they required settling.

"Right," said the pale man, gathering his coat. "Window seat, then."

The red-faced man muttered something I chose not to hear, but he moved to the table by the bar without further complaint.

Jem and I exchanged a look. No words were necessary. We both understood that these small altercations were becoming more frequent, and we both knew why. Desperation had a way of making men quarrelsome over things that would not have mattered in better times.

Late in the afternoon, a man appeared at the kitchen door. Not the front door. He knew which door to use. That told me something about his circumstances. He was thin, wearing a coat that had been military issue before someone removed the insignia and brass buttons. His boots were held together with twine and what looked like a strip of leather from a harness.

He did not ask for charity. He asked if there was work.

There was not.

I studied him for thirty seconds, the way I studied everyone who came to the inn. He held himself straight despite the obvious hunger, and his eyes met mine without desperation. He was simply a man who had once been a soldier and was now something else, looking for a way to earn his bread.

"There's soup," I said.

He nodded. "I'm grateful."

I gave him bread and the end of the pot of broth that had been simmering since morning. To make it a transaction rather than charity, I handed him a crate of empty bottles and gestured toward the yard. "Those go to the stable when you've finished."

He ate slowly, carefully. When he was finished, he carried the crate to the yard as I'd asked, then returned to set the bowl and spoon on the table.

"Good broth," he said.

It was such an ordinary remark, the sort of thing any guest might say, that it landed with unexpected weight. I nodded and took the bowl to wash it.

When I looked up, he was walking down the road, his patched boots raising small clouds of dust. I watched until I could no longer see him, thinking a thought I didn't share with anyone, not even myself. Then I went back to work.

Locking up the inn at night was a ritual I could have performed in my sleep. Check the rooms, bank the fires, bolt the doors, test the latches. Count the day's takings and lock them in the strongbox behind the bar. The bolt on the kitchen door stuck, as it did every night, and I had to lean my weight against it to make it catch. The window in the guest room above the taproom wouldn't quite close. It hadn't closed properly since a storm in August. But it was secure enough to keep out anything larger than a sparrow.

As I moved through the empty rooms, my mind kept its own accounts. What had been used today, what needed ordering tomorrow, whether the chandler's new prices would force me to raise the cost of rooms by a penny or whether I could absorb the difference until trade improved. The mathematics of survival, calculated in halfpenny increments.

In the taproom, I wiped down the bar one final time, though it was already clean. My cloth caught on a gouge in the wood that I hadn't noticed before, or had noticed and forgotten. Probably from the keg Jem had dropped last week, though it might have been a chair leg or a guest's boot. Such things happened. The wood bore the marks of twenty years of use, and would bear twenty more if I was fortunate.

I thought about Jem's missing arm, not with pity but with the practical calculation of whether he could manage the new barrel delivery on Thursday. He'd managed every delivery for the past two years without difficulty, but the barrels were getting heavier as the brewery raised its prices and compensated with larger sizes. Thursday's delivery would be the test.

The lease was in Stainforth's name, and always had been. I knew that. My mother had known that. My grandmother had known that when she'd first hung it on its nail twenty-one years ago. Three women running a business on a man's sufferance, and none of us had possessed the wit or the option to change it.

I wrung out the cloth and hung it on the hook my mother had installed beside the sink. Her hands had performed this same motion the night before she died. The same cloth, or one like it, the same twist of the wrist to squeeze out the water. The memory arrived and departed without ceremony. I had work to finish.

The inn was locked, the guests settled, the day's business concluded. I stood alone in the kitchen, listening to the silence that followed sixteen hours of voices, footsteps, and the endless small collisions of people managing to live alongside one another for the space of a meal or a night's lodging.

It was then that I found the letter.

It lay on the bar where someone had placed it during the day's bustle, though I hadn't seen it arrive. The seal caught the lamplight. Stainforth's seal, heavy and official and utterly unmistakable. I knew what it meant before I touched it. I'd been expecting it for months.

I did not open it.

Instead, I picked it up, feeling its weight. Heavier than ordinary correspondence, with the particular heft of legal documents. I placed it on the shelf behind the bar, next to the ledger where tomorrow's accounts waited to be tallied. The letter would be there in the morning. Whatever Mr. Stainforth had to say about the White Hound's lease could wait until I'd had my tea and built up the fire and prepared myself to hear it.

I blew out the lamp and climbed the stairs to my room, leaving the letter in the darkness below.

The ink had bled through to the second page of the ledger, which meant I'd pressed too hard on the nib again, which meant the numbers were worse than I'd hoped. Six weeks. Six weeks until the quarter's rent came due, and the column of figures I'd been staring at since before dawn did not rearrange themselves into a more agreeable sum no matter how long I let the candle gutter over them. I closed the ledger and set my palm flat on the cover as though I could press the trouble back inside. The kitchen was cold. Not the kind of cold that announces itself -- the kind that has been sitting in the walls all night, patient and familiar, waiting for you to notice. The fire I'd built twenty minutes ago was only now beginning to argue with it. I pulled my sleeves down over my wrists, tucked the ledger onto its shelf behind the flour bin, and got on with the morning. There is a particular kind of want that lives in the body rather than the mind -- not a wish, not a daydream, but a low ache that settles behind the breastbone when the house is dark and still and you are the only thing moving in it. I wanted -- well. What I wanted had no place in the accounts. I wanted one morning where the numbers came out right without my having to shave a halfpenny off every line to make them balance. I wanted to sit at this table with a cup of tea that I had not made myself and feel, just for a quarter of an hour, that someone else was minding the gates. I did not sit. I checked the kitchen fire, then crossed through to the taproom and knelt at the second hearth. The ash was cold and pale. I raked it out, laid the kindling, struck the flint, blew until the flame caught. Behind the bar I counted bottles by touch in the half-dark -- gin, brandy, the last of the decent claret. Fewer than yesterday. I noted it. Then I wiped down the bar with a rag that needed replacing, tallied the previous night's take against the chits Nan had left in the tin, and found the discrepancy I'd expected: fourpence short, which meant Mr. Howell had talked his way into a second measure again and Nan had let him. The windows were rimed at their edges. Beyond the glass the yard was grey, the cobbles slicked with overnight damp. Nothing moved out there yet except the stable cat, who had opinions about the cold and was expressing them from the mounting block. I adjusted the fire screen, straightened two chairs, and set the dining room for breakfast with the efficiency of a woman who has done this several thousand times and no longer requires light to find the salt cellar. Jem was in the stable yard when I opened the back door to check the weather. He had the big bay's bridle across his knee and was working at the cheekpiece with his one hand and the blunt hook he'd rigged to the stump of the other. He did not look up. He did not need to. The yard was swept, the water trough was full, and the hay net outside the first stall had been rehung at the height the coach horses preferred. I noted all of this in the time it took to draw a breath, and I went back inside without a word. Nan came through the kitchen door a few minutes later, pins half-set in her hair, apron already knotted. She took her place behind the bar as though she had materialised there. We had done this before. Many hundreds of times. The inn opened the way a clock strikes -- not because anyone announced it, but because every part moved when it was supposed to. The London coach was late, which was not unusual. What was unusual was the shortness of it: two passengers instead of the expected four, and both cross enough to strip paint. The first was a stout woman in a travelling cloak who had a trunk with a split hinge and wanted the world to know about it. I sent Jem to deal with the trunk before she could get properly started, and the sight of him lifting it one-handed onto the barrow silenced her more effectively than anything I could have said. The second passenger went upstairs without comment. I did not learn his name. I did not need to. The coachman found me in the yard. He was called Burrell, and I had known him four years. He had the particular stiffness of a man who has been on the box since Islington and wants to complain but knows he has only two minutes in which to do it. "North road's gone to ruin above Hatfield," he said. "Ruts deep enough to lose a wheel. Someone ought to speak to the turnpike trust." "Someone has. Twice. They've promised gravel before the frost." He snorted. "They promised gravel last year." "Then I suggest you keep to the left side past the Wrestlers. It's firmer ground." I stepped back from the horses. "Anything else?" He shook his head, climbed back up, and was gone. The yard settled into the particular quiet that follows a coach's departure -- the dust still turning in the air, the smell of horse and road lingering, and then nothing but the sparrows in the eaves. Nan had been wiping the same stretch of bar through all of this, one eye on the door. I noticed. I said nothing. The chandler came at half past ten, which was his custom, carrying the month's order of tallow candles in a crate on his shoulder and the expression of a man who believed he was about to get away with something. His name was Ferris. He was not unpleasant. He was merely predictable. "Morning, Miss Dale. Three dozen, as agreed. I'll need one and four the dozen this month." I set down the cloth I'd been using on the dining room table and looked at him. "You charged one and two last month." "Tallow's gone up." "Tallow's gone up a farthing the pound since March. You've raised your price twopence the dozen in seven months. That's a margin of sixpence you didn't have in April, Mr. Ferris, and I doubt Mrs. Partridge at the King's Arms is paying it." He shifted the crate on his shoulder. "Mrs. Partridge orders twice what you do." "Mrs. Partridge waters her gin and hasn't scrubbed her kitchen floor since the peace. I'll give you one and three, and I'll take four dozen instead of three, and you'll have the money today rather than on account." A pause. The crate shifted again. I could see him calculating -- the walk back to his cart with thirty-six unsold candles against the coins I was offering him now. "One and three," he said. "Set them by the cellar hatch." He set them by the cellar hatch. He left. The candles stayed. I allowed myself one moment -- standing alone in the kitchen with the door still open and the October air coming through it -- to feel the small, precise satisfaction of having paid less than he wanted and more than he deserved. It was not triumph. It was arithmetic. But it was mine. The thought snagged, as it sometimes did, on the word. Mine. The lease, the accounts, the negotiations, the early mornings and the last-locked doors -- all of it was mine only so long as I remained as I was. Unmarried. Unattached. Under the law, a husband would own every farthing of it the moment the register was signed, and no amount of tallow arithmetic would change that. I had refused two proposals for this reason, and I had stopped entertaining the possibility of a third. It was not bitterness. It was the same calculation I had just performed with Ferris, only the stakes were the whole of my life instead of twopence a dozen. I closed the door and went to check the cellar stores. He came around the back, as the sign asked. I saw him through the kitchen window before he knocked -- young, thin, standing on the step with the careful stillness of someone deciding whether he was welcome enough to try the door. His coat was too large for him and had been mended at the shoulder with thread that did not match. His boots were coming apart at the welt. He could not have been older than twenty. I opened the door before he knocked. He startled, then steadied. His eyes went to my face, then to my apron, then back. Not threatening. Measuring. "Have you eaten?" I asked. A beat. "Not recently, ma'am." "Kitchen, not taproom. Sit there." I pointed to the bench by the hearth. He sat. I cut bread, ladled broth from the pot I kept going through the day, and set both in front of him without ceremony. He ate the way hungry people eat -- not fast, but thoroughly, as though each mouthful required his full attention. He held the spoon carefully, the way you hold something that might be taken away. When he finished, he stood and touched his forehead. "Thank you, ma'am. I'm obliged." "Where are you headed?" "North," he said. Then, as though that were insufficient: "There's family in Nottingham. Or there was." I nodded. He went out the way he'd come, around the side of the building and onto the road, and I watched through the window until he was past the gate. His boots had been army issue. I had seen enough of them to know. The leather was cracked straight through the sole, and he had stuffed paper into the gap to keep out the wet. He was somebody's son, walking north on paper soles, and the broth I had given him would last him until evening if he was careful and not at all if he wasn't. I washed the bowl. I dried it. I put it back on the shelf with the others. In the taproom, two men who had been sitting too long over too little ale had found something to disagree about. I heard the pitch of it change -- the shift from grumbling to the particular sharp note that precedes broken furniture -- and crossed from the kitchen in four strides. "Gentlemen. You'll settle it outside or you'll settle your accounts and go. Choose now." They chose to go quiet. Nan, behind the bar, had not moved. She did not need to. The afternoon passed the way afternoons pass at a coaching inn between rushes -- in small tasks that accumulate into hours without marking themselves. I reset the rooms the morning passengers had used. I reconciled the kitchen stores against tomorrow's needs. I wrote out the week's order for the brewer and left it on the bar for the drayman. Nan polished glasses. Jem reshod the grey cob in the yard, the ring of the hammer carrying through the open window with the steadiness of a clock. No incidents. No drama. Only the work, and the ordinary weight of it. Evening came on early, the way it does in late October -- not falling so much as thickening, the grey light turning to dusk while your hands are still busy. I served the remaining guests their supper, cleared the plates, washed up, and began the closing circuit. Taproom fire raked down to coals and the screen set in front. Bar tallied, the tin locked, the key on its hook. Cellar hatch bolted. Dining room chairs pushed in, the cloth folded for tomorrow. Kitchen banked and the chimney damper half-closed. I moved through it all with the ease of long practice, the inn settling around me the way a house does when it has been used hard and is finally permitted to rest. My hands at the end of the day were what they always were -- reddened from cold water and the scrubbing brush, the knuckles rough, a small cut on my index finger where the edge of Ferris's delivery note had caught me when I wasn't paying attention. I noticed it the way you notice a bruise you don't remember getting. It stung when I flexed my hand. I flexed it again anyway, because the front door still needed locking. I crossed the yard on the last circuit. The stable was quiet -- Jem would have settled the horses and gone to his room above the tack store. The cobbles were dark with damp. Above me, on the second floor, there was a lit shape at Bridget's window: the small, upright silhouette I had seen a thousand times, watching the yard the way she always watched it, missing nothing. I did not look long. I bolted the front door. Set the bar. Checked the latch on the yard gate. Came back through the kitchen and stood for a moment with my hand on the back of the chair where I'd sat that morning with the ledger. The inn was quiet. I was alone in it, the way I was alone in it every night, and most nights I did not let myself notice. Tonight I noticed. The letter was on the kitchen table. It had not been there at supper. Nan must have taken the afternoon post and left it for me, and I had walked past it twice without seeing it. The seal was dark red, pressed deep into the paper. I knew the hand on the direction before I read the name -- the cramped, precise letters, the particular way he formed his capital D. Stainforth. I picked it up. Turned it over once. Broke the seal. The letter was brief. Stainforth's letters were always brief. He did not waste words any more than he wasted advantage. *Miss Dale -- I write to inform you that I have received an offer of considerable interest regarding the property on which the White Hound presently stands. The gentleman in question proposes a use for the premises that I believe would better serve the needs of the parish and the traffic of the road. I would not, of course, act without first extending to you the courtesy of a meeting. I shall expect you at Stainforth House on the fifth of November, at which time we may discuss the terms under which your present lease might be concluded. I trust you will appreciate that this matter cannot be indefinitely deferred.* I read it twice. I set it down on the table, smoothed the fold with my thumb, and looked at it lying there on the scrubbed wood next to the salt cellar and the stub of the morning's candle. He had a buyer. He had a date. He expected me to come to his house and discuss the terms under which I would agree to lose everything my grandmother had built. I stood in the empty kitchen with the fire burned low and the draught coming under the door, and I said, to no one, to the room, to the letter on the table: "Then I suppose Mr. Stainforth had better be told what my terms are."

THE LAST RETREAT

Chapter One: The White Hound

The grate was dead. I rebuilt it.

Whoever had banked it last night had done a pitiful job of it — half-ash, half-hope, neither sufficient. I didn’t waste the time to wonder who. There were only three of us and the odds were even enough.

— — —

Forty years of coaching traffic had worn the flagstones uneven. Forty years had smoked the ceiling beams to the colour of old bone. Forty years, and the place still stood, which was more than I could say for the hinge on the cellar door.

The White Hound was not beautiful. It had never pretended to be. The taproom had six tables, a bar of elm I’d sanded myself after Jem pointed out that the old pine had gone soft enough to splinter, and a ceiling low enough that any man over six feet ducked on instinct when he crossed the threshold. The bar top was good now. Smooth, the grain running even, the knots sealed against spilled ale. I’d worked it on a Tuesday in March because March is the dead month on the Great North Road — too late for Christmas traffic, too early for the summer coaches — and there had been nothing else pressing except the damp patch above the third window and seventeen other things I couldn’t fix without spending money I didn’t have. I’d done the bar instead. There is a satisfaction in fixing what can be fixed.

The damp patch was still there.

The lease hung on its nail behind the bar, where my grandmother had put it in the year I was born, which tells you how long we had been holding the thing with both hands and hoping. Stainforth’s name was at the top of it. Abel Dale’s name below that, though Abel Dale had been dead sixteen years and had never, in any practical sense, run a single day of this inn. My name was nowhere on it. Three women had kept the White Hound standing through two wars, a failed harvest, and my father’s cheerful inability to keep his accounts — and none of our names were on the lease.

I noticed the nail needed straightening. I straightened it. I hung the lease back on it and turned to get the bread going.

— — —

Nan came in at half past five tying her cap and saying, without preamble and without looking up from the strings, “Coach company’s switching agents. Heard it last night from Dawes at the Bull. Chipping Norton man, apparently. New rates.”

“New rates up or down.”

“Up.” She tied the final bow, tucked a strand of hair that wouldn’t behave, and reached past me for the apron on the hook. “Dawes said the Hatfield coach is already paying sixpence more a head.”

“Dawes is a gossip.”

“Dawes is a gossip who hasn’t been wrong yet.” She pulled the apron over her head and turned to face me. “I’m only saying.”

I cracked the first egg against the rim of the bowl. “If the rates change, the rates change. We’ll adjust.”

“We always adjust.” Nan said it pleasantly, which was worse than if she’d said it with a complaint inside it.

— — —

The morning coach arrived at seven with nine passengers, three trunks, a birdcage, and a coachman in a temper about the state of the road past Stevenage. The yard became itself in the way it always did when a coach came in — noise and motion and everyone knowing their place without being told. Jem was already at the horses and the lead pair were haltered and the first trunk was down from the roof and the passengers were stepping out blinking into the cold and the coachman was calling for water and Nan was there with the manifest and the yard smell was horse and leather and dead October leaves, all of it in motion, all of it working, the way a clock works — you don’t see the mechanism, you only see the hands moving.

Nine passengers. I counted.

“Left rear wheel wants looking at,” Jem said, when I came past with a trunk for the luggage room. He said it to the wheel, not to me.

I already knew. I’d heard it come in. “How bad.”

“Won’t make York.” He didn’t say it with any particular feeling. It was a wheel. It wanted attention. These were the facts.

“I’ll tell him.”

“Tell him before he loads back up or we’ll be having a worse conversation.”

We were agreed. That was all.

— — —

The coachman’s name was Hartley and he was working himself toward a scene about the trunk. It belonged to a woman travelling to Biggleswade, was the size of a small cottage, and was currently in my luggage room because Hartley had declined to carry it up himself on account of it being, in his opinion, the woman’s own business to make arrangements for her own property, to which I said, with every courtesy I owned: “Mr Hartley. You may speak with Mr Pelling at the coaching office in Hatfield on Thursday — he’s there before ten — and he will tell you what the company policy is about passenger luggage. Or I can tell you now, which will save you the journey.”

Hartley looked at me.

“It’s the coachman’s responsibility to the first interior door,” I said. “Mr Pelling was very clear on this in his July letter. I have it, if you’d like to see it.”

He would not like to see it. He picked up the trunk.

— — —

The woman from Biggleswade had requested the trunk be brought to her room, immediately, and had communicated this request with the confidence of someone who had never personally moved anything heavier than a teacup. I arranged for it. She said the room was cold, which it was not — I had laid the fire myself — and that the sheets wanted airing, which they did not, and that she hoped the establishment would be rather more attentive than it had yet appeared. I said I hoped so too. She gathered as much composure as she could locate and swept into the room. I closed the door behind her with exquisite care.

— — —

The chandler came at eleven. His name was Murchett. He’d supplied the White Hound for four years, and for the first three of those years the arrangement had been satisfactory, and for the fourth it had become an occasion for him to stand in my kitchen rearranging his sample box and telling me that costs had risen.

He set the box on the table. He looked at the window. He put his hands in his coat pockets and then took them out again.

“It’s the tallow,” he said. “Prices out of London.”

“You told me that in August.”

“It’s got worse.”

“By how much.”

He told me. He looked at the window again when he said it, which is how I knew the number was generous in his favour.

I went to the shelf and took down the ledger. I opened it on the table between us, my finger on the column for October last year, which showed me his margin at the old rate and showed me, more importantly, that I knew what his margin was. “Mr Murchett.”

“Miss Dale.”

“I can pay the August rate and I can go to Baldock.”

“Baldock’s too far.”

“It’s eleven miles. I can send Jem.”

He was quiet for a moment. His hand settled on the counter, not quite gripping it.

“Fourteen miles,” he said. “Baldock’s fourteen miles.”

“Eleven,” I said. “I’ve done it.”

He looked at the ledger. He looked at me. He said, “I might manage to hold at the September figure. Given the volume you take.”

“The August figure,” I said, “given the volume I take.”

He picked up the sample box. He moved it six inches to the left and back again, which accomplished nothing for him or for the box. “August, then. Miss Dale.”

“Thank you, Mr Murchett.”

The ledger went back on the shelf. The anger was there — it always was, the low familiar heat of being squeezed by every man who thought a woman alone wouldn’t know her own accounts — and I named it to myself and moved past it. I went to check on the bread.

— — —

The two men in the taproom had been at the bench by the fire since the late-morning coach came in, which made it three hours, and in that time they had consumed enough of the ordinary ale to make the dispute about who had scuffed whose boot feel considerably more significant than it actually was. The taller one had the other’s collar by the time I came through the door. A stool scraped. I knew the sound of a stool scraping.

“Put him down,” I said.

The tall one sat down.

His companion rubbed his collar and looked as though he might say something, and then looked at my face and decided against it. One of them muttered something into his ale. I heard it and chose not to.

I went back behind the bar. Jem was in the far corner of the room, leaning on the wall with his arms crossed — his arm, rather, and the stump of the other — and he looked at me and I looked at him, and that look contained everything: the frequency of it, the tedium of it, the question of how men managed to make a quarrel about a boot into an occasion requiring a woman to stand in a doorway like an army.

— — —

He came to the kitchen door at half past three, just after the afternoon coaches had gone through and before the evening rush, when the yard was quiet and the smell of the stockpot had been working at the air for two hours. He knocked. Not with the confidence of a guest, who would have gone to the front — with the particular hesitance of a man who knew which door to use and wasn’t sure he had the right.

I opened it.

His coat was the right cut for a soldier — old infantry, by the collar — but it had been let out at the shoulders, which meant it hadn’t been his coat originally, or he had lost weight since it was. His boots were repaired, the toe-stitching recent, the heel leather not — someone had done what they could for them, and it showed. He stood straight in the doorway, his hands at his sides, his eyes at my shoulder rather than my face.

He did not ask for charity. He asked if there was work.

I looked at him for thirty seconds. “I’ve a floor wants sweeping in the luggage room,” I said. “The broom is on the left side of the door. When you’re done, come in.”

He swept the floor. I ladled bread and a bowl of stockpot onto the table and said nothing while I shaped the next morning’s loaves, and he ate, and we did not perform anything for each other.

After a while, he said, “Road was frozen past Stevenage.”

“It’s been this cold since Sunday.”

He nodded. He ate the last of the bread. He didn’t say it was good, and I didn’t say anything about giving it, and we were both satisfied with this.

He finished the bread and thanked me and went. I watched his boots go through the kitchen door — the good stitching on the right, the worn heel on the left — and then they were gone, and the yard was empty, and the afternoon light was already failing.

I went back to work.

— — —

Closing up is a thing that happens by the body, not the mind. The bolt on the front door has swollen with the autumn damp and requires a firm shoulder and a particular angle; I leaned and pushed and felt it give with a scrape of iron on iron. The window in the taproom that has never quite hung straight caught in its frame when I pulled it and let in a thread of cold air past the latch, as it always does, until I reached up and eased the top corner into the groove. The strongbox behind the bar opened with a click of three tumblers that my mother taught me when I was twelve, and closed with the same, and the sound of it in the empty room is the same sound it always is — small and definite and mine. The fire in the kitchen wanted banking, not killing; I pushed the coals together and set the guard. The candles in the taproom went out in order, left to right. The last one smelled of tallow for half a minute after.

I wiped down the bar. It was already clean — Nan had done it after the last customers went — but I wiped it anyway, the cloth moving in the same circles, the elm smooth under my hands. There was a gouge near the far end, an inch long, just where the bar curved. I ran my thumb over it. A keg, I thought. Thursday’s delivery. The front left corner of the big barrel, the way Jem tilts it when he’s bringing it through alone — he tilts it rather than carry it, which is the sensible solution, but it catches the corner sometimes. I’d have to talk to him about the Thursday delivery. Whether he could manage the heavy cask, whether we needed to ask for the smaller measure.

The lease was on its nail.

Three women on a man’s sufferance. My grandmother, who had held this place for twenty years through a husband’s death and the mill closures and the bad winter of ‘84 when there was no passing traffic for a week and she’d fed the staff from the cellar stores and not lost a single booking. My mother, who had finished the dinner service the night she died. And me.

I wrung out the cloth. I hung it on the hook.

The hook was one my mother put in.

— — —

The letter was on the shelf beside the ledger, where I’d set it when it arrived that afternoon. I’d seen the seal before I’d seen the direction, which is how I’d known, and I’d set it on the shelf and gone back to the afternoon’s business, and I’d been knowing ever since.

Stainforth’s seal. His man had brought it, not the post.

I looked at it for a moment. I picked it up. The wax was smooth under my thumb, the device pressed clean — he was a careful man, Stainforth, in all his correspondence, nothing rushed, nothing careless, everything managed. That was the particular quality of his threat. Not volume. Weight.

I set the letter on the shelf beside the ledger.

I took the candle and went to bed.

Tallied the bar stock first, because the night's receipts never balanced themselves.

The ledger lay open on the kitchen table, the figures from yesterday's coach trade entered in my own hand -- two shillings four from the taproom, eight shillings six from the dining room, the night's rooms at the agreed quarterly rate that came out, when divided by occupancy, to something I preferred not to examine too closely before the fire had taken hold. I added the column again. The answer was the same as the first time, which meant it was right and I had no excuse to keep adding it.

Six weeks, give or take, before the quarter rent came due and Stainforth's agent came round with his ledger and his pleasant, patient manner. I closed the book.

The kitchen fire had been built up an hour ago, banked with the good coal that cost more than it had in September and would cost more still by December. I checked the draw, adjusted the damper by a quarter turn, and moved to the back door to bring in the first of the morning's wood. The yard was still dark, the cobbles sheened with frost that had not yet decided whether to melt or deepen. My breath came white in the cold air. I stacked the wood against the wall beside the range, three trips, and went through to the taproom.

The taproom smelled of last night's ale and tallow and the particular closeness of a room that has held a dozen people and now holds none. I opened the shutter on the east window a hand's width -- enough to let the air move, not enough to drive out what warmth remained. The fire there was ash and one dull coal. I rebuilt it from the grate up: paper, kindling, the smaller split logs from the box by the settle, the larger piece across the top. Struck the steel. The paper caught.

Bar stock next. Two hogsheads of the Hertford brown ale, one of the London porter, the small cask of cider that the coachmen favoured and which I ordered in greater quantity than I could reliably sell because the coachmen talked to each other and their preference was worth more than the margin I lost on the unsold gallons. Spirits: the gin decanted and the bottle replaced. The brandy, which I kept behind the secondary shelf since the French peace had not yet brought the price down to where I could serve it without watching the measure. I wrote down what needed ordering and tucked the note under the edge of the till.

The last night's accounts I had already tallied -- the bar stock confirmed what the receipts said. Nothing was missing. Nothing had been helped off the premises by someone who thought my attention was elsewhere. It never was.

I pulled my sleeves down over my wrists and went back to the kitchen.

Jem was in the stable yard when I looked out through the kitchen window. The sky had gone from black to the dark grey that precedes light without yet being it, and in that grey I could make out his shape at the near stall: straightening tack, moving along the rail in the methodical way of a man who knows every hook and strap by touch. He had not needed me to ask him. The morning had asked him, and he had answered it. I watched him for a moment. His one good hand moved efficiently along the leather, checking, adjusting, passing on.

Nan came through the kitchen door perhaps ten minutes later, cap not yet pinned, apron already tied. She went to the range without pausing, checked the heat, and shifted the heavy kettle from the side hook to the main bar. Then she took her place at the kitchen shelf where the breakfast provisions waited and began setting out what she would need. Neither of us spoke. The morning had its own language and we had been speaking it together long enough not to need another.

The first sound of the coach came before I expected it -- the horn at the half-mile post was late by my reckoning, which meant the coach itself was not late but early, which was unusual enough that I went to the front door to look. The London coach came through the gate at a pace that suggested the horses had been pushed on the last stretch, the coachman up on the box with his hat pulled against the wind and an expression I could read from twenty yards: a man who has been managing difficulties since before dawn and has opinions about it.

Two passengers, not four. I counted the figures descending before they had fully descended. The first, a woman of perhaps forty-five in a good travelling pelisse, came down the step already frowning at the trunk that Jem was lifting from the boot. The second passenger, a young man with a hat pulled low, went straight past me to the dining room door without a word, which suited everyone.

The woman's trunk had a split hinge -- I saw it as Jem set it down on the yard stones, the brass bracket half-sheared away from the wood, the lid sitting crooked on its frame. She saw me looking.

"It was not in this condition when I boarded," she said. The implication was that the inn and everything connected to it bore responsibility for the roads, the coach, the hinge, and the general unreliability of the world.

"Jem," I said.

He had already crouched to look at the bracket. He glanced up at me, gave the smallest nod. He would manage it.

"There's a carpenter in Hatfield who can have a proper bracket fitted by this afternoon," I told the woman, "or Jem can strap it now and it will hold to wherever you're going. Which is more useful to you?"

She looked at me, then at the trunk, then at Jem -- who had, by this point, already produced a length of good leather strap and was threading it through the intact bracket with the matter-of-fact efficiency of a man who has done this before. Her frown shifted from outrage toward something more like reassessment.

"The strap," she said, and went inside.

The coachman came around from the horses, which the yard boy had taken. He was a solid man, sixtyish, with a face that had been weathered past all expression except the functional ones. He had complaints, but he was a man who expected no resolution from them -- he offered them the way a butcher's accounts are offered: not for negotiation, just for the record.

"The road above Hatfield," he said. "Past the second tollgate. Ruts cut deep enough to break a wheel if you take them wrong in the dark."

"How deep."

"Wheel deep, near the verge. Some fool's been driving a loaded wagon off the road surface and cut channels. Happened since last week."

"I'll send word to the turnpike trust this morning," I said. "I can't do more than that before you come back through."

"No," he agreed. "Well." He looked back at his horses, then at me. "Same ruts both sides of the gate or only south."

"I'll ask."

That was the end of it. He had more road to cover and I had an inn to open. We both knew this and neither of us wasted further words on ceremony. He went back to his box.

The yard cleared in the way yards clear after a coach has discharged itself: suddenly, all at once, and then completely. The woman with the trunk had gone inside. Jem had finished the strap and was leading the coach horses toward the water trough. The young man with the hat had not reappeared. Nan was behind the bar when I came back through the taproom, cloth in one hand, watching the front door with the particular quality of attention she used when something was worth noting. I noted that she was noting it, and moved on.

The chandler arrived at half past ten. I heard his cart in the yard before I heard him at the door, the particular rattle of stacked wooden boxes that I had been hearing every month for four years and had learned to identify the way you learn to identify any regular sound that has financial consequence.

He came through the kitchen door -- I had told him once to use the trade entrance and he had agreed immediately and continued to use the kitchen door -- and set his box on the table with the air of a man who has been generous and knows it.

"Morning, Miss Dale." He named his price: fourpence a dozen for the tallow candles, up a farthing from last month.

I looked at the box. I looked at the delivery note he had set beside it. I said, "Threepence halfpenny."

He had a speech prepared about tallow prices, about the cost of the rendering, about what the other establishments on this stretch of road were paying. I let him give most of it. Some of it was true.

"Threepence three-farthings," he said, when he had finished.

"I know what Hartley's at the Spread Eagle pays you," I said. "I know what the new place outside Welwyn pays you. Threepence halfpenny is more than Hartley's and less than Welwyn, and you pass this yard twice a month regardless of whether I buy from you or send to the merchant in Stevenage, which means your cost to deliver here is nothing."

A silence.

"Threepence halfpenny," I said again.

He picked up the delivery note. He wrote the adjusted figure in the margin. He left without the small pleasantry about the weather that had previously ended our transactions, which meant he was irritated, which meant I had found the floor.

After he went I wrote the same figure in the margin of my own copy of the delivery note, folded it, and set it with the morning's correspondence. My index finger had caught the paper edge as I folded it. A small cut, nothing. I noticed it the way you notice such things: once, briefly, and then not at all.

The thought came the way working thoughts came -- between the chandler and the next task, not summoned, not dwelt upon. The ledger with its six weeks. The chandler's margin that I knew and he knew I knew. All of it possible because the inn was mine to negotiate for, and the inn was mine only so long as it stayed mine, and the one action that would end that was the one action most women in my position were expected to take as the natural and obvious solution to everything I was managing. I had made this calculation before. I made it again, quickly, came to the same answer, and put it away. Nan needed the luncheon provisions brought up from the cellar.

He was standing outside the kitchen door, not knocking.

I saw him through the window while I was crossing to the larder. He had come around the back as the notice on the gate asked -- this much was correct, this much meant he could read or had been told -- and he was standing on the kitchen step in the particular way of someone who has decided to knock and then, at the moment of knocking, has reconsidered. Not threatening. Assessing. He was young, twenty at most, thin in the way of a man who has been eating irregularly for long enough that thin has become his shape. His coat had been good once. His boots were coming apart at the welt, the left one worse than the right, and the stitching at the toe had gone entirely.

He stood there for another moment, looking at the door without looking at it. Then he raised his hand and knocked.

I opened the door.

He was not what he had looked like through glass. Closer, he was younger. His face was bony and careful, the face of a man practising stillness.

"Have you eaten," I said.

Not a question, quite. He understood this.

"Not -- no. Not recently."

"Kitchen," I said. "Not the taproom. Sit there."

I gestured to the end of the kitchen table, away from the fire and away from the door through to the bar. He understood this too. He sat where I pointed and folded his hands on the table and waited with the patience of someone who has learned to make himself small in the spaces other people allow him.

The bread was from this morning. I cut two slices, brought them with the pot of dripping from the larder shelf. The broth had been on since daybreak -- I ladled a bowl and set it in front of him.

He ate without hurrying, which told me something. Men who have been hungry a long time sometimes cannot slow down and sometimes have learned how to. He had learned.

When the bowl was empty he looked up. "Thank you," he said. Plain, not effusive. Good.

I was already moving to clear the larder shelves for the afternoon's ordering. "Where are you headed."

He named a town north of here, maybe thirty miles. His mother's people, something in the way he said it that suggested uncertainty about his welcome.

"The road's passable," I said. "Past Hatfield there are ruts on the left side after the second gate. Keep to the right of the centre."

"Right of centre," he said. "Yes."

He stood, pushed in the chair, and picked up his coat from where he'd set it on the bench. He had good instincts about not staying longer than was offered. He went out through the kitchen door and I heard his footsteps on the cobbles, and then the gate, and then not at all.

I cleared the bowl and wiped down the end of the table and moved to the afternoon's accounts.

The trouble in

THE LAST RETREAT

Chapter One: The White Hound

The grate was dead. I rebuilt it.

Whoever had banked it last night had done a pitiful job of it. Half ash, half hope, neither sufficient. I didn’t waste the time to wonder who. There were only three of us and the odds were even enough.

— — —

Forty years of coaching traffic had worn the flagstones uneven. Forty years had smoked the ceiling beams to the colour of old bone. Forty years, and the place still stood.

The White Hound was not beautiful. It had never pretended to be. The taproom had six tables, a bar of elm I’d sanded myself after Jem pointed out that the old pine had gone soft enough to splinter, and a ceiling low enough that any man over six feet ducked on instinct when he crossed the threshold. The bar top was good now. Smooth, the grain running even, the knots sealed against spilled ale. I’d worked it on a Tuesday in March because March is the dead month on the Great North Road. Too late for Christmas traffic, too early for the summer coaches. There had been nothing else pressing except the damp patch above the third window and seventeen other things I couldn’t fix without spending money I didn’t have. I’d done the bar instead. There is a satisfaction in fixing what can be fixed.

The damp patch was still there.

The lease hung on its nail behind the bar, where my grandmother had put it in the year I was born. Stainforth’s name was at the top of it. Abel Dale’s name below that, though Abel Dale had been dead sixteen years and had never, in any practical sense, run a single day of this inn. My name was nowhere on it. Three women had kept the White Hound standing through two wars, a failed harvest, and my father’s cheerful inability to keep his accounts. None of our names were on the lease.

I noticed the nail needed straightening. I straightened it. I hung the lease back on it and turned to get the bread going.

— — —

Nan came in at half past five tying her cap and saying, without preamble and without looking up from the strings, “Coach company’s switching agents. Heard it last night from Dawes at the Bull. Chipping Norton man, apparently. New rates.”

“New rates up or down.”

“Up.” She tied the final bow, tucked a strand of hair that wouldn’t behave, and reached past me for the apron on the hook. “Dawes said the Hatfield coach is already paying sixpence more a head.”

“Dawes is a gossip.”

“Dawes is a gossip who hasn’t been wrong yet.” She pulled the apron over her head and turned to face me. “I’m only saying.”

I cracked the first egg against the rim of the bowl. “If the rates change, the rates change. We’ll adjust.”

“We always adjust.” Nan said it pleasantly.

— — —

The morning coach arrived at seven with nine passengers, three trunks, a birdcage, and a coachman in a temper about the state of the road past Stevenage. The yard became itself when a coach came in. Noise and motion and everyone knowing their place without being told. Jem was already at the horses and the lead pair were haltered and the first trunk was down from the roof and the passengers were stepping out blinking into the cold and the coachman was calling for water and Nan was there with the manifest and the yard smell was horse and leather and dead October leaves, all of it in motion, all of it working.

Nine passengers. I counted.

“Left rear wheel wants looking at,” Jem said, when I came past with a trunk for the luggage room. He said it to the wheel, not to me.

I already knew. I’d heard it come in. “How bad.”

“Won’t make York.” He didn’t say it with any particular feeling. It was a wheel. It wanted attention. These were the facts.

“I’ll tell him.”

“Tell him before he loads back up or we’ll be having a worse conversation.”

We were agreed. That was all.

— — —

The coachman’s name was Hartley and he was working himself toward a scene about the trunk. It belonged to a woman travelling to Biggleswade, was the size of a small cottage, and was currently in my luggage room because Hartley had declined to carry it up himself on account of it being, in his opinion, the woman’s own business to make arrangements for her own property, to which I said, with every courtesy I owned: “Mr Hartley. You may speak with Mr Pelling at the coaching office in Hatfield on Thursday, he’s there before ten, and he will tell you what the company policy is about passenger luggage. Or I can tell you now, which will save you the journey.”

Hartley looked at me.

“It’s the coachman’s responsibility to the first interior door,” I said. “Mr Pelling was very clear on this in his July letter. I have it, if you’d like to see it.”

He would not like to see it. He picked up the trunk.

— — —

The woman from Biggleswade had requested the trunk be brought to her room, immediately, and had communicated this request with confidence. I arranged for it. She said the room was cold, which it was not, since I had laid the fire myself, and that the sheets wanted airing, which they did not, and that she hoped the establishment would be rather more attentive than it had yet appeared. I said I hoped so too. She gathered as much composure as she could locate and swept into the room. I closed the door behind her with exquisite care.

— — —

The chandler came at eleven. His name was Murchett. He’d supplied the White Hound for four years, and for the first three of those years the arrangement had been satisfactory, and for the fourth it had become an occasion for him to stand in my kitchen rearranging his sample box and telling me that costs had risen.

He set the box on the table. He looked at the window. He put his hands in his coat pockets and then took them out again.

“It’s the tallow,” he said. “Prices out of London.”

“You told me that in August.”

“It’s got worse.”

“By how much.”

He told me. He looked at the window again when he said it, which is how I knew the number was generous in his favour.

I went to the shelf and took down the ledger. I opened it on the table between us, my finger on the column for October last year, which showed me his margin at the old rate and showed me, more importantly, that I knew what his margin was. “Mr Murchett.”

“Miss Dale.”

“I can pay the August rate and I can go to Baldock.”

“Baldock’s too far.”

“It’s eleven miles. I can send Jem.”

He was quiet for a moment. His hand settled on the counter, not quite gripping it.

“Fourteen miles,” he said. “Baldock’s fourteen miles.”

“Eleven,” I said. “I’ve done it.”

He looked at the ledger. He looked at me. He said, “I might manage to hold at the September figure. Given the volume you take.”

“The August figure,” I said, “given the volume I take.”

He picked up the sample box. He moved it six inches to the left and back again. “August, then. Miss Dale.”

“Thank you, Mr Murchett.”

The ledger went back on the shelf. The anger was there. It always was. The low familiar heat of being squeezed by every man who thought a woman alone wouldn’t know her own accounts. I named it to myself and moved past it. I went to check on the bread.

— — —

The two men in the taproom had been at the bench by the fire since the late-morning coach came in, which made it three hours. The taller one had the other’s collar by the time I came through the door. A stool scraped. I knew the sound of a stool scraping.

“Put him down,” I said.

The tall one sat down.

His companion rubbed his collar. He looked at my face and decided against it. One of them muttered something into his ale. I heard it and chose not to.

I went back behind the bar.
