I pulled the ledger from beneath the bar and opened it to the current page. The numbers ran in clean columns—income, expenses, the narrow margin between them. I'd kept it balanced for three years. Longer, if I counted the time before my husband died, when I'd been the one doing the figures anyway. The chandler's reduction would help. But the rent was due in a fortnight, and the rent was set by men who didn't negotiate. If I married again, the inn would be my husband's to manage, the ledger his to keep, the chandler's bill his to argue or accept as he saw fit. Or not argue at all. I closed the ledger and went to get the cash box. The chandler took his payment, touched his hat, and drove off. The crates sat stacked inside the door. Jem would move them to the storage room when he came back down. Nan was still at the bar, sorting through the morning's glassware. Through the window, the yard was quiet. I straightened the ledger on the shelf and went to check on the dining room.

The letter sat on the kitchen table where I had left it at four in the morning, and the kitchen was cold because I had not yet laid the fire. Stainforth's hand was a copperplate so even it might have been ruled. He wrote the way men wrote who had never had cause to hurry. He wished to discuss the terms. He hoped the hour of his call would not inconvenience me. He remained, with every assurance of regard, my most obliged servant. I knew what the letter meant in pounds and shillings before I had finished reading it the first time. The next quarter's rent was due in six weeks. I had, after the malt bill and the coal and Nan's wages and the farrier, eleven pounds four. The rent was twenty-three. Coaches were lighter this autumn because the harvest had failed and the half-pay officers were drinking small beer at home, and there was no honest arithmetic by which eleven became twenty-three in six weeks unless I emptied the cellar, which I would not, or sold the grey mare, which I would not yet. Stainforth wished to discuss the terms. I had seen his hand on a writ once and recognised the slope of the H. He wrote the way he stood. Pleasantly. The cut on my index finger had opened again in the night and stuck to the linen. I peeled it loose without looking at it and put the kettle on the hook. The ledger was where I had set it down. I did not open it. I knew the numbers, and a number does not change because a woman stares at it. I laid the fire. I wiped the table. I put the letter inside the lid of the tea caddy because I did not want Nan to read it before I had decided what to tell her, and then I went into the taproom and swept a floor that had been swept the night before. By eight I had the bar wiped, the brass on the ale taps polished, the cellar checked, the windows in the passage opened and shut again because the air carried rain. Nan came down with her hair half-pinned and her sleeves up and looked at me once, and looked at the polished brass, and did not say anything. She went to the back to fetch the bread. I heard the carriage on the road before it turned in. There is a sound a coach makes when the springs are good and the horses are matched, a sound entirely unlike the post coaches and the carrier carts. It came up the lane with the unhurried weight of a man who knew the door would open whether he knocked or not. He stepped down himself rather than wait for the boy. His coat was a heavy double-breasted wool of the colour they call iron, cut close at the waist, and the smell of it as he came through the door was the clean, oily smell of wool that has never been mended. His gloves were York tan. He drew them off finger by finger and folded them in his hand. "Miss Dale. Forgive the early hour. I was anxious not to keep you from the day." "Mr Stainforth." "I trust your aunt is well." "Well enough, sir. Thank you." "And the road, I see, has spared you the worst of last night's weather. We had it badly at Chitterley. Trees down at the lower lodge." "I'm sorry to hear it." He smiled. It was a courteous smile that asked nothing of his eyes. "You will think me presumptuous, Miss Dale, but I so rarely have the chance to see the place properly. Might I beg a turn through it before we sit down? I am a bad correspondent at desks. I think better when I have walked a building." I stepped back from the doorway and let him in. There was nothing else to do, and we both knew it. He walked the taproom slowly. He set his gloves on the bar and ran one finger along the brass tap of the porter cask, then along the rail. He looked up at the beams. He looked down at the floor where the boards had darkened in front of the fire. He did not say what he was looking for. He did not need to. I had stood at that bar for sixteen years, and I knew the shape of an inventory when I saw one. "How many rooms let in an average week, Miss Dale?" "Sixteen, sir. Twenty in the season." "And the coaches?" "Four north and four south daily. Five on the Royal Mail nights." "You service the Highflyer?" "And the Wellington." "Mr Pickford is content?" "Mr Pickford is content." He nodded once, as if filing each of these into a column whose total he would not show me. He moved on. I followed two paces behind, the way Patience had taught me to follow tradesmen who had come in to argue prices. Give them the number and the door, she had said, and not the explanation. The explanation is what they came for. The cellar door was at the back of the passage. He paused with his hand on the latch and ran his thumb along the hinge. He lifted the door an inch and let it settle, listening to the way it sat on the jamb. Then he opened it and went down. I followed with a candle. He looked at the casks. He looked at the drainage trough cut into the flag at the foot of the stair. He bent and put his palm flat on the stone. "It is dry." "It is." "Your mother had it relaid, I think. I remember." "Eight years ago." "A handsome piece of work." "Yes." He straightened and turned in the small space and looked at me, and there was a moment in which he might have spoken his purpose, and did not, because not speaking it was the purpose. He went up the stair ahead of me, and I came after him with the candle, and I shut the cellar door behind us and slid the bolt because the bolt was mine and the door was mine and I needed to put my hand on something that was. He went out through the back into the stable yard. The rain had stopped and the air was sharp with hay and wet stone. The yard was a long rectangle bound by the stables on one side and the wash-house on the other, with the fence running down the open end toward the lane. The post nearest the gate had gone crooked in the autumn wet, and the rail above it sat low. I had meant to have Jem set it the day before and had spent the day instead with the malt-man. Stainforth walked the length of the yard with his gloves still folded in his hand. He stopped at the fence and put two fingers to the crooked post and gave it the smallest sideways pressure, watching the rail dip. He did not push hard. He did not need to. "A pity," he said. "It will go in the winter if it is not seen to." "It will be seen to." "Of course." He let his hand fall. "Miss Dale, I should not like you to think this visit anything other than friendly. I have a great regard for what your family has done at the White Hound. Three generations is no small thing." "Thank you, sir." "It is, however, a great deal of work for one person." "It always was." "Your mother had your father, in the first years." "My father drank." He did not blink. "She had, at any rate, the appearance of a household." There it was. He had laid it down as gently as a man laying down a glass on a polished table, and he watched to see whether I would pick it up. I did not. I stood where I was and let the silence do what silence does between two people who both understand it. "An establishment of this size," he said, after a moment, "might benefit from"—and he let the sentence sit, the way he had let his thumb sit on the hinge. "From?" He smiled, and inclined his head, and did not finish. I had run the arithmetic in the kitchen at four in the morning, and I ran it again now while I looked at him. Eleven and four. Twenty-three. Six weeks. A man with a lease in his pocket and a nephew at his elbow did not need to threaten a woman with the law of coverture; he needed only to wait until she could not pay, and then he could be courteous about what came next. The courtesy was the knife. The lease was the hand on the hilt. "Mr Stainforth," I said, and I heard the edge in my own voice and did not soften it, "if you have business to put to me, put it. I can answer business. I cannot answer a sentence you will not finish." He absorbed it. That was the worst of it. He did not flinch and he did not flush. He nodded once, as if I had handed him a useful piece of information about the temper of the inn, and then he picked his gloves up off the rail where he had laid them. "You misunderstand me, Miss Dale. There is no business this morning. I came only to see the house and to pay my respects." He drew on one glove. "You keep it remarkably well. Truly. The cellar is a credit to you." "Thank you, sir." "We shall speak again before the quarter." "As you wish." He drew on the second glove. He looked once more along the line of the fence, and the look was the kind a man gives a field he has already walked. Then he bowed, very correctly, and went round to the front, and his coachman was already there with the step down. The carriage turned in the lane and went south, and I stood in the yard until I could no longer hear the wheels. I unclenched my jaw. I had not known it was clenched. The back of my neck ached as though I had carried a yoke. I went into the kitchen and stood at the table with both hands flat on the wood until my hands stopped shaking, and then I poured the water from the kettle into the basin and washed my finger and bound it with a fresh strip of linen, because the cut had opened again and I had bled into my cuff without noticing. Then I went upstairs. Bridget's door was on the latch, as it always was when she had seen something from her window. The good china was already out on the small table by the fire, two cups and the pot with the chipped lid she would not let me replace. The room smelled of lavender and tea and the wool of the blanket she kept across her knees, and the fire was high because Bridget kept her fire high and we did not argue about the coal. "Sit down, child." I sat. My shoulders dropped without my permission, the way a horse drops its head when the harness comes off. Bridget poured. Her hands were knotted at the knuckles and slow at the spout, and she watched the tea, not me. "He stayed forty minutes," she said. "Forty-three." "He is very tidy." "He is." She passed me my cup. The china was warm. I held it in both hands. "Your mother saw off Mr Greville over the stabling, you remember. He wanted four more stalls along the south side. He had drawn the plans himself. He brought them in a leather case." "I remember the case." "She told him no. She told him the drainage on that side would not bear the weight of horses on top of what was already there. He said she did not understand the matter. She told him she had grown up with her ear to that drain and he had not, and would he like another cup of tea." Bridget sipped. "He did not." "She was right about the drain." "She was right about the drain. She was also right that he would come back the next year with the plans redrawn and the same case, and she was ready for him. She held that yard for thirty years because she knew where it could bear weight and where it could not." Bridget set her cup down. "Holding on and holding back are not the same thing, Dinah." I reached for the pot. "Will you have more?" "In a moment." "The tea is good. Is it the new caddy?" "It is the same caddy it has been for ten years." "Mm." She looked at me, and she let me look back, and she did not say it again. Bridget had never been a woman who said a thing twice. She had said it once and put it on the table between us with the sugar and the milk, and what I did with it was my business. I drank my tea. I asked after her hands. She said her hands were her hands. I said I would send Nan up with the dinner tray, and she said she would prefer the broth if there was broth, and I kissed the top of her head where her hair was thin and warm, and I went down the stair and shut the kitchen door behind me. I did not think about what she had said. I had work to do. The day went on. The Wellington came through at three with a full inside and two outside passengers who wanted hot rum. A carrier broke an axle on the lane and brought his load into the yard to be stowed until morning. Nan got the supper up, the fire wanted feeding twice, a man in the snug argued with his wife about a hat and I told him quietly to take the argument outside, and he did, because men generally do when a woman does not raise her voice. By ten the taproom was empty. By half past, I had banked the fire and counted the till and bolted the front door, and I was standing in the passage with my hand on the bolt when the knock came. It was not a loud knock. It was the knock of a man who had walked a long way and did not wish to be more trouble than he had to be. I drew the bolt back. He was tall. The lamp behind me threw him into outline before it caught his face. A long coat, the wool wet through at the shoulders. Boots that had been good leather once and were now worn down at the heel until the seam showed. A dog at his knee, lean and grey-brindled, with the long legs and tucked belly of a lurcher, sitting where it had been told to sit and watching me with the patient worry of an animal that had learned not to expect supper. The man took off his hat. "Forgive the hour, ma'am. I was told in the village there might be work." His voice was quiet and used the words sparingly. It was a voice that did not have to be raised to be heard, and I noted that, and I noted the boots, and I noted that the dog had not moved. I had read a hundred men at this door at this hour. He was not drunk. He was not dangerous. He was tired in the way that took weeks to put down and could not be put down tonight. "What sort of work." "Any sort. Stable, yard, fetching. I can mend most things. I do not ask wages tonight. A corner of the stable for the dog and me, and we will speak in the morning if it suits you." "It suits me. The end stall on the left is dry. There is straw. There is a horse blanket on the peg by the door. The pump is at the back. Breakfast is at six." "Thank you, ma'am." "Dale. Miss Dale." "Miss Dale." He did not give me his name. I did not ask. I shut the door behind him and slid the bolt. I went into the kitchen because I went into the kitchen every night before bed, to see that the range was banked and the cat was in and the back door was on its chain. The window over the sink looked out into the yard. I lifted the muslin. He had set his pack down by the stable door. The dog had laid itself flat across the threshold, nose on its paws, eyes on him. He was on one knee at the door, with his hand on the lower hinge, lifting the door an inch and letting it settle, listening to the way it sat on the jamb. Then he took something from his pack, a small thing, I could not see, and he tightened the hinge pin, and the door sat true. He stood. He put a hand on the dog's head as he passed it. He went down the yard to the fence. He stopped at the crooked post. He did not touch it the way Stainforth had touched it. He set his shoulder to it and lifted, and worked it upright in the wet earth with the heel of his boot, and went round behind it and braced it with a stone that he carried over from the wall, and tested the rail with his hand. The rail sat level. He did not look pleased with himself. He looked at the post the way a man looks at a small thing crossed off a list, and he picked up his pack and went back to the stable and the dog rose and followed him in. I watched his hands until he was out of the lamp's reach. Then I let the muslin fall, and I turned the key in the kitchen door, and I went up to bed.

THE LAST RETREAT

Chapter One: The White Hound

The grate was dead. I rebuilt it.

Whoever had banked it last night had done a pitiful job of it. Half ash, half hope, neither sufficient. I didn’t waste the time to wonder who. There were only three of us and the odds were even enough.

— — —

Forty years of coaching traffic had worn the flagstones uneven. Forty years had smoked the ceiling beams to the colour of old bone. Forty years, and the place still stood.

The White Hound was not beautiful. It had never pretended to be. The taproom had six tables, a bar of elm I’d sanded myself after Jem pointed out that the old pine had gone soft enough to splinter, and a ceiling low enough that any man over six feet ducked on instinct when he crossed the threshold. The bar top was good now. Smooth, the grain running even, the knots sealed against spilled ale. I’d worked it on a Tuesday in March because March is the dead month on the Great North Road. Too late for Christmas traffic, too early for the summer coaches. There had been nothing else pressing except the damp patch above the third window and seventeen other things I couldn’t fix without spending money I didn’t have. I’d done the bar instead. There is a satisfaction in fixing what can be fixed.

The damp patch was still there.

The lease hung on its nail behind the bar, where my grandmother had put it in the year I was born. Stainforth’s name was at the top of it. Abel Dale’s name below that, though Abel Dale had been dead sixteen years and had never, in any practical sense, run a single day of this inn. My name was nowhere on it. Three women had kept the White Hound standing through two wars, a failed harvest, and my father’s cheerful inability to keep his accounts. None of our names were on the lease.

I noticed the nail needed straightening. I straightened it. I hung the lease back on it and turned to get the bread going.

— — —

Nan came in at half past five tying her cap and saying, without preamble and without looking up from the strings, “Coach company’s switching agents. Heard it last night from Dawes at the Bull. Chipping Norton man, apparently. New rates.”

“New rates up or down.”

“Up.” She tied the final bow, tucked a strand of hair that wouldn’t behave, and reached past me for the apron on the hook. “Dawes said the Hatfield coach is already paying sixpence more a head.”

“Dawes is a gossip.”

“Dawes is a gossip who hasn’t been wrong yet.” She pulled the apron over her head and turned to face me. “I’m only saying.”

I cracked the first egg against the rim of the bowl. “If the rates change, the rates change. We’ll adjust.”

“We always adjust.” Nan said it pleasantly.

— — —

The morning coach arrived at seven with nine passengers, three trunks, a birdcage, and a coachman in a temper about the state of the road past Stevenage. The yard became itself when a coach came in. Noise and motion and everyone knowing their place without being told. Jem was already at the horses and the lead pair were haltered and the first trunk was down from the roof and the passengers were stepping out blinking into the cold and the coachman was calling for water and Nan was there with the manifest and the yard smell was horse and leather and dead October leaves, all of it in motion, all of it working.

Nine passengers. I counted.

“Left rear wheel wants looking at,” Jem said, when I came past with a trunk for the luggage room. He said it to the wheel, not to me.

I already knew. I’d heard it come in. “How bad.”

“Won’t make York.” He didn’t say it with any particular feeling. It was a wheel. It wanted attention. These were the facts.

“I’ll tell him.”

“Tell him before he loads back up or we’ll be having a worse conversation.”

We were agreed. That was all.

— — —

The coachman’s name was Hartley and he was working himself toward a scene about the trunk. It belonged to a woman travelling to Biggleswade, was the size of a small cottage, and was currently in my luggage room because Hartley had declined to carry it up himself on account of it being, in his opinion, the woman’s own business to make arrangements for her own property, to which I said, with every courtesy I owned: “Mr Hartley. You may speak with Mr Pelling at the coaching office in Hatfield on Thursday, he’s there before ten, and he will tell you what the company policy is about passenger luggage. Or I can tell you now, which will save you the journey.”

Hartley looked at me.

“It’s the coachman’s responsibility to the first interior door,” I said. “Mr Pelling was very clear on this in his July letter. I have it, if you’d like to see it.”

He would not like to see it. He picked up the trunk.

— — —

The woman from Biggleswade had requested the trunk be brought to her room, immediately, and had communicated this request with confidence. I arranged for it. She said the room was cold, which it was not, since I had laid the fire myself, and that the sheets wanted airing, which they did not, and that she hoped the establishment would be rather more attentive than it had yet appeared. I said I hoped so too. She gathered as much composure as she could locate and swept into the room. I closed the door behind her with exquisite care.

— — —

The chandler came at eleven. His name was Murchett. He’d supplied the White Hound for four years, and for the first three of those years the arrangement had been satisfactory, and for the fourth it had become an occasion for him to stand in my kitchen rearranging his sample box and telling me that costs had risen.

He set the box on the table. He looked at the window. He put his hands in his coat pockets and then took them out again.

“It’s the tallow,” he said. “Prices out of London.”

“You told me that in August.”

“It’s got worse.”

“By how much.”

He told me. He looked at the window again when he said it, which is how I knew the number was generous in his favour.

I went to the shelf and took down the ledger. I opened it on the table between us, my finger on the column for October last year, which showed me his margin at the old rate and showed me, more importantly, that I knew what his margin was. “Mr Murchett.”

“Miss Dale.”

“I can pay the August rate and I can go to Baldock.”

“Baldock’s too far.”

“It’s eleven miles. I can send Jem.”

He was quiet for a moment. His hand settled on the counter, not quite gripping it.

“Fourteen miles,” he said. “Baldock’s fourteen miles.”

“Eleven,” I said. “I’ve done it.”

He looked at the ledger. He looked at me. He said, “I might manage to hold at the September figure. Given the volume you take.”

“The August figure,” I said, “given the volume I take.”

He picked up the sample box. He moved it six inches to the left and back again. “August, then. Miss Dale.”

“Thank you, Mr Murchett.”

The ledger went back on the shelf. The anger was there. It always was. The low familiar heat of being squeezed by every man who thought a woman alone wouldn’t know her own accounts. I named it to myself and moved past it. I went to check on the bread.

— — —

The two men in the taproom had been at the bench by the fire since the late-morning coach came in, which made it three hours. The taller one had the other’s collar by the time I came through the door. A stool scraped. I knew the sound of a stool scraping.

“Put him down,” I said.

The tall one sat down.

His companion rubbed his collar. He looked at my face and decided against it. One of them muttered something into his ale. I heard it and chose not to.

I went back behind the bar. Jem was in the far corner of the room, leaning on the wall with his arms crossed, his arm rather and the stump of the other, and he looked at me and I looked at him, and that look contained everything: the frequency of it, the tedium of it, the question of how men managed to make a quarrel about a boot into an occasion requiring a woman to stand in a doorway like an army.

— — —

He came to the kitchen door at half past three, just after the afternoon coaches had gone through and before the evening rush, when the yard was quiet and the smell of the stockpot had been working at the air for two hours. He knocked. Not with the confidence of a guest, who would have gone to the front, but with the hesitance of knowing which door to use and not being sure he had the right.

I opened it.

His coat was the right cut for a soldier, old infantry by the collar, but it had been let out at the shoulders. His boots were repaired, the toe-stitching recent, the heel leather not. Someone had done what they could for them, and it showed. He stood straight in the doorway, his hands at his sides, his eyes at my shoulder rather than my face.

He did not ask for charity. He asked if there was work.

I looked at him for thirty seconds. “I’ve a floor wants sweeping in the luggage room,” I said. “The broom is on the left side of the door. When you’re done, come in.”

He swept the floor. I ladled bread and a bowl of stockpot onto the table and said nothing while I shaped the next morning’s loaves, and he ate, and we did not perform anything for each other.

After a while, he said, “Road was frozen past Stevenage.”

“It’s been this cold since Sunday.”

He nodded. He ate the last of the bread. He didn’t say it was good, and I didn’t say anything about giving it, and we were both satisfied with this.

He finished the bread and thanked me and went. The good stitching on the right, the worn heel on the left. I watched his boots go through the kitchen door and then they were gone, and the yard was empty, and the afternoon light was already failing.

I went back to work.

— — —

Closing up is a thing that happens by the body, not the mind. The bolt on the front door has swollen with the autumn damp and requires a firm shoulder and a particular angle; I leaned and pushed and felt it give with a scrape of iron on iron. The window in the taproom that has never quite hung straight caught in its frame when I pulled it and let in a thread of cold air past the latch, as it always does, until I reached up and eased the top corner into the groove. The strongbox behind the bar opened with a click of three tumblers that my mother taught me when I was twelve, and closed with the same, and the sound of it in the empty room is the same sound it always is. Small and definite and mine. The fire in the kitchen wanted banking, not killing; I pushed the coals together and set the guard. The candles in the taproom went out in order, left to right. The last one smelled of tallow for half a minute after.

I wiped down the bar. It was already clean because Nan had done it after the last customers went, but I wiped it anyway, the cloth moving in the same circles, the elm smooth under my hands. There was a gouge near the far end, an inch long, just where the bar curved. I ran my thumb over it. A keg, I thought. Thursday’s delivery. The front left corner of the big barrel, the way Jem tilts it when he’s bringing it through alone . I’d have to talk to him about the Thursday delivery. Whether he could manage the heavy cask, whether we needed to ask for the smaller measure.

The lease was on its nail.

Three women on a man’s sufferance. My grandmother, who had held this place for twenty years through a husband’s death and the mill closures and the bad winter of ‘84 when there was no passing traffic for a week and she’d fed the staff from the cellar stores and not lost a single booking. My mother, who had finished the dinner service the night she died. And me.

I wrung out the cloth. I hung it on the hook.

The hook was one my mother put in.

— — —

The letter was on the shelf beside the ledger, where I’d set it when it arrived that afternoon. I’d seen the seal before I’d seen the direction.

Stainforth’s seal. His man had brought it, not the post.

I looked at it for a moment. I picked it up. The wax was smooth under my thumb, the device pressed clean. He was a careful man, Stainforth. Nothing rushed, nothing careless, everything managed.

I set the letter on the shelf beside the ledger.

I took the candle and went to bed.

The letter sat on the kitchen table where I had left it at half past four, folded once along the crease Stainforth's clerk had made, addressed in a hand that pretended it was doing me a favour by being legible. I did not reopen it. I knew what it said. *Wishes to discuss the terms.* Six words buried in a paragraph of courtesies, and six weeks until the next quarter's rent came due. I had the arithmetic by heart. Three hundred and eighty pounds owed at Michaelmas, two hundred and forty in the strongbox, the rest owed to me by coaching companies who paid in their own time. The gap was not a gap I could close by working harder. I was already working as hard as a body could without breaking. The kitchen was cold. I had not let the fire go out, only banked it too low, and the kettle on the crane looked as sulky about the business as I felt. I stirred the coals. I set the kettle properly. I put my reddened hands flat on the table and looked at the cut on my right index finger, which had opened again overnight, and I thought about what my mother would have done. My mother would have read the letter once and then swept the taproom. So I swept the taproom. I swept it harder than it wanted sweeping. I wiped the bar down twice, ran a cloth along the pewter, turned up the wicks and turned them down again, and went into the cellar with a lantern to check what did not need checking. The barrels were where they had been yesterday. The drainage was holding. The slate floor was dry. I ran my palm along the shelf above the gin and found no dust because Nan had done it on Tuesday, as Nan had done every Tuesday for seven years. I was making sure he would find nothing. I heard his carriage before I saw it. The road had been frozen hard since Saturday, and a good carriage sounded different on that kind of ground than a mail coach or a farmer's cart: lighter on its springs, slower, the horses better fed. I was in the doorway with my apron off and my hands behind my back before the groom had turned the team. Stainforth climbed down without hurry. His coat was dark and heavy and too well cut for a parish visit, and when he came within three paces I caught the smell of it, which was the smell of good wool that had never been wet. He drew off his gloves one finger at a time and folded them into his left hand. "Miss Dale." He gave me the small bow a man gives a woman he considers tenant rather than adversary. "I hope I find you well." "Mr Stainforth." "And Mrs Dale upstairs? My respects to her." "She keeps her fire going." "As she ought, at her age." He looked past me into the taproom, which looked back at him, scrubbed and inoffensive. "A raw morning. You've had frost on the road." "Since Saturday." "The coaches getting through?" "All week." He smiled at me the way a man smiles at a shopkeeper whose wares he has not yet decided whether to buy. "I wonder if I might beg a few minutes of your time. I so rarely have the chance to see the place properly." He had already stepped past me on the word *properly.* I closed the door behind him. He walked the taproom floor the way a man walks a field he is thinking of buying. He stopped at the bar and laid the flat of his hand on the oak. He went to the window and rattled the latch, very gently, as though testing whether the sash rose true. He did not comment. Comment was not the point. He was taking an inventory, and his manner said he had every right to take it, and the inn was making no objection. "The cellar stair is through here, I think." "Yes." "May I?" "As you wish." He opened the cellar door and ran his thumb along the hinge. He did not look at me while he did it. He looked at the hinge. Then he went down three steps, turned on the fourth, and came back up, and closed the door behind him with his left hand while the right stayed at the jamb a moment longer than it needed to. "The drainage," he said. "How do you manage it in a wet spring?" "The slate runs to a drain in the back wall. The drain runs to the yard." "And the yard?" "Holds." "Always?" "It held in '11." He accepted that without argument, because '11 had been a bad year and everyone in the county knew it. He nodded as though I had answered a question put for my benefit rather than his. "The coaching companies," he said. "Royal Mail. The Wellington. The York Express through the summer. Are they content?" "They come." "How many rooms let per week, on average?" "Fourteen in a slow week. Twenty in a full one." "And staff?" "Four in the kitchen. Nan at the bar. Jem in the yard. A boy in the stable when we need him." "You keep it lean." "I keep it paid." He smiled at that. It was the first smile of his that cost him anything at all, and it cost him very little. "My father used to say that the best inns were run by women, because women did not drink the profits. I have always thought that was unfair to the men." "My mother said the same thing." "Did she." "In fewer words." He looked at me then, properly, for the first time, and I understood what my mother had meant about Greville and what Bridget meant about every landlord who had ever stood where this one was standing. He was pleasant. He was going to continue to be pleasant. That was the shape of the thing. "Shall we walk the yard?" "If you like." The cold outside was cleaner than the cold inside. He drew his coat closed at the throat. Sapper — no. I did not know his name was Sapper yet. The yard was empty. Jem had taken the grey to be shod at the smith's, and the bay pair were still in their boxes. I led Stainforth past the mounting block and along the wall to the paddock gate, and he stopped at the fence between the yard and the lane because that was where the fence wanted stopping at. The corner post leaned. The top rail had worked loose on the peg and sat out of true by an inch and a half. He laid his hand on the post. He did not push it. He only laid his hand on it, and felt the give, and nodded once to himself. "It wants doing," he said. "It does." "Before the winter." "Yes." "An inn of this size." He left the sentence where a sentence like that always sat, hanging in the cold air, waiting for someone to pick it up. "An inn of this size might benefit from a steadier hand in the yard. A man about the place with an interest in the property. I don't mean Bowker — Bowker is a good servant, I don't doubt it. I mean the kind of interest that comes with ownership." I said nothing. "You have done remarkably, Miss Dale. I have said so in the parish and I say it to you. But there is a question of what is sustainable." "The inn is sustainable." "At what cost to you, though." "At whatever cost is required." He studied the fence post. He did not study me, which was a courtesy, and I was meant to notice it. "The lease comes up for renewal at Lady Day," he said. "I should like us to have a frank conversation about the terms before we reach that date. There are those in the parish who would argue that an establishment of this character, with the class of traveller we are now seeing on the road, might be better served by — a different arrangement. I am not one of those people. But I should not like to be obliged to become one." "No," I said. "I should not like that either." It came out sharper than I had meant it, and I heard it, and I saw him hear it, and I watched him absorb it without a twitch of the mouth or a shift of the shoulders. He took the sharpness and put it somewhere inside his coat and went on standing with his hand on my fence. "I am glad we understand one another." "I believe we do." He lifted his hand from the post. "I have taken too much of your morning. Thank you, Miss Dale. It is a well-kept house. Your mother would be proud." He inclined his head. "My respects to Mrs Dale." "I'll give them." He walked to his carriage without hurry. The groom handed him up. The horses went out of the yard at a slow trot, and I stood where I was until the carriage turned at the oak and the sound of its wheels had thinned into the road. Then I unclenched my jaw. It hurt in a particular place below the left ear, and the back of my neck was damp at the hairline, and my right hand was a fist at my side with the cut finger reopened against the palm. I put the hand in the pocket of my apron. I walked back across the yard. I shut the door behind me as a woman shuts a door when she has a great deal to do and no time to consider why she is not yet doing it. I went upstairs to Bridget. She had seen the carriage. Of course she had seen the carriage. Bridget sat in the chair by the window from the first cup of tea in the morning to the last candle at night, and nothing came up the road or left it without her account of it. The good china was already out. The teapot was on the hearth with a cloth over its handle. She did not look up when I came in. "Shut the door, there's a draught." I shut it. "Sit." I sat. She poured. The cups were the pink ones with the gilt band that had been her mother's before they were hers, and she used them every day, as she had always done, because in Bridget's view the purpose of a good cup was to be drunk out of. The room smelled of lavender and of the damp wool of the blanket on her knees and of the tea, and under those smells of the mustiness of her books, which she had stopped reading three years ago and would not let me move. "Drink it while it's hot." I drank it while it was hot. "He touched the hinges, did he." "And the window latch. And the fence post." "Greville used to touch the bar rail. He'd come round the same time of year, stand where the customers stood, run his thumb along the rail as if he were making sure the varnish was his. Your mother took the rail down the following spring and put a new one in, just to spite him." "I remember." "No you don't. You were six." "I remember the new rail." She smiled into her cup. "Well. Perhaps you do." We drank. She did not ask me what he had said. She never asked what people said. She asked what they had done, and she listened to my account of what they had done, and she made her own arithmetic from it, and she was very seldom wrong. "He wants a man in the yard," I said. "He wants a man in the house." "He did not say so." "He never does." I set my cup down. The gilt was wearing off the rim where my mouth had been. "I'll manage the lease, Bridget." "I don't doubt it." "I have managed worse." "You have managed everything." "I will manage this." She looked at me for a long moment over the pink cup. Her eyes were not as clear as they had been a year ago, but they were clear enough for what she wanted to do with them. "Your mother held this place through a war and a bad harvest and your father's best efforts to drink the cellar dry," she said. "She held on with both hands because it was hers. The question I have watched you not ask yourself, child, is whether holding on and holding back are not the same thing." I reached for the teapot. "Shall I warm yours?" "You shall not change the subject." "I shall pour you more tea." I poured her more tea. I poured myself more tea. I set the pot down on the hearth with the cloth over its handle, the way she liked it, so the spout would not drip on the tiles. I asked after her knee. She told me about her knee. I told her I would send Nan up with a hot brick after dinner, and she said she did not need a hot brick, and I said she would have one regardless, and by the time I kissed her forehead and took the tray away I had put what she had said behind a door in a part of my head I did not intend to visit today. I went down the back stairs with the tray. I returned the cups to the scullery. I washed them myself because the good china did not go in with the rest. I dried them. I put them back on Bridget's shelf. I went into the kitchen and took up the list I had been working from since dawn, and I crossed off *cellar* and *latches* and *yard* and *Stainforth,* and I added *fence post* in the margin in pencil, and I did not look at the letter on the table, which I had still not put away. By the time Nan had brought the taproom shutters in and banked the fire and gone up to bed, it was past eleven, and the yard was quiet, and the cold had set hard enough to crack a bucket. I bolted the front door. I took the lantern into the kitchen and set it on the sill. I was about to blow it out when the knock came. Three knocks. Not hurried. Not drunk. A man who knew he was asking at an hour when he should not have been. I drew the bolt back and opened the door a hand's width. A dog looked at me first. A grey-brindled lurcher with a long nose and an expression of worried patience, sitting on the step with its tail across its paws. Then the man: tall, tired, a cloak that had been a good cloak once, and boots that had walked from somewhere a long way off. The leather was good. The leather was finished. He had his hat in his hand, which told me something about him that I did not have the energy to examine. "Forgive the hour," he said. His voice was lower than I had expected and more careful. "I was hoping for work, or a place to sleep. Either will do. I can pay for the bed if I must." I looked at the dog. The dog leaned against his leg. "I'm full inside," I said, which was not true, and he knew it, and did not press. "There's clean straw in the second stable, the box at the far end. You and the dog may have it till morning. Come to the kitchen door at six and we'll talk about the work." "Thank you." "Don't thank me yet. The wind gets under the door and the cat will try to sleep on your face." The corner of his mouth moved. It was not quite a smile. It was the shape a smile takes in a man who has forgotten how to wear one at short notice. "I'll take my chances with the cat." "Stable's across the yard. There's a lantern by the mounting block if you need it." "I'll manage." He touched the brim of his hat to me, though it was in his hand and not on his head, which is the gesture of a man who is used to a hat being there, and he turned and went down the step with the dog at his heel, and I shut the door and drew the bolt. I ought to have gone to bed. I had been awake since four. The cut on my finger was throbbing, and my jaw was sore from the morning, and there was a ledger still to balance at the kitchen table. I took up the lantern to carry it upstairs. At the kitchen window I stopped. I was not watching him. I was checking the yard, as I did every night, because a woman who runs a coaching inn alone learns to look at her yard before she sleeps. The moon was high enough to put the stable roof in outline and the paddock fence in silhouette and the man in shape rather than detail. He had the lantern. He had set it on the mounting block. He was bent at the stable door, his hand at the hinge, and he moved the door once on it and then again, listening to it, and then he took something from his pocket — a pin, a nail, I could not see — and he worked at the hinge until the door swung true. He closed it. He opened it. He closed it again. Then he went to the paddock fence. The corner post that Stainforth had laid his hand on that morning. The post that leaned an inch and a half out of true. He did not lay his hand on it. He put his shoulder to it and his palms flat on the rail and he rocked it, once, to find the play, and then he walked round to the far side and pressed his knee into the earth at its base, and he worked the post upright with his whole weight behind it until it stood clean against the sky. He drove a stone in at the foot with his boot heel. He tested it. He set the top rail back on its peg. He tested the rail. He stood back from the fence and looked at it for a long moment in the way a man looks at a job to see whether it has been done. The dog sat by the mounting block, watching him. He bent and picked up the lantern. I watched his hand close around the handle, and his other hand go to the small of his back as he straightened, the small stiff movement of a man with something wrong in one of his legs that he had decided not to attend to. He took the lantern into the stable. The yard went dark except for the square of warmer light under the stable door. I turned from the window. I blew out my own lantern. I set it on the sill. The cut on my finger was still bleeding, a little, under the linen. I pressed my thumb to it and went up to bed.

The ledger sat open to the page I had been avoiding for a week, and the numbers on it had not improved overnight. Six weeks, near enough, before the quarter's rent came due, and the column under income did not agree with the column under expenditure in the way I needed it to. I added the figures a third time, because a third time was what I gave myself before I was allowed to close the book. They came out the same. I wanted a cup of tea I did not have time to make. I closed the ledger and set it on the shelf above the flour crock, where Nan would not see it and ask. The kitchen fire I had already built up; it was the taproom that wanted me now. I went through, sleeves pushed back, and knelt to the grate. The inn was cold in the particular way of a building that had given its warmth to strangers all week and been left to cool between coaches. Frost rimed the insides of the window corners. I laid kindling. I struck the flint. The first shaving caught, and I fed it with the patience the thing required, which was not very much if you knew what you were doing and a great deal if you did not. Bar stock next. Porter: two barrels, one broached. Small beer: sufficient. Brandy: low. I noted it on the slate. Candles would be a conversation I intended to have this morning, and I did not intend to lose it. My sleeves had ridden up. I pulled them down over my wrists and went out into the yard to look at the weather. Jem was already there. He had the tack across the trestle and was working the strap of a bridle between his remaining hand and the stump of the other, bracing the buckle against the bench and drawing the leather through. He had taught the stump to do the work of the other hand over a year of patient invention, and he did not like to be watched doing it. I did not watch. I looked at the sky, which was a sky, and I looked at the cobbles, which had a skin of frost on them, and I went back inside. Nan came in through the kitchen door five minutes later, pins half in, apron tied, and took her place behind the bar without saying good morning. We had been saying good morning to each other for seven years, and we had lately agreed, without discussion, that we could stop. The London coach was late. This was not news. What was news was that it was short, and the coachman's temper with it. Two passengers got down where four ought to have, and one of them had a trunk with a split hinge that was already gaping by the time Jem reached it. She was the sort of woman who made opinions into weather. The hinge, the road, the hour, the fact that the sign on the road from Hatfield was ill-placed and had misled her late husband once in 1807: all of it, apparently, my affair. "Ma'am," I said, when she had reached a natural breath. "Jem will have the hinge mended before you are upstairs. Mrs. Hollis in room four is vacating. I will put you there. There is hot water in ten minutes and breakfast in twenty." She opened her mouth to argue and then, reading my face, shut it. "Thank you," she said, surprised into civility. "Mind the second step," I said. "It sings." The coachman wanted me next. He was a big man gone grey around the jaw, and he smelled of horse and the particular damp of a man who had been on the box since four. "Miss Dale," he said. "The road above Hatfield is a disgrace." "I believe you." "Ruts to the axle. I nearly lost a wheel." "I'll send word to the surveyor this week. I owe him a letter anyway." "It wants more than a letter." "It will have a letter from me, which he dislikes, and a visit from my cousin-by-marriage at the quarter session, which he dislikes more. Will that do for now?" He looked at me under his brows. "Aye. That'll do." "Good. Nan has coffee in the taproom. Two minutes. I haven't more." He went. The yard cleared as a yard clears when a thing is over: trunks through the side door, passengers up the stairs, Jem leading the team toward the stable with the split-hinge trunk balanced under his arm as if it weighed nothing, which it did not, if you were Jem. Nan wiped the bar with one eye on the door through the whole of it. I saw her do it. I said nothing. She would not have thanked me for the notice. Mr. Partlet arrived at ten with his tallow. I had been negotiating with Mr. Partlet for four years, in which time he had raised his price four times, three of them in the last seven months. He knew his margins. I knew them better. He also knew I knew, which was the part of the arrangement that kept our conversations civil. "Miss Dale," he said, setting the crate on the table and taking off his hat with the slow courtesy that meant he was about to ask for more than I wished to give. "Mr. Partlet. How is Mrs. Partlet?" "Indifferent, thank you. It's her knees." "Give her my respects. What are you charging me for the dozen?" He named a figure. I let it sit in the air between us, where it could be observed. "That is sixpence above September." "Tallow's gone up." "Tallow has not gone up sixpence a dozen to anyone else between here and Baldock. I had a word with Mr. Easton at the Cock last Sunday." He had the grace not to pretend surprise. "Mr. Easton takes a smaller order." "Mr. Easton takes half my order and pays fourpence less the dozen. I can do arithmetic, Mr. Partlet. So can you." "It's a fair price for the quality." "It is a price," I said. "I will pay you last month's, plus a penny, and not a farthing more. You will sell me the dozen because I take more candles than anyone else on this stretch of road and because you do not wish to drive back to Hitchin with a full cart explaining to your wife why you did not sell them." He looked at the crate. He looked at me. The side of his mouth did the small movement that meant he had decided before he spoke. "Last month's plus a penny." "Thank you." He counted the candles out. I counted the coins. He touched his hat at the door and went, and the door shut behind him with the decent small click of a deal that has been completed without anyone calling anyone a name. I allowed myself one breath of private pleasure, the sort a person takes after lifting something heavy and setting it down without dropping it on her foot. *A good morning's work, Dinah. And it is not yet eleven.* I set the candles in the cupboard and stood a moment with my hand on the shelf. The ledger, the rent, the candles, the road, the coachman, the trunk: all of it came back, in the quiet of the cupboard, to the one fact I refused to let sit in the front of my mind for long. If I married, none of these negotiations would be mine to make. The lease would be my husband's. The ledger would be my husband's. Mr. Partlet would have tipped his hat to my husband and charged him sixpence above September, and my husband, who would not know Mr. Easton at the Cock, would have paid it. I had refused two men already on those grounds. I had liked one of them well enough to miss him for a fortnight. I closed the cupboard. The next task was the larder, and the next task was the one that got done. He came round the back, as the painted sign on the yard gate asked them to. I saw him through the kitchen window before he knocked, because I see the yard from the kitchen as a matter of practice. He was young. Thin inside a coat that had been made for a larger man. His boots had given up at the welt on the left foot and were working on the right. He stood at the step without raising his hand, assessing whether he was welcome enough to knock, and arriving, I could see, at no answer. I opened the door. "Have you eaten?" I said. "Not recent, ma'am." "Kitchen, not taproom. You will sit at that end of the table where you will not be in Nan's way. You will not come through into the front." "Yes, ma'am." I gave him bread and the end of last night's broth, which was thicker than broth had any business being and the better for it. I set down a spoon and a cup of small beer. He sat. He ate with the slow care of a man who has learned, recently, that eating too fast costs him the meal. He held the spoon with both hands, as my father had the winter he was ill. I turned to the stockpot so that I was not watching him. "Where are you headed?" I said, over my shoulder. "North, ma'am. Nottingham, if I can make it. I've a sister there." "The road is bad above Hatfield. Keep to the verge where you can." "Thank you, ma'am." He finished. He stood. He set the spoon down exactly square to the bowl, as if squareness mattered, and I suppose for him it did. "Thank you," he said again, at the door. "I'll not forget it." "Mind how you go." He went. I stood at the sink a moment with my hands on the rim of the basin. I was sorry about the boots. I was sorry he was somebody's, and I did not let myself finish the sentence. The stockpot wanted stirring. I stirred it. The afternoon moved the way afternoons move between coaches. Two men in the taproom near one o'clock took offence at each other over a question about a horse that neither of them owned. I went through. "Gentlemen," I said. "Not in this room." They looked at me. "Not in this room," I said again, in the voice my mother had used on drovers. "Or not in this inn." They found, upon consideration, that they had not been quarrelling after all. Nan, behind the bar, did not look up from the glass she was polishing. She had known I would not need her. I had known she would be there if I had. The inn was quiet. It was always quiet between coaches. I did the accounts at the kitchen table, reset the two vacated rooms on the first floor, spoke to Cook about the evening joint, and carried a scuttle of coal up the back stairs because Piers was not yet hired and the scuttle was not going to carry itself. By six the evening coach had come and gone. By nine the taproom had thinned. By half past ten the last guest had been given his candle and sent up, and the fire in the taproom was a low red bed of coals that would hold until morning. I did my circuit. Taproom fire: down to coals, guard set. Cellar hatch: bolted. Back door: barred. Kitchen fire: banked. Stable yard: quiet. Jem had been through; the horses were settled and the lamp in the tack room was out. Second floor: the line of doors dark under the doors, except for the small warm seam at the turn of the passage where Aunt Bridget's window gave onto the yard. I crossed the cobbles to the stable door and back, and when I looked up I could see her shape behind the glass, as I always could. I did not look long. She would be watching me the same as she watched everything, and she had done it for enough years that neither of us needed to remark upon it. I set the bolt on the front door last. It was a heavy bolt and the fit had stiffened in the cold. I leaned my shoulder to it and felt it go home. I stood a moment in the passage with my hand on the iron. My palms smarted. The right had a small cut across the pad of the index finger from the sharp edge of Mr. Partlet's delivery note, which I had not noticed taking, and there was dried blood at the base of the nail. I turned the hand in the candlelight. A small thing. It had been a long day. I was tired, and the inn was quiet around me, and there was no one in any of the rooms I loved who was mine, and I let myself notice that for the length of one held breath before I moved on. The letter was on the kitchen table. I had seen it there when I came through at nine, and I had walked past it twice on purpose. Now there was nothing else to do and nowhere else to be, and I picked it up. Stainforth's seal. His hand on the direction, which I knew as well as I knew my own. I turned the letter over once, which changed nothing. I broke the seal. *Miss Dale,* he had written, in the cramped even script of a man who considered himself reasonable, *I write to you in the hope that we may bring a long-standing matter to a civil conclusion. It has been represented to me by a gentleman of good standing that the lease of the White Hound might be put to more profitable use under different management, and I am minded to give the suggestion serious consideration. My nephew, Mr. Edmund Stainforth, is prepared to take the premises at Lady Day, with terms that would allow you a reasonable sum for the goodwill and fixtures. I have a buyer. I do not doubt that you will see the advantage to all parties of a prompt and orderly transfer.* *I ask that you attend me at Whitcombe on the fifteenth of November, at eleven o'clock, to discuss the particulars. I trust you will not require me to repeat the invitation.* *Yr obedient servant,* *P. Stainforth* I read it twice. The second time I read it slowly, because I wanted to be certain of what he had written and what he had not. He had not mentioned my mother. He had not named the grounds. He had not said *vagrant* or *public nuisance*, which meant he was keeping those in reserve. He had said *buyer*, and he had said *Lady Day*, and he had given me three weeks to come to his house and be told how to lose my life politely. I set the letter on the table. I smoothed it flat with the side of my hand. The cut on my finger had opened again. I put the finger in my mouth, which was what my mother had done, and which had always annoyed me, and I stood in the kitchen and thought. "Mr. Stainforth," I said aloud, to the empty room, in a voice that was quite steady and quite my own, "you have written to the wrong woman."

Chapter One

The White Hound

The ledger was open on the kitchen table at a quarter to five, because there was no other hour when I could count a column in peace. Six weeks. That was the figure I had come to, and the figure did not grow kinder the second time I added it. Six weeks and one day, if I gave myself the benefit of the doubt, which I didn’t ordinarily do. Rent quarter fell at Christmas. Whatever was owed at Christmas had to be in the rent-box by Christmas, and no creditor in Hertfordshire had ever done me the courtesy of being mistaken about a date.

I closed the ledger. There was no sum to be reached by staring, and my mother had told me, often enough that it lodged: work the figures once, then work the inn.

The kitchen held the cold of a room whose fire had been banked seven hours. I pulled my sleeves down over my wrists and went to build up the grate. The window above the sink was rimed at its edges. My breath showed when I bent to the embers, which I disliked. Breath in a kitchen was untidy.

What I wanted, at five in the morning of a Tuesday in October, was a quarter-hour longer with the coals before the inn began to want me in six places at once. I gave myself the ten minutes I could afford instead of the quarter-hour I preferred. The fire caught. I fed it in the careful order my mother had taught me and I had taught myself. When I could leave it without watching it, I did.

The taproom came next. I laid the fire there and lit it from a splint off the kitchen. I counted the ale casks in the bar and found them at the number I had left them at. That was not a thing to be taken for granted in October. Men on the road had been hungry long enough to muddle the difference between begging and helping themselves, and I had learned to count whatever could walk. The bar stock I checked by touch as much as sight, because the light was still poor and the stock was in the places I had put it. Beer cellar. Wine cellar. Spirits lock-box. Glassware shelf. Everything at the count I had left it. Alice, the chambermaid, had left me a note on a folded scrap about a broken pane in the second-floor passage window. She had papered it for the night. Jem would glaze it in the morning. She had drawn a small rectangle on the note to show me which pane, which was Alice all over. She was fourteen and had been with us eighteen months, and she wrote as neatly as a clerk. That was more than I could say for either of the men on the payroll. I tallied the night’s receipts against the cash-box and was pleased, in a small and practical way, that the accounts ran true to within a shilling and ninepence. That was the ninepence the chambermaid had taken for soap out of the petty, and she had written it on the scrap by the box, as was proper.

I set the soap-shilling aside. I went to check the cellar door. I went to check the back door. I opened the yard door, because the morning was what it was and the inn couldn’t be opened from inside.

Jem was in the yard already, as he was always in the yard already, straightening a bridle I hadn’t asked him to straighten. He did not look up when I came to the step. I did not call to him. We had done this a thousand mornings. He knew I was at the door; he knew I wouldn’t keep him.

Nan came through the kitchen a few minutes later with her pins not yet set in her hair and her apron already on, which was the proper order of Nan’s priorities. She nodded at me. She went to the bar. She began wiping down the counter with the rag I had told her, twice, to replace. This was not a battle I intended to fight at six in the morning.

The London coach came in at a quarter past seven, which was twenty minutes late and two passengers short. I knew it was two short before the door opened, because the roof of the coach was light. A full coach sat heavier on its springs. I had listened for coaches coming off the high road for most of my life. I had learned to tell a coach’s weight by the sound it made at the gate.

Jem had the horses’ heads before the wheels had stopped turning. I had the coach door. The first passenger down was a woman in a grey bonnet who had begun her complaint before her boots touched the yard.

“The hinge on my trunk,” she said, “is split.”

“I see it is, madam. Jem will take it to the shed and mend it before your onward coach. Tea in the dining room in the meanwhile?”

“The hinge is split.”

“It is,” I said. “And it will be mended. Tea is in the dining room.”

She had expected to be indignant in the yard. I had offered her the chance to be indignant indoors, which was warmer. She took it. She was, I noted, the sort of woman who would be tolerable by the time the second cup was poured. I had met her sort often enough not to be wrong about her.

I sent her through with Nan to seat her by the window where the sun would be by ten. Indignant women were, in my experience, more tractable once warmed by actual sunlight than by any argument I could make. The dining room was set for six. By the time Nan had her in a chair and a teapot in her hands, she would have forgotten the specifics of her grievance in favour of its general outline. By the time the onward coach came, she would be describing me to anyone who would listen as a perfectly capable woman.

The second passenger was a man of about sixty. He spoke to no one. He took a room-key from me with a nod and went up the stairs with a carpet-bag in one hand and his own quiet in the other. I wished him a small private well, as I did all men who paid their rooms without conversation, and I turned to the coachman.

The coachman had the look of a man who wanted his two minutes more than he wanted his ale.

“Road’s bad above Hatfield,” he said. “Wheel ruts a foot deep in places. Lost a mailbag off the back going through.”

“Did you find it?”

“Aye. But if you’ve passengers booked for the eleven, they’ll walk the last mile.”

“Thank you. I’ll send word to the parish about the road.”

“Much good it’ll do you.”

“Probably none. But I’ll send it.”

He gave me a small tilt of the head that might have been a laugh in a man with more time for laughing, and went to see to his horses. The yard emptied. I counted the coach out of the gate. A coach that left the yard and did not reach the road was a coach that had become a yard problem. This one made the turn, and I heard it take the post-road within thirty seconds.

Jem took the split trunk to the shed. The passengers had gone upstairs or to the dining room. The trunk would be back mended by half past two. The Norwich coach came through at three, and any trunk that went to the shed before noon came back by half past two. Jem understood the trade. Nan had kept one eye on the door throughout and hadn’t needed to be called. She caught my look. I caught hers. I went on to the kitchen.

Mr Pargeter the chandler came at half past nine with a case of tallow candles and a price I had not agreed to.

“Sixpence farthing the dozen,” he said, setting the case on the back step. “Prices have moved, Miss Dale.”

“They have. Yours more than most.” I dried my hands on my apron and came out to him. “That’s the fourth time in seven months, Mr Pargeter. I’m beginning to think the tallow is moving along with you.”

He gave me the smile he had learned was meant to look apologetic. “Hard season. Beef’s up.”

“Beef’s up a penny in the hundredweight. Your tallow’s up threepence in the dozen. One of us has the arithmetic wrong, and it isn’t me.”

He looked at me. He looked at the case. He looked, very briefly, at the empty road behind him. That was the tell of a man considering whether he had anywhere better to take candles at a quarter to ten on a Tuesday in October. He did not. The Black Bull bought from Highfield out of St Albans. The George at Baldock used rush-lights for the bar and wax for the rooms and never touched tallow at all. I knew this because it was my business to know it, and Mr Pargeter knew I knew it. I let the sentence sit. The yard was quiet behind him. A sparrow had come down from the stable eaves onto the paving and was picking at something the midday coach had dropped. Mr Pargeter watched me. I waited. A tallow-chandler was a man whose year had four good months and eight thin ones. The thin ones were the months you survived by knowing which house would take your candles at your price. I was not going to be the house that took his candles at his price. He knew that now. I had refused his first rise in the spring, and his second in July, and his third in September. This was the fourth. Three of the four refusals had been conducted over the same back step in the same tone of voice. On each occasion Mr Pargeter had behaved as though he had not been refused on the three previous. That was the form of the negotiation. I did not enjoy it. I also did not allow myself to dislike it, because to dislike a negotiation was to do it badly, and I had never been taught to do any work of the inn badly. The sparrow flew off.

“Five and three-ha’pence,” he said.

“Five even. And I’ll take three dozen instead of two.”

He did the multiplication behind his eyes. I watched him do it. The chandler was a close man with a close man’s habits, and I owed him no prettiness in the watching.

“Right,” he said. “Five even. Three dozen.”

“Leave them on the step. Nan will count them in.”

He left them on the step. He touched his hat, which was a courtesy he did not ordinarily pay me, and went back down the yard to his cart. I watched him to the gate. My mother had taught me to watch a man out of the yard if there had been money on the step between you. I had never known her to be wrong about a chandler.

I went back to the kitchen and washed my hands. Four pennies and a farthing, across three dozen candles, that I had not meant to save. I allowed myself, in the privacy of the basin, one small satisfaction on the occasion of having done that particular thing well. Four pennies and a farthing wouldn’t buy a pension. It would buy tomorrow’s eggs. And, if I was being truthful with myself at the basin, the smaller thing it bought was the quieter satisfaction of having been underestimated by a man who had known me nine years.

It didn’t do to take too much pride in a small victory over a candle-man. The small victories were the whole of what I had. The ledger, the rent, the chandler’s price: all of it counted against the single fact that none of it was mine in any way the law would recognise. Let me marry, and the White Hound passed by the same Tuesday post to whatever husband I had selected. This was why I had said no to Mr Eveleigh two years ago, and no again to the young Reverend Hibbert last spring, who had taken it better than he needed to. There were easier trades than this one; I was not in the market for any of them.

A man came around the back of the inn a little past noon. I saw him through the kitchen window before he knocked, because he did not knock immediately. He stood on the step with his hands at his sides and looked at the door with the face of a man trying to decide whether he was welcome enough to knock on it.

He was young. His coat was the grey that had once been another colour. His boots were coming apart at the welt of the right foot; I could see the flash of paler cloth where a stocking showed through. He settled his cap against his chest. He knocked.

I opened the door.

“Have you eaten?” I said.

“Not today, ma’am.”

“Kitchen, not taproom. The bench by the window. Bread and broth, whatever’s going.”

“Thank you, ma’am.”

He took his cap off and sat where I pointed. He kept his hands between his knees while I ladled. I went to the stockpot on the range and lifted the lid. The stock was deeper than it had been at noon. I had put a chicken carcass in at ten for the evening’s service, and it had given up what it had to give. I ladled a bowl three-quarters full and took a piece of the morning’s bread from the rack, the heel, because the heel was mine to give and no one would miss it. I carried the bowl to him. He looked at the bread for a moment before he took it up. He ate with the care of a man who had learned, somewhere, that eating too fast was its own punishment. I cut him a second slice without asking. He looked up.

“Where are you headed?” I said.

“Lincoln, ma’am. My mother’s sister.”

“That’s a week on foot.”

“Aye.”

He didn’t elaborate. I didn’t press. When he had finished the broth and mopped the bowl with the second piece of bread, he stood and put his cap back on. He said thank you again. He said God bless, and he meant it. I opened the back door for him, because his hands were occupied with his cap.

“God speed,” I said, because that was what one said.

He went.

I stood at the door after it was shut. His boots, I thought. His boots were not going to take him to Lincoln. I knew that, and he knew it, and I had fed him bread and broth that wouldn’t carry him any further than tomorrow morning. That was what I felt, standing there. He had a week’s walking ahead of him on boots that would not last a day. I had given him what I could give him. It had not been enough. He was also somebody’s son, and I minded that too, although I didn’t intend to dwell on it.

I minded it for about half a minute. Then I went back to the stockpot and took the knuckle-bone out of it because it was done. I set the pot back. I did not think about his boots again until the evening.

Two farmers had gone the wrong distance into a bottle in the taproom after the midday coach. They were now arguing about the price of barley in a tone that was beginning to carry. I went in. Nan was behind the bar, and she lifted one eyebrow at me as I came through, which was her way of saying she had it and preferred not to.

“Gentlemen,” I said. “The barley price is what the broker pays, not what we argue about over my ale. Any voice raised in this room past the next minute will leave it on its own two feet. Sit down.”

They sat down. I went back to the kitchen.

The afternoon did itself, which was the best an afternoon could do in October. I made up the first-floor rooms for the evening’s custom and sent Nan to the village for more salt, because we were always running out of salt. The gap between coaches was the only hour of the day when I could hear myself think. The thought I had, when I let myself have one, was that the knuckle-bone had been generous of me, and I had done it anyway. That was the thought. I moved on.

At eight I sent Nan home. At nine Jem came in from the yard to tell me the horses were settled and the coach-house bolts drawn, and I told him to go, and he went. At a quarter to ten I began the circuit.

The taproom fire I banked to coals. I bolted the cellar hatch and damped the kitchen range. The dining room I walked through with the lamp and found nothing out of place, which was Nan’s doing. I drew the great bolt across the front door. The back door I left for last. I checked the back corridor for anything the staff had left out. A bucket, a coat, a wet rag over a chair-back. Nothing out of place. Nan had at least learned, in the three years she had been with me, that a coaching inn cleaned for the following morning and not for the previous evening. She had not always understood this. She had come to it, and the business of teaching her had taught me as much as it had taught her about how much patience was a form of arithmetic. My hands were raw by that point in the day. Reddened from the cold water in the scullery, and from the cleaning, and from the wind through the kitchen window when Nan had left it open again by the bread. There was a small cut on the side of my right index finger that I had taken off the edge of Mr Pargeter’s delivery note when I signed it, and forgotten. It stung now, in the cold. I held the finger to my mouth for half a breath and went on. The inn rested. That was the word for it. A coaching inn at ten at night in October has a quiet of its own. Twenty rooms mostly full. Fires down. Horses quiet in the stalls. It is a quiet like no other building in the world, and I had lived inside the sound of its resting for all my life. I stood at the bottom of the back stairs with the lamp and listened to it. It made the same sound it had made the night before, and the night before that. Every night since I had been tall enough to be the one who carried the lamp.

The hall was dark above. Bridget’s door at the end of the upper corridor had no light beneath it. That meant she had taken her lamp to her window where she sat most nights, and that was Bridget’s business and not mine. The bolts were drawn. The fires were down. The inn was full. There was nobody in the house I needed to speak to, and nobody in the house I could have called down if I had. It was the hour, I allowed myself to notice, when I would have liked somebody to be there to say good night to. That was all. I noticed it. I did not sit down with it.

I crossed the yard one last time to check the taproom chimney against the sky. A fire banked wrong showed itself as a brightness against the night an hour before it showed itself in the room. My mother had taught me that, and I had never stopped doing it. The chimney was quiet.

Bridget’s window was lit as I crossed back. It was lit most nights. The shape of her at it was the shape I had seen a thousand times. I did not look up long.

The letter was on the kitchen table when I came back in. I had not seen it when I went out, because I hadn’t gone back through the kitchen after Nan had gone, and the afternoon post must have come in between. Nan must have set it there. It had Stainforth’s seal on it, and Stainforth’s handwriting on the direction: Miss D. Dale, the White Hound, Stevenage Road.

I set the lamp down beside it. I turned the letter over once in my hands. I picked up the kitchen knife, because I had never carried a letter-opener in a kitchen in my life, and I broke the seal with the edge of it.

I did not sit down to read. I stood at the table with the lamp close, which was how I read all correspondence that required an answer. A man who wrote to me from a desk was not a man I was going to read from a chair.

He wrote, as he wrote everything, with the courtesy of a man confident he was doing one a kindness. He requested a meeting on Wednesday next, the 6th of November, at my earliest convenience. The matter of the White Hound’s lease had become, in his phrasing, pressing. He had received an offer for the premises from a party in London, who proposed to establish his nephew in the trade, and the offer was, in his professional judgment, both generous and final. He had no wish to see the matter proceed without the consideration of my own circumstances, which he understood to be considerable. He remained hopeful, nonetheless, that an equitable arrangement might yet be reached between us. Should he not hear from me by Monday fortnight, he would be obliged to regard my silence as a release of any further claim upon the lease.

I read it twice, standing over the table with the lamp close. The ink, under the lamp, looked as if it had been bled into the paper rather than drawn on it. The second time I read the phrase equitable arrangement, I made a sound that was not quite a laugh. An equitable arrangement with Mr Stainforth had always meant, in my experience, an arrangement by which Mr Stainforth was made comfortable and I was made grateful. I was not going to be grateful for the loss of the White Hound to his nephew. I had seen the nephew’s face twice. I had resolved on neither occasion to commit his name to memory. I set the letter down flat on the table. The seal had broken clean. Monday fortnight. Eleven days. Eleven days to answer the letter, and eleven days to decide in what temper I meant to answer it, which were two different counts on the same clock. He had a buyer. He had a price. He had, in his own estimation, the law and the custom of the county and the run of his own certainty. And he had given me eleven days to find a reason for him to change his mind. There were eleven days. There was the lease. There was me. And I had spent the evening locking up an inn that by Christmas might not be mine to lock.

I folded the letter. I put it in the drawer below the knife where I kept correspondence that required answering. I blew out the lamp.

Then, because the room was empty, I said it aloud to the kitchen. I used a voice I would not have used in the hearing of any soul who drew breath.

“Wednesday it is, Mr Stainforth,” I said. “Bring your buyer.”

The letter sat where I had left it, opened, refolded, opened again. Stainforth's hand was the kind that had been taught by a master and then improved by money. Every loop closed. Every line ran true. He wished, at my convenience, to discuss the terms. I knew what at my convenience meant. It meant today. The kitchen was cold because I had not stirred the fire, and I had not stirred the fire because I had been sitting at the table with my hands flat on the wood and the arithmetic running through me like a draft under a door. Six weeks to the next quarter. Forty pounds owed and twenty-eight in the strongbox. Twelve to find by the first week of December, and a man on the road from Hertford who could turn that twelve into nothing if he chose. He would not choose, today. Today he would be courteous. Today was the wind, not the cliff. The ledger was where I had left it last night. I did not open it. The numbers were behind my eyes whether I looked at them or not. My finger had bled into the seam of the apron pocket. I had cut it on a hoop nail two evenings ago and forgotten about it the way one forgets the weather, which is to say it kept reminding me. I sucked the side of it, tasted iron, and stood up. The fire took on the second match. The kettle went on the hook.
