I put bread on the board for Nan and turned the loaf so the cut side faced down, the way Patience had shown me when I was eight and convinced that bread was a thing one simply ate. Then I went into the taproom and made the inn ready for inspection. I swept what did not need sweeping. I wiped the bar with a cloth that had wiped it last night. I went down into the cellar with a candle and counted casks I had counted on Saturday, and I did it because if Mr Stainforth ran his eye along my taproom floor and found a curl of ash from the grate I would have given him something, and I had decided some weeks ago that I would give him nothing he had not paid for. By the time I heard the carriage I was at the front door with my hands clean and my apron straight and the bone-deep certainty that I had already lost the morning's first round, because waiting for him on my own threshold was a posture he had chosen for me without saying a word. He stepped down from the carriage and came up the path with the unhurried walk of a man who had never been late to anything that mattered. "Miss Dale." "Mr Stainforth." "How good of you to receive me at such short notice." He pulled off his gloves a finger at a time and folded them into one hand. His coat was the kind of dark wool that costs what a coaching inn earns in a fortnight, and it carried with it the particular warm smell of good cloth in cold air. "I trust your aunt is well." "She is, thank you." "And the road from Hatfield was a credit to the parish. One forgets, in town, how passable the country can be in October." "We are fortunate in our weather." He smiled. It was a small smile, exactly proportioned to the exchange. "May I come in?" I stood aside. He did not say thank you, because thanking a tenant for the use of one's own door would have collapsed the architecture he had walked in carrying. "I so rarely have the chance," he said, looking up at the lintel as if noticing the inn for the first time, "to see the place properly. Would you indulge me?" "Of course." He walked into the taproom ahead of me, which was also a thing he had chosen. I let him. Patience had taught me that you do not contest the small ground when the large ground is what you mean to keep. He moved along the bar with one hand trailing the rail. He stopped at the window and tested the latch with his thumb, lifting it and setting it back. He bent at the cellar door and ran his thumb along the upper hinge, where I had oiled it on Sunday, and his thumb came away clean and he looked at the clean thumb for a moment before he straightened. "How is your drainage, in the cellar?" "Sound. We laid new gravel in the spring." "And your supply? You take your ale from Welwyn still?" "From Welwyn. Mr Pratchett delivers on Tuesdays." "And the coaching companies. Are they satisfied?" "They are." He waited, the way a man waits for a servant to elaborate. I let the wait happen. Patience had taught me that too. Give him the number, not the explanation. Give him the answer to the question he asked, and not the question he might have asked next. "Sixteen rooms let last week," I said, when the silence had stopped being his and become mine, "of the twenty. Three coaches a day on the up road, two on the down. I can show you the book if you wish." "Oh, I should not dream of troubling you." He had no intention of looking at the book. The book was where the answers were, and he had not come to see the answers. He had come to see the inn. We went down the cellar stair. He noted the casks. He noted the gravel I had told him about, and bent and put a finger to the floor, and lifted his finger and considered it. He did not ask another question for the length of the cellar, and I did not offer one. We came back up. He admired the woodwork on the dining-room door, which was original, and the brass on the dining-room fender, which was not, and he admired both with the same attention, which told me what his admiration was worth. In the passage to the yard he paused at the kitchen entry and looked in. Nan was at the range with her back to us. He looked at her the way a man looks at a piece of furniture he is not yet sure he means to keep. "Out the back?" he said. "This way." The stable yard had a frost on it that had not lifted, and our breath came out white. The mended half of the fence stood square. The unmended half listed where the post had taken last winter's weight and never quite recovered. Stainforth walked to the post and put his gloved hand on the top of it and rocked it gently, the way one rocks a tooth. "A pity," he said. "It will be done before the month is out." "I do not doubt it." He let go of the post. "Miss Dale, may I speak frankly?" "You may speak as you wish, Mr Stainforth. I cannot guarantee what I shall hear." The corner of his mouth moved. He liked that, in the way a man likes a coin he was not expecting to find in his pocket. Then he set the smile down. "An establishment of this size," he said, looking past me at the stable doors, "with the traffic it now carries, and the kind of trade we are seeing on the road since the summer, might benefit from —" He did not finish. He was offering me the end of the sentence, the way one offers a child a sweet, to see if she will reach. I did not reach. He let the unfinished sentence stand a moment longer, then folded it neatly away. "I only mean," he said, "that the demands upon a single proprietor must be considerable. The parish has been remarking upon it." "The parish remarks upon a great many things." "It does." "My mother used to say the parish is a creature with one mouth and no ears." "Your mother," he said, "was a remarkable woman." He meant it as an offering. He meant it as the small soft thing one places on the table before raising the larger one. I took it for what it was. "She was. She is missed." "By more than yourself, I should imagine." He looked at me then, properly, for the first time that morning. His eyes were a pale grey and very clean. "I should be sorry to see anything happen to the White Hound that might diminish its standing. I think you know that." "I know what you have said." "Then we understand one another." "We understand the words, Mr Stainforth. I do not think we understand the same thing by them." I had not meant to say it as cleanly as that. The sentence had come out a half-inch sharper than the one I had drafted in my head, and I felt it land. He did not flinch. He did not even pause. He inclined his head a quarter of an inch, as though I had pointed out a small error in his reckoning, and accepted the correction without conceding the sum. "Quite," he said. "Quite so. Well. I have taken enough of your morning." He walked me back through the passage at the same unhurried pace. At the door he drew on his gloves a finger at a time, in the reverse of the order in which he had removed them. "You keep the place very well, Miss Dale. I have always said so." "Thank you." "Do give my regards to your aunt." "I shall." I watched the carriage to the bend in the road, and past the bend, and a little after that to be sure. Then I closed the door. I leaned my shoulder against it and let out a breath I had been carrying since I heard the wheels on the gravel. My jaw ached. I unclenched it and the ache stayed. The cut on my finger had opened again. I had not noticed when. Bridget had seen the carriage. Of course she had. Her window looked down over the yard and Bridget had not missed a carriage in the boulevard du Cange — no, in the White Hound's yard — in twenty-six years. The good china was already out when I came up the stair. The teapot was on the hob and the cups were on the little table by the window, and the room smelled of lavender and tea and the particular dryness of old wool blankets that had been Bridget's smell for as long as I had known her. The fire was high. It always was. I did not argue about her coal. "Sit down, child." I sat. She poured. The good cup came to me with its old chip on the rim turned away, because Bridget had been turning the chip away from guests for so long she could not stop doing it for me. "Did he take his gloves off?" "He did." "Slowly?" "Slowly." "Mm." She drank. I drank. The tea was strong enough to stand a spoon in and she had put a finger of milk into mine because she knew I took it that way and did not need to ask. "Your mother," Bridget said, "had a man in here once. Before your time. Greville. Owned the land before Stainforth's father bought it off him in '99." "I know the name." "He wanted the stable yard expanded. Twice the stalls, he said. Twice the trade. Patience walked him round the yard same as you walked Stainforth this morning, and at the end of it she said to him, Mr Greville, the drainage will not bear it, and if I put twice the horses on this ground I shall have a yard of standing water by Michaelmas, and you will have an inn nobody stops at. And he said, Mrs Dale, you are a stubborn woman. And she said, I am, Mr Greville. Thank you for noticing." I smiled into the cup. I could hear my mother saying it. I could hear the dry note at the end of the thank you. "She knew," Bridget said, "what to hold. She was good at knowing what to hold." "She was." Bridget set her cup down. The chip on the rim caught the light. "Holding on and holding back are not the same thing, Dinah." The room did not change. The fire ticked. The tea steamed. Somewhere below, Nan dropped a pan and swore at it and the swear came up through the floorboards small and far away. I picked up the teapot. "More?" "A little." I poured for her. I poured for myself. My hand was steady, which surprised me. I set the pot down and watched the steam come off the surface of the cup. "Did he stand at the cellar door long?" Bridget said. "Long enough." "Mm." We finished the tea. She talked about the apothecary's boy who had come to deliver her drops and stayed too long at the door asking about Nan, and I let her talk because Bridget talking about Tobias Meade was Bridget being kind, and I had run out of room for the other kind of kindness this morning. I went down the stair. I shut the kitchen door behind me. I did not think about what she had said, because thinking about it was a thing I could do later, when I had the time, and time was a thing I had never had. The day went the way days go. Dinner went out. The plates came back. Nan and I scrubbed and dried and set out for tomorrow, and Jem came in from the yard with a list of small repairs and we agreed which would be done this week and which would wait, and the fire in the taproom was banked and the front bolt slid home and the inn arranged itself for sleep around me as it had every night for two years. I was crossing the taproom with the lamp when the knock came. It was not a coachman's knock. A coachman knocks with the side of his fist and keeps knocking. This was three taps with the knuckles, evenly spaced, and then nothing. I drew the bolt and opened the door a hand's breadth. A man. Tall. Standing back from the threshold, not crowding it. A dog at his heel — long-legged, grey-brindled, sat without being told. The man's boots had walked a long way. They were good leather once. They were not good leather now. "Forgive the hour." His voice was low and careful. The voice of a man who had learned to make it do what he wanted. "We're shut." "I see that. I'd ask a corner of your stable for the night, if you can spare it. The dog won't trouble the horses. I can pay for it in the morning, or in work, whichever suits." I looked at him. Thirty seconds is enough for most men. He was not drunk; the consonants were too clean. He was not dangerous in any of the ways I had learned to recognise; his hands were where I could see them, and the dog was the kind of dog that does not sit still for a man it does not trust. He was tired. He was very tired. The kind of tired that has been tired for months. "The stable's open. Empty stall at the back, on the left, has clean straw. There's a bucket by the trough." "Thank you." "We talk in the morning. Not before." "Understood." He touched the brim of his hat — once, brief — and turned. The dog rose with him as though they had agreed on it years ago. I shut the door and slid the bolt. I went through to the kitchen and set the lamp down and looked out of the window into the yard, because I looked out into the yard every night before bed, and the man was in it, and that was a fact about the yard tonight I needed to take account of. He had the dog settled against the wall of the stable, out of the wind. He stood a moment with his hand on the dog's head. Then he went to the stable door and tried the hinge and the hinge gave, and he stood looking at it. He did not call for tools. He went round to the wood pile by the kindling shed and came back with a stone in his hand. He set the hinge pin with the stone, two careful taps, and tried the door again, and it sat true. He turned then, and looked at the fence. The crooked post. He walked to it. He set his shoulder against it and rocked it the way Stainforth had rocked it that morning, and then he went down on one knee in the frost and worked his hand into the dirt at its base and packed the loose earth back round it, and packed it again, and rose and pressed the post upright with the flat of his hand and held it there until the ground had taken the weight. He stood a moment, looking at the post, his hand still on it. I turned from the window and put out the lamp.

The letter was folded on the kitchen table where I had left it, because I had not wanted it near the ledger and I had not wanted it near the bread. Stainforth's hand was the kind that had been taught by a good tutor and corrected by a better one. Every downstroke as obedient as a servant. He wished to discuss the terms. He wrote *the terms* as though the terms were a creature of their own, and he were only its custodian, kind enough to walk it over on a lead. I knew the arithmetic. I had known it since Michaelmas. The next rent quarter was due on the twenty-fifth of December, and between now and then the inn would have to earn one hundred and twelve pounds clear, which meant a full coach in most nights and two full on market days, which meant weather we would not get and luck we had not been having. Stainforth's visit was six weeks early, which was the whole of his courtesy. A man who meant to take something did not wait for the last hour. He arrived when the taking could still be called a conversation. The fire was banked low and the kettle was cold. My hands were the hands I had gone to bed with: the knuckles cracked red from the cellar air, and the cut on the pad of my index finger still open enough to catch on the muslin when I reached for it. I did not reopen the ledger. The numbers were where I had left them. I got up and laid the fire. • • • By nine I had swept the taproom twice, wiped down the bar until the oak showed its grain, checked the cellar for damp and the cellar stair for the loose tread that had been loose since August, and taken a rag to the window latch in the passage where last winter's paint had started to blister. None of it wanted doing this morning. All of it wanted doing. I was setting the inn the way my mother used to set a table for the vicar — nothing better, nothing different, only every fork at its right angle and the salt where a man could reach it without asking. A house that gave him nothing to correct. Nan came through with the breakfast plates stacked up her arm and looked at the rag in my hand and the bucket by the door, and did not say a word. She was twenty-one and she could read a room faster than most people could read a bill. She set the plates down, took the rag from me, and went to do the bar rail a third time herself. "He'll be here by eleven," I said. "I know," said Nan. • • • I heard the carriage before I saw it — the soft four-wheeled roll of a gentleman who paid to keep his springs in order, not the rattle of the post coaches we fed and turned round in twenty minutes. By the time I reached the front step he was already on the gravel, and his man was already walking the horse. Stainforth's coat was good wool, cut close, and smelling faintly of cedar where it had hung in a press that knew its business. Next to it the inn's doorway looked like what it was: whitewash that wanted fresh, a boot scraper worn to a shine by thirty years of boots. He took off his hat. His hair was iron grey at the temples and combed with the care of a man who did not like surprises. "Miss Dale." He inclined his head. "Forgive the hour." "Mr Stainforth." "I hope you had my note." "I had it." "And Mrs Dale, she is well?" "My aunt keeps very well. Thank you." He drew off his gloves slowly, one finger at a time, and folded them together in his left hand as though he meant to hold them for the length of the visit. He did not give them to me. He did not ask after my day. He stood on my step with his gloves in his hand and looked past my shoulder at the passage, as if the inn were a thing he had heard described and was now politely verifying. "A wretched road up from Hatfield," he said. "Quite rutted." "It has been a wet autumn." "So it has. So it has." He smiled, a small, symmetrical smile that cost him nothing. "I wonder, Miss Dale — I so rarely have the chance to see the place properly — if I might impose on you to walk the premises. Not for any formal purpose. Only for my own interest." I stood in my doorway a moment longer than civility required, and he waited for me to stand aside, as patient as a man waiting for a cat to move off a chair he had decided to sit in. "Of course," I said, and stood aside. • • • He walked the taproom the way a man walks a horse he is thinking of buying. He did not touch the tables. He touched the bar. He ran two fingers along the underside of the oak, where a poor carpenter would have left splinters and where my grandmother's carpenter had not. He seemed pleased that he could find no splinter, and also, I thought, faintly disappointed. "Your drainage in the cellar," he said. "Holds, does it?" "It holds." "Even in the wet?" "Even in the wet. The floor is tiled to fall to the drain at the north wall. My mother had it done in the year six." "Ah. Your mother." He paused at that. "A formidable woman, by all accounts." "By all accurate ones." He gave a thin laugh at that and did not look at me. He opened the cellar door. The hinge was new — I had put it on myself in June, because the old one had begun to drop and the door caught on the flagstone. He found the hinge with his thumb, and ran his thumb up the length of it, from the plate to the pin, feeling the set of the screws. He did not need to do that. Nothing asked him to do that. He did it, and then he looked at me, and then he closed the door. "Very sound," he said. "Thank you." "The coaching companies. I understand the Edinburgh Mail still stops with you." "Four nights a week. And the Leeds, and the York day coach." "And they are satisfied." "They are satisfied, or they would not stop." "Quite. Quite." He walked to the window and ran a finger along the latch and drew the finger back and looked at the pad of it. There was no dust. He had not expected any. "And the rooms?" "Twenty. Seventeen let last Wednesday. Twelve on Thursday. A slow Friday. The book is on the bar if you would like to see it." "I would not presume." "It is no trouble." "Another time, perhaps." He turned from the window. "I confess I am always impressed, Miss Dale, when I come here. One hears — one hears, you understand, I do not say one believes — one hears that the house is run on thinner ice than is comfortable. And then one arrives, and the bar is clean, and the hinge is sound, and the book is full, and one feels rebuked for listening." "You should not listen," I said. "We are very well." I gave him the answer in the number of words it wanted, and not one more. It is how Patience taught me to handle a man who came to argue a price — you give him the number. You do not give him the reasoning. If he wants the reasoning he may buy it by asking. He did not ask. • • • Outside, the stable yard was cold enough to show our breath. Jem was at the far end, settling the Leeds team for their morning feed. He did not look up at us, which was his form of greeting and his form of refusal at once. Stainforth did not greet him. One does not greet an ostler one does not know. This is the kind of thing men like Stainforth do not have to be told. He walked the yard. He came to the fence at the paddock corner where the post had gone crooked in last week's frost, the top rail loose at its joint, and he touched the post, and tested it with the weight of his palm, and did not right it. "This wants a hand," he said. "It wants half an hour of one." "Mm." He did not take his palm off the post. "Miss Dale, may I speak plainly." "You may speak however you prefer." "An inn of this size" — he said it as though measuring a coat — "an inn of this size might benefit from —" He did not finish the sentence. He left it in the cold between us for me to pick up, and when I did not, he smiled as if I had confirmed something for him. "From?" I said. "From a firmer hand on certain matters. The yard. The suppliers. The parish — you will know there has been talk about the soldiers on the road, and the feeding of them, and what that draws to a respectable stop. Nothing serious. Only talk. But talk is how trouble begins, and it is easier borne by a household with — let us say — more than one shoulder to set against it." I felt the cold come up the back of my neck the way it comes up when a fire has gone out behind you and you have not noticed. I did not look away from him. I had been hearing this sentence in one form or another since I was fifteen years old. I did not need it finished. I knew the end of it as well as I knew the cut on my finger. "The yard has been held by a woman's hand for forty-four years, Mr Stainforth. My grandmother's, then my mother's, now mine. It has not fallen down yet." He considered the fence post. He did not flinch. A man who flinched could be met. A man who absorbed was another matter. "No," he said. "No, it has not. And I hope very much, Miss Dale, that it will not. Truly. I mean that." He took his hand off the post. "I only wish to say — and I would not say it if I did not respect your mother's memory — that the terms of the lease come up for review in the new year, and it is my duty as landlord to see that the parish is served. I am sure we understand each other." "I think we do." "Good." He smiled again, warmer than he had smiled all morning. "I shall not trespass on your day any further. You keep this place very well, Miss Dale. Anyone can see it." It was the compliment a man gives a tenant he intends to replace, and he knew I knew it, and he gave it anyway, because the giving of it was also the visit. He put on his gloves. He found his carriage. I stood on the step until the wheels turned out of the yard and onto the road and the sound of them was lost in the sound of the rooks in the elm. Then I went inside and shut the door. • • • I set my forehead against the cold wood for the count of three and then I straightened. My jaw ached. I had been holding it for two hours without knowing I was holding it. • • • Bridget's room was at the top of the back stair and it was warm before you reached the door — the one constant warm place in the inn from October to April, because Bridget kept her fire, and I did not argue with her about the coal. I had stopped arguing with her about the coal when I was eight. She was in her chair by the window with the good china already set on the table and the pot under the knitted cosy she had made in 1803. The cosy had been new when I was fourteen. The china had been in the family since before any of us could remember, and she used it, as she had always used it, because there was no one we were keeping it for. "Sit down, girl," she said. I sat. She poured. Her hands were knotted at the knuckles and she held the pot with both of them, and she did not spill. She had seen the carriage. She had been at the window when it came and at the window when it went. She did not ask me what Stainforth had wanted. Bridget never asked a question she already had the answer to. "He touched the cellar hinge," I said. I had not meant to say it. "Did he." She handed me the cup. The cup was warm already. "Your mother had a landlord before him. Greville. This was in the year four, five. You were a child." "I remember Greville." "Do you." "The red face." "The red face, yes. He wanted the stabling expanded. He had a nephew in Baldock with a notion about a larger yard. He wrote your mother three letters and came twice. The second time he brought a surveyor." She drank her tea and set the cup down. "Your mother knew the drainage in the yard would not bear it. The low end floods in a wet March — you know this. So she refused him. She refused him flat, on paper, with a copy kept. And the third letter never came, because Greville had a stroke at the Angel in Ware and that was the end of Greville." "I don't remember the third letter not coming." "You would not. You were seven." She looked at me over the rim of the cup. "I mention it because your mother refused Greville for the right reason, which was the drainage. She was always refusing for the right reason. It was her particular gift. She could tell a stone wall from a man, which is more than most of us manage." I drank my tea. The room smelled of lavender and old wool and the particular sweetness of the linseed Bridget used on her writing box. I had sat in this room as a child while Patience worked the bar downstairs, and my shoulders dropped an inch whether I wished them to or not. "And now," Bridget said, "there is Stainforth." "There is Stainforth." "And you will refuse him for the right reason." "I will refuse him for every reason I have." She smiled a small, Irish smile that had nothing soft in it. "That is my girl. That has always been my girl." She turned the cup in its saucer. "Dinah." "Yes." "Holding on and holding back are not the same thing." I reached for the teapot. "Is this one cold? I'll go down and —" "It is not cold." "I'll go down anyway. Nan is short-handed with the Leeds coach." "Mm," said Bridget. She did not press me. She never pressed me. She said what she had come to say, and whether I had heard it was my problem, not hers. She picked up her cup again, and I set mine down, and I kissed the top of her white head where the hair was thinnest, and I went. On the stair I put the sentence away. I put it in the place I put things that I would not be thinking about today, and I shut the lid on it, and I went back down to the kitchen and started the dough for the evening's bread, because we were short by a loaf and I had said nothing to Nan about it and it was my fault, not hers. • • • By ten at night the last coach had gone through, the taproom was empty but for Jem's mug on the end of the bar where he had left it out of habit and an apology, and I was drawing the bolt on the front door when someone knocked on it from the outside. Not hard. Not the knock of a man who thought he was owed an answer. Three even raps, spaced the way a man knocks when he would rather not have troubled you. I opened it on the chain. A man stood on the step with a dog at his knee. Tall — I had to look up a little, and I am not short. His coat had walked a long way. His boots had walked further. They were good leather, once, and now they were the colour of the lane after rain and worn so thin at the toe that I could see where the stitching was giving. The dog was a lurcher, long-legged, grey-brindled, and it sat when he had not told it to sit, and it watched me with an expression I might have called worried if I had been in the habit of assigning expressions to dogs. "Madam," he said. His voice was low and even, and tired. "I am sorry for the hour. I would not ordinarily — I wondered if you had work, or a bed for the night. I can pay for one. I would rather earn the other." "Both tonight or one?" "Whichever you have." He did not smile. He did not lean on the doorpost. He stood straight in the cold as though standing straight were what he did, and waited for me to decide. The dog leaned against his leg. I read him in thirty seconds, which is the length of time reading a man at a door takes if you have been doing it since you were fourteen. Not drunk. Not dangerous. Not a problem I was going to solve tonight. "The stable," I said. "There's clean straw in the end stall and a blanket on the hook. The dog goes with you. We'll talk in the morning." "Thank you." "Breakfast is at six. If you're not in the yard by seven you've missed me." "I will be in the yard." I unhooked the chain long enough to point him round to the side gate, and he went, and the dog went after him without being called. I shut the door. I drew the bolt. • • • I should have gone up. Instead I went through to the kitchen, because the kitchen window gave onto the yard and because I checked the yard every night before bed — the stable door, the pump, the coal — and because there was no reason not to look as well tonight as any other night. He was in the yard by the stable door. The dog had already lain down against the step with its chin on its paws. He set his pack against the wall, and he did not go in. He stood a moment with his hand on the stable door, and then he ran his palm down the edge of it to the hinge, and found the screw that had been loose since September, and tightened it a quarter turn with his thumb against the head of it, because he had no tool and it would hold until morning. Then he crossed the yard to the paddock fence. He set his shoulder against the crooked post where Stainforth had put his hand that afternoon, and he shifted it straight, working it in the frozen ground until it took. He lifted the loose rail back onto its joint. He put both hands on the top of the post, and leaned, testing it, and leaned again, and took his hands off. The post stood. I turned from the window. I banked the kitchen fire. I went up to bed.

The London coach came in forty minutes late and two passengers short, which meant the coachman was already talking before the wheels had stopped turning.

I went out to meet it. The yard was cold, the cobbles dark from last night's rain, and my breath came in short clouds as I crossed to the step. Jem was at the horses before I reached the coach door, which was where he was supposed to be and where he always was without being told.

"Road above Hatfield," the coachman said, climbing down. His name was Aldous. He'd been running this route four years and never wasted a word. "Three inches of mud from Welwyn to the toll gate. We took twenty minutes off the schedule and still came in behind."

"Same stretch as September?"

"Worse."

"I'll note it."

He nodded and went to his horses.

The two passengers who had made it were a woman of middle age in a grey travelling cloak and a younger man whose hat was pulled low and whose jaw was set tight. The woman went straight toward the door without looking at me. The man came last and then stopped and looked back at the boot.

"That trunk," he said.

I looked. The hinge on the near corner had split, and the lid stood open a finger's width, held only by the rope the guard had lashed across it.

"I'll have Jem see to it," I said.

"It was locked when I handed it over in London."

"The road above Hatfield is three inches of mud. Your trunk is intact. You've lost a hinge."

He looked at me. He was waiting for something—an apology, or an argument. I offered neither.

"I'll want a room with a bolt," he said.

"You'll have one."

He went inside. Jem was already there, hands on the rope, working it loose without being asked.

The guard had the mail bag down. Aldous was at the trough. The woman in grey was through the door. I stayed until the last of it was settled.

Aldous came back around the rear of the coach, hands in his coat pockets.

"Same stretch will be impassable by December. Tell Barker if you see him."

"Barker came through Tuesday. He knows."

He nodded once. That was the end of it.

The yard cleared in the way it always did: not at once, but in pieces, one thing finishing and then the next until there was only mud and wheel ruts and the smell of horse. Jem went through the door with the trunk balanced on his shoulder. The passengers were upstairs or headed to the dining room. The coachman was gone.

I went back inside.

Nan was at the bar. She had been pulling a cloth across the same length of counter for longer than the wood required, which was how I knew she had been watching the yard. I did not say anything about it. She did not say anything about anything. I went through to the office.

Hollis came an hour later, earlier than his usual Monday, and that alone told me something. He had his delivery cart outside and his boy with him, and he set the first box of candles on the counter before he had said good morning.

"Prices have come up," he said. "Tallow's dearer now than it was at summer."

"How much dearer."

"Sixpence the dozen above what we agreed in March."

I knew what he paid for tallow. I knew what he charged at the Falcon in Hatfield and the Cock at Welwyn, because Barker talked and the girl from the Cock came in on Sundays. This was the fourth time since March he had arrived naming a new price. Sixpence above our agreed rate was not the market. It was what he wanted to see if I would pay.

"You charged the Falcon fourpence above March in September," I said. "You're asking me sixpence."

"Different quantities."

"Same quantities. I've kept the record."

He looked at the box on the counter. He looked at me.

"Fourpence and a half."

"Fourpence," I said. "Same as the Falcon. And you'll be back next month."

He left the candles and went out to his boy.

I carried the first box through to the storeroom and set it on the shelf above the October account. Four increases since March. This one I had held.

The rent was due in eleven days. The ledger balanced, barely, because I had managed every line of it myself.

If I had a husband, the inn would be his. The ledger, the candles, the argument with Hollis, the note about the Hatfield road—all of it would pass from my hands by law and by the ordinary course of things. I set the second box on top of the first and went back out to the bar.

Nan had stopped pretending about the counter and was drawing ale for the man from the coach, who had come down from his room and apparently found his temper somewhere on the stairs. There was the dining room to check and the afternoon coach to account for.

I went to the dining room.

Chapter One: The Forger's Daughter

I could forge anything.

Signatures in three hands---copperplate, clerk's scrawl, that loose flourish gentlemen used when they signed away fortunes. Seals with the right weight of wax; too much and it looked new, too little and it looked desperate. Letters of reference that smelled like attics and old leather; I knew seventeen ways to age paper and three to make ink fade just right.

The letter under my hands was a gentleman's introduction to a shipping firm in Bristol. Simple work. The kind Thomas charged twenty pounds for because desperation had a fixed price in Seven Dials.

Dawn was grey through the shop window; coal smoke made everything the color of old copper. I worked by candlelight even with the sun rising---better for seeing the watermark, that faint tracery that separated my work from a hemp rope at Newgate. The paper was good stock; Thomas had sources for that, men who worked at the mills and understood the economics of silence. I'd matched the seal to the original Thomas kept in the third drawer; pressed it in red wax that I'd mixed myself, just enough vermillion to catch light without looking fresh.

Looking at the finished letter, I felt that old lift of pride---wrong, probably, that satisfaction in work that could get us both hanged.

"Veri."

My father's voice from the doorway.

I didn't look up. "Almost done."

"Leave it. We have---" He stopped. Started again. "Someone's coming."

That made me look.

Thomas Blackwood was a forger; a good one; the kind Seven Dials whispered about when the Fleet Street printers needed something special. He'd taught me everything---how to read a hand, how to match ink, how to lie with paper and wax and confidence. Fifty-three years old and he'd never been caught; not once; but his hands shook now when the creditors called.

He wasn't shaking. His face had gone---no, wrong word. His face was plaster-white, that particular shade I associated with fear the way other girls associated it with weddings.

I set down my pen. "Who?"

"Don't know his name. But he sent word yesterday through Mick Flynn."

That was bad. Mick Flynn ran a flash house in St. Giles; the kind of place where cutpurses drank and housebreakers planned work and everyone understood that law was something that happened to other people. If someone sent word through Flynn, they weren't ordinary clients.

Three exits in this room. The door Thomas stood in. The window that opened onto the alley---two-story drop, but there was a rain barrel. The passage behind the false wall that led to the cellar. I'd counted them a thousand times; part of how I worked; always knowing where to run.

"He's coming at dawn?"

"He's coming now."

I looked at the letter on my desk, at the careful lines of false reference and forged regard. Twenty pounds for this. Forty if the client was desperate. Death if we were caught.

"Put it away," Thomas said.

I did. Blotted the signature---Henry Carstairs, shipping merchant, a man who'd never existed but whose hand I knew better than my own---and slid the letter into the drawer with seventeen others. Evidence enough to hang us both; but we'd always worked from the shop; always kept our life and our work in the same three rooms because rent was dear and secrets were expensive.

The knock came.

Not loud. Not aggressive. Three taps, perfectly spaced, the kind of knock that suggested the person on the other side had knocked on many doors and knew exactly how to make it sound like they belonged.

Thomas looked at me. I looked back. We'd had this conversation before---without words, just that exchange of glances that said run or stay, fight or fold, trust this or cut our losses.

He nodded. Once. I was staying.

Thomas crossed to the door; his hand steady now; that particular steadiness that came from decades of lying. He opened it.

The man who entered wore Mayfair tailoring in a Seven Dials dawn.

Wrong. Everything about him wrong for this place, this time, this kind of business. His coat was superfine---I knew fabric the way other girls knew fashion---and his boots were polished to that mirror-shine that required a valet. He carried a walking stick; the handle caught what light the candle gave; silver, probably real, the kind of thing that would get a man murdered three streets over.

But he walked into our shop like he owned it.

Like he owned Seven Dials and everyone in it.

"Mr. Blackwood." His voice was officer-class. That particular accent that came from the right schools and the right regiment and never having to wonder where the next meal was coming from. "Thank you for seeing me."

Thomas said nothing. I watched him do the calculation I'd done myself---this man was dangerous; but turning him away might be more dangerous; we needed money; but some money came with a rope attached.

"And this must be your daughter."

The man's gaze found me.

I was still at my desk; still wearing my work clothes; ink on my fingers despite the rag I'd used. My hair was pinned back with the same pins I'd been using for three years; bent in places but too expensive to replace. I'd been working since midnight and probably looked it.

But I met his eyes.

He was handsome---that distant, cold kind of handsome that came from good bones and better breeding. Forty-five, maybe. Dark hair going grey at the temples in that way that looked distinguished on men and old on women. His face was unreadable; a perfect mask of polite interest; the kind of expression that gave away nothing while seeing everything.

"My daughter helps with---" Thomas started.

"With the work. Yes. I'm aware." The man smiled. It was the smile of a hanging judge who enjoyed his job. "Miss Blackwood. A pleasure."

I didn't correct him on the Miss---I'd never been formally acknowledged, never had that luxury---just nodded.

"May I?" He gestured to the chair across from my desk.

Thomas looked at me. I looked at the man's boots---expensive, yes, but with mud from Seven Dials streets. He'd walked here. That was either confidence or stupidity, and he didn't look stupid.

"Sit," I said.

He did. Placed his walking stick across his knees; those perfect knees in their perfect trousers; and looked at me like I was a curiosity. A specimen. Something to study.

"I have a proposition," he said. "One that requires someone with your particular talents."

I waited. Thomas had taught me that too---never speak first when someone is buying lies.

"There's a house party," the man continued. "At Kelmarsh Park. The Duke of Kelmarsh's estate in Northamptonshire. Week-long affair. Very exclusive. Very observed."

I knew about house parties the way I knew about peacocks---both existed, both were expensive, neither had anything to do with me.

"There will be a guest," he said. "An Italian contessa. Contessa Oriana Castelfranco. She'll be traveling from Venice. Letters of introduction. Aristocratic connections. Fluent Italian, of course."

Of course. I'd been teaching myself Italian for two years---street Latin and opera phrases and anything else I could steal from the books Thomas brought home. My accent was probably terrible but better than nothing.

"Unfortunately," the man said, and his smile widened, "the Contessa will not be attending."

Silence.

I understood. Of course I understood. I'd been understanding the unspoken language of crime since I was eight years old.

"You want me to impersonate her."

"Very good, Miss Blackwood."

"Why?"

"The Duke has something I need. Documents in his private study. I need them retrieved. Carefully. Without suspicion."

"And you need a thief who speaks Italian."

"I need someone who can play a role. Who can forge a life as easily as she forges a letter. Who can walk into Kelmarsh Park as a contessa and walk out with what I require."

I looked at Thomas. His face was careful---too careful. That blank expression he wore when magistrates asked questions.

"No," I said.

The man's eyebrows rose. "I haven't mentioned the payment."

"Don't care about the payment. This is---" I searched for a word. "Mad. I can't pass for a contessa. I've never been north of Camden. I don't know the first thing about---"

"You know everything about being someone you're not." His voice was quiet. Final. "That's what forgery is, Miss Blackwood. Creating truth from lies. You've been doing it all your life."

"On paper. Not---"

"I'll provide everything you need. Documentation. History. Training. You'll have a week to prepare. Mrs. Fielding will coach you---she's an expert in deportment, speech, all those little aristocratic habits. You'll know everything the real Contessa knows. You'll be perfect."

"And if I'm caught?"

"You won't be."

"But if I am?"

His smile didn't change. "Then you'll hang. Along with your father. I have enough evidence in my possession to ensure that."

The room was very quiet.

Thomas's hand found the edge of the doorframe. Knuckles white.

"You're blackmailing us," I said. Because calling things by their names was sometimes all you had left.

"I'm offering you employment. Very well-paid employment. Fifty pounds for the week. More if you successfully retrieve the documents."

Fifty pounds. We owed sixty-two to various creditors; three of them the breaking-fingers kind; one of them worse. Fifty pounds would clear most of that. Let us breathe. Let us work without looking over our shoulders.

But this wasn't work. This was walking into a duke's house and playing aristocrat and stealing from someone who probably had the power to have us both transported.

"I need to think about it," I said.

"No." The man stood. "You need to say yes. Because I've taken the liberty of ensuring your father's arrest on forgery charges. He's expected at Newgate this afternoon. Unless, of course, you agree to my terms."

Everything stopped.

I looked at Thomas. Thomas looked back. His face was that terrible empty calm.

"You're lying," I said.

"I rarely lie, Miss Blackwood. It's so much easier to simply arrange reality to suit my purposes." He reached into his coat---I tensed, but he only pulled out papers. Set them on my desk. "There's your documentation. The Contessa's history. Her letters of introduction. A miniature portrait for reference---you do look rather alike, which is fortunate. And the address where Mrs. Fielding will meet you tomorrow at noon."

I didn't touch the papers.

The man picked up his walking stick. Gave me that hanging-judge smile. "You have twelve hours to prepare yourself, Miss Blackwood. After that, Mrs. Fielding will begin your transformation. One week from today, you'll travel to Kelmarsh Park. You'll arrive as Contessa Oriana Castelfranco. You'll retrieve the documents I've specified. And you'll leave with your father's life intact and fifty pounds richer."

"And if I refuse?"

"You won't refuse. Because you love your father. And because---" He paused at the door. "Because you're curious. About that world. Those people. That life you've spent your whole life forging but never living."

He was right.

God help me, he was right.

He left. The door closed. The room was grey dawn and coal smoke and the particular silence that came after disaster.

Thomas sat down. Not gracefully---just sat, like his legs had given out.

"It's a trap," I said.

"Of course it's a trap."

"We should run."

"Where?" He laughed, but it was glass breaking. "Veri, I'm fifty-three. I've got bad knees and worse lungs and three creditors who'll find me anywhere in London. You're twenty-four and the most talented forger I've ever trained. Between us, we've got maybe forty pounds and one chance."

"One chance to get hanged."

"Or one chance to clear the debt and start clean."

I looked at the papers on my desk. At the miniature portrait lying face-up. The Contessa was beautiful---dark hair like mine, similar bone structure, but her face had never known hunger. Never counted exits. Never wondered if the next knock was magistrates.

"I could be anyone," I said. Out loud this time. "That's my gift."

"And your curse." Thomas's voice was rough. "Your mother used to say that. That you'd be anything you chose."

I looked at him. Really looked. At his greying hair and his forger's hands and the stoop in his shoulders that came from thirty years bent over a desk. At the man who'd taught me everything except how to live any other way.

"You'll go to Newgate if I don't do this."

"Yes."

"And if I do this and fail, we both hang."

"Probably."

I picked up the miniature portrait. Studied the Contessa's face. Her serene expression. Her perfect aristocratic bearing. Everything I'd never been and never could be.

Except.

Except I could forge anything.

"Tell me about the client," I said. "Who is he?"

"Don't know. Calls himself the Sphinx. Deals in information. Blackmail, mostly. Very connected. Very dangerous."

"The Sphinx." I tested the name. It fit. Riddles and secrets and impossible questions.

I set down the portrait. Looked at my ink-stained fingers. At the work I'd been doing when the knock came. At the letter of recommendation that would free some desperate merchant from his gambling debts and cost us nothing but skill and risk.

And I thought about my mother.

Catherine Blackwood, dead twelve years now. Consumption and poverty and Seven Dials winters. She'd taught me to read when I was four---taught me with stolen books and candle-stubs and that fierce determination that I be more than her circumstances. That I be educated. That I be capable.

That I be better.

"You're meant for more than this," she'd said. Over and over, until it became prayer or prophecy or both. "You're meant for more."

I'd never believed her.

But standing at the window now, looking toward Mayfair and Belgravia and all those glittering places I'd only ever seen from Seven Dials streets, I wondered. If maybe. Just maybe.

I could forge a contessa as easily as I forged a signature.

I could walk into that world wearing silk instead of threadbare muslin.

I could see---just for a week---what it felt like to be someone else. Someone better. Someone who belonged.

"I'll do it," I said.

Thomas exhaled. "Veri---"

"I'll do it. But not for the money. Not even for you." I turned from the window. Met his eyes. "I'll do it because I want to know. If I can. If I'm good enough. If all these years of forging other people's lives means I can forge my own."

"That's not forging. That's lying."

"What's the difference?"

He didn't answer. Maybe there wasn't an answer. Maybe the whole of Seven Dials was built on that question---the difference between survival and sin, between what you had to do and what you chose to be.

I picked up the papers the Sphinx had left. The Contessa's history. Her letters. The address for Mrs. Fielding.

"In seven days," I said, "I'll be a contessa. Or I'll be dead."

Thomas stood. Crossed to me. Put his forger's hands on my shoulders. "Your mother would be proud."

"My mother would be horrified."

"Both those things can be true."

He kissed my forehead. Left the room. Left me standing there with a dead woman's life in my hands and my own future balanced on the edge of a lie.

I looked in the cracked mirror propped against the wall. Studied my face. My dark eyes and my sharp cheekbones and my Seven Dials pallor. Tried to imagine myself in silk. In jewels. In that world of house parties and dukes and casual wealth.

"Buongiorno," I said to my reflection. The accent was terrible. "Mi chiamo Contessa Oriana Castelfranco."

The words felt like forgery.

Like truth.

Like the best lie I'd ever told.

Outside, Seven Dials was waking. The costermongers and the pickpockets and the girls who sold what they had to because selling anything else required capital. The world I'd known all my life. The world I was about to leave---temporarily, dangerously, possibly fatally.

I touched the miniature portrait again. The Contessa's face stared back, serene and distant.

"I could be you," I whispered. "I could be anyone."

And somewhere in the grey dawn, in a room that smelled of ink and secrets, I became someone else.

I became the Counterfeit Contessa.

The London coach came in twenty minutes late and two passengers short, which I already knew from the yard boy's face before the wheels had stopped turning.

I was at the door before the coachman had handed down the second passenger. There were four in all: a wool merchant I recognised from the autumn run, his face dark with something that had nothing to do with the cold; a woman in grey travelling alone who descended carefully, testing each step; a clergyman who went straight past me as though I were a post; and a young man in a new coat who stood looking at his trunk with an expression that prepared me for trouble.

The trunk had a split hinge. One corner of the lid had buckled outward, and the strap across it was doing work the hinge should have done.

The young man said, "Your man dropped it."

"Let's see what we have," I said.

Jem was already there. He had not been there a moment before and I had not called him; he was simply there, with his hands on the trunk before the young man could say anything further. I watched him test the hinge with his thumb.

"Came to it already split," Jem said, not looking up. "The leather's dried clear through. See how it's cracked along the fold?"

The young man looked. The cracking was there, plain enough.

"I'll have Watts look at it this afternoon," I said. "He'll have it right before the evening. If the goods are undamaged, the repair costs nothing."

He wanted to argue. I could see it gathering in him. But there was nothing in what I'd said to argue with, and after a moment he let it go and turned toward the door. Jem lifted the trunk and took it toward the back passage.

The coachman caught me before I turned back to the others. His name was Briggs and he was a careful man, not given to complaint.

"North road above Hatfield," he said. "There's a section coming loose before the Stanborough turn. Six feet, maybe eight. I took it slow but the next rain will finish it."

"I'll send word to the surveyor's office today."

"Aye." He looked at his horses. "That's all."

The yard emptied by degrees. The wool merchant went to the dining room still wearing his dark expression. The woman in grey went upstairs with the chambermaid. The clergyman had already vanished. The young man followed when he saw Jem carry his trunk through the back. The horses went to the stable in pairs, their breath hanging white in the cold air. Briggs climbed back to his box and the coach went through to the far yard.

The silence after a coach arrival is not a quiet silence. It is the silence of a room that has just stopped being loud. I stood a moment in the doorway and watched the last of the mud settle in the wheel-ruts.

Behind me I heard Nan moving at the bar, the clink of a glass set down and taken up again. She had been watching the whole of it through the serving hatch, her cloth going in slow circles on the counter. She did not say anything and I did not turn around.

The chandler came just before eleven.

His name was Perrin. He had held the account six years, and in that time I had paid him on the first of every month without exception. He set his delivery down in the passage and named his price, and the price was higher.

I said, "That's the fourth rise since March."

"Tallow's up across the county. I'm not the only one."

"You're charging the King's Arms at Welwyn sixpence less per dozen."

A pause. He had not expected that. "Different quantities," he said.

"I take more than they do." I let that sit for a moment. "I pay on time and I don't send things back. That's worth something to a man carrying three accounts that don't." I knew two of the three by name. He knew I knew. "I'll pay what I paid in September."

He looked at the candles. He looked at me.

"Split the difference," he said.

"September's price," I said.

He left the candles.

I carried the tally book through to the back room and wrote the figure down. The rent had gone up in August. The coal price had gone up before that. Every number I wrote came back to the same fact: every lease, every account, every supplier on this road would belong to a husband if I had one, and the inn along with them. I would have no more standing to negotiate than the hall table. I noted the figure and closed the book.

In the passage, Watts was already at the young man's trunk with a strip of new leather and a pot of paste. The hinge, as Jem had said, had been dry for months.

Chapter One: The Forger's Daughter

The Earl of Pemberton's signature required three things: confidence in the downstroke, hesitation on the capital P, and complete disregard for the fact that the Earl had been dead for six months.

I pressed the seal into red wax—not too hot, which made it glossy and obvious, but cooled just enough to catch light the way old seals did. The letter beneath my hands was a recommendation for a merchant's son. Fifteen pounds, which would keep us in coal for two months and maybe convince Mrs. Hartley downstairs that we'd pay February's rent.

"That's Pemberton's third son you've gotten positions for."

I didn't look up. Thomas had a way of appearing in doorways without sound, which made him excellent at forgery and terrible at fatherhood. "Fourth. The second son got two positions. Different names, different cities."

"Christ." But he was smiling. "Your mother would have—"

"Don't."

The smile vanished. We had rules, Thomas and I. Don't talk about Mother. Don't talk about the life before Seven Dials. Don't count how many years we'd been doing this or how many more we could manage before someone noticed the pattern.

I blotted the signature—Thomas Pemberton, who'd never existed but whose handwriting I knew better than my own—and held the letter to the light from our single window. Dawn was happening somewhere above the smoke, turning everything the color of old pennies. The watermark showed through perfect. Another ghost successfully documented.

"We've got company coming." Thomas's voice had changed. Flat now. The voice he used when bailiffs knocked.

I set down the letter. "Who?"

"Don't know. Message came through Mick Flynn last night."

That stopped my hands completely. Mick Flynn ran the Pewter Crown, which wasn't a pub so much as a gathering place for people who made their living in ways that required never being remembered. If someone sent word through Flynn, they were either desperate or dangerous.

"What did the message say?"

"Special client. Dawn. Come alone." Thomas touched his coat pocket, checking for the knife he always carried. "Might be nothing."

"Might be magistrates."

"Magistrates don't send warnings."

True. Magistrates kicked in doors and sorted through the debris afterward. This was something else. Something that wanted us nervous and waiting.

I looked at our shop—calling it that gave it more dignity than it deserved. One room with a bed behind a curtain, a desk where I worked, shelves holding our stock: paper of seven different weights, ink in four colors, wax in six. The tools of our trade, which was lying and getting paid for it.

Three exits. I'd counted them so many times they'd become automatic. The door Thomas stood in. The window that opened onto a two-story drop, but there was a barrel below if you got desperate. The false panel behind the shelves that led to the cellar, and from there to the tunnels that ran under half of Seven Dials like veins under skin.

"I'm staying," I said.

"Veri—"

"If they're coming for us, running won't help. And if they're really a client, we need the money."

We needed more than money. We owed sixty-two pounds across five different creditors, three of whom had threatened to break Thomas's fingers if we didn't pay by month's end. The other two had threatened worse, in ways that didn't require specifics.

Thomas rubbed his face. He looked old suddenly, though he was only fifty-three. Forgery aged you the way worry did, etching itself into the lines around your eyes until you couldn't remember what you'd looked like when you still believed in things like safety.

"I'm making tea," he said, which meant yes, you can stay, but I don't like it.

While the kettle boiled on our ancient stove, I cleaned ink from under my fingernails and tried to shake the feeling that had settled into my chest. Not quite fear. Something sharper. The sense that the world was about to tilt and I'd need to be ready.

"Buongiorno," I said to my reflection in the window glass. "Mi chiamo Contessa Oriana Castelfranco."

"Your accent's getting better." Thomas handed me tea in a chipped cup. "Why Italian?"

"Why not?" But we both knew. I'd been teaching myself for two years now, stealing grammar from books Thomas brought home, memorizing opera phrases from the singers who worked the flash houses. Learning Italian because it was beautiful and useless and everything Seven Dials wasn't.

Learning it because my mother had once said I was meant for more than this, and I'd never figured out if she meant more than poverty or more than survival or just more than her.

The knock came at exactly six o'clock.

Not loud. Not aggressive. Three taps, perfectly spaced, the kind of knock that said I know you're listening and I can afford to be patient.

Thomas set down his tea. I stood, brushing wrinkles from my dress—useless gesture; the dress was three years old and wrinkles were permanent—and tried to look like someone who had conversations with dangerous people regularly.

Thomas opened the door.

The man who entered wore money.

Not just expensive clothes, though his coat was definitely superfine and his boots had that mirror shine that required a valet. He wore money the way other people wore skin, like it was so fundamental to his existence that removing it would leave him naked.

Wrong. Everything about him wrong for Seven Dials, for dawn, for our shop. His walking stick had a silver handle that would get him murdered three streets over. His cravat was tied in some complicated arrangement I didn't have words for. He looked at our room the way a naturalist might look at an interesting species of beetle.

"Mr. Blackwood." His voice was officer-class, the kind of accent that came from good schools and better breeding. "Thank you for receiving me."

Thomas said nothing. I watched him do the calculation—turn this man away and risk his displeasure, or let him stay and risk whatever he'd brought with him.

The man's gaze found me. "And this must be your daughter."

I was still at my desk, ink on my fingers despite the washing, hair pinned with bent pins because new ones cost money. I'd been working since midnight and looked it.

His eyes were grey-green. Cold. The color of Thames water in winter.

"She helps with the business," Thomas said carefully.

"I'm aware." The man smiled, which somehow made him more dangerous. "Miss Blackwood. May I sit?"

I gestured to the chair across from my desk. He sat, placed his walking stick across his knees with the precision of someone who'd never knocked anything over accidentally in his life.

"I have a proposition," he said. "One that requires your particular talents."

I waited. Thomas had taught me that—never speak first when someone's buying lies.

"There's a house party. Kelmarsh Park in Northamptonshire. Week-long affair. The Duke of Kelmarsh is hosting twenty guests, all very exclusive, all very observed."

I knew about house parties the way I knew about peacocks—they existed, they were expensive, they had nothing to do with me.

"There will be a guest," he continued. "An Italian contessa. Contessa Oriana Castelfranco. She's traveling from Venice with letters of introduction, aristocratic connections, fluent Italian."

I understood before he finished. The way you understand when someone's about to hit you, that split-second awareness before impact.

"Unfortunately," he said, "the Contessa will not be attending."

The room was very quiet. Outside, Seven Dials was waking—costermongers calling, someone singing badly, the constant background noise of people trying to survive another day.

"You want me to impersonate her."

"Very good, Miss Blackwood." He sounded pleased, like I was a student who'd correctly identified a Latin conjugation. "The Duke has something I need. Documents in his private study. They need to be retrieved carefully, without suspicion."

"I can't—" I started.

"You can forge anything." He pulled papers from his coat, set them on my desk. "Letters of introduction. The Contessa's history. A miniature portrait for reference—you do look rather alike. And the address where Mrs. Fielding will meet you. She'll coach you in deportment, speech, all those aristocratic habits you'll need."

I looked at the papers. At the miniature portrait lying face-up. The Contessa was beautiful—dark hair like mine, similar bone structure, but her face had never counted exits or measured the weight of desperation.

"And if I'm caught?"

"You won't be."

"But if I am?"

His smile didn't change. "Then you'll hang. Along with your father. I have enough evidence to ensure it."

Thomas's hand tightened on the doorframe. White knuckles.

"You're blackmailing us," I said, because naming things was sometimes all you had.

"I'm offering employment. Fifty pounds for the week. More if you succeed."

Fifty pounds. Enough to clear most of our debts. Enough to breathe. Enough to maybe, possibly, almost be safe.

But this wasn't forgery. This was walking into a duke's house and playing aristocrat and stealing from someone who could have us both transported for the presumption.

"I need to think—"

"No." He stood. "You need to say yes. Because your father is expected at Newgate this afternoon. Unless you agree to my terms."

Everything stopped.

Thomas's face was empty. That terrible calm he got when things were very, very bad.

"You're lying," I said.

"I rarely lie, Miss Blackwood." He picked up his walking stick. "It's so much easier to arrange reality. You have twelve hours to prepare. Mrs. Fielding will begin your transformation tomorrow at noon. One week from today, you'll travel to Kelmarsh Park. You'll arrive as Contessa Oriana Castelfranco. You'll retrieve my documents. And you'll leave with your father's life intact and fifty pounds richer."

"And if I refuse?"

"You won't." He paused at the door. "Because you're curious. About that world. Those people. That life you've spent your whole life forging but never living."

The bastard was right.

He left. The door closed. The room filled with grey light and the particular silence that came after disaster.

Thomas sat down heavily. "It's a trap."

"Obviously."

"We should run."

"Where?" I laughed, but it sounded wrong. "You're fifty-three with bad knees. I'm twenty-four with ink under my fingernails. Between us we have forty pounds and enemies everywhere."

"We have each other."

"We have debts." I picked up the miniature portrait. Studied the Contessa's face—her serene expression, her casual wealth, her complete ignorance that somewhere in Seven Dials, a forger's daughter was about to steal her entire life.

"Your mother—" Thomas started.

"Don't." But I was thinking about her anyway. Catherine Blackwood, dead twelve years, who'd taught me to read with stolen books and desperate hope. Who'd said I was meant for more and then died before explaining what more looked like.

I touched the papers on my desk. Letters written in Italian. The address for Mrs. Fielding. An entire life laid out like a forgery waiting to be completed.

"I'll do it," I said.

"Veri—"

"Not for the money. Not even for you." I looked at him. "I'll do it because I want to know if I can. If all these years of forging other people's lives means I can forge my own."

"That's not forging. That's lying."

"What's the difference?"

He didn't answer. Maybe there wasn't one. Maybe the whole of Seven Dials was built on that question—survival or sin, necessity or choice, who you had to be versus who you chose to become.

Thomas stood. Crossed to me. Put his forger's hands on my shoulders. "She would be proud."

"She would be horrified."

"Both can be true."

He kissed my forehead and left me alone with a dead woman's life in my hands and my future balanced on the edge of a lie.

I looked in the window glass. Tried to see myself in silk instead of threadbare cotton. In jewels instead of bent pins. In that world of house parties and dukes where fifty pounds was pocket money.

"Mi chiamo Contessa Oriana Castelfranco," I whispered. The accent was still terrible. The words felt like forgery.

Like truth.

Like the best lie I'd ever told.

Outside, Seven Dials woke fully. The pickpockets and costermongers, the girls who sold what they had to because selling anything else required capital. The world I'd known all my life. The world I was about to leave—temporarily, dangerously, possibly fatally.

I touched the portrait one last time. The Contessa stared back, serene and distant and completely unaware that in seven days, I would become her.

Or die trying.

The letter had been sitting on the kitchen table since half past five that morning, and I had not looked at it again because I did not need to. I knew the shape of the thing. Stainforth's hand was the sort of hand that signed leases and pew rents and letters to the parish, slanted just enough to suggest education, never enough to suggest haste. He wished to discuss the terms. He would call upon me this afternoon, if convenient. Everything about the letter was civil, and every civil word in it was a boot set down an inch closer to my door. Six weeks to the quarter. Forty-two days. I had done that arithmetic on the back of a bill of lading the night before, and the numbers had not changed overnight, because numbers did not. I had seven pounds nine shillings in the cash box and another eleven owed me by the Highflyer and the Regulator between them, neither of which paid before the month's end. The rent, when it came due, would be forty. I knew what I could squeeze from the taproom in the next forty-two days, and I knew what I could not. What I could not was forty pounds. My hands were on the table. The right one still had the split on the index finger from yesterday's cask, a clean cut that had scabbed overnight and would open again the first time I lifted anything heavier than a tray. I put the kettle on. The ledger was where I had left it. I did not open it. There was nothing in the ledger that would not be in the ledger this afternoon, and whatever Stainforth wanted to see, he would not see it on my face first. I did the morning faster than the morning needed. I swept the taproom and wiped the bar and took the cellar stairs two at a time to check the tap on the October ale, which did not need checking. I wiped the brass on the coaching clock. I put fresh sand in the spittoons. None of it was for him. All of it was for him. Nan came down at seven and took one look at me and said nothing, which was one of the kindnesses I had come to rely on in her. She tied her apron and went to the kitchen. I heard her begin the bread. His carriage was on the road before ten. I heard it at the bend by the elms, the particular check of a coachman who knew a yard and was slowing for it, and I was at the door before the wheels stopped. Stainforth climbed down without hurry. His coat was the colour of wet slate and cut by a man who had been paid not to cut it like anyone else's, and the wool of it, even from the step, carried the smell of good wool, which is to say of money kept dry. "Miss Dale." "Mr Stainforth." "How good of you to receive me. I trust Mrs Dale is well? Your aunt, I should say. Forgive me." "She is well. Thank you." "And the road? I found it better than last month. They have mended the stretch by the mill." "So I am told." He drew off his gloves, finger by finger, and folded them against his palm, and held them in one hand for the rest of the conversation, as if to have put them down would have conceded something. I stood in my own doorway and did not invite him past it, and he did not ask. He stood on the step and looked past me into the taproom with an expression of mild, pleasant interest, which is the expression a man wears when he is counting. "A raw morning." "Yes." "I so rarely have the chance to see the place properly, Miss Dale. Would you indulge me?" It was not a request. It was the shape of a request laid over something else, and we both knew which shape was underneath. I stepped back from the door. "As you like." He walked the taproom floor first. He did not stride; he moved at the pace of a man who had the afternoon. He paused at the bar rail and set his hand flat on it, as though testing the grain, and then he ran his thumb along the inside edge where the wood had worn silky from twenty years of elbows. He looked up at the beams. He looked at the hearth. He looked at the window latch and tried it with two fingers, and it held, because I had oiled the latches myself in September, and then he tried it again, because a latch that held once might not hold twice. "Well kept." "Thank you." "Your mother kept it so." "She did." "And you after her. Remarkable." I did not answer that. My mother used to say that a woman who argued with a compliment was a woman who did not yet know what it had cost her, and I had learned that lesson at thirteen across a counter full of mutton, watching her tell a butcher his price was fair and then pay him exactly what she had meant to pay. Give them the number, she had said later, not the explanation. The explanation is what they are fishing for. He moved toward the cellar door. "May I?" "The step's worn. Mind your foot." He opened the door and stood a moment at the head of the stair, and then he bent and ran his thumb along the hinge. Not the handle. The hinge. He tested the pin with his nail. He did this as naturally as a man checks his own watch. "Drainage?" "To the soakaway at the back. It was cut in '03. It takes what we put in it." "In wet years?" "In wet years also." "And your supplier for the October is still Howland's?" "It is." "A good house." "Yes." "You take, what, two firkins a week through this season?" "Three, when the London mails are running. Two otherwise." "And the rooms? You let, on average?" "Twelve a night through autumn. Fourteen on a Saturday." He nodded at each answer, small and courteous, the way a clerk nods at figures he is not writing down because he will remember them without. He did not ask me anything he did not already half know. He was not learning the inn. He was confirming it. I gave him the numbers and not the explanation, and when he paused as though to leave room for the explanation, I let the room stay empty. We went out into the yard. The yard was the yard. The muck had frozen in ridges overnight where Jem had not yet raked it, and the light was thin and grey and did not flatter anything. Stainforth walked the length of the stabling without speaking, which was its own kind of speech. At the fence where the paddock met the road he stopped. The post at the corner had shifted in last winter's frost and had been leaning since, and the top rail had worked its peg loose in August, and I had meant, and meant, and meant. He put his hand on the post. He did not push it. He only rested his hand against it, as a man rests his hand against a piece of furniture in a house he is considering taking. "An inn of this size," he said, and left the sentence there, in the cold, for me to pick up. I did not pick it up. "You were saying, Mr Stainforth." "Only that an inn of this size might benefit from a steadier hand on certain matters. The outbuildings. The yard. Small things. A woman alone cannot be expected to mind every post and rail." "The post will be set by Sunday." "I do not doubt it." "Then we are agreed." He smiled, briefly, without warmth and without coldness. It was the smile of a man who had been smiled at a great deal in his life and had learned the use of it. "Miss Dale. I think very well of you. I hope you know that. I think very well of what your mother built here, and of what you have kept. I came today only to speak of the lease, and of what might be best for the parish, which is a thing I must consider whether I wish to or not." "The lease is in good standing." "It is." "The rent will be paid at quarter day." "I have every confidence." He said this and he meant it, and that was the worst of it. He did have every confidence. He had every confidence because he had already worked out what forty pounds did to seven pounds nine, and he had the patience of a man who did not need to win the conversation today. He only needed to have had it. I heard myself say, "You will have your rent, sir. And the post will be straight. Was there anything else?" It came out sharper than I had meant. A quarter of an inch sharper. I watched him absorb it without a flinch, the way a good wall absorbs a knock, and I knew at once that I had given him more than he had given me. "Nothing else. Thank you for your time. The inn is very well kept, Miss Dale. Truly." He drew his gloves back on, finger by finger, and climbed into his carriage. I stood in the yard until the wheels turned at the elms, and then I stood a moment longer, because I did not trust my hands yet to do anything small. My jaw ached. I had been holding it. I unclenched it, and the back of my neck went hot, and I walked to the pump and put my wrist under the water until the cut on my finger stung and the stinging gave me something to think about that was not him. Then I went upstairs. Bridget's door was open the width of a hand, which was how she left it when she had seen something from her window and wished to be found. The good china was out. It was always out now, and had been since the day she decided, somewhere in her seventieth year, that she was finished saving crockery for a funeral she had already attended in her head. The room smelled of lavender and of tea and of the particular mustiness of wool blankets that have been folded and unfolded for fifty years, and I sat down in the chair across from her, and my shoulders dropped, and I did not know I had done it until she poured. "Drink it while it's hot, child." "Yes." She did not ask. She had seen the carriage. She had seen me in the yard. She had seen Stainforth put his hand on the fence post, which I had not known she could see from her window, and which she had seen anyway, because Bridget saw what she meant to see. She drank. She set her cup down with both hands, because her hands were not what they had been. "Greville," she said. "What about him?" "Before Stainforth. Before you were born, nearly. The last landlord but one. He wanted your mother to double the stabling. Said the coaching trade would only grow, said she was throwing money away by not building. He came with plans. He had a man of his own who would do it for a price he called fair." "And she refused." "She refused. The yard wouldn't take it, she said. The drainage would foul by Michaelmas and the horses would founder by Christmas, and then where would he be with his doubled stabling. She was right. She was almost always right about the ground under her feet." "Well, then." "Well, then. Greville went away and he came back. He came back twice a year for four years, and each time she gave him the same answer in the same words, and each time he went away thinking he had nearly had her." "And?" "And one day he stopped coming, because he had died, and his son had no taste for it, and that was that. Your mother buried three landlords, child. She held on to this place through every one of them." "Then I will do the same." Bridget looked at me. She had a way of looking that was not sharp, exactly, but that arrived at you from further off than you had expected, as though she had been considering you from the other side of her life. "Holding on and holding back are not the same thing, Dinah." I reached for the teapot. "Shall I warm yours?" "You shall not. I have not finished it." "Then I shall warm mine." I poured. My hand was steady, which was a small grace I granted myself. The tea went into the cup and the steam went up, and Bridget watched me do it, and said nothing further, because she had said what she had come upstairs to say, and whether I took it in was not her afternoon's business. "The post wants setting before Sunday," I said. "I told him Sunday." "Then I expect you will set it." "I will." I drank the tea. I kissed her on the top of her head, where the hair was very fine and very white, and I went down the stairs and I did not think about what she had said, because there was no room for it in the afternoon, and the afternoon was what I had. By nine the taproom had emptied. By ten the fire was banked and the bolt was in my hand at the front door, and my feet hurt in the particular way that meant I had been on them since before light, and I was counting stairs to my bed when the knock came. Two knocks. Measured. Not loud. I drew the bolt and opened the door the breadth of my shoulder. A man. Tall.
