His hat was in his hand, which was the first thing I noticed, because the men who did not take off their hats were the men I did not let past the threshold. A dog sat at his heel, long-legged and grey-brindled, with a face like a creature that had been given a job and was not sure of its hours. His boots were good leather worn past good, the kind of boots that had been bought in a shop once and had walked out of their price. "Ma'am. I'm looking for work, or a bed, or both, if you can spare either." His voice was low and careful, pitched to a tired woman at a late door. "The inn is shut." "Yes, ma'am." "The stable is dry. There's clean straw in the end stall, and water at the pump. I'll not take a penny from you and I'll not give you a penny, and we'll talk in the morning about the rest." "Thank you." "The dog stays with you." "He would anyway." I looked at him for another half a breath. He was not drunk. He was not dangerous in any way I could read at a door at ten o'clock at night, and I had learned to read a door at ten o'clock at night by the time I was fifteen. He was tired to the bone. So was I. It could wait. "Round the side. Jem will be in the loft. Don't wake him." "No, ma'am." He put his hat back on and stepped off the flag, and the dog rose with him and went. I shot the bolt. I went through to the kitchen and damped the range for the night and, because I did every night, looked out through the window at the yard to make sure the yard was as I had left it. It was not. He was crouched at the fence. The crooked post. He had the dog sitting three paces off, patient as a churchwarden, and he was working the base of the post with his hands, clearing the frozen muck from round it with the side of his boot, and then he lifted. He lifted the post clean out of the hole, set it aside, and knelt, and felt the hole with his fingers for what was in it, and took a stone from the pile by the trough and bedded it under where the post would go. He set the post back. He pushed it true with his shoulder, and held it, and kicked earth round its foot, and tamped the earth with his heel, and then with his hand on the top of the post he rocked it to see if it would rock, and it did not. I watched his hands. Not his face. Not his shoulders. His hands, bare in the cold, doing the thing I had told Stainforth would be done by Sunday, on a Tuesday night, by a stranger who had not been asked. He moved to the top rail next, and found the loose peg, and worked it in with the heel of his hand. I turned from the window. I went up the stairs to my bed.

The letter sat on the kitchen table where I had left it last night, unopened, beside the salt. I had promised myself I would not let it keep me from sleeping, and I had kept that promise by not reading it. Which was, I understood now, its own kind of sleeplessness. I cut the seal with the edge of my thumbnail. Stainforth's hand was what it always was: small, even, entirely without flourish. A gentleman who does not waste ink on a leaseholder. He would call today, between the hours of ten and noon, upon a matter concerning the renewal. He remained my obedient servant. I read it twice. Then I put it down next to the ledger, smoothed it flat under my palm, and went to find my apron. "Is that the letter?" Nan said from the doorway. "It is." "And?" "He'll be here before noon." I tied the apron strings twice at the back, the way my grandmother had done, because a single knot worked itself loose by dinner. "Sweep the taproom proper. Not the quick sweep. The proper one." "Yes, ma'am." "And leave the ledger where it is." Nan looked at the ledger on the table and then at me, and then she went to fetch the broom without saying anything, which was one of the many reasons I had hired her. I checked the fire. I checked the bread. I put the kettle on because the kettle being on was what a working kitchen looked like, and I wanted him to walk into a working kitchen. I did not change my dress. Changing my dress for Phineas Stainforth would have been a concession, and I did not mean to concede anything before he had even climbed out of his gig. --- He arrived at a quarter past ten. The gig was dark green, very clean, and drawn by a bay that had been brushed that morning. A single groom held the reins, a young man I did not know, who looked at the front of the inn with the mild contempt of a servant borrowed for the day. Stainforth's boots struck the flagstones of the yard in that measured way I remembered from the two previous occasions he had called. My grandmother used to say you could tell a landlord by how he entered a yard, and that the worst ones entered as though they already owned the flagstones. My grandmother had received his father, once, standing in this same doorway with her hands on her apron, and had afterwards described him, in the kitchen, as a man who counted his own buttons. I had carried this forward as a practical instruction: these are men who count. Be ready to be counted. "Miss Dale." "Mr Stainforth. You had good weather for the drive." "I did. A clear morning is a pleasant thing at this season." He did not take off his hat until he was inside the hall, and then he set it on the side table with the brim turned up, the way a man does who expects to pick it up again quite soon. The coat was good wool, very dark, cut two seasons ago but cut well. "I hope I have not called at an inconvenient hour." "You named the hour, sir." "So I did." The faintest movement at the side of his mouth. "Forgive me. One falls into civilities." "Will you take tea?" "Thank you, no. I mean to be brief." He had not come to be brief. Men who mean to be brief do not arrive with a letter the night before. "Then perhaps you will tell me how I may be of service." "I had hoped, if the morning allows it, to walk through the premises. It has been some time since I saw the inn properly, and a landlord who does not see his property is an inattentive landlord." He said this with the air of a man apologising for his diligence. "A quarter of an hour, no more." "Of course." I had known he would ask. I had known, and still the word *premises* hit me somewhere under the ribs. I put my hands together at my waist so that he could not see me close them. "Shall we begin with the taproom?" --- He walked through my inn as though he were reading it. The taproom first, where he stopped at the near end and looked at the beams, and then at the hearth, and then at the long bench under the window, and did not say anything for long enough that I understood he wished me to hear him not saying anything. He laid two fingers briefly on the edge of a table. He moved on. "The cellar stair is through here, I believe." "Yes." He went down the cellar stair ahead of me, which no guest had ever done, and which I permitted because the alternative was to stand alone at the top of the stair while he went down. He paused at the foot. He put his hand on the cellar door's hinge, the lower one, the one that sticks in damp weather and that Jem has been meaning to see to since Michaelmas. He moved the door a quarter inch on the hinge. He let it go. "A little stiff." "It wants oil. Jem will see to it this week." "Ah. Your ostler." He considered the door a moment longer, as though the door might have more to tell him. "He is a capable man, I understand." "He is." "Missing an arm." "The lower part of one." "Remarkable, what a determined fellow can do." "Yes." He went up the stair before me. I had never known a man not to stand back at a stair and let a woman pass; it was the sort of thing Stainforth would never fail at on a morning call, and the sort of thing he would permit himself to forget inside a property he considered, in the abstract, already his. At the first-floor landing he spoke without turning round. "Twenty rooms, I think." "Twenty, yes." "And your occupancy this past quarter?" "Sixteen on average. Eighteen through the autumn fair. Twelve in the week after Christmas." "You have the figures to hand." "I have them in the ledger on the kitchen table. You are welcome to see them." "I do not doubt you, Miss Dale." He smiled without moving the rest of his face. "I merely remark upon your command of them." We did not go into any of the bedchambers. He glanced along the passage, noted the door at the end, and turned back toward the stair. A man who already knows what is inside a room does not need to open it; a man who wishes the woman beside him to feel that her rooms are his to open on another occasion does not need to open it either. I could not tell which man he was being. It did not, in the event, matter. The passage to the stable yard was the last of it. He paused at the door into the yard, hand on the frame, and looked out at the cobbles and the stalls and the fence along the kitchen-garden wall. Jem was at the far end with a horse; he did not look up. A good ostler knows when not to look up. "The post is leaning," Stainforth said. "Yes." He stepped out into the yard, crossed to the crooked post, and set two fingers against the top of it. The post moved a quarter inch toward him. He let it go. "A small matter." "Jem means to reset it this week." "Jem means to do a great deal, it seems." *And a man in a very good coat means to count my hinges before lunch,* I thought, and I did not say it. I thought also that my hands were cold, which is the body's way of reminding a woman that she is working harder than she looks. He turned from the post and faced me across two yards of cobble. "Miss Dale, I shall be plain." "Please." "The lease comes up at the Lady Day quarter. I have been, as you know, content to leave matters as they stand for some years, out of a proper respect for your late mother. But I am a trustee as well as a landlord, and the parish takes an interest in a house of this size upon the London road." "The parish has made no complaint." "No. Not yet." He did not raise his voice. He had no reason to. "There is, however, a question of succession. A house at this crossroads will outlive any one tenant. It is reasonable for the parish to ask where it will stand in ten years, in twenty. A lease held in a single pair of hands, without settlement or successor named, is a matter the vestry will wish to consider." "The lease is in my name, Mr Stainforth. It was in my mother's before me." "Indeed. And at renewal the terms may be reviewed. I have a nephew, a steady young man of good habits, who has expressed an interest in a house of business such as this. I mention it to you in confidence, as the matter will want time." "How much time." "The quarter. Perhaps a little beyond. I should not wish you to be hurried." "I am obliged for the consideration." "You understand, I trust, that I raise this as a kindness. A woman in your position may find a suitable arrangement in the meanwhile. Many do." "Many do." He inclined his head half an inch. He had what he came for, which was the knowledge that I now knew what he had. He had lost nothing. I had held every inch of ground I stood on, which was not the same as gaining any. "I shall write again before the quarter." "I shall look for the letter." He crossed back to the passage, through the hall, to the front step. His hat went on in the doorway. The groom brought the gig forward without being called; good servants have the instincts of well-trained dogs. "Good morning, Miss Dale." "Good morning, Mr Stainforth." The gig turned in the yard and went out under the arch, and the sound of the wheels on the road carried clearly for longer than it ought to have done. I stood on the flagstones a moment. Then I went inside, because standing on the flagstones was what he would have wanted me to do, and I would not give him that either. --- The stair up to Bridget's rooms has eleven treads, and the third from the top is warped, and I have been climbing it long enough that my foot knows to lift a half-inch higher on that step without my telling it to. She did not ask me what had happened. She was sitting where she sat every morning, in the chair by the window that looked down into the yard, and the tray was already set. "Sit." "Aunt." "Sit down, child. The tea is poured." The good china. Always the good china, because Bridget had decided in 1802 that she would not die with unused china in the cupboard, and she had not yet died, and the china was by now a little chipped at the rim, because use wears things. She handed me the cup with hands that were knotted at every joint and steady on the handle. "Green gig," she said. "Yes." "Same groom as last time?" "A new one." "Hm." She sipped. "Well. He's bought a second servant since we saw him last. Good. It means he's borrowing." "Or that the first one left him." "Or that." She looked at me over the rim of her cup. Her accent was gentler than it had been when I was small, but it rose at the edges when she was amused. "Did he touch the hinge?" "He did." "And the post?" "And the post." "Of course he did." She set her cup down. "His father used to run a finger along the top of the mantelpiece. For the dust, do you see. I watched him do it once and I thought, well, here is a man who has decided to be disappointed before he has sat down. This one is the same man with better manners." "He was civil." "They are always civil. Civility is how they do it." She reached and took my hand, briefly, and let it go. "Drink your tea, Dinah. You look as though you haven't sat down since Tuesday." "I haven't." "Well, sit now." I drank. The tea was very hot, and I had not realised until I tasted it how badly I had wanted something hot. Bridget watched the yard through the window, because that was what she did, and did not watch me, because that was also what she did. "He named a successor," I said. "Did he?" "A nephew." "Of course a nephew." "He gave me the quarter." "That is generous of him, in his own accounting." She did not look away from the window. "And what will you do?" "I will keep the books clean and the rooms full and give him no ground he can stand on at the vestry." "Yes. That is what you will do during the day." I set the cup down. "Don't, Aunt." "Dinah." "Please." She turned her head then, and she looked at me with the particular attention of a woman who has earned, at seventy-two, the right not to apologise for what she is about to say. "Your mother held this inn with both hands for fourteen years, and I loved her, and I watched her. And I will tell you what I have been waiting these two years to tell you, because you are old enough now to hear it and because that man has just driven out of the yard. There is a difference between holding on and holding back, child. Your mother did not know it. I would like you to know it before it costs you what it cost her." The kitchen clock below us struck the half. I heard it through the floor. "I am holding on to the inn," I said. "I know you are." "That is not the same." "I know it isn't." She did not press. She never pressed. She had said the thing, and she would let the thing sit where it was. "Finish your tea." I finished my tea. I set the cup on the tray, and I stood, and I smoothed my apron over my skirt because my hands wanted something to do. "I have the accounts to go over before supper." "Then go and do them." "Aunt." "Go, Dinah. I have sewing." She did not have sewing. She had the window and the yard and whatever she had been thinking before I came up. I kissed the top of her head, which smelled of the lavender water she had used since I was a child, and I went down the stair, and on the third step from the top my foot lifted, and I felt the line she had said move inside me like a coin dropped into a pocket I had meant to keep empty. I did not take it out to look at. I left it where it had fallen, and I went back down to the work. --- The afternoon went. Nan tended the bar. Jem was in the yard with a pair of post-horses from a gentleman bound for Baldock. The weather turned early; a thin cold rain came on by four, and I had the lamps lit before five because the kitchen windows had gone slate-coloured and there was no use pretending otherwise. I did the accounts. I settled the butcher. I ate standing up. --- The knock came at the kitchen door, not at the front. It was past eight. I had the lantern in my hand because I had been on my way to the pantry for onions, and when I opened the door the lantern lit him before anything else did: a tall man on the step, a long dark coat that had seen rain before this rain, boots that had been good once and were nearly through at the toe, and at his heel a lean grey dog that kept its head low and its eyes on me without moving any other part of itself. "Ma'am." "Yes." "I'm looking for work, if there's any, and a place out of the rain for the night. I can pay for the night if I cannot work for it." His voice was quiet and not at all servile. He did not step closer. He did not pull off his hat until he had finished the sentence, which was a small thing, and I noticed it the way I notice small things. I was very tired. There had been a man in a good coat in my yard this morning, and a line in Bridget's parlour that I had not yet taken out of my pocket, and I did not want, tonight, to sit down with another question. "The stable's warm," I said. "There's clean straw at the back, past the bay mare. You can have it until morning. I'll not promise work until I've seen you in daylight." "That's fair." "There's bread on the sill of the kitchen window in half an hour. Take it or don't." "Thank you, ma'am." He said a word then that might have been his name, offered flat and short the way a man offers a coin he is not sure you will accept, and I did not repeat it, and I did not ask him to. The dog shifted its weight against his leg. The rain ran off the brim of his hat onto the step. Vagrants in this weather were as regular as the almanac; my mother used to say you could set your clock by the turn of the year and the knock at the door. "The stable's through the passage. The second door on the left opens into the yard." "I'll find it." He touched the brim of his hat, and he turned, and the dog turned with him as though they had agreed on it long before either of them reached the step. I shut the door. I leaned my forehead a moment against the wood, because there was no one in the kitchen to see me do it, and then I went and got my onions. --- I was paring the onions at the kitchen window when I saw him again. The window looked down the length of the yard toward the stable. The rain had thinned to a mist. He had set the dog in the doorway of the stable with a folded coat under it, and he had come back out into the yard because something had caught his eye on the way in. He was at the cellar door, which stood in the lee of the kitchen wall. He had taken the hinge between his fingers, the lower one, the one Stainforth had moved that morning, and he was working it up and down with a patience that had nothing to do with showing anyone anything. After a minute he stepped back, went into the stable, came out with a small tin, and oiled the hinge, and worked it again, and shut the door, and opened it, and shut it. It sat true. Then he crossed to the crooked post at the corner of the kitchen-garden wall. He stood and looked at it a moment. He set his shoulder against it, and he shifted his weight, and he moved the post upright in its footing and packed the stones in around the base with his boot. He did not make a performance of it. He did not look toward the house. I stood at the window with the knife in my hand and the onion half pared on the board, and I looked at his hands. They were working hands, dark with rain and whatever the hinge had left on them, steady at the post, steady at the stones. He set the last stone with his palm. I turned back to the onion, and I cut it, and I set the pieces in the bowl.

The letter was on the kitchen table where I had left it last night, and the seal was still unbroken, and the cup of tea I had set beside it to give myself courage had gone cold and scummed over, which served me right. I broke the seal. Stainforth wrote as he spoke. Three sentences, the middle one doing the work. He would call at the White Hound today, between the hours of eleven and one, to discuss matters pertaining to the lease and to the inn's continued accommodation of irregular persons. He hoped I would make myself available. He signed himself as always. I read it twice. The second time was to see whether I had invented the word *irregular*, because it was the sort of word I would have invented for him, and he had saved me the trouble. "Is that him, then?" Nan said, coming through with the ashes in a pan. "That's him." "What time?" "Any time that suits him." "Of course." She tipped the ashes out. "I'll do the taproom first. You'll want it swept." "I'll want the ledger on the table," I said. "Open to the quarter. He can look at it or not, as he chooses." Nan nodded once and went back out. She had been at the inn seven years and did not waste words when she understood a situation, which was nearly always. I set the letter face down beside the ledger, put the kettle back on, and went to find a clean apron because the one I had on had a smear of soot at the hip from the range, and I would not receive Stainforth with a smear at the hip. I did not examine the business of the apron. I tied it and went to look at the taproom. The taproom was already in order. Nan had been at it before breakfast. I straightened a settle that did not need straightening and checked the state of the window-glass, and looked at the room as Stainforth would look at it — not as a traveller, but as a man measuring rent. The light was good. The boards were clean. The fire was laid but not lit, because it was not cold enough yet to justify the wood. I laid it anyway. It made the room look occupied. Back in the kitchen I opened the ledger to Michaelmas and set it square on the table. I pushed the letter to one side and set the inkwell at the top of the page. If he wanted to see my numbers, he would see them. If he wanted to comment on them, I would let him, and I would not explain. The gig came through the yard gate a little before noon. One horse, a neat bay. A single groom, who did not dismount. Stainforth handed down his gloves to the groom and stepped across the flagstones to the main door, and the first crunch of his boots carried through the open kitchen window. I went to receive him at the door. "Miss Dale." "Mr Stainforth. Good day to you." "Good day. A fine dry one, for the season." "It is. Will you come through? The taproom is warmer than the hall." "Thank you." His coat was a very good wool, close-fitted, the cuffs showing no wear. The hat he handed me was new within the year. I took it and set it on the hook by the door where my grandmother had set the hats of his father, when his father had called, which his father had done twice a year for the last ten years of his life. "I trust your aunt is well," Stainforth said. "Well enough. Thank you." "And your people." "All in good health." "Excellent." Two exchanges and we had arrived at the taproom. He looked at the laid fire and did not remark on it. He set his stick against the settle. "I shan't detain you long, Miss Dale. I should like to walk through the premises, if I may. A matter of the lease renewal, which, as you are aware, falls at Lady Day." "Of course." "Shall we begin here, then." He began, though the *shall we* was the shape of the thing and not the substance of it. He moved along the wall to the fireplace and set his hand on the lintel beam. He did not press. He set the hand there, took it away, looked at his fingers as though for dust, and found none. "A good old beam." "It is." "Oak, I should think." "Oak. My grandmother's time." "Quite." He went next to the cellar door. I went with him because leaving him to the cellar alone was not to be thought of. He bent at the latch and set a finger against the lower hinge. "This sticks in damp weather, I imagine." "In wet weather. Jem sees to it." "Jem is your ostler." "He is." "A useful man, I've no doubt." "He is." He straightened. He did not open the cellar door. It was not the cellar he had come to see; it was the hinge, which he had now registered, and he was satisfied. We went up the stair to the first-floor landing. He touched the newel post at the turn. He did not touch the rail. "How many rooms, Miss Dale." "Twenty. Sixteen chambered at present, the four small ones at the back shut up since August." "Shut up for what reason." "I hadn't call to open them. Trade has been steady but not full." "Steady but not full." He repeated the phrase as though it might be a fact or might be an opinion, and he had not yet decided. "And the takings for the quarter?" "On the table in the kitchen, open to the page, if you wish to see them." "I shall, presently. And your custom — coaches as before?" "The Edinburgh mail, the Baldock, the York through-coach three days the week. Private traffic as it comes." "And the parish — no complaints that have come to you." "None that have come to me." "I ask," he said, and here was the register, the civil measured one I had been waiting for, "because there has been some remark in vestry about the number of discharged men who have taken shelter here in recent months. I do not raise it as an accusation. Merely as a concern for the parish, which you will understand I am obliged to register." "They take bread in the kitchen and move on, Mr Stainforth. They sleep in the stable when the stable is empty of horses and not when it isn't. I have not had a theft or a disturbance in nine months." "I am gratified to hear it." "I should hope so." He looked at me, briefly, and then he went on along the landing, and I went with him. It occurred to me, with the dry clarity that comes when a person has not quite enough sleep and far too much to lose, that I was walking through my own house behind a man in a very good coat who was counting my hinges. The absurdity was exact. It did not make him less dangerous. It made him more tiresome. We went out by the back passage to the stable yard. Jem was not in the yard. He was in the far stall with the roan mare and had the sense not to come out. Stainforth stopped at the fence post by the water trough. The one that had come loose in August, that Jem had been meaning to reset before the frost, and which I had been meaning to remind him about and had not. He did not touch it with his hand. He set the end of his stick against it and tested the give. "This wants reseating." "It does." "Before winter, I should think." "Before winter, yes." "Miss Dale." He turned to me with the stick still resting against the post. "I shall be plain with you, because I believe plainness is a kindness. The lease falls at Lady Day. I have, as you know, discretion at renewal. I have held that discretion in your favour for four years, on the strength of your mother's tenancy and in regard for the difficulty of your position. I do not propose to continue to hold it indefinitely." "No." "There is a view within my family that the lease on a coaching inn of this standing is better held by a man. I do not offer this view as my own preference. I offer it as a fact of the circumstances in which we find ourselves. A successor of my choosing — a younger man, a man of the family — would take the lease on terms favourable to the parish and to the establishment, and would look to make certain improvements which the present tenancy has not found it convenient to undertake." "I see." "I say this now so that you have the quarter before you to consider your position. I do not require an answer today." "I shouldn't have given you one today." "No. I did not suppose you would." He took the stick away from the post. The post did not move; the stick did. "I shall look at the ledger, if I may, before I go." "Of course." He looked at the ledger for eleven minutes. I counted the minutes on the kitchen clock because I had nothing else to do with them, and because counting was the thing my mother had taught me to do when I wanted to throw something. He turned three pages. He did not ask a single question. He closed the ledger and rested his hand on the cover as though he had read a letter from a friend. "Your accounts are admirably kept, Miss Dale." "Thank you." "I shall write again before Lady Day." "I shall expect it." He took his hat from the hook. He set it, adjusted the brim, and inclined his head. "Good day to you." "Good day, Mr Stainforth." The gig went out of the yard the way it had come in. I stood in the kitchen doorway until the crunch of the wheels had gone off down the lane, and then I went back inside and picked up the ledger, and set it on the shelf above the range where it lived when it was not in use, and then I picked it up again and set it back on the table, open to the page, because I did not want it out of sight. Then I went up the stairs to Bridget. The third stair from the top warped in November and creaked when you trod it square. I trod it square. I did not want to go up softly. I wanted her to hear me coming. "Come in, child," she said, before I had knocked. Her window gave on to the yard. She had been at it since breakfast; she had seen the gig come and the gig go, and she did not need to be told what had passed between. The teapot was already on the tray — the good china, the blue-and-white with the chipped saucer she would not throw out — and she was pouring. "Sit yourself down." "Bridget." "Sit." I sat. She handed me the cup. Her hands were bad with the damp; the pour was careful and slow, and she did not apologise for it and I did not offer to take the pot. "He stayed the hour, then." "A little less." "Did he touch the cellar hinge." "He touched the cellar hinge." "He touched it for his father, too." She sat back in her chair, the one by the window with the worn patch on the arm where her right hand lived. "His father was a worse man and a better-mannered one. Drink your tea." I drank. "Lady Day?" "Lady Day." "And a man of the family waiting in the wings." "So he tells me." "He would tell you. He likes a timetable. Gives him the pleasure of watching the clock." "Bridget." "I'm not making a speech, girl. Drink your tea." I drank my tea. The cup was hot through the china and I held it with both hands, and for a moment I was not the woman who had walked through her own inn behind Phineas Stainforth; I was a girl of eleven at this same table, and Bridget had poured then, too, and had not made a speech then either. "Your mother," she said. "Yes." "Your mother held on to this place through worse than this man and his nephew. She held on with both hands and both feet and her teeth for good measure." She took a sip. "And there is a thing I should have told her forty years ago and never did, and I will tell you now because you are sitting in front of me and she is not." "Bridget." "Holding on and holding back are not the same thing, Dinah. Your mother never learned the difference. It is not a thing you inherit. It is a thing you choose. And you have been choosing the one when you thought you were choosing the other for rather a long while now." I set the cup in the saucer. The chipped one. The chip was at the four-o'clock position and I had known it since I was seven. "I'll think on it," I said. "You will not," she said, not unkindly. "You'll set it aside and do the evening service. But the thought will sit where I've put it, and that's enough for one afternoon." "I've a great deal to do." "I know you have." I stood. I took the cup with me to the tray and set it down, and I took her empty one and set it beside mine, and I did not kiss her forehead because we did not do that, and she did not ask me to. "Send Nan up later," she said. "I'll want a bit of supper." "I'll send her." The third stair creaked going down as it had going up. I had thought about asking Jem to plane it, once, and had not, because the creak was useful; it told me when a guest was coming down at an hour they had no business on the stairs. In the kitchen I put the kettle on again and did the next thing. I wrote out the orders for the brewer. I spoke to Nan about the two chambermaids and about the linen that wanted turning. I did not set the ledger back on the shelf. I left it on the table, open, because the table was where it lived now, and I was not going to shift it to suit a man who had gone. The afternoon went the way afternoons go. The weather thinned. By four the light had begun to go blue at the edges, and by five Nan had lit the lamps at the taproom end, and a thin rain came on with a colder wind behind it and Jem was in and out of the yard with the horses for the Baldock coach, which was late as the Baldock coach usually was. I ate standing at the range and did not remember, afterward, what I had eaten. The knock came at the kitchen door a little after eight. Not the main door. Not the taproom. The kitchen door, which opens on the yard, and which is the door a man uses when he has worked out that the main door will turn him away. I took the lantern from its hook and opened the door on the chain, and then, because of what I saw, I took the chain off and opened it the rest. A man stood in the rain. Tall. A long dark coat, soaked at the shoulders, and boots that had been good boots and were now walked past pity. A dog at his heel, long-bodied, quiet, keeping close to the man's left leg. I could not see the man's face under the hat-brim. I did not try. "Good evening," he said. "Good evening." "I was told at the green that you sometimes had work for a man passing through. I'd take a night's shelter for it if you had the work. The stable would do." His voice was tired and steady and not servile. The dog sat down on the flagstone and looked up at me and did not beg. "I haven't work tonight," I said. "I've a stable with dry straw in the end stall and a pump in the yard. You can have that till morning. I'll not decide past that tonight." "That will do. Thank you." "There's bread on the scrubbed table inside the tack room. Take what you want. Don't let the dog in the kitchen." "He won't come in where he's not asked." "Good." He touched the brim of his hat. He did not offer a name. He turned and went across the yard toward the stable with the dog following at his heel, and I shut the door and put the chain back on and hung the lantern up. I was tired. I had been tired at breakfast; I was tireder now. I did not want another thing to decide tonight. I was, as my mother used to say, at the end of my useful hours, and past the end was where mistakes got made. The stranger could sleep on straw until daylight and I could sort him out then with a morning's wits about me, and if that was soft I would live with being soft for one evening in a year that had not asked me to be. I went back to the range and finished scouring the pan I had left. The kitchen window gave on the yard, and the lantern the stranger carried moved across the flagstones in a slow path toward the stable door, and I watched it without meaning to. He set the lantern down inside the stable. I could see its light in the door-gap. He came back out. He went to the stable door and lifted it, the way a man lifts a door that has dropped on its hinge, and he set his shoulder to it and eased the pin back in place — the hinge that stuck in wet weather, the hinge Jem had not got to. Then he went across to the fence post by the trough. He did not look at it long. He took his weight against it, rocked it free of the wet earth, and reseated it square. He tamped the ground at the foot of it with the heel of his boot. He did not look up at the kitchen window. He had not seen me. He went back to the stable and the dog went with him and the door shut behind them both. I finished the pan. I set it on the rack. I stood at the window with the cloth still in my hand and I watched the stable door a moment longer, and what I noticed — what came to me last, after the hinge and the post and the quiet economy of a tired man doing two small pieces of work before he slept — was his hands. They had moved at the post with the sureness of hands that had done harder work than that, and worse, and had not forgotten how. I set the cloth down on the rail above the range and turned back to the kitchen.

The London coach came in forty minutes late and two passengers short. I was in the yard when it turned under the arch, and I could see from the set of Dobbins's shoulders that it had been a hard run.

He brought the team down and called to me before he was properly stopped. "Road above Hatfield's cut up bad, Mrs. Dale. Three inches of mud and worse where it narrows past the mill."

"How far past the mill?"

"Half a mile, then it eases. But loaded, you'll feel it."

"I'll send word to the Hare." That was enough. He nodded and turned back to his team.

Two of the four passengers got down in a temper. The first, a heavyset man in a damp coat, wanted to know why the journey had taken three hours longer than advertised. I told him the road was responsible, and the road was not mine. He wanted to complain further and I let him have his say, then asked whether he required a room or the dining room. He required the dining room. I sent him in.

The second passenger had a trunk. It came down from the boot badly, one of the leather hinges split along the strap, the whole side of it gaping. The passenger, a woman of perhaps forty with a good wool travelling dress and a look that suggested she had learned to expect things to go wrong, stood over it and waited.

"We can bind that today," I told her. "There's leather and rivets in the tack room. Jem will see to it before you travel on."

"And if it opens on the road?"

"It won't. Not with a rivet through both sides." I met her eye. "I've had trunks mended here before. This is a straightforward repair."

She looked at the trunk, then at me. "Very well."

Jem was already there. I hadn't called him. He crouched beside the trunk and had his hand on the hinge before the woman had finished speaking, and she went indoors, and that was the end of it.

The coachman came round once more before he took the team through to the stables. "One more thing, Mrs. Dale. Coming through Hatfield we passed two men on horseback asking whether anyone had seen a young woman travelling alone. Couldn't say what she looked like, only that she had a bandbox and a green cloak."

I thought of the two missing passengers. "Where did they ask?"

"At the toll. Constables, they looked like. Or close to it."

"Thank you, Dobbins."

He touched his hat and went.

The yard took perhaps ten minutes to clear after that. The ostlers finished with the horses. Jem took the trunk through to the tack room. The cobbles were wet and the yard smelled of horse and cold mud and the particular sharpness of October mornings when the wind comes off the north fields.

I went back through the main door. Nan was behind the bar, wiping glasses. She had been there when the coach came in and she was there still, working through the rack with her back half-turned, but I had seen her watching. I said nothing. There was nothing to say.

The chandler came at ten. Norris, who had been supplying the White Hound since before I took over the lease, and who had a workshop in the town and supplied four other inns on this road that I knew of. He set his ledger on the bar and named his price before he had properly taken off his hat.

"Seven shillings the dozen for the tallow," he said. "And eight and six for the wax."

"Last month you charged six and four for the tallow," I said. "And eight even for the wax."

"Cost has risen. It's the same for everyone on this road, Mrs. Dale."

"I know what you charge at the Hart," I said. "Six and nine for tallow. Eight and two for wax. This morning."

He had his pen out. He set it down again.

"I need forty dozen tallow and eight dozen wax," I said. "I'll pay six and nine for the tallow and eight and two for the wax, same as the Hart. If that's not agreeable you're welcome to take the order back to your cart."

He looked at his ledger. He did not look at me.

"That's a fair price," I said. "It's the going price. You know it and I know it."

He picked up his pen. "Six and nine for the tallow," he said. "Eight and two for the wax."

"Good."

He wrote out the bill and I signed it and he took his hat and went. The candles stayed.

I was halfway back to the office with the copy of the bill when the thought came, the way it sometimes did between one thing and the next. The rent, the ledger, the price of candles—all of it resting on my own name in the accounts, my own signature on the bill. If I married, the inn would not be mine to negotiate for. I noted it the way I noted the state of the north road, and moved on.

I sat down at the desk and opened the ledger.

The spring wore a thin ring on the rock, the way a wife keeps a promise when years turn mean. Cass saw the pale line first. It circled the bowl at knee height, a faint collar where water had rested through dry times. Now the stone gleamed bare. A stake stood where men in blue had driven it in. The stake held a square of paper that scratched in the wind.

He slid from the saddle and let the reins drop. The mare knew how to stand when the air smelled like rifles and starch. No one spoke. The soldiers watched with pale eyes under brims. Their boots had scuffed a tidy gravel pad around the stake. Tidy, that was the word for it. Too tidy for a mouth of water.

Rosa Vigil sat her horse with hands on the horn. Her black skirt had road dust up to the knee and a seam mended with red thread. She looked past the paper. She looked at the cattle down on the slope, where sun caught a false shine in a hollow. The animals nosed and pulled back, ears flicking. Bees went wide of the shine. A dragonfly came, stalled. veered off.

“My father watered here,” she said. “His father before him.”

A captain in clean wool stepped forward without hurrying. He had a jaw that liked orders. He unfolded the paper by its corners and let a few words carry. Authority. Reservation. Public health. Sanitary troughs to be constructed. He read like a man measuring time with his tongue.

Cass did not answer him. He climbed the few steps of rock toward the cairn above the spring, the one that should mark the line where her rights began and ended. Stones rose in a neat cone. He touched the top rock and felt grit bite back.

Lichen broke bright under his thumb. Fresh as a scraped knee.

He lifted one stone, turned it, set it down the way he had found it. The wrongness stayed. It had a look, wrongness. Edges that had not learned wind. Faces sharp where there should be a roundness from years of rain. The cone pointed true in a way that felt false. He kept his hands loose. Men shot quicker when they believed you held answers.

“Sir,” the captain said. “You are standing on federal aid land. Kindly step away from the marker.”

“The marker stepped away from itself,” Cass said.

The captain folded the paper and let a smile sit on his mouth like a storekeeper with the till shut. “I am Captain Arlen Pike, Third Infantry, Fort Constance. This site has been surveyed and recorded. Water will be distributed equitably according to allotments. A trough is to be built at the base of that slope. Your cattle will water orderly. The chaos you have known will end.”

Rosa did not blink. “Chaos is dust that blows when men make rules who have not wintered here.”

Eliseo Vigil had been quiet until then. He stood with one foot in the stirrup and the other on stone. Heat lit in his cheekbones. “You hear her?” he said to Cass. “Do you hear our sister talk while you trace stones like a parson?”

“I hear her,” Cass said.

“And you do nothing,” Eliseo said. “You guided for the Army once. Maybe you still carry their compass in your pocket. Maybe that is why your hands are clean when ours are dry.”

Cass looked at Eliseo’s boot. Grit had worked into the seams, the way it does when a man runs short distances too often. He let the insult pass. He fixed his eye on the cairn again and stepped around it. On the downhill side the base stones showed a faint ruffle of powder where they had been lifted and set. The powder had not had weather yet. He sank a finger in. Sharp jags in the crumbs. Fresh shovel bites leave teeth marks in sand if a man is clumsy.

One of the privates shifted his rifle. Another private was young enough to watch the dragonfly instead of the men. Sergeant Daughtry, the one with square hands, stood where he could reach the captain’s shoulder without moving more than a step. His boots bore a nicked polish, as if he had a rock habit to his walk.

Pike tipped his chin. “Mr. Calder. Yes? I know the name. You guided our column through the Guadalupe a number of years back.”

“I took a wage,” Cass said. “The river was high that season.”

“It is high now in different ways,” Pike said. “People come floodwise. Paper orders the water where need demands.”

Cass walked from the cairn to a juniper that grew on a split shelf above the spring. He pressed his palm to the trunk and felt the heat of afternoon stored in bark. A scar crossed the wood at shoulder height. He used his knife to scrape a hair’s breadth where the scar had healed. The scent rose plain. Resin sweet, not sap bright. Old cut. The scar held a story of a hand that had marked something years ago before the juniper swallowed it slow.

He moved back to the cairn and looked along the ridge where wind came in a certain way. A proper pile takes the push of weather and grows a tell that reads from far off. This cone read like a stone school lesson.

Rosa’s breath went shallow. “Will you say it?”

Cass looked at the captain. “This pile was rebuilt. The top three courses. That paper of yours sits on a stake set where women nurse their babies. The spring is on her side of the line.”

Pike smoothed the corner of his paper. “Mr. Calder, if you have objections, present them at the fort. There is a court day scheduled in three. Bring your claims. Bring your witnesses. Today the Army secures this site for mission use. You and yours will not interfere.”

“Bees refuse your false trough,” Cass said softly. “They know more than paper.”

Eliseo leaned down hard on his horn. “Say it like a man, Cass. Or ride on and do not come back.”

Cass let the heat move up his neck and away. He put his knife away. He knelt and slid two fingers under one stone at the cairn’s base. Gently, he pried a knife’s width. Sand fell. In that thin void he saw a line of dust darker than the rest. Shovel smear. He brought his fingers out and held them up. Grit glinted where quartz had scraped him clean. He showed the captain his palm.

“These were moved,” he said.

Pike looked without looking. He had a way of moving his eyes as if that was enough. He spoke to Sergeant Daughtry. “Make note that Mr. Calder claims disturbance of federal markers.”

“Yes, sir,” Daughtry said. His eyes had the dull shine of a stockman’s spade.

“Rosa Vigil,” Pike said, and turned the paper so it flashed. “You and your people are to vacate the immediate area of the spring. You will find a trough down slope within the week. A quarter mile is not a burden. We have a public health outbreak in town. The spring must be kept free of hoof filth.”

“Where is the outbreak?” Rosa asked.

“In town,” Pike said. “I have reported it up the chain. Orders have been cut.”

“What kind of sickness?” she asked.

“That is not your concern,” he said. “Comply and it will be.”

Eliseo’s face worked. “If you wish me to leave, Captain, lay a hand on me and see what my concern is.”

Cass stood and faced Eliseo, close enough for the young man to smell the sweat on his shirt. “We do not throw a first punch at a spring,” he said.

Eliseo lowered his eyes and spat into the dust. “Then what do we do. Beg?”

“We read the earth,” Cass said. “And we swear to it in the open, with the sun watching.”

Pike’s smile did not move. “Ah. You are one of those romantic surveyors. Shadows and sticks. We have learned lines now, Mr. Calder. We carry them on mules. The law appreciates ink.”

Cass looked at the sky. Noon had already slid past but not by much. The sun still cast short things. He found a straight willow shoot laying with the other camp litter the soldiers had dropped. He sunk one end into crack and let the other point straight up.

“You see that rock on the shelf,” he said. “The one old men call the anvil because thunder likes it. When the sun stands true, the shadow of that stick will kiss the notch on its south edge. It has done that since men cut the first corral in this country. That line is not mine. It is the sky’s. Your cone sits a hand’s width to the east of where it sat last summer.”

The young private forgot himself. He looked at the rock and at the stick with the hunger of boys who like to see tricks earned. Sergeant Daughtry did not look. He watched Cass’s hands.

“You will step away from that stake now,” Pike said. “We will not destroy your illusions. Time will do it.”

Rosa unclenched her fingers. “Captain Pike,” she said. “If you hold this spring today, you will hold it while our cattle nose mud that cannot give water. We will hold our tempers today because this is a holy place for us. Tomorrow we will go to your fort with witnesses and our papers. But know that our papers come last. We will go first with a man who can read what the country wrote before any of us arrived.”

Pike tucked the paper smooth and pinned it with his thumb. “You are warned. Trespass again and you will be detained.”

Eliseo straightened in the saddle. “Detain me now,” he said.

Cass shot him a look that held enough years to slow a colt. “Ride down to the false trough,” he said. “Bring me a handful of that shine.”

Eliseo’s mouth worked but he put his heel to his horse and slid off the shelf. He rode to the hollow with the false gleam and dismounted, then crouched as if the ground might answer him in a whisper. He dipped his hand and came back with water pouring through his fingers like a joke. No smell of life on his skin. He sniffed, scowled, and rubbed the liquid away on his trouser seam.

“It is seep water,” Cass said. “It sits shallow, warms quick. dies. Not a gnat is foolish enough to trust it. Your trough will sit there. Your captain will point to it with his paper and talk about order while bees fly past looking for what is real.”

The captain’s face did change and also Sergeant Daughtry’s moved a degree. It was not belief. It was calculation.

Tala Whitebird came in quiet then. No one heard her ride up. She slid to the ground and led her pony to shade and stood a long time with a palm on its neck. Only then did she look at Pike. “Captain,” she said, and it had the respect of a woman who had buried her share and knew the math of grief. “We have an old agreement with the town. One day’s water a week for the trough by the mission. The agreement mentions this spring by name in our tongue.”

“Agreements lapse,” Pike said. “Nations change. Charities are always revisited.”

“Stones remember longer,” she said.

Pike nodded to Daughtry. “Post a board, Sergeant.”

They hammered the board to the stake. The board read in paint. Military reservation. No stock. No cutting. No fires. Violators will be arrested.

A dragonfly sketched a blue loop and returned to the true seam under the shelf where water still sang, faint and hidden.

Cass walked down to the lip. He squatted and listened. He heard three notes. The drip that counts days. The faint run through stone veins. The whisper that water makes when it has a place to go. He stood and faced everyone, his shoulders square. He was tired of the small talk that men use when they wish to eat time.

“I will walk this boundary at noon in three days,” he said. “I will do it with anyone who can stand the sun and the truth. No papers. Just eyes and hands. We will carry a chain to measure our steps. We will carry a knife to read scars. We will bring a saw to take a ring from a limb if words turn ugly. And we will plant a pole and watch the shadow cross the rock at the hour when the sky does not lie. If the line points where this cone sits now, I will ride to the fort alone and you can lock me up for the trouble of my words. If the line points where Rosa says it does, you will move your stake and let the spring breathe.”

Pike laughed without sound. “You do enjoy theater.”

Tala stood with her hands behind her back. “It is not theater,” she said. “It is a school where every man can see the same lesson. Even soldiers like schools. Bring your men, Captain. Bring the ones you trust. Let them stand in the open and witness.”

Pike considered for a time and then folded the paper again as if that gave him comfort. “I am a generous man,” he said. “I will humor this performance. Noon in three. But understand this. The Board remains. The guard remains. If any of you touch the marker, you will be dealt with.”

Eliseo slid from his saddle and stepped toward him. “I will not wait three days while you feed your mules from my sister’s fist.”

Cass put his hand on Eliseo’s breastbone and pushed once, slow. The younger man stepped back like a man who has been reminded that blood and breath are rented, not owned. “You will wait,” Cass said, “because in three days a thing will happen that your anger cannot give you. Men will see. That is how lies end here.”

Pike lifted his eyebrows a hair. “And if I order my men to arrest you for threatening an officer?”

Cass smiled with one side of his mouth. “Do it now and your court day begins with a free lesson in why a wrist should not guide a boundary. Do it in three and the lesson will have students.”

The captain looked at the young private with the honest eyes. He looked at Daughtry. He looked at Tala, who had not shifted her feet. He looked last at Rosa, who would outstare a cliff if it took all of summer. He chose to put the moment in his pocket.

“Three days,” he said. “Noon. I will bring a clerk to record your show.”

Cass nodded once. He tilted his head toward the false trough. “Take your clerk there,” he said. “Teach him why flies do not love a lie.”

No one laughed. The soldiers moved off a few paces and pretended to be busy while they listened. Pike tied a blue ribbon to the stake and made a neat square knot. He believed in the square of things. It comforted him when the land would not.

Rosa took a breath that loosened her shoulders. “Thank you,” she said. She did speak to Cass as if he had given her a gift. She spoke like a woman who had been handed a job to do. “We will bring Old Vidal. His hands shake and also his eye can still lay a chain right. We will bring a saw for the witness tree over by the dry wash.”

“Bring Boone,” Eliseo said. spat again. “He rides with the Army now and likes a payday. Let him watch while I pound that cone back into the captain’s teeth.”

“Boone sees what horses see,” Cass said. “He knows where a leg will snap before the ground does. He can stand in the sun with us. If the truth burns him, he can ride out of it.”

Tala touched the juniper scar with two fingers. “Bring a pan,” she said calmly. “If the captain grows thirsty at noon, we will share what the spring gives. That will be its own lesson if he has the wit to learn.”

Eliseo snorted. “I will share with a man who swings at me after.”

“You may have that chance,” Tala said, and her mouth had a line in it that could cut vines.

Cass walked to the shelf again. He knelt and pressed his ear to stone. He heard the spring speak faint as breath. He set the top stone on the cairn a finger’s width off center. He could do it without anyone seeing it done. Then he decided not to. He let the wrong cone stand where it stood. The lie needed witnesses to end it with grace.

They rode away by the narrow path that crossed the slope below the shelf. Cass brought the mare down without sliding the stones that held the trail. Dust lifted in a prayer. The cattle milled short and then settled. Skimmers found the shadowed seam and drew light lines on the water. The false hollow brightened with heat and went brassy. A horsefly landed and flew off quick, as if it had touched a skillet.

They rode without talk until the ridge hid the soldiers from sight. Eliseo broke first.

“You should have let me swing,” he said. “The man has a face that invites a lesson.”

“And fists make poor chalk,” Cass said.

“I hate you,” Eliseo said. “I hate the way you go slow with the truth. It is ugly.”

Cass smiled again with one corner of his mouth. “You will like it when it has teeth.”

They reached a stand of apple trees on the Vigil place below the mesa. The fruit was small and hard in this dry year. The trunks bore old scars where boys had carved letters. Rosa dismounted and tied her reins and stood for a time with both palms on a trunk as if to steady herself. She lifted her head and spoke to the place, not to them.

“Three days,” she said. “We do not sleep in those days. We gather the men with steady feet. We clean the chain. We lay out food for the ones who will travel. We ask the priest for a prayer that is humble enough not to vex the spring.”

Eliseo kicked at a fallen apple. “Ask for bullets while you are at it.”

“There will be time for that if men choose it,” Rosa said. “I choose a different first move.”

Cass looked up through the leaves. Heat bent the world a little. A hawk sat the blue and made a tight circle. He remembered a day in a different uniform when he had guided a column to high ground, and a captain with a different name had held a paper that said ride here. He remembered a young man who had wanted to be right as often as he was brave. He remembered the first time he set a pole at noon and watched the shadow draw true and felt his chest go quiet with a kind of peace.

He walked to the barn and found a coil of chain on a peg. He let it unspool across the packed dirt and straightened the links with his foot. He ran the chain through his hands once, slow. Metal can be a liar when it is stretched by men who want it to say a number they can live with. This chain felt honest. A link with a flat on one side told him a story of a blacksmith who had worked hot and tired one night. He liked that story.

Tala appeared in the doorway. She had dust on her cheekbone and a nick on a knuckle. “You spoke of a pole,” she said. “The rock you named will do?”

“It has done for years,” Cass said. “Old Vidal will remember how it looks when the shadow grazes. He is particular.”

“Good,” she said. She watched him for a moment. “The captain called the noon lesson theater. It is not. It is church. Some men cannot tell the difference.”

“Maybe we sing then,” Cass said.

She smiled with only her eyes. “Sing low so the spring can hear. If you sing loud, you scare the gnats and they will take offense and tell their friends.”

Eliseo threw a piece of apple at a post and missed. “I will carry the saw,” he said.

“You will carry it. you will not swing it until we have words to cover the cut,” Rosa said.

He nodded and looked anywhere but at her.

Cass lifted the chain and coiled it again. He hung it on the peg and checked the brass compass in his vest out of habit. The needle spoke a language he knew, but out here he trusted other teachers. He snapped the case shut with a quiet anger that felt like hunger sharpening into work.

Outside, a breeze came up from the wash and worked through the apples. Leaves tossed a brighter underside. The day’s heat settled into the kind of slow that asks men to decide what sort of men they are. In three days he would plant a pole where a spring had taught patience to stone. He would watch a shadow cross a notch while bees did their own reading of truth. He would speak plain where any ear could hear.

He would swear himself to that line with all the weight a life can put on a word.

“Three days,” he said, and the word felt like a saddle cinch pulled snug. He had told himself he would not be the one to keep lines, not again. Yet here was a job that fit the shape of his hands. Rosa cleaned a tin pan at the pump with a kind of prayer in the way she scrubbed. Tala wrapped a rag around her knuckle and watched the door as if trouble could be seen in the dust. Eliseo took the saw down and tested a tooth with his thumb and bled a little and did not flinch.

Cass stepped into the yard and let the sun find his face. He looked at the mesa and saw the anvil rock taking light on its crown. The spring under it would keep its song whether men wrote or argued. He felt a weight shift inside him. Not dread. Not joy. Work settling into place.

“Three days,” he said again, to make sure the world knew he meant it. The mare lifted her head and flicked an ear. A bee worked the apple blossoms late for reasons of its own.

Up on the mesa a blue ribbon stirred on a stake that had no right to that kind of color. It would stir three days more. Then the sun would teach. Men would choose whether to learn.

Cass found a straight stick and set it by the door so he would not forget the feel of grain in his hand.

He slept that night with his boots by the bed and his hat on a peg where he could reach it blind. The wind ran small errands through the brush. Somewhere a coyote made three short barks and then quit, as if he had changed his mind about the story he meant to tell. The world kept its own clocks. Hooves. Weather. Light sliding along stone. He let those clocks wind him down and then wind him back up before dawn.

The second day he walked the wash with Old Vidal and listened to the old man mumble to rocks like friends he had not seen since the spring before last. Vidal touched the juniper scar and nodded and said the year he first saw that cut, his youngest boy had broken a tooth. The memory had fixed the date in his head. They cut a ring from a dead limb farther off and counted in the shade of a hat crown. The count matched Vidal’s year. The count made Eliseo breathe faster.

On the third morning Cass packed the chain and the stick and a tin pan and two apples. He saddled with the same care he had the day he first rode for pay with a column that believed bullets move policy faster than words. He checked the cinch twice. He checked his temper a third time and found it sitting quiet where he had left it.

They rode toward the spring as the sun rose. Men fell in behind without being told. Some wore hats with old sweat rings like maps of seasons. Some carried the memory of being told once to stand aside because paper said so. Tala rode with the kind of posture that takes its authority from the way a tree grows.

When they came up through the rocks near the shelf, Captain Pike was already there. He had more men today. He had a clerk with a ledger and a pen that clicked neat against the board. He had a look that said the morning would be his.

Cass set the pole in the crack and planted it firm. He folded his hands and waited for the sun to climb the small measure it had yet to climb. When you work with light you learn the worth of stillness. He stood without speaking. The wind ran a finger along the rim of his hat and moved on. A bee found the seam and told its friends. The telling brought more life than any word on paper ever had.

Pike shifted once. The clerk cleared his throat and held his pen like a weapon. Eliseo shifted the saw and breathed like a man getting to the first rise in a day’s ride. Rosa stared at the shelf without blinking. Tala watched everyone and everything and also nothing at all.

The shadow shortened. It steadied. It reached, thin as truth, toward the notch on the anvil rock.

Cass did not look at faces. He looked at stone. When the line touched the notch, he spoke one word.

“Now.”

He did not raise his voice. He did not have to. Men leaned forward as if the world had whispered to them by name.

The pole’s shadow kissed the notch and lay quiet. The cone sat a hand’s width east of where it belonged.

Somewhere below, where the false trough had baked in the sun, a horsefly landed, tasted dust. lifted away as if it had remembered something important too late.

Cass breathed out. He did not look at the captain yet. He looked at the spring and saw the ring on the stone as clear as a woman’s hand.

“I will reset your cone,” he said. “I will do it gentle. After we move your stake.”

He picked up the first stone and waited in case anyone wished to make a fool of himself in front of the sky.

No one moved.

He smiled without showing teeth and began.

---

CONTINUITY LEDGER (W1)

Line of proof advanced: cairn disturbance shown; noon-shadow alignment; false trough (seep) evidence.

Public witnesses gained: Rosa Vigil, Eliseo Vigil, Tala Whitebird, Pvt. (unnamed), Sgt. Daughtry, Capt. Pike's clerk.

Debts or favors logged: Cass to Rosa (boundary stand), community to Cass (no first punch).

Pike escalation: Board posted; guard maintained; court day scheduled; ribbon marker.

Next set piece seeded: noon boundary walk; possible orchard raid; juniper scar ring-cut; fort confrontation.

THE LAST RETREAT

Chapter One: The White Hound

The grate was dead. I rebuilt it.

Whoever had banked it last night had done a pitiful job of it. I didn’t waste the time to wonder who. There were only three of us and the odds were even enough.

*     *     *

Forty years of coaching traffic had worn the flagstones uneven. Forty years had smoked the ceiling beams to the colour of old bone.

The taproom had six tables, a bar of elm I’d sanded myself after Jem pointed out that the old pine had gone soft enough to splinter. The ceiling was low enough that any man over six feet ducked on instinct when he crossed the threshold. The bar top was good now. Smooth, the grain running even, the knots sealed against spilled ale. I’d worked it on a Tuesday in March because March is the dead month on the Great North Road. There had been nothing else pressing except the damp patch above the third window and seventeen other things I couldn’t fix without spending money I didn’t have. I’d done the bar instead.

The damp patch was still there.

The lease hung on its nail behind the bar, where my grandmother had put it in the year I was born. Stainforth’s name was at the top of it. Abel Dale’s name below that, though Abel Dale had been dead sixteen years and had never, in any practical sense, run a single day of this inn. My name was nowhere on it. Three women had kept the White Hound standing through two wars, a failed harvest, and my father’s inability to keep his accounts and none of our names were on the lease.

I noticed the nail needed straightening. I straightened it. I hung the lease back on it and turned to get the bread going.

*     *     *

Nan came in at half past five tying her cap and saying, without preamble and without looking up from the strings, “The coach company’s switching agents. I heard it last night from Dawes at the Bull. A Chipping Norton man, apparently. There’ll be new rates.”

“The new rates — up or down.”

“Up.” She tied the final bow, tucked a strand of hair that wouldn’t behave, and reached past me for the apron on the hook. “Dawes said the Hatfield coach is already paying sixpence more a head.”

“Dawes is a gossip.”

“Dawes is a gossip who hasn’t been wrong yet.” She pulled the apron over her head and turned to face me. “I’m only saying.”

I cracked the first egg against the rim of the bowl. The second.

“We always adjust.” Nan said it pleasantly.

*     *     *

The morning coach arrived at seven with nine passengers and a coachman in a temper about the state of the road past Stevenage. The yard became itself when a coach came in. Jem was already at the horses and the lead pair were haltered and the first trunk was down from the roof. The passengers were stepping out blinking into the cold and the coachman was calling for water and Nan was there with the manifest. The yard smell was horse and leather and dead October leaves.

“The left rear wheel wants looking at,” Jem said, when I came past with a trunk for the luggage room. He said it to the wheel.

I already knew. I’d heard it come in. “How bad is it.”

“It won’t make York.” 

“I’ll tell him before he loads back up,” I said.

“I’ll tell him.”

“Tell him before he loads back up or we’ll be having a worse conversation.”

Jem went back to the wheel.

*     *     *

The coachman’s name was Hartley and he was working himself toward a scene about the trunk. It belonged to a woman travelling to Biggleswade, was the size of a small cottage, and was currently in my luggage room because Hartley had declined to carry it up himself on account of it being, in his opinion, the woman’s own business to make arrangements for her own property. I waited until he’d finished. “Mr Hartley. It’s the coachman’s responsibility to the first interior door.”

Hartley looked at me.

Hartley opened his mouth. I looked at him until he closed it.

He picked up the trunk.

*     *     *

The woman from Biggleswade had requested the trunk be brought to her room, immediately, and had communicated this request with confidence. I arranged for it. She said the room was cold, which it was not — I had laid the fire myself — and that the sheets wanted airing, which they did not, and that she hoped the establishment would be rather more attentive than it had yet appeared. I said I hoped so too. She gathered as much composure as she could locate and swept into the room. I closed the door behind her with exquisite care.

*     *     *

The chandler came at eleven. His name was Murchett. He’d supplied the White Hound for four years, and for the first three of those years the arrangement had been satisfactory, and for the fourth it had become an occasion for him to stand in my kitchen rearranging his sample box and telling me that costs had risen.

He set the box on the table. He looked at the window. He put his hands in his coat pockets and then took them out again.

“It’s the tallow,” he said. “It’s the prices out of London.”

“You told me that in August. How much worse.”

He told me. He looked at the window again when he said it.

I went to the shelf and took down the ledger. I opened it on the table between us, my finger on the column for October last year, which showed me his margin at the old rate and showed me, more importantly, that I knew what his margin was. “Mr Murchett.”

“Miss Dale.”

“I can pay the August rate and I can go to Baldock.”

“Baldock’s too far. Fourteen miles.”

“Eleven. I’ve done it. I can send Jem.”

He was quiet for a moment. His hand settled on the counter, not quite gripping it.

He looked at the ledger. He looked at me. He said, “I might manage to hold at the September figure. Given the volume you take.”

“August,” I said.

He picked up the sample box. He moved it six inches to the left and back again. “August, then. Miss Dale.”

“Thank you, Mr Murchett.”

The ledger went back on the shelf. My hands were tight on it when I set it down.

*     *     *

The two men in the taproom had been at the bench by the fire since the late-morning coach came in, which made it three hours, and in that time they had consumed enough of the ordinary ale to make the dispute about who had scuffed whose boot feel considerably more significant than it actually was. The taller one had the other’s collar by the time I came through the door. A stool scraped. I knew the sound of a stool scraping.

“Put him down,” I said.

The tall one sat down.

His companion rubbed his collar and looked at my face and decided against it. One of them muttered something into his ale. I heard it and chose not to.

I went back behind the bar. Jem was in the far corner of the room, leaning on the wall with his arms crossed and he looked at me and I looked at him, and that look contained everything: the frequency of it, the tedium of it, the question of how men managed to make a quarrel about a boot into an occasion requiring a woman to stand in a doorway like an army.

*     *     *

He came to the kitchen door at half past three, just after the afternoon coaches had gone through and before the evening rush, when the yard was quiet and the smell of the stockpot had been working at the air for two hours. He knocked. Quietly, and then again.

I opened it.

The collar on his coat was old infantry, but it had been let out at the shoulders. His boots were repaired, the toe-stitching recent, the heel leather not — someone had done what they could for them, and it showed. He stood straight in the doorway, his hands at his sides, his eyes at my shoulder rather than my face.

He asked if there was work.

I looked at him for thirty seconds. “I’ve a floor wants sweeping in the luggage room,” I said. “The broom is on the left side of the door. When you’re done, come in.”

He swept the floor. I set bread on the table and ladled a bowl of stockpot beside it and said nothing while I shaped the next morning’s loaves, and he ate, and neither of us made a show of it.

After a while, he said, “The road was frozen past Stevenage.”

“It’s been this cold since Sunday.”

He nodded. He ate the last of the bread. He didn’t say it was good, and I didn’t say anything about giving it, and we were both satisfied with this.

He finished the bread and thanked me and went. I watched his boots go through the kitchen door and then they were gone, and the yard was empty, and the afternoon light was already failing.

*     *     *

The bolt on the front door had stuck — the frame around it swelled with the autumn damp every year — and required a firm shoulder and a particular angle; I leaned and pushed and felt it give with a scrape of iron on iron. The window in the taproom that had never quite hung straight caught in its frame when I pulled it and let in a thread of cold air past the latch, as it always did, until I reached up and eased the top corner into the groove. I snuffed the last candle and the smell of tallow hung in the air for half a minute after.

I wiped down the bar. It was already clean — Nan had done it after the last customers went — but I wiped it anyway, the cloth moving in the same circles, the elm smooth under my hands. There was a gouge near the far end, an inch long, just where the bar curved. I ran my thumb over it. A keg, I thought. Thursday’s delivery. The bottom rim of the big barrel, the way Jem tilts it when he’s bringing it through alone — he tilts it rather than carry it, which is the sensible solution, but it catches the corner sometimes. I’d have to talk to him about the Thursday delivery. Whether he could manage the heavy cask, whether we needed to ask for the smaller measure.

The lease was on its nail.

My grandmother had held this place for twenty years through a husband’s death and the mill closures and the bad winter of ‘84. There was no passing traffic for a week and she’d fed the staff from the cellar stores and not lost a single booking. My mother had finished the dinner service the night she died. And me.

I wrung out the cloth. I hung it on the hook.

The hook was one my mother put in.

*     *     *

The letter was on the shelf beside the ledger, where I’d set it when it arrived that afternoon. I’d seen the seal before I’d seen the direction, and I’d set it on the shelf and gone back to the afternoon’s business, and I’d been knowing ever since.

Stainforth’s seal. His man had brought it.

I looked at it for a moment. I picked it up. The wax was smooth under my thumb, the device pressed clean.

I set the letter on the shelf beside the ledger.

I took the candle and went to bed.

The letter was on the table where I had left it, face up, his handwriting small and even and courteous as the handwriting of a man who had never in his life had to write anything urgent. Mr Stainforth wished to discuss the terms. He hoped the hour would be convenient. He remained, as ever, my obedient servant. I had read it twice last night and once at four this morning, and I did not intend to read it again. The kitchen was cold. The fire had not yet taken. I laid the sticks the way my mother had laid them, smaller to larger, breath of air between, and struck the flint harder than I needed to because my hands were stiff and the cut on my index finger had opened again in the night. Six weeks until the quarter's rent. Sixty-eight pounds, eleven shillings, and the coaching office's receipt for October not yet paid out because the Royal Mail paid slow and paid late and considered a woman's patience one of the perquisites of the arrangement. I knew the arithmetic. I had known it since September. Stainforth's letter was only the wind at my back, and I had been bracing against wind all autumn. The ledger was still on the table where I had shut it last night. I did not open it. I knew what was in it. I drank my tea standing up. Then I went and did the morning. I swept the taproom until the boards showed pale at the edges. I wiped down the bar rail. I checked the cellar stair for the loose tread and checked the barrels for the weep we'd mended last week and found it dry. I turned the bread in the larder. I set Nan to the upstairs fires. I did every task a little harder than it needed to be done, which was how I knew I was afraid. My mother used to say fear was a useful servant and a miserable master. She said a great many things. Most of them I heard now in my own voice. I heard the carriage before I saw it. A private carriage at walking pace, no outrider, harness in good order. The wheels on our gravel made a cleaner sound than the mail coaches did, because the mail coaches came loaded and his came empty but for him. I met him in the doorway of my own inn. "Miss Dale." He removed his hat. "I hope I have not come at an inconvenient hour." "Not at all, Mr Stainforth." "And Mrs Dale — your aunt. She keeps well?" "She does. Thank you for asking." His coat was dark green wool, cut close, and smelled faintly of cedar and the good kind of tobacco. He drew off his gloves one finger at a time and folded them together and held them in his left hand. He did not look past me into the taproom, which was the courtesy of a man who intended to look at it at length later. "A raw morning," he said. "It will rain before noon." "You think so?" "The sky is flat and the wind has dropped. It will rain." He smiled slightly at that, as if I had confirmed something pleasant about the countryside. "I shall not keep you long. I so rarely have the chance to see the place properly, and I find I am negligent in my duties as landlord if I do not, from time to time, take advantage of an hour. You will indulge me, I hope." He was not asking. I stepped back and let him in. He walked the taproom the way a man walks a field he is thinking of buying. He did not hurry. He stopped once at the long table by the window and ran his thumb along the grain, where the oak had silvered from decades of elbows. He stopped again at the bar. He put his hand on the rail, not to lean, simply to put it there. He looked up at the beam and then down at the boards under his boot. "Oak," he said. "Yes." "Original to the building?" "As far as I know." "Your grandmother's time." "Before that." He nodded, as though I had given him a number he could enter into a column. "How many rooms do you let in an average week, Miss Dale?" "Eighteen, this time of year. Twenty in summer." "And the coaching trade? The Edinburgh mail still comes through?" "Every night but Sunday. The Leeds and York three times a week. The Glasgow, twice." "And they are satisfied, your coaching companies? The drivers, the passengers?" "The companies pay on the nail. The drivers come back. If they were not satisfied they would not." "Admirable." He moved on. He opened the cellar door.
